# Part 5 — Rendering Platform & Runway Integration

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

> Single Source of Truth (SSOT). Expanded to Master Specification granularity (Part 6 parity).

---

# Part 5.0.1 — Specification v2.1 Agent Review / Rendering Boundary

## Governing rule

Agent Review Mode is a planning review integration, not a rendering provider integration. It shall complete before `render-approval` and before any Runway submission, monitoring, download, composition, or provider-spending operation.

```text
sealed planning bundle
  -> deterministic planning gates
  -> Agent Review (optional by mode)
  -> human render-approval bound to plan hash and review-report hash
  -> Rendering Platform / Runway
```

## Separation of providers

The OpenAI Agents SDK integration shall be exposed behind an `AgentReviewProvider` owned by the planning review boundary. It shall not implement, extend, or be registered as a `RenderProvider`. Runway remains a rendering provider. Provider capability discovery, render prompt assembly, submit, monitor, download, retry, reconciliation, and cost accounting remain unchanged.

The Review Agent shall never:

* call Runway or any rendering provider;
* produce a render task, provider prompt, image, video, or final asset;
* read `RUNWAY_API_KEY` or any render-provider credential;
* change render strategy, task identity, manifest history, or cache entries;
* approve rendering or publication;
* retry or compensate a render operation.

`OPENAI_API_KEY` shall not be available to render, monitor, download, compose, package, or publish workers. `RUNWAY_API_KEY` shall not be available to the Agent Review job.

## Rendering admission in review mode

The rendering preflight shall verify all existing requirements and, when `INSYNERGY_AGENT_REVIEW_MODE=review`, additionally verify:

1. `agent-review-report.json` exists and validates against Part 9;
2. the report input hashes match the exact artifacts referenced by the Execution Plan;
3. the selected report content hash matches the hash recorded by Story Approval;
4. the approval record binds the same Execution Plan content hash and Agent Review report content hash;
5. the report is not superseded, corrupted, or associated with another build;
6. no deterministic planning gate is failed.

A valid agent `PASS` without human approval is insufficient. An approval for one plan/report pair is invalid for any other pair. `MANUAL_REVIEW_REQUIRED`, `UNAVAILABLE`, or `ERROR` shall block provider contact until Part 7 permits and records an explicit human exception. A missing report, schema failure, unresolved evidence, deterministic gate failure, or hash mismatch is structurally non-waivable and shall block provider contact unconditionally.

In `off` mode, the rendering preflight shall not require an Agent Review artifact and shall preserve existing behavior.

## Failure and retry isolation

Agent Review timeout, invalid output, quota failure, authentication failure, or service unavailability is classified as a planning review failure. It shall not enter the Render Queue, increment a render attempt, trigger render compensation, or consume a Runway retry budget. Re-running Agent Review shall use the review identity and immutability rules in Parts 1 and 6.

## Render identity and cache invariants

Agent Review provenance is approval evidence and shall not be inserted into provider prompts or render cache keys. An unchanged approved Execution Plan produces the same render task identities regardless of review model metadata. If a different review report is selected, only approval becomes invalid until the new plan/report bundle is approved; existing unexecuted render tasks remain non-admitted.

## ADRs

| ADR | Decision |
| --- | --- |
| ADR-5.0.1-A | Agent Review is outside the Rendering Platform and outside the `RenderProvider` interface. |
| ADR-5.0.1-B | OpenAI and Runway credentials are mutually isolated by job and environment. |
| ADR-5.0.1-C | Rendering admission verifies the approved plan/report hash pair in review mode. |
| ADR-5.0.1-D | Agent Review failures never consume rendering attempts, retries, or compensation budgets. |
| ADR-5.0.1-E | Agent provenance does not affect render task or render-cache identity. |

## Acceptance criteria

This boundary is complete when no Agent Review code can invoke a render provider, no render worker can read the OpenAI credential, review failures leave the Render Queue untouched, a mismatched or absent report blocks Runway contact in `review` mode, a non-pass disposition cannot proceed without a permitted attributable exception, and `off` mode requires no report.

---

# Part 5.1.1 — Purpose & Rendering Boundary

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the **purpose of the Rendering Platform** and fixes the **boundary** that separates rendering from every creative layer above it.

The Rendering Platform exists for one reason: to convert an **already approved** Storyboard into cinematic visual assets, faithfully and reproducibly, without altering a single creative decision.

It is the **execution layer** of the platform. It is not a creative layer.

Everything upstream of rendering — Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Shot Planning, Render Strategy — decides *what* the trailer says and *how* it is composed. The Rendering Platform decides only *how that fixed plan is materialised into pixels*, within the technical and cost envelope it is given.

This section establishes, as normative law for all of Part 5:

* what the Rendering Platform **is** responsible for;
* what the Rendering Platform **is not** responsible for;
* the exact boundary between rendering and the approved creative artifacts (Story, Screenplay, Storyboard);
* the **Story First** invariant — creative truth is decided upstream and is immutable at render time;
* the **execution-only** invariant — rendering never authors, reinterprets, or repairs story content.

Every other subsection of Part 5 inherits these boundaries. Where a later subsection describes strategy selection (**5.2**), provider integration (**5.3**), queue and lifecycle mechanics (**5.4**), prompt assembly (**5.5**), or validation (**5.6**), it operates **inside** the boundary fixed here and may never widen it.

The Rendering Platform is a disciplined transformer. Its dignity is in its restraint.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the definitional purpose of the Rendering Platform;
* the classification of rendering as an execution layer;
* the rendering boundary against Story, Screenplay, and Storyboard;
* the enumerated responsibilities rendering **holds**;
* the enumerated responsibilities rendering **must never assume**;
* the "Story First / execution-only" boundary as governing principle;
* the design objectives that make the boundary enforceable (`RBD-301`+);
* the architectural invariants that make the boundary auditable (`RBD-401`+);
* the boundary decision records (`ADR-401`..`ADR-403`).

This section does **not** specify:

* the rendering design principles and their full elaboration (defined in **5.1.2**);
* the Rendering Platform's position in the wider system architecture (defined in **5.1.3**);
* the concrete input and output artifact contracts (defined in **5.1.4**);
* the end-to-end rendering pipeline stages (defined in **5.1.5**);
* render strategy models and selection (defined in **5.2**);
* provider interfaces and the Runway adapter (defined in **5.3**);
* queue, state machine, retry, and timeout mechanics (defined in **5.4**);
* deterministic prompt assembly and injection (defined in **5.5**);
* asset and visual quality validation (defined in **5.6**).

This section is **definitional**. It fixes meaning and boundary. The mechanisms that honour these definitions live in the sections referenced above. Where this section and a later section appear to conflict, this section's boundary and invariants prevail; the later section is in error and must be corrected.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle as the root of all of Part 5.

> **Story is decided first, upstream, and forever. Rendering only executes it — it never authors, reinterprets, or repairs it.**

This is the **Story First / execution-only** boundary.

Two clauses bind together and neither may be relaxed:

1. **Story First.** By the time any render task exists, the creative truth — narrative, pacing, dialogue, blocking, camera language, visual style — has already been decided and approved upstream and is **immutable** for the duration of rendering.
2. **Execution-only.** The Rendering Platform's entire function is to materialise that fixed truth into validated assets. It has no authority to add, remove, reorder, reinterpret, or "improve" any creative decision — not even to rescue a failing render.

A corollary follows directly:

> **When rendering cannot faithfully execute the approved plan, it fails closed and escalates — it never substitutes its own creative judgement.**

If a shot cannot be rendered to specification, the correct outcomes are retry, fallback to a *pre-authorised* cheaper strategy for the *same* creative intent, or escalation to Manual Review. Inventing a different shot, softening the plan, or emitting a "close enough" asset is **prohibited**. A partial or creatively-altered trailer is worse than no trailer (Fail Closed, elaborated in **5.1.2**).

The boundary is not a guideline. It is the load-bearing invariant of the entire Rendering Platform.

---

# Architectural Position

The Rendering Platform sits strictly **downstream** of all creative approval and strictly **upstream** of composition and publication.

```text
        CREATIVE LAYERS  (author truth — immutable at render time)
        ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
                     Story Engine
                          │
                          ▼
                   Screenplay Engine
                          │
                          ▼
                   Storyboard Engine
                          │
                     ✓ Approved  ── Quality Gate ──►  (blocks if not passed)
                          │
        ══════════════════╪══════════════════  RENDERING BOUNDARY
                          │       (nothing below may mutate anything above)
                          ▼
        EXECUTION LAYER  (materialise truth — never author it)
        ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
                 Render Strategy Planner
                          │
                          ▼
                  Rendering Platform
                          │
                 ┌────────┴─────────┐
                 ▼                  ▼
          Provider Renders    Deterministic Assets
        (Runway / motion)     (SVG / typography)
                 │                  │
                 └────────┬─────────┘
                          ▼
                  Asset Validation
                          │
                          ▼
                   Asset Repository
                          │
                          ▼
                   FFmpeg Composer
```

Three facts are fixed by this position:

* **The boundary is a one-way membrane.** Approved artifacts flow *down* into rendering as read-only inputs. Rendered assets flow *down* toward composition. Nothing produced at or below the rendering boundary may flow *up* and mutate Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard.
* **Rendering never starts early.** No render task may exist until the upstream Storyboard has passed its Quality Gate (elaborated as a principle in **5.1.2**). The membrane opens only after approval.
* **Rendering is the sole materialiser.** Every publishable visual asset in a trailer is produced through the Rendering Platform. There is no side channel by which creative layers directly emit final assets, and no channel by which rendering emits story.

---

# The Rendering Boundary

The rendering boundary is the formal line between **creative authorship** and **technical execution**. This subsection states it exhaustively, because every later ambiguity in Part 5 resolves against it.

## Boundary Definition

```text
ABOVE THE BOUNDARY                 BELOW THE BOUNDARY
(Creative Layers)                  (Execution Layer)
──────────────────────────────    ──────────────────────────────
Decides WHAT the trailer says      Decides HOW to render the plan
Owns narrative & meaning           Owns pixels & technical fidelity
Owns pacing & rhythm               Owns throughput & scheduling
Owns dialogue & voice              Owns prompt assembly from refs
Owns camera language               Owns provider camera parameters
Owns visual style intent           Owns visual style enforcement
Immutable once approved            Reproducible and disposable
Authored by Story/Screenplay/      Executed by Rendering Platform
Storyboard engines                 and its providers
```

## The Three Immutable Upstream Artifacts

The boundary protects three approved artifacts. Rendering treats each as **read-only, authoritative, and immutable** for the lifetime of a build.

| Artifact       | Owns                                                        | Rendering may                                  | Rendering may never                                            |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Story**      | Narrative, theme, message, arc                             | Read for provenance only                       | Re-tell, summarise, extend, or infer new narrative            |
| **Screenplay** | Scene structure, dialogue, pacing intent                   | Read scene/shot ordering                       | Rewrite dialogue, re-time scenes, change pacing               |
| **Storyboard** | Shot list, blocking, camera plan, emotion, style intent    | Read each shot as a render specification       | Add, drop, reorder, merge, split, or reinterpret shots        |

Rendering consumes these artifacts strictly through the input contract defined in **5.1.4**. It resolves references (characters, locations, camera, style) into deterministic prompts as defined in **5.5**. At no point does resolution grant authority to *change* what was referenced.

## What Rendering IS Responsible For

The Rendering Platform is responsible for, and only for, the faithful technical materialisation of the approved plan:

* accepting approved Storyboard artifacts as read-only render inputs;
* generating exactly one render task per shot (see **5.4** and **5.1.5**);
* selecting a render **strategy** per shot from the approved strategy assignment (see **5.2**);
* assembling **deterministic** provider prompts from immutable references — character, location, camera, lighting, blocking, emotion, shot purpose, visual style (see **5.5.1**);
* submitting render work to interchangeable providers behind a common interface (see **5.3**);
* scheduling, ordering, and bounding render execution (see **5.4.1**);
* monitoring provider jobs, downloading assets, and retrying transient failures (see **5.4.5**);
* validating assets technically and for visual quality (see **5.6.1**, **5.6.2**);
* caching and reusing previously validated assets for unchanged shots (see **5.6.3**);
* enforcing the approved visual style as a technical constraint (see **5.5.5**);
* emitting the render manifest, results, and validated asset repository for composition (see **5.1.4**, **5.4.4**);
* failing closed and escalating to Manual Review when faithful execution is impossible.

## What Rendering IS NOT Responsible For

The Rendering Platform must **never** assume any authorship responsibility. The following are permanently outside the boundary:

* creating, extending, or altering the **story** or its meaning;
* writing, rewriting, or paraphrasing **dialogue**;
* deciding or adjusting **pacing**, scene timing, or narrative rhythm;
* designing or overriding **camera language** (lens, movement, angle originate only from the Shot Planner — see **5.5.4**);
* choosing or changing **blocking**, staging, or performance intent;
* inventing **characters** or **locations**, or altering their approved descriptions (see **5.5.2**, **5.5.3**);
* redefining the **visual style intent** (rendering *enforces* it; it does not *author* it — see **5.5.5**);
* deciding which shots exist, how many there are, or their **order**;
* substituting a different creative solution when a render fails;
* judging whether the *story* is good — only whether the *render* is technically and visually valid.

Any capability that would let a provider or a rendering component author or reinterpret creative content is a boundary violation and is prohibited (see `RBD-401`, `RBD-402`, `ADR-401`).

---

# Responsibilities

Summarised as the canonical is / is-not table that every Part 5 section inherits.

The Rendering Platform **is** responsible for:

* faithful technical execution of an approved visual plan;
* one render task per shot, deterministically derived;
* provider-agnostic submission and monitoring;
* deterministic prompt assembly from immutable references;
* asset download, technical validation, and visual quality validation;
* caching and reuse of unchanged, previously validated shots;
* visual style **enforcement** as a technical gate;
* fail-closed escalation when execution cannot satisfy validation.

The Rendering Platform is **not** responsible for:

* authoring or altering story, screenplay, or storyboard (see the boundary above);
* pacing, dialogue, camera language, or blocking decisions (owned upstream);
* creative fallback or "improvement" of failing shots (escalated to Manual Review);
* composition and publication (owned by the FFmpeg Composer, downstream of validation);
* judging narrative quality (only technical and visual fidelity).

---

# Design Objectives

The Rendering Platform shall satisfy the following boundary objectives. Each is normative and each exists to make the Story First / execution-only boundary **enforceable**, not merely aspirational.

## RBD-301 — Execution, Not Authorship

The platform shall provide only capabilities that materialise an approved plan. It shall expose **no** capability whose effect is to create, alter, or reinterpret narrative, dialogue, pacing, blocking, or camera language.

Authorship capability that does not exist cannot be misused.

---

## RBD-302 — Read-Only Upstream

Story, Screenplay, and Storyboard artifacts shall be consumed as **read-only** inputs. The Rendering Platform shall hold no write path, mutation API, or feedback channel that can modify an approved upstream artifact.

The boundary is a one-way membrane by construction.

---

## RBD-303 — One Task Per Shot, Faithful

Every approved shot shall map to exactly one render task carrying that shot's approved specification unchanged. Shots shall never be added, dropped, merged, split, or reordered by the Rendering Platform.

The shot set that leaves the Storyboard is the shot set that rendering executes.

---

## RBD-304 — Deterministic Materialisation

Given identical approved inputs, provider versions, and configuration, rendering shall produce byte-stable prompts and reproducible strategy decisions (elaborated in **5.5.1** and **5.2.3**). Rendering shall not introduce creative variance.

Determinism is the observable proof that rendering added no authorship.

---

## RBD-305 — Style Enforcement, Not Style Authorship

The platform shall **enforce** the approved visual style as a technical constraint (see **5.5.5**) and shall reject renders that violate it. The platform shall **never define or relax** the style intent to make a render pass.

Rendering guards the style; it does not own it.

---

## RBD-306 — Fail Closed at the Boundary

When faithful execution cannot satisfy validation, the platform shall retry within policy, fall back only to a pre-authorised strategy for the *same* creative intent, or escalate to Manual Review. It shall never emit a creatively-altered or partial asset to satisfy completion (see **5.6.2**, and Fail Closed in **5.1.2**).

Silence at the boundary is preferable to a lie in the trailer.

---

## RBD-307 — Provider Containment

Providers shall act only as rendering backends. No provider shall be granted, or be able to exercise, authority to generate stories, determine pacing, create dialogue, or design camera language (see **5.3.1**, **5.3.2**).

The boundary binds every provider identically, present and future.

---

## RBD-308 — Auditable Provenance

Every rendered asset shall be traceable to the exact approved shot specification it materialises. The platform shall record provenance sufficient to prove that no creative content was authored below the boundary.

A boundary that cannot be audited cannot be trusted.

---

# Boundary Enforcement Model

The boundary is enforced structurally, not by convention. Three mechanisms combine.

```text
1. CAPABILITY ABSENCE
   Rendering components expose no authoring API.
   (RBD-301) — you cannot misuse what does not exist.

2. READ-ONLY INPUTS
   Upstream artifacts arrive immutable; no write path exists back up.
   (RBD-302) — the membrane is one-way by construction.

3. DETERMINISTIC PASS-THROUGH
   Prompts and strategy are derived, not invented; identical inputs
   yield identical execution. (RBD-304) — variance would reveal authorship.
```

The enforcement chain, per render task:

```text
Approved Shot (immutable)
        │  read-only
        ▼
Reference Resolution  ── characters/locations/camera/style by ID only
        │  (no reinterpretation; 5.5)
        ▼
Deterministic Prompt  ── assembled, never authored
        │  (5.5.1)
        ▼
Provider Execution    ── backend only; camera params pass through
        │  (5.3 — providers contained by RBD-307)
        ▼
Validation            ── technical + visual + style enforcement
        │  (5.6 — reject, never rewrite)
        ▼
Validated Asset       ── provenance-linked to the approved shot
        │  (RBD-308)
        ▼
Composition           ── downstream; boundary already honoured
```

At no node does the chain acquire authority to change the approved shot. Each node either **reads**, **derives**, **executes**, or **rejects**. None **authors**.

---

# Boundary Violation Cases

A boundary violation is any behaviour by which a rendering component authors, reinterprets, or repairs creative content. The following are enumerated violations. Each is **prohibited** and each shall be prevented by construction, not merely detected.

| # | Violation                                                                 | Prohibited because                                            | Guarded by            |
| - | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------- |
| 1 | A provider generates narrative or "fills in" story from article text      | Providers are backends only                                   | `RBD-307`, **5.3.1**  |
| 2 | Rendering rewrites or paraphrases approved dialogue                        | Dialogue is owned by the Screenplay                           | `RBD-301`             |
| 3 | Rendering re-times a scene or adjusts pacing to fit a render              | Pacing is owned upstream                                      | `RBD-301`, `RBD-303`  |
| 4 | Rendering overrides camera lens, movement, or angle                       | Camera language originates only from the Shot Planner         | `RBD-301`, **5.5.4**  |
| 5 | Rendering adds, drops, merges, splits, or reorders shots                  | The shot set is fixed at approval                             | `RBD-303`             |
| 6 | Rendering alters an approved character or location description            | References are immutable                                      | **5.5.2**, **5.5.3**  |
| 7 | Rendering relaxes the visual style to make a failing render "pass"        | Rendering enforces style; it does not author it               | `RBD-305`, **5.5.5**  |
| 8 | Rendering emits a "close enough" creatively-altered asset on failure      | Fail Closed forbids a partial or altered trailer              | `RBD-306`, **5.6.2**  |
| 9 | Any write path from rendering back into an approved upstream artifact     | The boundary is a one-way membrane                            | `RBD-302`             |

If any such behaviour becomes possible, the boundary is broken and the platform is non-conformant regardless of output quality. A high-quality asset produced by an authorship violation is still a violation.

---

# Escalation at the Boundary

When rendering cannot faithfully execute, it does not improvise. It escalates through defined, non-creative channels.

```text
Faithful execution impossible?
        │
        ▼
Is failure transient?  ──yes──►  Retry within policy (5.4.5)
        │ no
        ▼
Is a pre-authorised cheaper strategy
available for the SAME intent?  ──yes──►  Fall back within budget (5.2.3)
        │ no
        ▼
Escalate to Manual Review  ──►  Human decision (never automatic authorship)
        │
        ▼
If unresolved:  Fail Closed  ──►  No publishable trailer (5.1.2)
```

Every branch preserves the boundary. Retry re-executes the *same* plan. Fallback swaps only the *technical means*, never the *creative intent*, and only among strategies pre-authorised upstream. Manual Review hands the decision to a human. Fail Closed refuses to publish. None of these branches lets rendering author story.

---

# Events

The Rendering Platform emits boundary-relevant lifecycle events so that adherence is observable and auditable.

```yaml
rendering_boundary_events:
  - approved_inputs_received        # read-only artifacts accepted at boundary
  - render_task_materialised        # one task per approved shot
  - strategy_selected               # from pre-authorised assignment (5.2)
  - prompt_assembled                # deterministic; refs resolved, not authored
  - provider_submitted              # backend execution begins (5.3)
  - asset_validated                 # technical + visual + style enforced (5.6)
  - render_faithful_complete        # asset provenance-linked to approved shot
  - render_fallback_applied         # same intent, cheaper pre-authorised strategy
  - render_escalated_manual_review  # non-creative escalation
  - render_failed_closed            # no publishable output produced
```

Every event references the originating `build_id`, `shot_id`, and the approved `storyboard_version`, and is durably recorded. No emitted event ever represents a mutation of an upstream artifact — such an event cannot exist because such a mutation cannot occur (`RBD-302`).

---

# Metrics

Boundary conformance is measured, not assumed.

```yaml
rendering_boundary_metrics:
  shots_in_storyboard              # approved shot count
  render_tasks_materialised        # must equal shots_in_storyboard (RBD-303)
  shot_task_parity_violations      # must be 0
  authorship_capability_surface    # count of exposed authoring APIs; must be 0
  upstream_write_attempts          # must be 0 (RBD-302)
  deterministic_prompt_mismatch    # must be 0 for identical inputs (RBD-304)
  style_enforcement_rejections     # rejects, never rewrites (RBD-305)
  fallback_same_intent_rate        # fallbacks preserving creative intent
  manual_review_escalations        # non-creative escalations
  fail_closed_events               # boundary-preserving refusals
```

`shot_task_parity_violations`, `authorship_capability_surface`, and `upstream_write_attempts` must be permanently zero. A non-zero value on any of these is a boundary breach and blocks the build (see the Quality Gate below).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The rendering boundary is also a **trust** boundary.

* Approved upstream artifacts are supplied to rendering as read-only, integrity-checked inputs; rendering holds no credential or path that could write them back.
* Article text and raw source material never cross the boundary into a provider (see **5.5.1**); providers receive only assembled prompts derived from approved references.
* Provider credentials are resolved at execution time from a secret store, never embedded in render tasks or manifests (consistent with **5.4.1**).
* Provenance records linking each asset to its approved shot are tamper-evident, so a boundary violation cannot be silently concealed (`RBD-308`).
* Every crossing of the boundary — inputs in, assets out, escalations up — is audited.

Least privilege at the boundary means rendering can *read* the plan and *write* assets, and nothing more.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* one render task materialised per approved shot; parity holds exactly (`RBD-303`);
* upstream artifacts are read-only — no mutation API is reachable (`RBD-302`);
* no rendering component exposes an authoring capability (`RBD-301`);
* deterministic prompts and strategy for identical inputs (`RBD-304`);
* style enforcement rejects violations rather than relaxing style (`RBD-305`);
* provenance links every asset to its approved shot (`RBD-308`).

## Integration Tests

* approved-inputs-received to validated-asset without any upstream mutation;
* provider containment — a provider cannot inject narrative or override camera params (`RBD-307`, **5.3.1**);
* fallback preserves creative intent and uses only pre-authorised strategies (`RBD-306`, **5.2.3**);
* escalation to Manual Review is non-creative and human-gated.

## Boundary Enforcement Tests

* attempted write from rendering into Story/Screenplay/Storyboard is impossible;
* attempted shot add/drop/reorder by rendering is impossible;
* attempted dialogue/pacing/camera override by rendering is impossible;
* article text never reaches a provider prompt.

## Failure Injection Tests

* provider returns creatively-altered content → rejected, not accepted (`RBD-306`);
* validation fails → retry / pre-authorised fallback / escalate, never rewrite;
* all execution paths exhausted → Fail Closed, no publishable asset emitted.

## Recovery Tests

* rebuild the shot→task mapping from the approved Storyboard and prove parity;
* prove, from provenance records, that no asset was authored below the boundary;
* reconstruct escalation history without any implicit creative mutation.

---

# Rendering Boundary Quality Gate

```yaml
rendering_boundary_quality_gate:
  execution_layer_only: true
  upstream_artifacts_read_only: true
  no_authoring_capability_exposed: true
  shot_task_parity_exact: true
  no_shot_add_drop_reorder: true
  no_dialogue_pacing_camera_override: true
  deterministic_materialisation: true
  style_enforced_not_authored: true
  providers_contained_as_backends: true
  fallback_preserves_creative_intent: true
  fail_closed_on_unfaithful_execution: true
  asset_provenance_complete: true
  upstream_write_attempts_zero: true
```

Every field must be `true`. Any `false` is a boundary breach; the build fails closed and no trailer is published.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RBD-401

The Rendering Platform is an execution layer. It never authors, extends, or reinterprets story, screenplay, or storyboard content.

## Invariant RBD-402

Approved upstream artifacts are read-only at render time. No path exists by which rendering mutates Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard.

## Invariant RBD-403

Every approved shot maps to exactly one render task. Rendering never adds, drops, merges, splits, or reorders shots.

## Invariant RBD-404

Dialogue, pacing, blocking, and camera language are owned upstream. Rendering may never override or adjust them.

## Invariant RBD-405

Rendering enforces the approved visual style as a technical constraint and never relaxes or redefines it to make a render pass.

## Invariant RBD-406

Providers act solely as rendering backends. No provider generates stories, determines pacing, creates dialogue, or designs camera language.

## Invariant RBD-407

When faithful execution is impossible, rendering retries, falls back only to a pre-authorised strategy for the same intent, or escalates — it never substitutes its own creative judgement.

## Invariant RBD-408

Rendering fails closed. It never emits a partial or creatively-altered asset to force completion.

## Invariant RBD-409

Every rendered asset is provenance-linked to the exact approved shot it materialises, so boundary conformance is auditable.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                        |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-401 | The Rendering Platform is defined as an execution-only layer; it exposes no capability to author or reinterpret creative content, and providers are permanently contained as rendering backends. |
| ADR-402 | The rendering boundary is a one-way membrane: Story, Screenplay, and Storyboard are read-only at render time, and no write or feedback path from rendering back into an approved artifact may exist. |
| ADR-403 | When faithful execution cannot satisfy validation, rendering fails closed — retrying, falling back only to a pre-authorised same-intent strategy, or escalating to Manual Review — and never emits a partial or creatively-altered trailer. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.1.1** is complete when:

* The Rendering Platform is formally defined as an **execution layer**, not a creative layer, converting approved Storyboard artifacts into validated visual assets without altering the creative plan.
* The **rendering boundary** against Story, Screenplay, and Storyboard is stated exhaustively, including what rendering **is** and **is not** responsible for.
* The **Story First / execution-only** governing principle is fixed as normative law, with its Fail Closed corollary, and inherited by all of Part 5.
* The boundary is shown to be enforced **structurally** — capability absence, read-only inputs, and deterministic pass-through — not by convention.
* Enumerated **boundary violation cases** are prohibited and mapped to the objectives and invariants that guard them.
* Non-creative **escalation** paths (retry, pre-authorised fallback, Manual Review, Fail Closed) are defined so that failure never becomes authorship.
* Objectives (`RBD-301`..`RBD-308`), invariants (`RBD-401`..`RBD-409`), and decision records (`ADR-401`..`ADR-403`) make the boundary enforceable, auditable, and testable.
* Events, metrics, security, testing, and the quality gate provide **sufficient detail for Codex to implement** a Rendering Platform that materialises the approved plan faithfully and can prove, by construction and provenance, that it never authored, reinterpreted, or repaired creative content.


---

# Part 5.1.2 — Design Philosophy & Rendering Principles

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the **normative design philosophy** of the Rendering Platform and enumerates the **rendering principles** that every rendering subsystem shall obey.

The principles in this section are not aspirational guidance. They are architectural law. Every downstream specification in Part 5 — strategy selection (**5.2**), provider integration (**5.3**), queue and execution (**5.4**), prompt assembly (**5.5**), validation and caching (**5.6**), and observability (**5.7**) — is a concrete elaboration of one or more principles defined here.

The purpose of codifying these principles as a distinct section is to establish a **single point of appeal**. When any later section, implementation, or operational decision is ambiguous, it shall be resolved by reference to these principles. When two later requirements appear to conflict, the principle hierarchy defined here breaks the tie.

The Rendering Platform is an **execution layer**. It converts an already-approved visual plan — the Storyboard, Camera Plan, Render Strategy, Character Bible, and Location Bible — into validated cinematic assets. It does not invent story, pacing, dialogue, or camera language. The principles below exist to keep that boundary absolute, to keep rendering deterministic and reproducible, to keep provider dependence contained, and to keep cost and failure behaviour predictable.

These principles bind **Codex** (the implementer). No rendering component may be implemented, merged, or deployed if it violates a principle in this section. Conformance is a merge gate, not a review suggestion.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the governing philosophy of the Rendering Platform;
* the eleven normative rendering principles;
* for each principle: its rationale, its architectural implications, the anti-patterns it prohibits, and how it is enforced;
* the principle hierarchy used to resolve conflicts;
* the design objectives that the principle set must collectively satisfy;
* the invariants that make each principle machine-checkable;
* the decision records that fix these principles into the architecture.

This section does **not** specify:

* the render strategy model or the decision matrix (defined in **5.2.1** and **5.2.2**);
* cost-aware selection formulas (defined in **5.2.3**);
* the provider interface contract (defined in **5.3.2**);
* the render queue mechanics (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the render task state machine (defined in **5.4.3**);
* retry, backoff, and timeout values (defined in **5.4.5** and **5.4.6**);
* deterministic prompt assembly mechanics (defined in **5.5.1**);
* validation thresholds and cache key construction (defined in **5.6.1**, **5.6.2**, and **5.6.3**).

Those sections implement the principles; this section states them. Where a value or algorithm is named here (for example the cost order, the retry ceiling, or the visual quality floor) it is quoted from the canonical source only to ground the principle. The authoritative definition remains in the owning section.

---

# Governing Principle

The Rendering Platform adopts the following principle above all others.

> **Rendering executes an approved plan; it never authors one.**

Every other principle in this section is a consequence of this one. Provider independence, determinism, cache-first behaviour, fail-closed validation, and cost ordering all exist to protect the boundary between *creative authorship* (which happens upstream, is human-approved, and is immutable by the time rendering begins) and *faithful execution* (which is all the Rendering Platform is permitted to do).

A corollary follows directly:

> **If rendering cannot faithfully execute the approved plan, it produces nothing publishable — it never substitutes its own judgement.**

The platform would rather emit no trailer, or escalate to Manual Review, than emit a trailer that silently diverges from the approved Storyboard. Absence of output is a safe failure. Unfaithful output is not. This corollary is the philosophical root of Principle 5 (Fail Closed) and Principle 10 (Execution-Only / No Creative Mutation).

---

# Architectural Position

These principles govern the entire Rendering Platform and constrain its relationship to the layers above and below it.

```text
        APPROVED CREATIVE PLAN  (immutable, human-approved)
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  Story  →  Screenplay  →  Storyboard  ✓     │
        │  Camera Plan · Render Strategy              │
        │  Character Bible · Location Bible           │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │  read-only
                          ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │        RENDERING PLATFORM (5.x)             │
        │   governed by the principles in 5.1.2       │
        │                                             │
        │   P1  Planning Before Rendering             │
        │   P2  Provider Independence                 │
        │   P3  Asynchronous Rendering                │
        │   P4  Incremental Rendering                 │
        │   P5  Fail Closed                           │
        │   P6  Determinism of Prompts                │
        │   P7  Consistency by Injection              │
        │   P8  Cost-Ordered Execution                │
        │   P9  Cache-First                           │
        │   P10 Execution-Only / No Creative Mutation │
        │   P11 Observability by Default              │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │  render requests only
                          ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  Interchangeable Providers                  │
        │  Runway · Motion · SVG · Typography · Cache │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
                          ▼
              Asset Repository → FFmpeg Composer
```

The principles form a horizontal contract across the whole Rendering Platform. They flow **downward** as constraints on providers, and **upward** as guarantees to the creative layers that their approved plan will be executed faithfully or not at all.

No arrow points back up from the Rendering Platform into the approved plan. The absence of that arrow is Principle 10 made visual.

---

# Responsibilities

This section is responsible for:

* declaring the complete, ordered set of rendering principles;
* giving each principle a rationale, implications, prohibited anti-patterns, and enforcement mechanism;
* defining the principle hierarchy for conflict resolution;
* making each principle checkable through invariants and quality gates;
* binding the principles to Codex as merge-blocking law.

This section is **not** responsible for:

* implementing any principle (that is the work of **5.2**–**5.7**);
* defining numeric thresholds, formulas, timeouts, or schemas beyond quoting them as grounding;
* specifying operational runbooks or deployment topology;
* selecting a specific provider or profile for any shot.

A principle without an enforcement mechanism is documentation, not architecture. Every principle in this section therefore names how it is enforced and how a violation is detected.

---

# Design Objectives

The principle set shall, collectively, satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative and is traceable to one or more principles.

## RPR-301 — Boundary Integrity

The approved creative plan shall be treated as immutable, read-only input. No rendering action shall mutate Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Camera Plan, or the Character/Location Bibles.

Traces to: Principle 1, Principle 7, Principle 10.

## RPR-302 — Provider Containment

Provider-specific knowledge shall not leak beyond the Rendering Platform's provider adapters. Swapping providers shall require no change in creative or planning layers.

Traces to: Principle 2.

## RPR-303 — Non-Blocking Execution

Rendering shall never synchronously block the pipeline. Long-running provider work shall be represented asynchronously through queues, leases, and delayed visibility.

Traces to: Principle 3.

## RPR-304 — Minimal Recomputation

Only work that has genuinely changed shall be re-executed. Previously approved and cached assets shall be reused whenever their inputs are unchanged.

Traces to: Principle 4, Principle 9.

## RPR-305 — Safe Failure

When faithful execution is impossible, the platform shall withhold publishable output and escalate, never emit a divergent or partial trailer.

Traces to: Principle 5, Principle 10.

## RPR-306 — Reproducibility

Given identical approved inputs and configuration, the platform shall produce identical prompts, identical cache keys, and identical execution plans. Provider pixel output may vary; platform decisions may not.

Traces to: Principle 6, Principle 7, Principle 9.

## RPR-307 — Economic Discipline

Expensive generative capacity (notably Runway motion) shall be used only when cheaper strategies cannot satisfy the shot's intent, in the fixed cost order.

Traces to: Principle 8, Principle 9.

## RPR-308 — Total Accountability

Every rendering decision, deferral, retry, and failure shall be observable through structured, durable telemetry sufficient to reconstruct why any asset does or does not exist.

Traces to: Principle 11.

---

# The Principle Hierarchy

Principles occasionally pull in different directions. Cost Awareness (P8) would defer expensive work; Fail Closed (P5) would rather escalate than degrade quality. Cache-First (P9) would reuse an asset; Consistency by Injection (P7) requires the reused asset to still match the current bibles.

Conflicts are resolved by the following fixed precedence, highest first:

```text
1. Execution-Only / No Creative Mutation   (P10)   — the boundary is inviolable
2. Fail Closed                             (P5)    — never publish divergent output
3. Determinism of Prompts                  (P6)
4. Consistency by Injection                (P7)
5. Planning Before Rendering               (P1)
6. Provider Independence                   (P2)
7. Cache-First                             (P9)
8. Incremental Rendering                   (P4)
9. Asynchronous Rendering                  (P3)
10. Cost-Ordered Execution                 (P8)
11. Observability by Default               (P11)   — never suppressed, but never a reason to publish
```

Two rules govern this table:

1. A higher principle is never sacrificed to satisfy a lower one. Cost (P8) never overrides quality or the creative boundary; caching (P9) never overrides consistency (P7).
2. Observability (P11) sits last only because it never *causes* a publish decision — but it is never suppressed to satisfy any other principle. Telemetry is emitted even when everything else fails.

This ordering is itself normative and is quoted by later sections when they must arbitrate (for example, Cache-First versus Consistency in **5.6.3**, and Cost versus Fail Closed in **5.2.3**).

---

# Principle 1 — Planning Before Rendering

Rendering shall begin only after all upstream Quality Gates have passed and the creative plan has been approved.

```text
Story
  ↓
Screenplay
  ↓
Storyboard
  ↓
✓ Approved   (human gate; immutable thereafter)
  ↓
Rendering
```

## Rationale

Rendering is the most expensive stage in the pipeline. Provider motion generation consumes GPU time, provider quota, wall-clock latency, and money. Spending that cost on a plan that has not been approved is waste multiplied by every shot. Worse, rendering an unapproved plan invites the temptation to "fix" the plan during rendering, which would destroy reproducibility and the creative boundary.

Planning-before-rendering also guarantees that every render request has a stable, complete input. A shot cannot be rendered correctly if its camera language, character reference, or location reference is still in flux. Freezing the plan first is what makes downstream determinism (P6) and caching (P9) possible at all.

## Implications

* Every render request depends on an **approved** Storyboard, Camera Plan, and Render Strategy. Rendering shall not read draft or in-review artifacts.
* Approval is a hard precondition of admission, mirrored in the queue admission contract of **5.4.1** (`storyboard_approved: true`).
* Preview rendering is the single sanctioned exception: it may run before final approval to validate pacing and continuity, but its outputs are explicitly non-final and are governed by the Preview profile in **5.6.4**.
* Once approved, the plan is immutable for the duration of the build (see Principle 10). Any change to the plan requires a new approval and a new build identity.

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Rendering shots from a Storyboard still marked draft or in-review.
* "Rendering to see how it looks" and then editing the Storyboard based on the result outside Preview mode.
* Kicking off final Runway renders in parallel with, or ahead of, human creative approval.
* Treating a failed upstream gate as advisory and proceeding to render anyway.

## Enforcement

* Queue admission (**5.4.1**) rejects any task whose Storyboard is not approved.
* The build identity is derived in part from the approved plan hash; an unapproved plan cannot produce a valid build id.
* Invariant RPR-401 makes approval a machine-checked precondition.
* The quality gate flag `all_renders_derive_from_approved_plan` must be true before any asset is admitted to composition.

---

# Principle 2 — Provider Independence

Rendering providers are interchangeable infrastructure. No creative or planning layer shall depend on a specific provider.

```text
Current provider:   Runway
Future providers:   Google Veo · Luma Dream Machine · Pika ·
                    OpenAI Video · Tencent Hunyuan Video
```

## Rationale

The generative video market moves faster than a cinematic platform can afford to re-architect. If the Story Engine, Screenplay Engine, Shot Planner, or Storyboard Engine embedded knowledge of Runway's API, model names, or quirks, then replacing Runway — or adding Veo alongside it — would require changes rippling through the creative core. That coupling is unacceptable in a system whose creative layers are meant to be stable and provider-agnostic.

Provider independence also protects reproducibility of *decisions*. The choice of which provider renders a shot is made by the Render Strategy (**5.2.1**), recorded in the manifest, and is itself deterministic. The creative plan never says "Runway"; it says "this shot needs genuine human motion," and the strategy layer maps that to a provider.

## Implications

* Every provider implements one uniform contract — `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, `cancel()`, `health_check()` — defined in **5.3.2**. No provider-specific method leaks past the adapter.
* Only the Rendering Platform's adapter layer may hold provider credentials or call provider APIs. The Story Engine, Screenplay Engine, Shot Planner, Storyboard Engine, and FFmpeg Composer shall never reference a provider API.
* Provider identity is data in the manifest, not a branch in creative code.
* The cache key incorporates `provider` and `provider_version` (**5.6.3**) so that switching providers correctly invalidates cached assets rather than silently reusing incompatible ones.

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* `if provider == "runway"` branches anywhere outside the Runway adapter.
* Storyboard or Screenplay artifacts naming a provider or model version.
* Leaking Runway-specific status codes, job-id formats, or throttle semantics into the queue or state machine as first-class concepts.
* A second provider that requires a different provider interface rather than implementing the common one.

## Enforcement

* The provider interface contract (**5.3.2**) is the only surface the platform calls; adapters are the only implementers.
* Static checks forbid provider string literals outside the adapter package.
* Invariant RPR-402 requires that removing any single provider adapter leaves the creative layers unchanged.
* The Runway adapter (**5.3.4**) is specified purely as an implementation of the common contract, proving the contract is sufficient.

---

# Principle 3 — Asynchronous Rendering

Rendering shall never synchronously block the pipeline. All provider execution is asynchronous.

```text
submit()  → returns immediately (task id, not asset)
   ↓
poll / delayed visibility (poll_interval: 20s)
   ↓
completion signalled asynchronously
   ↓
download → validate
```

## Rationale

A provider render may take seconds or tens of minutes; the overall render deadline is 45 minutes per task. Blocking a pipeline thread for that duration would waste runner capacity, prevent parallelism (a trailer has many shots), and couple pipeline liveness to provider latency. Asynchronous rendering lets every shot progress independently and lets the queue reclaim workers while providers work.

Asynchrony is also what makes failure survivable. If a submission is represented as a durable queue item with a lease rather than a blocked call, a worker crash mid-render loses no work — the lease expires and the task is re-delivered idempotently.

## Implications

* `submit()` returns immediately with a provider task id; it never waits for the asset (**5.3.3**).
* Provider progress is observed through delayed-visibility polling, not busy-waiting (**5.4.1**), on a 20s poll interval with an overall 45m ceiling (**5.4.6**).
* The pipeline never waits for one shot before submitting another (Principle from **5.13**, elaborated in **5.4.2**).
* All consumers must be idempotent because at-least-once delivery is assumed (**5.4.1**, RQM-304).

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* A synchronous `render_and_wait()` call that blocks until the asset is ready.
* Busy-polling a provider in a tight loop instead of delayed visibility.
* Serial rendering where shot N+1 cannot submit until shot N completes.
* Holding a pipeline lock across a provider call.

## Enforcement

* The provider contract's `submit()` is specified as non-blocking; blocking implementations fail the contract tests in **5.3.2**.
* The queue represents in-flight work as leased, delayed-visibility items (**5.4.1**), not blocked threads.
* Invariant RPR-403 forbids any synchronous wait on provider completion.
* Metrics `queue_wait` and `render_time` are measured separately, proving submission and completion are decoupled.

---

# Principle 4 — Incremental Rendering

Only modified shots shall be rendered. Previously approved assets shall be reused.

```text
Shot changed?  ── no ──►  reuse approved asset (cache hit)
     │
    yes
     ▼
render only this shot
```

## Rationale

A thought-leadership trailer is regenerated many times across its life — a line of narration changes, one location is re-lit, a single shot is re-blocked. Re-rendering every shot on every build would multiply cost and latency by the number of unchanged shots, which is usually most of them. Incremental rendering makes the cost of a change proportional to the size of the change, not the size of the trailer.

Incremental rendering depends on immutable planning (P1) and deterministic keying (P6, P9): a shot is "unchanged" precisely when its deterministic inputs hash to the same cache key.

## Implications

* The unit of incrementality is the **shot**. Scenes and trailers are composed from shot-level assets; they are not rendering units.
* Change detection is by cache key, not by timestamp or heuristic. If the key matches, the asset is reused; if any input changed, the key changes and the shot re-renders (**5.6.3**).
* Reuse spans builds: an asset approved in an earlier build is eligible for reuse in a later build if and only if its key still matches under current bibles and strategy.
* Incrementality composes with resume: an interrupted build continues from the last successful shot rather than restarting.

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Re-rendering an entire trailer because one shot changed.
* Detecting change by file mtime or "rebuild everything to be safe."
* Reusing an asset whose inputs changed but whose key was not recomputed.
* Treating the scene as the smallest re-render unit.

## Enforcement

* The cache (**5.6.3**) is the mechanism of incrementality; a cache hit means no render.
* The cache key is deterministic over shot inputs, provider, provider version, and profile.
* Invariant RPR-404 requires that an unchanged shot is never re-submitted to a provider.
* The metric `cache_hit` and the SLO target (cache hit rate ≥ 70%) measure incremental effectiveness.

---

# Principle 5 — Fail Closed

If rendering quality cannot satisfy validation requirements, no publishable trailer is produced.

```text
Validation satisfied?  ── yes ──►  publishable asset
        │
        no
        ▼
withhold output → retry (≤3) → Manual Review
        │
        ▼
NO partial / degraded trailer is published
```

## Rationale

A thought-leadership trailer carries institutional reputation. A trailer with a broken face, a continuity jump, or a substandard shot is worse than no trailer, because it ships a defect under the institution's name. The platform therefore treats "publish something degraded" as the unsafe outcome and "publish nothing, escalate" as the safe outcome.

Fail-closed is also what gives every other guarantee teeth. Determinism, consistency, and cost discipline would be hollow if the platform quietly lowered the bar whenever a render failed. Fail-closed means the quality floor is real.

## Implications

* Technical validation (resolution, duration, codec, frame rate, decode success, integrity) and visual quality validation (human consistency, continuity, framing, lighting, motion) are both mandatory (**5.6.1**, **5.6.2**).
* The visual quality floor is `minimum_score: 0.90`. A render below the floor is not accepted as final.
* Retries are bounded (`max_retry: 3`) and only for recoverable failures — provider timeout, temporary API failure, corrupted download. Failed quality validation, invalid prompt, and unsupported input are **not** retried (**5.4.5**).
* When retries are exhausted or a failure is non-retryable, the task escalates to Manual Review; it never degrades silently. Budget exhaustion likewise routes to Manual Review rather than producing a partial trailer (**5.4.1**).

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Lowering the quality threshold at build time to "get something out."
* Publishing a trailer with a subset of shots when others failed.
* Retrying a quality failure or an invalid prompt (retries are for transient faults only).
* Substituting an unrelated cached or stock asset for a shot that could not render.

## Enforcement

* The Render Quality Gate blocks any asset below `minimum_score` from composition (**5.6.2**).
* Retry classification (**5.4.5**) forbids retrying non-transient failures.
* Manual Review and Dead Letter routing (**5.4.1**) capture unrenderable work as evidence, never as silent success.
* Invariant RPR-405 requires that no published trailer contains an unvalidated or sub-threshold shot.

---

# Principle 6 — Determinism of Prompts

Rendering prompts and rendering decisions shall be deterministic. Given identical approved inputs and configuration, the platform shall assemble byte-identical prompts and make identical execution decisions.

```text
Character + Location + Camera + Lighting +
Blocking + Emotion + Shot Purpose + Visual Style
        ↓  (fixed order, fixed formatting)
Deterministic prompt  →  stable cache key
```

## Rationale

Determinism is the precondition of caching, resume, reproducibility, and debuggability. If prompt assembly were nondeterministic — if field order, whitespace, or optional inclusion varied run to run — then the cache key would vary, cache hits would collapse, incremental rendering would break, and two identical plans would render differently. Determinism at the platform layer is what lets the platform reason about *what it will do* even though the provider's pixels are not deterministic.

Note the careful scope: **platform decisions** are deterministic; **provider output** is not. Runway may return different pixels for the same prompt. That variability is acceptable and expected. What is not acceptable is variability in the prompt the platform constructs or the strategy it selects.

## Implications

* Prompt assembly has a fixed field order and fixed formatting (**5.5.1**). The eight components — character, location, camera, lighting, blocking, emotion, shot purpose, visual style — are assembled in the same order every time.
* Prompts are assembled exclusively from approved storyboard artifacts. The provider never receives article text (**5.5.1**).
* The cache key is a deterministic hash of shot inputs, provider, provider version, and render profile (**5.6.3**); identical inputs yield identical keys.
* Execution planning is deterministic even though provider pixels are not (mirrors Part 6 Principle 14).
* Any randomness required (for example poll jitter) is derived deterministically from the task id, never from a wall clock or RNG (**5.4.1**).

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Assembling prompts by iterating an unordered map or including optional fields nondeterministically.
* Seeding jitter, ordering, or selection from `now()` or an unseeded RNG.
* Feeding raw article text or free-form creative additions into a prompt.
* Cache keys that depend on non-input state (timestamps, machine identity, run counter).

## Enforcement

* Prompt assembly (**5.5.1**) specifies a canonical serialization; assembly tests compare byte output across runs.
* The cache key construction (**5.6.3**) is a pure function of declared inputs.
* Invariant RPR-406 requires prompt and cache-key stability under identical inputs.
* The queue derives all jitter deterministically from `task_id` (**5.4.1**), and the metric `output_hash` supports reproducibility auditing.

---

# Principle 7 — Consistency by Injection

Character, location, and camera identity shall be injected into every render from authoritative, immutable references — never restated, paraphrased, or improvised by the renderer.

```text
character: { ref: maya_v1 }              location: { ref: operations_center_night }
        ↓  resolve from Character Bible          ↓  resolve from Location Bible
   canonical description injected            canonical environment injected
camera: { lens: 50mm, movement: slow_push, angle: eye }  ← from Shot Planner only
```

## Rationale

Cinematic consistency is the difference between a trailer and a slideshow. The same character must look the same across shots; the same location must be lit and dressed the same way; the camera language must be exactly what the Shot Planner specified. The only way to guarantee this at scale is to make identity a **reference resolved from a single source of truth**, injected identically into every prompt — rather than a description the renderer re-writes per shot.

Injection also protects the creative boundary. If the renderer could paraphrase a character or invent a camera move, it would be authoring, not executing. Injection makes the renderer a faithful transcriber of upstream identity.

## Implications

* Characters are referenced by ID (e.g. `maya_v1`); their descriptions are injected automatically from the Character Bible (**5.5.2**). The renderer never invents appearance.
* Locations are immutable references (e.g. `operations_center_night`); environment consistency is guaranteed by injecting the canonical Location Bible entry (**5.5.3**).
* Camera parameters originate **only** from the Shot Planner and may never be overridden by a provider (**5.5.4**). Lens, movement, and angle are transcribed, not chosen.
* Visual style is enforced by injecting the approved style descriptors and forbidding proscribed styles (**5.5.5**).
* Because injection is deterministic (P6), the same reference yields the same injected text and the same cache key. Consistency and caching reinforce each other.

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* A renderer paraphrasing or "improving" a character or location description.
* A provider overriding camera lens, movement, or angle.
* Inlining a character description into the Storyboard instead of referencing the bible by ID.
* Two shots of the same character resolving to different descriptions.

## Enforcement

* Injection (**5.5.2**–**5.5.4**) resolves references from the bibles at assembly time; the renderer receives resolved text, not authority to change it.
* Style enforcement (**5.5.5**) rejects forbidden styles before submission.
* Invariant RPR-407 requires that identical references inject identical descriptions across all shots and builds.
* Cache stability (P9) fails loudly if injection is nondeterministic, surfacing consistency defects.

---

# Principle 8 — Cost-Ordered Execution

Rendering shall prefer the cheapest strategy that satisfies the shot's intent. Expensive generative motion is used only when cheaper strategies cannot.

```text
Cache  →  Animated Still  →  Motion Graphics  →  Runway
cheapest ─────────────────────────────────► most expensive
```

## Rationale

Runway (and any generative motion provider) is the most expensive resource in the platform — in money, quota, and latency. Much of a thought-leadership trailer does not need generative motion: titles are typography, diagrams are SVG, dashboards are animated stills, countdowns are motion graphics. Spending Runway capacity on a shot a cheaper strategy could serve is pure waste. Cost-ordered execution makes the platform reach for genuine motion generation only when motion genuinely contributes to storytelling.

Cost awareness is a discipline, not a quality compromise. It never lowers the quality bar (that would violate the higher-precedence P5); it only ensures the *cheapest adequate* strategy is chosen for each shot.

## Implications

* Every shot has exactly one render strategy from the fixed set: `runway_video`, `animated_still`, `motion_graphics`, `typography`, `svg`, `cached_asset`. Mixed strategies within a shot are prohibited (**5.2.1**).
* Strategy selection follows the decision matrix (**5.2.2**) and the cost-aware policy (**5.2.3**), which encode the fixed cost order: Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway.
* Within budget, cheaper strategies are admitted first; expensive provider work is deferred first under budget pressure, in reverse cost order (**5.4.1** backpressure).
* Budget is a first-class scheduling input. Budget exhaustion defers or escalates work; it never degrades quality to fit budget (P5 outranks P8).
* Runway is reserved for shots where genuine human motion, camera push, or emotional performance is required (**5.2.2**).

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Using Runway for a title, a static diagram, or a dashboard that a cheaper strategy renders equally well.
* Mixing two strategies inside a single shot.
* Silently dropping work to stay under budget (defer or escalate instead).
* Sacrificing the quality floor to reduce cost.

## Enforcement

* The strategy model (**5.2.1**) permits exactly one strategy per shot from the fixed set.
* Cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**) and the decision matrix (**5.2.2**) encode the cost order as normative.
* The queue enforces cost-ordered admission and reverse-cost backpressure (**5.4.1**).
* Invariant RPR-408 requires that no shot uses a more expensive strategy than the cheapest one that satisfies its intent, and that budget pressure never lowers the quality floor.

---

# Principle 9 — Cache-First

Before any render is planned, the cache shall be consulted. A matching cached asset eliminates the render entirely.

```text
Compute cache key  →  lookup
        │
   hit  │  no render — reuse asset (READY→CACHED)
        │
  miss  ▼  admit to render pipeline
```

## Rationale

The cheapest render is the one that never happens. Cache-first is the operational expression of "eliminate before optimizing": rather than making a render faster, avoid it. Because planning is immutable (P1) and prompts are deterministic (P6), a shot's cache key is a stable fingerprint of everything that determines its asset. If the key has been rendered and validated before, the asset can be reused without touching a provider.

Cache-first is the top of the cost order (P8) and the mechanism of incrementality (P4). It is placed above Incremental Rendering in the hierarchy because caching is the *check* that makes incrementality safe: incremental rendering reuses an asset precisely because the cache says its key is unchanged.

## Implications

* Cache lookup precedes queue admission. A cache hit bypasses the queue entirely and is marked `READY→CACHED` in the manifest; a cache-hit task is never submitted to a provider (**5.4.1**, **5.6.3**).
* The cache key is `sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile)` (**5.6.3**). Changing provider, provider version, or profile correctly invalidates the entry.
* Cache reuse is subject to the higher-precedence Consistency principle (P7): a cached asset is only valid if its key still matches under current bibles and strategy. If a bible changed, the shot hash changed, the key changed, and the cache misses — as it must.
* The SLO target is a cache hit rate ≥ 70%; cache effectiveness is measured continuously.

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Submitting a shot to a provider before checking the cache.
* Reusing a cached asset whose provider version or profile differs from the request.
* Caching keyed on anything other than the shot's deterministic inputs.
* Treating a cache hit as authoritative render *state* — the manifest, not the cache, owns truth (**5.4.4**).

## Enforcement

* Cache elimination precedes all queueing (**5.4.1**, ADR-482 in that section).
* The cache key is a pure function of declared, deterministic inputs (**5.6.3**).
* Invariant RPR-409 requires that a cache hit never triggers a provider submission and that a changed input never yields a stale hit.
* The metrics `cache_hit` and `cache_bypass_count` and the ≥70% SLO measure conformance.

---

# Principle 10 — Execution-Only / No Creative Mutation

The Rendering Platform executes the approved plan and shall never modify Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Camera Plan, or the Character/Location Bibles.

```text
APPROVED PLAN  ──read-only──►  RENDERING PLATFORM  ──►  ASSETS
      ▲                                                    │
      └────────────  NO write-back  ◄──────────────────────┘
```

## Rationale

This is the load-bearing principle of the entire Part — the Governing Principle in concrete form. The platform's legitimacy rests on the promise that what a human approved is exactly what gets rendered. If rendering could alter pacing, rewrite dialogue, invent camera language, or "improve" a shot, then human approval would be meaningless and reproducibility would be impossible. Providers, in particular, must never author: Runway renders; it does not decide story, pacing, dialogue, or camera language.

Execution-only is also what makes the platform safely provider-swappable (P2) and deterministic (P6): if no rendering decision feeds back into the plan, then the plan is a fixed input and everything downstream is a pure function of it.

## Implications

* Rendering treats the approved plan as immutable, read-only input for the whole build (reinforces P1).
* Providers may never generate stories, determine pacing, create dialogue, or design camera language (**5.3.1**, **5.3.4**). Their role is submission, polling, downloading, cancellation, and retries only.
* Camera parameters flow one way from the Shot Planner and cannot be overridden by a provider (P7, **5.5.4**).
* There is no write path from rendering back into the creative artifacts. Any creative change requires a new approval and a new build; it is never a side effect of rendering.
* Dead-letter and manual-review records are evidence, not creative decisions; they escalate to humans rather than mutating the plan (**5.4.1**).

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* A renderer or provider adjusting pacing, dialogue, or camera language.
* Any write-back from rendering into Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Camera Plan, or the bibles.
* Substituting a creatively different asset when the approved one cannot be rendered (escalate instead — P5).
* Provider-side "creative enhancement" that diverges from the injected plan.

## Enforcement

* The provider boundary (**5.3.1**) restricts providers to execution verbs only.
* The creative artifacts are opened read-only by the Rendering Platform; there is no API to mutate them.
* Invariant RPR-410 requires zero writes from any rendering component into approved creative artifacts.
* The quality gate flag `no_creative_mutation_detected` must be true; any detected write-back fails the build closed (P5).

---

# Principle 11 — Observability by Default

Every rendering decision, deferral, transition, retry, and failure shall emit structured, durable telemetry. Observability is mandatory, never optional and never suppressed.

```yaml
metrics:
  submission_time
  queue_wait
  render_time
  download_time
  validation_time
  cache_hit
  retry_count
  provider_latency
```

## Rationale

A rendering platform that cannot explain why an asset does or does not exist cannot be trusted, debugged, or improved. Observability is what makes every other principle auditable: you cannot verify Fail Closed, Cost Awareness, or Cache-First without telemetry proving they held. Observability sits last in the hierarchy only because it never *causes* a publish decision — but it is never traded away to satisfy any other principle. Even a total failure emits evidence.

Structured telemetry also feeds the SLO review (cache hit rate ≥ 70%, render retry rate ≤ 5%) and the cost accounting that makes Principle 8 measurable rather than aspirational.

## Implications

* Every material transition emits an event, and every event references `render_task_id`, `queue_class`, `attempt`, and `build_id`, durably recorded for audit and replay (**5.4.1**, **5.7.1**).
* The minimum metric set (submission time, queue wait, render time, download time, validation time, cache hit, retry count, provider latency) is recorded for every task (**5.29**, **5.7.1**).
* Telemetry is emitted even on failure paths — deferrals, retries, dead-letters, and manual-review escalations are all observable, never silent.
* Metrics are stored for historical trend analysis and feed the SLO and cost reviews.
* Observability never blocks or gates publishing; a slow metrics sink must never cause a render to fail, but a render must never publish without having emitted its telemetry.

## Anti-Patterns (Prohibited)

* Silent deferral, retry, drop, or dead-letter with no emitted event.
* Optional or best-effort metrics that are omitted under load.
* Suppressing telemetry to satisfy latency or cost targets.
* Telemetry that cannot reconstruct why an asset exists or is missing.

## Enforcement

* The observability specification (**5.7.1**) makes the metric and event set mandatory.
* The queue emits an event for every material transition (**5.4.1** events list).
* Invariant RPR-411 requires that no material rendering transition occurs without a corresponding durable event.
* SLO review (cache hit rate ≥ 70%, retry rate ≤ 5%) depends on and therefore verifies telemetry completeness.

---

# Principle Conformance Signals

Each principle is bound to observable signals so that conformance is measured, not asserted.

| Principle | Primary Signal(s) | Failure Indication |
| --------- | ----------------- | ------------------ |
| P1 Planning Before Rendering | `all_renders_derive_from_approved_plan` | any render from a non-approved plan |
| P2 Provider Independence | provider literals confined to adapters | provider reference in creative layer |
| P3 Asynchronous Rendering | `queue_wait` vs `render_time` decoupled | synchronous wait on completion |
| P4 Incremental Rendering | `cache_hit`, re-submission count | unchanged shot re-submitted |
| P5 Fail Closed | quality-gate blocks, manual-review count | sub-threshold shot published |
| P6 Determinism of Prompts | `output_hash` stability | prompt/key varies on identical input |
| P7 Consistency by Injection | injected-description equality | same ref, different description |
| P8 Cost-Ordered Execution | strategy mix vs cost order | costlier-than-needed strategy chosen |
| P9 Cache-First | `cache_hit`, `cache_bypass_count`, SLO ≥70% | provider hit despite cache match |
| P10 Execution-Only | `no_creative_mutation_detected` | write-back into approved artifact |
| P11 Observability by Default | event completeness per transition | material transition with no event |

These signals are surfaced through the metrics and events of **5.4.1** and **5.7.1**. A principle whose signal cannot be produced is treated as unenforced and blocks the quality gate.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The principles impose security constraints that later sections implement.

* **Provider secrets are contained (P2).** Provider credentials live only in the adapter layer's secret store and are resolved by workers at execution time. They never appear in queue items, prompts, manifests, events, or the creative layers (**5.4.1**, **5.3.2**).
* **The creative plan is read-only (P10).** The Rendering Platform holds read-only access to Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Camera Plan, and the bibles. No component has a write capability into approved artifacts.
* **Prompts carry no article text (P6).** Providers receive assembled prompts only, never source article content, protecting institutional material from leaving the platform boundary (**5.5.1**).
* **Telemetry excludes secrets (P11).** Events and metrics reference identifiers and timings, never credentials, prompt bodies, or asset payloads.
* **Namespace isolation.** Concurrent builds are isolated; a worker receives only the references it needs and resolves sensitive data under least privilege (**5.4.1**).

A violation of any of these boundaries is a security defect and fails the build closed (P5).

---

# Configuration Model

The principles are enforced through configuration that is validated at startup and fails closed on invalid values (P5).

```yaml id="rpr-config"
rendering_principles:
  planning:
    require_approved_plan: true          # P1 — no draft rendering
    allow_preview_before_approval: true  # P1 — the single exception
  providers:
    interface_only: true                 # P2 — common contract enforced
    secrets_in_adapter_only: true        # P2 / security
  execution:
    asynchronous_only: true              # P3 — no synchronous wait
    idempotent_consumers_required: true  # P3
  incremental:
    cache_first: true                    # P9 — lookup before admission
    unit: shot                           # P4 — shot-level incrementality
  fail_closed:
    minimum_visual_score: 0.90           # P5 — quality floor (owned by 5.6.2)
    max_retry: 3                         # P5 — transient failures only (owned by 5.4.5)
    escalate_on_exhaustion: manual_review
  determinism:
    canonical_prompt_order: true         # P6
    deterministic_cache_key: true        # P6 / P9
    deterministic_jitter_from_task_id: true
  consistency:
    inject_from_bibles: true             # P7
    provider_may_override_camera: false  # P7 / P10
  cost:
    strategy_cost_order: [cache, animated_still, motion_graphics, runway]  # P8
    one_strategy_per_shot: true          # P8
    budget_never_lowers_quality: true    # P8 < P5
  boundary:
    no_creative_mutation: true           # P10 — read-only plan
  observability:
    telemetry_mandatory: true            # P11 — never suppressed
    slo_cache_hit_rate_min: 0.70
    slo_retry_rate_max: 0.05
```

All values are environment-overridable. Configuration that would disable a principle's enforcement (for example setting `no_creative_mutation: false` or `telemetry_mandatory: false`) is rejected at startup; principle enforcement is not a tunable that can be switched off.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* an unapproved Storyboard is rejected before any render (P1);
* no provider string literal exists outside adapter packages (P2);
* `submit()` returns without blocking on completion (P3);
* an unchanged shot's cache key is stable across runs (P4, P6, P9);
* a sub-threshold visual score is not accepted as final (P5);
* identical inputs produce byte-identical prompts and identical cache keys (P6);
* identical character/location references inject identical descriptions (P7);
* a shot resolves to exactly one strategy, the cheapest adequate one (P8);
* a cache hit yields no provider submission (P9);
