* no rendering code path writes to an approved artifact (P10);
* every material transition emits a structured event (P11).

## Integration Tests

* full pipeline refuses to render a draft plan, permits Preview only (P1);
* provider swap (Runway → mock second provider) requires no creative-layer change (P2);
* many shots submit concurrently without serial blocking (P3);
* a one-shot change re-renders only that shot (P4);
* an unrenderable shot escalates to Manual Review, no partial trailer publishes (P5);
* two identical builds produce identical prompts, keys, and plans (P6);
* the same character across shots is visually consistent via injection (P7);
* strategy distribution respects the cost order under a mixed shot set (P8);
* a warm cache achieves the ≥70% hit-rate target on an unchanged rebuild (P9);
* an end-to-end build performs zero write-backs into the approved plan (P10);
* telemetry reconstructs the full life of every task, including failures (P11).

## Determinism & Reproducibility Tests

* prompt assembly is byte-stable across repeated runs and machines;
* cache keys are invariant under identical inputs and change under any input change;
* execution plan is identical for identical inputs despite provider pixel variance;
* jitter and any randomness derive solely from `task_id`.

## Failure Injection Tests

* provider timeout and 429 are retried (transient) but a quality failure is not (P5);
* budget exhaustion escalates to Manual Review rather than degrading quality (P5, P8);
* a metrics-sink outage does not block rendering but is itself observable (P11);
* an attempted write into an approved artifact is detected and fails the build (P10).

## Conformance Tests

* each principle's conformance signal from the table above is producible and asserted;
* disabling any principle in configuration is rejected at startup;
* the principle hierarchy is applied correctly when P8 conflicts with P5 and when P9 conflicts with P7.

---

# Rendering Principles Quality Gate

```yaml
rendering_principles_quality_gate:
  all_renders_derive_from_approved_plan: true     # P1
  provider_references_confined_to_adapters: true  # P2
  no_synchronous_provider_wait: true              # P3
  incremental_unit_is_shot: true                  # P4
  no_subthreshold_shot_published: true            # P5
  prompts_and_keys_deterministic: true            # P6
  identical_refs_inject_identical_text: true      # P7
  strategy_respects_cost_order: true              # P8
  cache_hit_never_submitted_to_provider: true     # P9
  no_creative_mutation_detected: true             # P10
  every_transition_emits_event: true              # P11
  principle_hierarchy_enforced: true
  principle_enforcement_not_disableable: true
  determinism_reproducibility_tests_passed: true
```

Every flag must be true. A false flag blocks the build closed (P5): the platform does not publish a trailer produced under a violated principle.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RPR-401

Rendering derives only from an approved, immutable creative plan; drafts are never rendered (Preview excepted). [P1]

## Invariant RPR-402

Provider-specific knowledge is confined to provider adapters; removing any single provider leaves the creative layers unchanged. [P2]

## Invariant RPR-403

No rendering component synchronously blocks on provider completion; all provider work is asynchronous and idempotently consumed. [P3]

## Invariant RPR-404

An unchanged shot is never re-submitted to a provider; the shot is the unit of incremental rendering. [P4]

## Invariant RPR-405

No published trailer contains an unvalidated or sub-threshold shot; unrenderable work escalates rather than degrades. [P5]

## Invariant RPR-406

Given identical inputs and configuration, prompts are byte-identical and cache keys are identical; all randomness derives from `task_id`. [P6]

## Invariant RPR-407

Identical character, location, or camera references inject identical descriptions across all shots and builds; providers never override camera parameters. [P7]

## Invariant RPR-408

No shot uses a strategy more expensive than the cheapest one satisfying its intent, and budget pressure never lowers the quality floor. [P8]

## Invariant RPR-409

A cache hit never triggers a provider submission, and no changed input yields a stale cache hit. [P9]

## Invariant RPR-410

No rendering component writes to Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Camera Plan, or the bibles; the creative plan is read-only. [P10]

## Invariant RPR-411

No material rendering transition occurs without a corresponding durable, structured event; telemetry is never suppressed. [P11]

## Invariant RPR-412

When principles conflict, the fixed principle hierarchy is applied, and no higher principle is sacrificed to satisfy a lower one.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR | Decision |
| --- | -------- |
| ADR-404 | The Rendering Platform is an execution layer; rendering executes an approved plan and never authors, mutates, or writes back to Story, Screenplay, Storyboard, Camera Plan, or the bibles. |
| ADR-405 | Rendering begins only after upstream Quality Gates pass and the plan is human-approved; Preview mode is the single sanctioned exception to render-before-approval. |
| ADR-406 | Providers are interchangeable infrastructure behind one common interface; only the adapter layer holds provider knowledge and credentials, and the creative layers are provider-agnostic. |
| ADR-407 | All provider execution is asynchronous with idempotent consumers; the platform never synchronously blocks on provider completion. |
| ADR-408 | Platform decisions — prompts, cache keys, strategy, execution plan — are deterministic; provider pixel output may vary but platform behaviour may not, and all randomness derives from `task_id`. |
| ADR-409 | Character, location, camera, and style identity are injected from immutable authoritative references; the renderer transcribes identity and never improvises it. |
| ADR-410 | Work is eliminated before it is optimized: the cache is consulted first, only changed shots are rendered, and strategies are chosen in the fixed cost order Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway. |
| ADR-411 | The platform fails closed and observes everything: it withholds and escalates rather than publish degraded output, and every material transition emits durable telemetry; principle enforcement is not a disableable configuration option, and conflicts resolve by the fixed principle hierarchy. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.1.2** is complete when:

* The governing philosophy — *rendering executes an approved plan and never authors one* — is stated normatively with its fail-closed corollary.
* All five original principles (Planning Before Rendering, Provider Independence, Asynchronous Rendering, Incremental Rendering, Fail Closed) are preserved and elaborated to full depth with rationale, implications, anti-patterns, and enforcement.
* The principle set is extended to Part-6 density with Determinism of Prompts, Consistency by Injection, Cost-Ordered Execution, Cache-First, Execution-Only / No Creative Mutation, and Observability by Default.
* Each principle is bound to a conformance signal, an invariant, and an enforcement mechanism, so that conformance is measured rather than asserted.
* A fixed principle hierarchy resolves conflicts, with the creative boundary and fail-closed behaviour outranking cost and caching, and telemetry never suppressed.
* Canonical facts are preserved without contradiction: the cost order (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway), the visual quality floor (0.90), the retry ceiling (3, transient failures only), the provider execution-only boundary, and single-strategy-per-shot.
* Design objectives (RPR-301..308), invariants (RPR-401..412), quality gate, configuration model, security boundaries, testing requirements, and decision records (ADR-404..411) are specified.
* The section provides sufficient detail for Codex to implement, and for every downstream Part 5 section to derive from, a rendering platform whose behaviour is faithful, deterministic, cost-disciplined, fail-closed, and fully observable — without ever crossing the boundary from execution into authorship.


---

# Part 5.1.3 — Position in Architecture

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section fixes the **position of the Rendering Platform within the overall platform architecture**.

It establishes, without ambiguity, what sits **upstream** of rendering, what sits **downstream** of rendering, which components are **lateral peers** invoked during rendering, and — most importantly — where the **trust and authority boundaries** fall at every edge that touches the Rendering Platform.

The Rendering Platform does not exist in isolation. It is a bounded execution stage in a longer creative pipeline that runs:

* Story Engine → Screenplay Engine → Storyboard Engine → **Render Strategy Planner** → **Rendering Platform** → Asset Repository → FFmpeg Composer → Publication.

Rendering is an **execution layer**, not a creative layer (established in **5.1.1**). Its architectural position is therefore that of a **faithful executor**: it consumes an already-approved visual plan from upstream, dispatches deterministic work to interchangeable providers laterally, and deposits validated visual artifacts downstream. It never reaches back up the pipeline to alter the plan, and it never reaches down the pipeline to compose or publish.

The purpose of this section is to make that placement precise enough that:

* every upstream artifact the platform is permitted to read is enumerated and typed;
* every downstream artifact the platform is obligated to produce is enumerated and typed;
* every edge is described as an explicit **contract** with a direction, a payload, and an authority rule;
* the trust boundary between the creative plane (which owns *what* and *why*) and the execution plane (which owns *how fast, how many, how retried*) is drawn once and reused throughout Part 5.

This section is the map. Later sections (**5.1.4** Inputs & Outputs Contract, **5.1.5** Rendering Pipeline Overview) fill in the payload schemas and the internal traversal; this section places the platform and names its edges.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the position of the Rendering Platform relative to all adjacent components;
* the full architectural diagram, elaborated from the original Part 5 §5.3 sketch;
* the classification of every edge as **upstream**, **downstream**, or **lateral**;
* the **upstream ingestion contracts** consumed from the Storyboard Engine and Render Strategy Planner;
* the **downstream production contracts** delivered to the Asset Repository and FFmpeg Composer;
* the **lateral provider contracts** with Runway and the Motion / SVG / Typography asset generators;
* the **trust and authority boundary** at each edge, stated as who owns which decisions;
* the **directionality invariants** that keep the pipeline acyclic and the plan immutable;
* the position-level objectives, invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* the byte-level schema of each ingested or produced artifact (defined in **5.1.4**);
* the internal traversal of the platform stage by stage (defined in **5.1.5**);
* strategy selection logic that runs *inside* the Render Strategy Planner boundary (consumed here, defined in **5.2**);
* the provider interface method contract (defined in **5.3.2**) or the Runway adapter (defined in **5.3.4**);
* queueing, leasing, and concurrency (defined in **5.4**);
* prompt assembly, validation, caching, or composition internals (defined in **5.5**, **5.6**).

The Rendering Platform **consumes** the upstream contracts and **honours** the downstream contracts named here; it does not own the components on the far side of any edge.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for its architectural placement.

> **The Rendering Platform reads the plan, renders the plan, and writes the assets — it never edits the plan and never publishes the result.**

Rendering occupies exactly one seat in the pipeline: between the component that **decides** the visual plan (upstream) and the component that **assembles** the finished trailer (downstream). It has read authority over the plan and write authority over the assets, and those two authorities never cross.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Every edge that touches rendering flows in exactly one direction, and the creative plane is always upstream of the execution plane.**

There is no edge along which the Rendering Platform sends a creative correction back to the Storyboard Engine, and no edge along which a provider sends a story decision into the Rendering Platform. Data flows down the pipeline and outward to providers; **authority never flows back up**. If an approved plan cannot be executed to specification, the platform fails closed (Principle 5, **5.1.2**) and escalates to Manual Review — it does not renegotiate the plan.

---

# Architectural Position

The Rendering Platform sits at the boundary between the **creative plane** and the **delivery plane**. The following diagram elaborates the original Part 5 §5.3 sketch into the full set of edges this section governs.

```text
                          ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
                          │              CREATIVE PLANE                 │
                          │        (owns WHAT and WHY — immutable)      │
                          └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

                                   Story Engine
                                        │
                                        ▼
                                 Screenplay Engine
                                        │
                                        ▼
                                 Storyboard Engine
                                        │
                        approved storyboard.json,
                        shot-list.json, camera-plan.json,
                        character-bible.yaml, location-bible.yaml
                                        │
                                        ▼
                             Render Strategy Planner
                                        │
                             render-strategy.json
                             (one strategy per shot)
                                        │
        ════════════════════════════════╪════════════════════════════════
              TRUST BOUNDARY  ▲ read-only plane  │  execution plane ▼
        ════════════════════════════════╪════════════════════════════════
                                        │
                                        ▼
                    ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │          RENDERING PLATFORM            │
                    │        (owns HOW — execution only)     │
                    │                                        │
                    │   Render Task Generation               │
                    │   Render Queue Manager        (5.4.1)  │
                    │   Prompt Assembly             (5.5)    │
                    │   Provider Dispatch           (5.3)    │
                    │   Asset & Quality Validation  (5.6)    │
                    └───────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │            │             │
             submit /     │            │             │  lateral: render only
             poll /       ▼            ▼             ▼
             download ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
                      │ Runway │  │ Motion │  │ SVG /         │
                      │Provider│  │Graphics│  │ Typography /  │
                      │(video) │  │ Assets │  │ Animated Still│
                      └────────┘  └────────┘  └──────────────┘
                          │            │             │
                          └────────────┼─────────────┘
                                       │  validated visual assets
                                       ▼
                              Asset Repository
                       (video_assets/, preview_assets/,
                        thumbnail_assets/, render_manifest.json,
                        render_results.json, metadata.json)
                                       │
                                       ▼
                               FFmpeg Composer
                                       │
                                       ▼
                          ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
                          │              DELIVERY PLANE                 │
                          │      (assembles + publishes the trailer)    │
                          └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Three facts are fixed by this diagram:

1. The Rendering Platform has exactly **one upstream feed** (the Render Strategy Planner, which itself carries the Storyboard Engine's approved artifacts), a set of **lateral provider edges**, and exactly **one downstream sink** (the Asset Repository, which FFmpeg Composer reads).
2. The **trust boundary** is drawn between the Render Strategy Planner and the Rendering Platform. Everything above the boundary is the read-only creative plane; everything at or below is the execution plane.
3. No arrow points upward. The graph is a **directed acyclic pipeline** with respect to creative authority.

---

# Responsibilities

At the level of architectural position, the Rendering Platform is responsible for:

* consuming the approved upstream artifacts exactly as delivered, without mutation;
* validating that every required upstream artifact is present and internally consistent before it begins;
* materializing render tasks from the plan (one task per shot, per **5.1.1**);
* dispatching those tasks laterally to the correct provider per the assigned strategy;
* collecting, validating, and depositing the resulting assets into the Asset Repository;
* producing the downstream manifest and result artifacts that FFmpeg Composer requires;
* failing closed and escalating to Manual Review when the plan cannot be executed to specification.

At the level of architectural position, the Rendering Platform is **not** responsible for:

* creating or amending any element of the story, screenplay, storyboard, or camera plan (owned upstream);
* selecting *which* strategy a shot uses (owned by the Render Strategy Planner, **5.2**);
* any creative decision embedded in a provider — pacing, dialogue, camera language, narrative (prohibited to providers, **5.3.1**);
* composing, sequencing, encoding the final trailer, or publishing it (owned downstream by FFmpeg Composer and Publication);
* persisting authoritative story state (owned by the upstream engines and the Render Manifest, **5.4.4**).

---

# Position Design Objectives

The Rendering Platform's placement shall satisfy the following objectives. Each is normative.

## RPO-301 — Single Upstream Authority

The Rendering Platform shall accept its work plan from exactly one upstream authority: the **Render Strategy Planner**, which delivers the approved Storyboard Engine artifacts together with the per-shot strategy assignment. No other component may inject render work.

The platform must never begin rendering from a storyboard that has not passed the upstream Quality Gate (Principle 1, **5.1.2**).

---

## RPO-302 — Read-Only Upstream

Every upstream artifact is consumed **read-only**. The platform shall never write, patch, reorder, or annotate `storyboard.json`, `shot-list.json`, `camera-plan.json`, `render-strategy.json`, `character-bible.yaml`, or `location-bible.yaml`.

The approved plan is immutable across the trust boundary.

---

## RPO-303 — Single Downstream Sink

The Rendering Platform shall deliver all of its output to exactly one downstream sink: the **Asset Repository**. FFmpeg Composer and every other downstream consumer read from that repository; they never read the platform's internal state directly.

The Asset Repository is the platform's sole published surface.

---

## RPO-304 — Lateral Providers Are Executors Only

Provider edges are **lateral**, not upstream or downstream. A provider receives a fully-assembled render request and returns a rendered asset. It contributes no plan and consumes no plan authority (consistent with **5.3.1**).

Providers are interchangeable behind a uniform contract (**5.3.2**); swapping one for another must not change any edge in this diagram.

---

## RPO-305 — Acyclic Authority

The creative plane is always upstream of the execution plane. No edge carries a creative decision from the execution plane back into the creative plane.

If execution cannot satisfy the plan, the platform escalates within the execution plane (Manual Review) and fails closed; it never emits an upstream edit.

---

## RPO-306 — Contract-Typed Edges

Every edge that crosses the platform boundary shall be a typed contract with a fixed direction, a defined payload, and an explicit authority owner. Untyped or ad-hoc data exchange across the boundary is prohibited.

The edge inventory in this section is exhaustive; a component not named here has no edge to the Rendering Platform.

---

## RPO-307 — Provenance Preservation

Every artifact the platform produces downstream shall carry provenance references back to the exact upstream artifacts and provider records that produced it (shot id, strategy, provider, provider version, render profile, cache key).

Downstream consumers must be able to trace any asset to its plan of record.

---

# Edge Inventory

Every edge that touches the Rendering Platform is enumerated below. Each edge has a **direction**, a **payload**, and an **authority owner** — the side that decides the content crossing the edge.

| Edge | Direction | Peer | Payload | Authority Owner |
| ---- | --------- | ---- | ------- | --------------- |
| E1 | inbound (upstream) | Storyboard Engine (via Planner) | `storyboard.json`, `shot-list.json`, `camera-plan.json` | Storyboard Engine |
| E2 | inbound (upstream) | Storyboard Engine (via Planner) | `character-bible.yaml`, `location-bible.yaml` | Story / Screenplay Engine |
| E3 | inbound (upstream) | Render Strategy Planner | `render-strategy.json` (one strategy per shot) | Render Strategy Planner |
| E4 | lateral | Runway Provider | render request → video asset | Rendering Platform (request) / Runway (pixels only) |
| E5 | lateral | Motion / SVG / Typography / Animated Still generators | render request → generated asset | Rendering Platform (request) / generator (pixels only) |
| E6 | outbound (downstream) | Asset Repository | `render_manifest.json`, `render_results.json`, `metadata.json`, `video_assets/`, `preview_assets/`, `thumbnail_assets/` | Rendering Platform |
| E7 | outbound (downstream, indirect) | FFmpeg Composer | reads validated assets + manifest from Asset Repository | FFmpeg Composer (read) |
| E8 | escalation (internal plane) | Manual Review | fail-closed escalation on unexecutable plan | Rendering Platform |

Edges E1–E3 are **inbound and read-only**. Edges E4–E5 are **lateral and stateless with respect to the plan**. Edges E6–E7 are **outbound**. Edge E8 stays inside the execution plane. **There is no ninth edge that returns creative authority upstream** — this is the defining structural property of the platform's position (RPO-305).

---

# Upstream Edges — Ingestion Contracts

The Rendering Platform consumes three logical inbound contracts. All are read-only (RPO-302). The full schemas are defined in **5.1.4**; the contract framing follows.

## E1 — Storyboard Contract (Storyboard Engine)

The Storyboard Engine, having passed its Quality Gate, delivers the approved visual plan through the Render Strategy Planner:

```yaml id="e1-storyboard"
storyboard_contract:
  storyboard.json:   # approved shots, composition, blocking, emotion, shot purpose
  shot-list.json:    # canonical ordered shot inventory (scene_index, shot_index)
  camera-plan.json:  # per-shot lens, movement, angle — authored by Shot Planner
  precondition:
    storyboard_approved: true    # upstream Quality Gate passed (Principle 1)
    shots_immutable: true
```

Authority rule: the Storyboard Engine owns every field. The platform reads shot identity, ordering, composition, blocking, emotion, shot purpose, and camera parameters. It **may not** override camera values (consistent with **5.5.4**) or reorder shots.

Trust boundary: this edge crosses the trust boundary from the creative plane into the execution plane. Data crosses; authority does not.

## E2 — Reference Bible Contract (Story / Screenplay Engine)

Character and location identities are delivered as immutable reference bibles:

```yaml id="e2-bibles"
reference_bible_contract:
  character-bible.yaml:  # characters referenced by stable id (e.g. maya_v1)
  location-bible.yaml:   # locations referenced by stable id (e.g. operations_center_night)
  precondition:
    references_resolvable: true
    identities_immutable: true
```

Authority rule: character and location descriptions are injected into prompts by reference id (**5.5.2**, **5.5.3**); the platform resolves the id to its canonical description but never edits the description. Environment and character consistency are guaranteed upstream, not synthesized by rendering.

## E3 — Render Strategy Contract (Render Strategy Planner)

The Render Strategy Planner assigns exactly one strategy to every shot and delivers the assignment:

```yaml id="e3-strategy"
render_strategy_contract:
  render-strategy.json:
    - shot_id: ...
      strategy: runway_video | animated_still | motion_graphics | typography | svg | cached_asset
  rules:
    one_strategy_per_shot: true          # mixed strategies within a shot prohibited (5.7)
    strategy_is_binding: true            # platform executes; it does not re-select
```

Authority rule: the Planner owns **which** strategy each shot uses; the platform owns **how** that strategy is executed. The six strategies and the cost-priority order (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway) are consumed here and defined in **5.2.1** and **5.2.3**. The platform must never substitute a different strategy for the one assigned, except by escalating to Manual Review when the assigned strategy is unexecutable (RPO-305).

This is the **primary upstream edge**: the Planner is the single upstream authority of RPO-301, and it carries E1 and E2 forward together with E3 across the trust boundary.

---

# Lateral Edges — Provider Contracts

Provider edges are lateral: the platform sends a fully-assembled request and receives rendered pixels. Providers hold **no plan authority** (RPO-304, **5.3.1**).

## E4 — Runway Provider Contract

Runway is the current video provider (Principle 2, **5.1.2**). It acts strictly as a rendering backend:

```yaml id="e4-runway"
runway_edge:
  platform_sends:
    - render_request      # assembled prompt + camera + profile (runway_preview | runway_final)
  runway_returns:
    - task_id, status, rendered_video_asset
  runway_may:
    - submit, poll status, download, cancel, retry-at-provider
  runway_must_never:
    - generate stories
    - determine pacing
    - create dialogue
    - design camera language
```

Authority rule: the platform owns the request; Runway owns only the pixels it renders in response. Every creative parameter in the request was decided upstream and assembled deterministically (**5.5.1**); Runway adds no narrative. The Runway adapter (**5.3.4**) implements the uniform provider interface (**5.3.2**), so Runway is replaceable by Google Veo, Luma, Pika, OpenAI Video, or Tencent Hunyuan without altering this edge's shape.

## E5 — Motion / Generated-Asset Contract

Motion Graphics, SVG, Typography, and Animated Still generators occupy the same lateral position as Runway for non-video strategies:

```yaml id="e5-motion"
generated_asset_edge:
  strategies:
    - motion_graphics   # e.g. countdown UI
    - animated_still    # e.g. enterprise dashboard
    - svg               # e.g. architecture diagram
    - typography        # e.g. title card
  platform_sends: render_request
  generator_returns: rendered_asset
  generator_authority: pixels_only     # no plan, no pacing, no story
```

Authority rule: identical to E4. These generators are cheaper deterministic executors selected ahead of Runway by the cost-priority order (**5.2.3**); they never contribute plan authority. The rendering decision matrix that maps shot type to strategy (**5.2.2**) is applied upstream in the Planner, not at this edge.

Lateral property: because E4 and E5 are stateless with respect to the plan and uniform behind the provider interface, the platform's position is unchanged by any provider swap — satisfying RPO-304 and the provider-independence principle.

---

# Downstream Edges — Production Contracts

The Rendering Platform delivers all output to a single sink, the Asset Repository (RPO-303). FFmpeg Composer reads from that repository; it is never handed the platform's internal state.

## E6 — Asset Repository Contract

The platform deposits both the binary assets and the descriptive artifacts:

```yaml id="e6-repository"
asset_repository_contract:
  descriptive_artifacts:
    render_manifest.json:  # authoritative per-shot render state (schema in 5.4.4)
    render_results.json:   # per-shot outcome: asset_uri, validation, provider record
    metadata.json:         # provenance, provider_version, render_profile, cache_key
  binary_assets:
    video_assets/          # validated final shot renders
    preview_assets/        # preview-profile renders
    thumbnail_assets/      # derived stills
  guarantees:
    every_asset_validated: true          # technical + visual (5.6.1, 5.6.2)
    minimum_visual_score: 0.90           # sub-threshold never published (Fail Closed)
    provenance_complete: true            # RPO-307
```

Authority rule: the Rendering Platform owns everything it writes here. Only **validated** assets reach the repository — assets that fail technical validation (**5.6.1**) or fall below the visual quality minimum of 0.90 (**5.6.2**) are never deposited as publishable; they route to Retry or Manual Review. This is the Fail Closed guarantee (Principle 5) expressed at the downstream edge: **no publishable trailer input is produced unless validation is satisfied.**

## E7 — FFmpeg Composer Contract (indirect)

FFmpeg Composer is downstream of the Asset Repository, not directly wired to the platform:

```yaml id="e7-composer"
composer_edge:
  composer_reads:
    - render_manifest.json     # which shots, in which order, with which asset_uri
    - video_assets/            # validated shot renders
    - metadata.json            # provenance for reproducible composition
  composer_owns:
    - sequencing, transitions, encoding, final trailer assembly
  platform_owns:
    - nothing past the repository handoff
```

Authority rule: the platform's authority ends at the Asset Repository boundary. Composition, encoding, and final assembly are owned by FFmpeg Composer; publication is owned further downstream still. The platform never composes and never publishes (governing principle). The manifest it writes at E6 is the interface that lets the Composer sequence shots deterministically without re-reading the creative plan.

## E8 — Manual Review Escalation (execution-plane, not upstream)

When the plan cannot be executed to specification — budget exhausted, visual quality unrecoverable after max retries (max_retry = 3, **5.4.5**), provider unavailable with no fallback — the platform escalates to Manual Review:

```yaml id="e8-escalation"
manual_review_edge:
  triggers:
    - visual_score < 0.90 after max_retry
    - budget exhausted before completion
    - provider unavailable, no fallback strategy
  effect: fail_closed          # no partial or substandard trailer input produced
  plane: execution             # escalation stays below the trust boundary
```

Authority rule: E8 is **not** an upstream edge. It escalates within the execution plane for human resolution; it never emits a creative correction back to the Storyboard Engine or the Planner. This preserves acyclic authority (RPO-305): the platform fails closed rather than renegotiating the plan.

---

# Trust & Authority Boundaries

The single most important structural feature of the platform's position is the **trust boundary** drawn between the creative plane and the execution plane.

```text
CREATIVE PLANE (above boundary)          EXECUTION PLANE (at/below boundary)
─────────────────────────────           ──────────────────────────────────
Owns: WHAT and WHY                       Owns: HOW, HOW FAST, HOW MANY, HOW RETRIED
  - story, screenplay                      - task generation
  - shots, composition, blocking           - queueing, concurrency, leasing
  - camera language                        - prompt assembly (deterministic)
  - character / location identity          - provider dispatch
  - strategy assignment                    - validation, caching
Immutable to rendering                   Provider swaps invisible upstream
Read-only across boundary                Writes only to Asset Repository
```

The boundary is enforced by the following authority rules, each restating an edge from the perspective of *who decides*:

| Decision | Owned By | Plane | Platform's Role |
| -------- | -------- | ----- | --------------- |
| What story is told | Story Engine | creative | none |
| Which shots exist, their order | Storyboard Engine | creative | read-only (E1) |
| Camera lens / movement / angle | Shot Planner | creative | read-only, may not override (E1, **5.5.4**) |
| Character / location identity | Story / Screenplay Engine | creative | resolve by id, read-only (E2) |
| Which strategy per shot | Render Strategy Planner | creative | execute as assigned (E3) |
| How the prompt is assembled | Rendering Platform | execution | owns (**5.5.1**) |
| Which provider, how dispatched | Rendering Platform | execution | owns (**5.3**, **5.4**) |
| Whether an asset is valid | Rendering Platform | execution | owns (**5.6**) |
| How shots are composed | FFmpeg Composer | delivery | none |
| Whether to publish | Publication | delivery | none |

Two boundary rules are absolute:

* **Nothing the platform does can change a decision in the creative plane.** The plan is immutable across the boundary (RPO-302).
* **Nothing a provider does can change a decision in any plane.** Providers render pixels; they hold no authority (RPO-304, **5.3.1**).

The corollary of the governing principle — every edge flows one way, creative always upstream of execution — is exactly the statement that this table has no row where the platform (or a provider) owns a creative decision.

---

# Directionality Invariants

To keep the pipeline acyclic and the plan immutable, the following structural rules hold at all times.

* The Rendering Platform reads from upstream and writes to downstream; it **never** writes upstream (RPO-302).
* Provider edges are lateral and carry no plan; a provider result never becomes a plan input (RPO-304).
* The only inbound work source is the Render Strategy Planner (RPO-301); no side channel injects render tasks.
* The only outbound published surface is the Asset Repository (RPO-303); no consumer reads platform internals.
* Escalation (E8) stays inside the execution plane; failure never round-trips as a creative edit (RPO-305).

Together these make the platform a **strict executor node** in a directed acyclic pipeline: exactly one plan in, validated assets out, providers to the side.

---

# Position in the Larger Pipeline

For orientation, the Rendering Platform's seat within the end-to-end platform is:

```text
Story ─► Screenplay ─► Storyboard ─► Render Strategy Planner
                                              │
                                       [TRUST BOUNDARY]
                                              ▼
                                     RENDERING PLATFORM   ◄── this Part (Part 5)
                                              ▼
                                       Asset Repository
                                              ▼
                                       FFmpeg Composer ─► Publication
```

Part 5 governs the single boxed node and its edges. Everything above the trust boundary is authored by earlier parts of the specification and arrives as immutable input; everything below the Asset Repository is governed by the composition and publication parts and consumes the platform's validated output. The platform's placement is therefore fully determined by two contracts — the upstream ingestion contract (E1–E3, detailed in **5.1.4**) and the downstream production contract (E6–E7, detailed in **5.1.4**) — bridged by an internal pipeline (**5.1.5**) that dispatches laterally to providers (**5.3**).

---

# Events

At the position level the platform emits boundary-crossing events so that adjacent components and audit can observe every edge traversal.

```yaml
position_events:
  - upstream_plan_received          # E1–E3 ingested, read-only snapshot taken
  - upstream_precondition_failed    # storyboard not approved / references unresolvable
  - provider_request_dispatched     # E4 / E5 lateral send
  - provider_asset_received         # lateral return
  - asset_repository_published      # E6 downstream write (validated asset)
  - composer_handoff_ready          # E7 manifest complete for a build
  - manual_review_escalated         # E8 fail-closed escalation
```

Every event references the originating `build_id`, the `shot_id` where applicable, and the edge identifier, and is durably recorded for provenance (RPO-307).

---

# Metrics

Position-level metrics quantify edge health.

```yaml
position_metrics:
  upstream_plans_ingested
  upstream_precondition_failures
  provider_requests_by_edge          # E4 vs E5 distribution
  assets_published_to_repository
  assets_rejected_pre_publish        # failed validation, never published
  composer_handoffs_completed
  manual_review_escalations
  provenance_completeness_rate       # target: 1.0 (RPO-307)
```

All metrics are stored for historical analysis and feed the platform-level SLO review (**5.7.1**).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The trust boundary is also a secret boundary.

* Provider credentials live in the execution plane only; no upstream artifact and no downstream artifact ever carries a provider secret.
* Upstream artifacts cross the boundary as **read-only snapshots**; the platform holds no write handle to any creative-plane store.
* Downstream artifacts are written only to the Asset Repository namespace scoped to the build; the platform holds no handle to the Composer or Publication internals.
* The lateral provider edges (E4, E5) resolve credentials at dispatch time from a secret store, never from queue items or manifests (consistent with **5.4.1**).
* Every boundary crossing is audit-logged with build namespace isolation between concurrent builds.

Least privilege applies per edge: read on the upstream store, write on the repository namespace, invoke on the provider adapters — and nothing more.

---

# Configuration Model

The position of the platform is fixed by architecture, but the concrete endpoints of each edge are configurable and environment-overridable.

```yaml id="rpo-config"
rendering_platform_position:
  upstream:
    strategy_planner_source: render-strategy.json
    storyboard_source: storyboard.json
    require_storyboard_approved: true      # precondition, fail closed if false
  lateral:
    providers:
      runway:   { role: video,   swappable: true }
      motion:   { role: motion,  swappable: true }
      svg:      { role: vector,  swappable: true }
      typography: { role: text,  swappable: true }
  downstream:
    asset_repository_namespace: build/<build_id>/
    publish_only_validated: true           # E6 guarantee
    minimum_visual_score: 0.90
  escalation:
    manual_review_on_fail_closed: true
```

Configuration is validated at startup. An invalid edge configuration — missing upstream source, unreachable repository namespace, `require_storyboard_approved: false` — fails closed and blocks the platform from beginning, rather than rendering against an unverified plan.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* upstream precondition check rejects an unapproved storyboard;
* read-only enforcement: any attempt to write an upstream artifact is refused;
* edge inventory completeness: every declared edge has a direction, payload, and authority owner;
* strategy assignment is executed as delivered, never re-selected;
* provenance fields populated on every produced artifact.

## Integration Tests

* full E1–E3 ingestion → task generation for a multi-shot build;
* lateral dispatch to Runway (E4) and to a Motion generator (E5) for mixed strategies;
* downstream publish (E6) of only validated assets;
* Composer handoff (E7) reads manifest + assets and never touches platform internals;
* provider swap (Runway → alternate video provider) leaves all edges unchanged.

## Concurrency Tests

* two builds ingest concurrently with namespace isolation preserved;
* lateral dispatch to multiple providers in parallel without plane crossing;
* downstream publish concurrent with upstream ingest of a different build.

## Failure Injection Tests

* upstream artifact missing or references unresolvable → precondition failure, no rendering;
* provider returns a plan-like field → rejected, provider authority denied (RPO-304);
* validation fails after max retries → E8 escalation, nothing published;
* attempt to write upstream → blocked and audited.

## Recovery Tests

* re-ingest an upstream plan snapshot deterministically after platform restart;
* reconstruct downstream manifest state from the Asset Repository;
* confirm no partial or substandard asset survives a failed build (Fail Closed).

---

# Position Quality Gate

```yaml
position_quality_gate:
  single_upstream_authority: true
  upstream_consumed_read_only: true
  single_downstream_sink: true
  providers_lateral_and_authority_free: true
  all_edges_typed_and_directional: true
  no_upstream_authority_edge_exists: true
  provenance_complete_on_all_outputs: true
  only_validated_assets_published: true
  fail_closed_escalation_stays_in_execution_plane: true
  provider_swap_leaves_edges_unchanged: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RPO-401

The Rendering Platform sits strictly between the Render Strategy Planner (upstream) and the Asset Repository (downstream); it occupies exactly one seat in the pipeline.

## Invariant RPO-402

All upstream artifacts are consumed read-only; the approved plan is immutable across the trust boundary.

## Invariant RPO-403

The Render Strategy Planner is the single inbound work authority; no other component injects render work.

## Invariant RPO-404

Provider edges are lateral and carry no plan authority; a provider result never becomes a plan input.

## Invariant RPO-405

The Asset Repository is the platform's sole published surface; downstream consumers never read platform internals.

## Invariant RPO-406

No edge returns creative authority upstream; the pipeline is acyclic with respect to creative decisions.

## Invariant RPO-407

Only validated assets (technical pass and visual score ≥ 0.90) are published downstream; sub-threshold work fails closed.

## Invariant RPO-408

Every produced artifact carries complete provenance to its upstream plan and provider records.

## Invariant RPO-409

Fail-closed escalation (Manual Review) stays within the execution plane and never emits an upstream creative edit.

## Invariant RPO-410

A provider swap leaves every edge in the architecture diagram unchanged; provider independence is structural, not incidental.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                     |
| ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-412 | The Rendering Platform occupies a single seat between the Render Strategy Planner (upstream) and the Asset Repository (downstream); upstream is consumed read-only and downstream is its sole published surface. |
| ADR-413 | Provider edges are lateral and authority-free; providers render pixels only, are uniform behind the provider interface, and any provider swap leaves the architecture's edges unchanged. |
| ADR-414 | The trust boundary between the creative plane and the execution plane is one-directional; no edge (including fail-closed escalation) returns creative authority upstream, keeping the pipeline acyclic. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.1.3** is complete when:

* The Rendering Platform's position is fixed as a single executor node between the Render Strategy Planner and the Asset Repository, with the full architecture diagram elaborated from the original §5.3.
* Every edge that touches the platform is enumerated with a direction, a payload, and an authority owner, and the inventory is exhaustive.
* The upstream ingestion contracts (Storyboard, reference bibles, render strategy) are specified as read-only, with the Planner as the single inbound authority.
* The lateral provider contracts (Runway and Motion/SVG/Typography/Animated Still) are specified as authority-free executors, uniform behind the provider interface and invariant under provider swap.
* The downstream production contracts (Asset Repository, and the indirect FFmpeg Composer handoff) are specified, with only validated assets published and full provenance carried.
* The trust and authority boundary between the creative plane and the execution plane is drawn once, with an explicit ownership table showing the platform owns no creative decision.
* Directionality invariants establish that the pipeline is acyclic with respect to creative authority and that fail-closed escalation stays inside the execution plane.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the Rendering Platform as a strictly-positioned executor node — reading the plan, rendering the plan, and writing validated assets, without ever editing the plan or publishing the result.


---

# Part 5.1.4 — Inputs & Outputs Contract

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Inputs & Outputs Contract** of the Rendering Platform.

The contract fixes, for all time and for every provider, the exact set of artifacts that may enter the Rendering Platform and the exact set of artifacts it is permitted to produce. It specifies each artifact's canonical schema, its required and optional fields, its provenance, the validation applied at ingest, and its immutability guarantees.

The Rendering Platform is an **execution layer** (see **5.1.1**): it converts an already-approved visual plan into cinematic assets and never authors story, screenplay, storyboard, pacing, dialogue, or camera language. The Inputs & Outputs Contract is the enforced boundary that makes this guarantee mechanical rather than aspirational: rendering can only read what the contract admits, and can only emit what the contract permits.

The purpose of this section is therefore twofold: to define the **input contract** as a strict precondition gate that rendering must pass before a single provider request is issued; and to define the **output contract** as the complete, immutable, provenance-bearing record of everything rendering produces. Every input named here is produced upstream, by an approved and quality-gated engine, and is treated by rendering as **read-only truth**. Every output named here is produced by rendering alone and, once sealed, is treated by all downstream consumers as **immutable evidence**. Nothing outside this contract may cross the Rendering Platform boundary in either direction.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the complete set of admissible rendering **inputs** and permitted rendering **outputs**, with their canonical schemas and required/optional fields;
* the **provenance** of every artifact — which upstream engine produced it, and how its authenticity is established;
* **validation-on-ingest** rules applied to every input before it is trusted;
* **immutability guarantees** for inputs (never mutated by rendering) and outputs (sealed on emission);
* the **input precondition gate** — the conjunctive set of conditions that must hold before rendering begins;
* the on-disk directory layout of the output artifact set and the content-addressing/lineage rules that make the contract auditable;
* the events, metrics, security boundaries, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and decision records governing the contract.

This section does **not** specify:

* how render tasks are generated from these inputs (the pipeline, defined in **5.1.5**);
* how the Render Strategy is chosen for each shot (defined in **5.2.1**–**5.2.3**);
* the internal structure of the Render Manifest at runtime (defined in **5.4.4**);
* the render queue item schema or queue topology (defined in **5.4.1**);
* prompt assembly from character, location, and camera inputs (defined in **5.5.1**–**5.5.4**);
* asset technical validation and visual quality scoring (defined in **5.6.1** and **5.6.2**);
* the cache key derivation and reuse policy (defined in **5.6.3**).

This section owns the **shape and admissibility** of what crosses the boundary. It does not own the **behaviour** of the components that consume or produce those artifacts; those behaviours are specified in the sections cross-referenced above.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **Rendering reads an approved plan and writes an immutable record; it never edits the plan and never rewrites its own record.**

Inputs are upstream truth: they enter rendering read-only, and rendering must never mutate, reorder, reinterpret, or "improve" them. Outputs are rendering truth: they are written once, sealed, and thereafter immutable — a correction is a new versioned record, never an in-place edit.

A corollary follows directly:

> **If it is not in the input contract, rendering cannot see it; if it is not in the output contract, rendering cannot emit it.**

The contract is closed on both sides: a field, file, or artifact not named in this section has no admissible path into or out of the Rendering Platform. Article text, unapproved storyboards, provider secrets, and speculative creative edits are all excluded by construction, not by convention. This closure is what allows the boundary of **5.1.1** to be enforced mechanically — the input gate rejects anything that would let rendering act on an unapproved plan, and the output seal prevents rendering from silently altering the historical record of what it produced.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        UPSTREAM (Creative Authoring — approved, quality-gated)
        │
   ┌────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  shot-list.json        storyboard.json            │
   │  camera-plan.json      render-strategy.json        │
   │  character-bible.yaml  location-bible.yaml         │
   └────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │  (read-only, provenance-verified)
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              INPUT PRECONDITION GATE                    │
│   presence · schema · provenance · approval · fixity    │
│   ── FAIL CLOSED: no gate pass ⇒ no rendering ──        │
└───────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                            │  (gate satisfied)
                            ▼
                  ┌───────────────────┐
                  │ Rendering Platform │
                  │  (execution only)  │
                  └─────────┬─────────┘
                            │  (writes, then seals)
                            ▼
   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  render_manifest.json   render_queue.json          │
   │  render_results.json    metadata.json              │
   │  video_assets/  preview_assets/  thumbnail_assets/  │
   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │  (immutable, provenance-bearing)
                            ▼
        DOWNSTREAM (Composition / Publication)
```

Inputs flow into rendering **only** through the Input Precondition Gate; outputs flow out **only** through the sealed output artifact set. There is no side channel: rendering does not reach back into the authoring engines' internal state, and downstream consumers do not read rendering's working memory — they read the sealed outputs. The Rendering Platform sits strictly between an approved plan and a composed trailer; it is the last stage that may fail closed before publication, and the input contract is the first place that failure can occur.

---

# Responsibilities

The Inputs & Outputs Contract is responsible for:

* enumerating the complete, closed set of admissible input artifacts;
* enumerating the complete, closed set of permitted output artifacts;
* defining the canonical schema, required/optional fields, and types of each artifact;
* establishing the provenance of every input (which engine produced it) and every output (rendering produced it);
* defining validation-on-ingest rules that every input must pass before it is trusted;
* defining the input precondition gate as a conjunctive, fail-closed admission condition for rendering;
* guaranteeing input immutability (rendering never mutates upstream artifacts);
* guaranteeing output immutability (rendering seals outputs on emission);
* defining content-addressed fixity and lineage for auditability.

It is **not** responsible for:

* generating render tasks from the inputs (see **5.1.5**);
* choosing a render strategy per shot (see **5.2**);
* the runtime lifecycle of the Render Manifest (see **5.4.4**);
* queue admission, ordering, or leasing (see **5.4.1**);
* prompt assembly or injection of character/location/camera data (see **5.5**);
* asset technical or visual validation logic (see **5.6**);
* provider API semantics (see **5.3**).

The contract defines *what may enter and leave*; the cross-referenced sections define *what happens in between*.

---

# Design Objectives

The Inputs & Outputs Contract shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RIO-301 — Closed Input Set

The set of artifacts rendering may read is **fixed and closed**. Exactly six input artifacts are admissible: `shot-list.json`, `storyboard.json`, `camera-plan.json`, `render-strategy.json`, `character-bible.yaml`, and `location-bible.yaml`. No other artifact may be read by any rendering component as a source of creative or execution truth.

---

## RIO-302 — Closed Output Set

The set of artifacts rendering may write is **fixed and closed**. Exactly four document outputs (`render_manifest.json`, `render_queue.json`, `render_results.json`, `metadata.json`) and three asset directories (`video_assets/`, `preview_assets/`, `thumbnail_assets/`) are permitted. Rendering emits nothing else across its boundary.

---

## RIO-303 — Schema Conformance

Every input and output must conform to its canonical schema before it is trusted (inputs) or considered complete (outputs). A schema-nonconformant input is rejected at the gate; a schema-nonconformant output must never be sealed.

---

## RIO-304 — Provenance Established

Every input carries verifiable provenance identifying the upstream engine that produced it and the build it belongs to. Every output carries verifiable provenance identifying rendering as its producer and the exact input set it was derived from. Provenance is machine-checkable, not narrative.

---

## RIO-305 — Input Immutability

Rendering must never mutate, reorder, delete, or write to any input artifact. Inputs are opened read-only and their content fixity is verified at ingest. Any observed mutation of an input during a build is a fatal integrity error.

---

## RIO-306 — Output Immutability on Seal

Once an output artifact is sealed, its bytes must never change. A correction or re-render is expressed as a new version with new provenance, never as an in-place edit of a sealed artifact.

---

## RIO-307 — Precondition Gate is Fail-Closed

Rendering must not issue a single provider request until the input precondition gate is fully satisfied. A missing, malformed, unapproved, or tampered input blocks the entire build (Fail Closed, Principle 5). Partial admission is prohibited.

---

## RIO-308 — Deterministic Derivation

Given an identical, gate-passing input set, the derived render tasks, manifest identity, and cache keys must be reproducible. The contract carries enough fixity information (content hashes, versions) that two runs over the same inputs are byte-for-byte comparable in their derived identities (consistent with Determinism, **5.1.2**).

---

## RIO-309 — Secret Exclusion

No input or output artifact may contain provider credentials, API keys, or signed provider URLs bearing embedded secrets. Secrets live in a secret store and are resolved by workers at execution time.

---

## RIO-310 — Reference, Not Duplicate

Bulky or authoritative payloads (binary assets, full character descriptions, article content) are referenced by stable identifier or content-addressed path, not copied inline. The document outputs remain lightweight, auditable indexes over the asset directories.

---

# Input Contract — Overview

Rendering admits exactly six input artifacts, each with a single authoritative producer upstream.

| Input | Format | Producer (Provenance) | Role |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `shot-list.json` | JSON | Storyboard Engine | Enumerates every shot to be rendered; the unit-of-work list. |
| `storyboard.json` | JSON | Storyboard Engine | Approved visual plan per shot: blocking, composition, emotion, purpose. |
| `camera-plan.json` | JSON | Shot Planner | Per-shot camera parameters: lens, movement, angle, framing. |
| `render-strategy.json` | JSON | Render Strategy Planner | Per-shot strategy assignment and render profile. |
| `character-bible.yaml` | YAML | Character Engine | Immutable character reference descriptions, keyed by ref id. |
| `location-bible.yaml` | YAML | Location Engine | Immutable location/environment reference descriptions, keyed by ref id. |

The four JSON artifacts describe **what to render and how to execute it**; the two YAML bibles describe **the immutable identities** (characters, locations) that prompt assembly (**5.5**) injects by reference. Rendering never invents a character or location — it resolves every reference id against the bibles. All six are produced only after their respective upstream Quality Gates have passed (Principle 1 — Planning Before Rendering); rendering treats their arrival as a signal that authoring is complete and approved, not as an invitation to revise it.

---

# Input — `shot-list.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-shot-list"
shot_list:
  schema_version: string          # required; contract schema version, e.g. "2.0"
  build_id: string                # required; owning build identity
  storyboard_id: string           # required; source storyboard identity
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required; upstream Quality Gate approval id
  generated_at: iso8601           # required; producer timestamp
  content_hash: sha256            # required; fixity over the canonical body
  shots:                          # required; ordered, non-empty
    - shot_id: string             # required; globally unique within build
      scene_index: int            # required; narrative scene ordering
      shot_index: int             # required; ordering within scene
      duration_seconds: number    # required; intended shot duration
      shot_type: string           # required; e.g. facial_emotion | walking | title
      character_refs: [string]    # optional; character ref ids used in shot
      location_ref: string        # required; location ref id for shot
      dependencies: [string]      # optional; shot_ids that must precede
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. `character_refs` is optional (a title or diagram shot may have none); `dependencies` is optional; every other field is required.

## Provenance

Produced by the **Storyboard Engine** as the authoritative enumeration of renderable units. It is the canonical answer to "which shots exist and in what order." Every render task derives from exactly one `shots[]` entry (one shot ⇒ one render task, per **5.1.5** and Part 5 acceptance).

## Validation-on-Ingest

* `approved` must be `true` and `approval_ref` must resolve to a passed upstream gate; otherwise reject.
* `shots[]` must be non-empty; every `shot_id` must be unique within the build.
* `scene_index`/`shot_index` must form a total order with no duplicate `(scene_index, shot_index)` pairs.
* every `location_ref` and every `character_refs[]` entry must resolve against `location-bible.yaml` / `character-bible.yaml` (referential integrity).
* every `dependencies[]` entry must reference a `shot_id` present in the same list, and the dependency graph must be acyclic.
* `content_hash` must match the recomputed hash of the canonical body.

## Immutability

Read-only to rendering. `shot_list` defines the fixed unit-of-work set; rendering may neither add, remove, split, merge, nor reorder shots (RIO-305).

---

# Input — `storyboard.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-storyboard"
storyboard:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  storyboard_id: string           # required; matches shot-list.storyboard_id
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  shots:                          # required; one entry per shot_id in shot-list
    - shot_id: string             # required; must match a shot-list shot_id
      composition: string         # required; framing/composition intent
      blocking: string            # required; subject placement and action
      emotion: string             # required; intended emotional beat
      lighting: string            # required; lighting intent
      shot_purpose: string        # required; narrative function of the shot
      visual_notes: string        # optional; supplementary approved notes
      continuity_group: string    # optional; continuity linkage id
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. `visual_notes` and `continuity_group` are optional; every other field is required.

## Provenance

Produced by the **Storyboard Engine** as the approved visual plan. It is the semantic content that prompt assembly (**5.5.1**) turns into deterministic prompts. It contains creative intent that rendering executes but never authors.

## Validation-on-Ingest

* `storyboard_id` must equal `shot-list.json.storyboard_id`.
* `approved` must be `true`; `approval_ref` must resolve to a passed Storyboard Quality Gate.
* the set of `shots[].shot_id` must be **exactly equal** to the set in `shot-list.json` — no missing, no extra (bijective coverage).
* required creative fields must be present and non-empty for every shot.
* `content_hash` must verify.

## Immutability

Read-only. Rendering must never edit composition, blocking, emotion, lighting, or purpose. This is the mechanical enforcement of "rendering never modifies the approved storyboard" (Principle in **5.1.1**).

---

# Input — `camera-plan.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-camera-plan"
camera_plan:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  shots:                          # required; camera params per shot
    - shot_id: string             # required; matches shot-list shot_id
      lens: string                # required; e.g. "50mm"
      movement: string            # required; e.g. slow_push | static | dolly
      angle: string               # required; e.g. eye | high | low
      framing: string             # required; e.g. medium | close_up | wide
      movement_speed: string      # optional; e.g. slow | medium
      focus: string               # optional; focus intent
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. `movement_speed` and `focus` are optional; every other field is required.

## Provenance

Produced **only** by the **Shot Planner**. Camera parameters originate here and nowhere else. Rendering providers may not override these values (original 5.21). Camera injection (**5.5.4**) reads this artifact verbatim.

## Validation-on-Ingest

* every `shots[].shot_id` must match a `shot-list.json` shot; coverage must be complete (one camera entry per shot).
* required camera fields present and non-empty.
* `lens`, `movement`, `angle`, `framing` values must be drawn from the approved enumerations; unknown values are rejected (they would leak provider-specific or unauthorized camera language).
* `content_hash` must verify.

## Immutability

Read-only. Camera parameters are authoritative and may never be altered by rendering or by any provider adapter (RIO-305).

---

# Input — `render-strategy.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-render-strategy"
render_strategy:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  budget:                         # required; build render budget envelope
    currency: string              # required
    max_cost: number              # required; hard budget ceiling
  shots:                          # required; strategy per shot
    - shot_id: string             # required; matches shot-list shot_id
      strategy: string            # required; one of the fixed strategy set
      render_profile: string      # required; e.g. runway_preview | runway_final
      cost_estimate: number       # required; estimated cost for this shot
      provider: string            # optional; resolved provider, if fixed upstream
```

The `strategy` field is constrained to the fixed strategy set (original 5.7):

```yaml
allowed_strategies:
  - runway_video
  - animated_still
  - motion_graphics
  - typography
  - svg
  - cached_asset
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. Only `provider` is optional (it may be resolved later by the strategy model in **5.2.1**); every other field is required.

## Provenance

Produced by the **Render Strategy Planner**. It assigns exactly one strategy per shot and carries the build budget envelope consumed by cost-aware admission (**5.2.3**, **5.4.1**). It never mixes strategies within one shot (original 5.7 — mixed rendering strategies inside one shot are prohibited).

## Validation-on-Ingest

* every `shots[].shot_id` matches a `shot-list.json` shot; coverage complete (one strategy per shot).
* `strategy` ∈ `allowed_strategies`; exactly one per shot (no mixed strategies).
* `render_profile` valid for the assigned `strategy`.
* `budget.max_cost` ≥ Σ `cost_estimate` is checked as a soft advisory; a shortfall is recorded but the build-time budget enforcement lives in **5.2.3**/**5.4.1**, not here.
* `content_hash` must verify.

## Immutability

Read-only. Rendering executes the assigned strategies; it must never reassign a strategy, change a profile, or edit the budget. Strategy re-planning, if required, is an upstream action producing a new `render-strategy.json` version.

---

# Input — `character-bible.yaml`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-character-bible"
character_bible:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  characters:                     # required; keyed by ref id
    - ref: string                 # required; stable character ref, e.g. maya_v1
      display_name: string        # required
      description: string         # required; canonical visual description
      wardrobe: string            # optional
      distinguishing_features: string   # optional
      consistency_lock: bool      # required; must be true (immutable identity)
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. `wardrobe` and `distinguishing_features` are optional; every other field is required.

## Provenance

Produced by the **Character Engine**. It is the immutable source of character identity. Character injection (**5.5.2**) resolves each `character_refs[]` id from `shot-list.json` against this bible and injects the canonical `description` into the prompt. Characters are referenced by ID; descriptions are injected automatically (original 5.19).

## Validation-on-Ingest

* every `ref` unique.
* every character referenced anywhere in `shot-list.json.character_refs` must exist here (referential integrity).
* `consistency_lock` must be `true` for every character (identity is locked; original 5.19–5.20 intent).
* required fields present and non-empty.
* `content_hash` must verify.

## Immutability

Read-only, and additionally **identity-locked**: character descriptions are canonical and may never be paraphrased, augmented, or overridden by rendering or by a provider. Consistency across the trailer depends on byte-stable injection of these descriptions.

---

# Input — `location-bible.yaml`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-location-bible"
location_bible:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  locations:                      # required; keyed by ref id
    - ref: string                 # required; e.g. operations_center_night
      display_name: string        # required
      description: string         # required; canonical environment description
      time_of_day: string         # optional; e.g. night | day
      environment_lock: bool      # required; must be true (immutable environment)
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. Only `time_of_day` is optional; every other field is required.

## Provenance

Produced by the **Location Engine**. It is the immutable source of environment identity. Location injection (**5.5.3**) resolves each `location_ref` from `shot-list.json` against this bible. Locations are immutable; environment consistency is guaranteed (original 5.20).

## Validation-on-Ingest

* every `ref` unique.
* every `location_ref` used in `shot-list.json` must exist here (referential integrity).
* `environment_lock` must be `true` for every location.
* required fields present and non-empty.
* `content_hash` must verify.

## Immutability

Read-only and **environment-locked**. Location descriptions are canonical and may never be altered by rendering or by a provider, guaranteeing environment consistency across every shot that shares a location.

---

# Input Precondition Gate

Rendering is a downstream execution stage. It must not begin — no task generation, no queue admission, no provider request — until **all** input preconditions hold. The gate is conjunctive and fail-closed (RIO-307; Principle 5 — Fail Closed).

```yaml id="rio-precondition-gate"
input_precondition_gate:
  # 1. Presence — all six inputs available for the build
  shot_list_present: true
  storyboard_present: true
  camera_plan_present: true
  render_strategy_present: true
  character_bible_present: true
  location_bible_present: true

  # 2. Schema — every input conforms to its canonical schema
  all_inputs_schema_valid: true

  # 3. Approval — every input carries a passed upstream Quality Gate
  shot_list_approved: true
  storyboard_approved: true
  camera_plan_approved: true
  render_strategy_approved: true
  character_bible_approved: true
  location_bible_approved: true

  # 4. Coverage — bijective shot coverage across artifacts
  storyboard_covers_all_shots: true       # storyboard shot set == shot-list shot set
  camera_plan_covers_all_shots: true      # one camera entry per shot
  render_strategy_covers_all_shots: true  # one strategy per shot

  # 5. Referential integrity — every ref resolves
  character_refs_resolve: true
  location_refs_resolve: true

  # 6. Fixity — content hashes verify and build_id is consistent
  all_content_hashes_verified: true
  build_id_consistent_across_inputs: true

  # 7. Secret exclusion — no credentials embedded in any input
  no_secrets_in_inputs: true
```

Gate semantics:

* The gate is evaluated **once per build**, before any render task is generated.
* Evaluation is **all-or-nothing**: if any predicate is false, the build is refused and routed to Manual Review with a precise, machine-readable reason; no provider request is issued (RIO-307).
* The gate is **idempotent**: re-evaluating an already-passing input set yields the same result with no side effects.
* A gate pass produces a signed **admission record** capturing the verified `content_hash` of each input. That record becomes part of output provenance (see `metadata.json`), binding every output to the exact inputs it was derived from (RIO-304, RIO-308).

The precondition gate is the point at which the boundary of **5.1.1** is enforced: a trailer can only be rendered from an approved, complete, internally consistent plan — never from a partial or unapproved one.

---

# Output Contract — Overview

Rendering produces exactly seven output artifacts — four documents and three asset directories.

| Output | Kind | Producer | Role |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `render_manifest.json` | document | Rendering Platform | Authoritative per-shot render record: task, state, attempts, asset uri, validation, cache key. |
| `render_queue.json` | document | Rendering Platform | Point-in-time snapshot of queue state for observability and recovery. |
| `render_results.json` | document | Rendering Platform | Final per-shot outcome roll-up: success/failure, chosen asset, quality score. |
| `metadata.json` | document | Rendering Platform | Build-level provenance, input fixity, versions, and lineage. |
| `video_assets/` | directory | Rendering Platform | Final-quality rendered video assets. |
| `preview_assets/` | directory | Rendering Platform | Preview-profile rendered assets. |
| `thumbnail_assets/` | directory | Rendering Platform | Extracted thumbnails/poster frames per shot. |

The documents are lightweight indexes; the heavy binary payloads live in the three asset directories and are referenced from the documents by content-addressed path (RIO-310). No document embeds binary asset data.

---

# Output — `render_manifest.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-render-manifest"
render_manifest:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  input_admission_ref: string     # required; ref to the passed precondition gate record
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  sealed: bool                    # required; true once the build is complete
  content_hash: sha256            # required; fixity over the sealed body
  shots:                          # required; one entry per shot_id
    - shot_id: string             # required; matches shot-list shot_id
      provider: string            # required; runway | motion | svg | typography | cache
      task_id: string             # optional; provider task id (null pre-submit)
      status: string              # required; state-machine state (see 5.4.3)
      attempt: int                # required; current attempt number
      asset_uri: string           # optional; content-addressed path in video_assets/
      validation: object          # optional; technical + visual validation record
      cache_key: sha256           # required; render cache key (see 5.6.3)
```

This schema is the canonical projection of the runtime Render Manifest whose full lifecycle is owned by **5.4.4**; this section fixes only its **on-disk output shape** and immutability on seal. The `status` values are drawn from the render task state machine defined in **5.4.3** (NEW → SUBMITTED → RUNNING → DOWNLOADING → VALIDATING → READY → COMPOSED, with FAILED → RETRY → MANUAL REVIEW).

## Provenance & Immutability

Produced by the Rendering Platform as the authoritative record of every render task and its outcome. It is written incrementally during a build (updated after every state transition, per **5.4.4**) and **sealed** when the build completes. While in progress it is append/update-only under the manifest's own concurrency rules (**5.4.4**); once `sealed: true`, its bytes are immutable (RIO-306). A re-render produces a new manifest version with a new `build_id`-scoped provenance chain, never an in-place edit of a sealed manifest.

---

# Output — `render_queue.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-render-queue"
render_queue:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  snapshot_at: iso8601            # required; time of snapshot
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  queues:                         # required; depth per queue class
    - queue_class: string         # required; READY | SUBMISSION | ... | DEAD_LETTER
      depth: int                  # required
      items:                      # optional; references only, never payloads
        - render_task_id: string
          shot_id: string
          attempt: int
          visible_at: iso8601
```

The queue item references here are the observability projection of the live queue items whose authoritative schema and topology are owned by **5.4.1**. As in **5.4.1**, items carry references only — never prompts, secrets, or binary payloads (RIO-309, RIO-310).

## Provenance & Immutability

Produced by the Rendering Platform as a **point-in-time snapshot** of queue state for observability and crash recovery. It is not the authoritative source of render truth — the Render Manifest is (see **5.4.1** governing principle) — and queue state must be reconstructable from the manifest, retry schedule, leases, and provider records even if this snapshot is lost. Each snapshot is immutable once written; a later snapshot is a new file/version, never an in-place edit (RIO-306).

---

# Output — `render_results.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-render-results"
render_results:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  input_admission_ref: string     # required; the inputs this result derives from
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  sealed: bool                    # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  overall_status: string          # required; complete | partial_blocked | failed
  shots:                          # required; one final outcome per shot
    - shot_id: string             # required
```
