```yaml id="rio-render-results"
      outcome: string             # required; rendered | cache_hit | manual_review | failed
      strategy: string            # required; the strategy actually used
      chosen_asset_uri: string    # optional; final asset in video_assets/
      preview_asset_uri: string   # optional; asset in preview_assets/
      thumbnail_uri: string       # optional; asset in thumbnail_assets/
      quality_score: number       # optional; visual quality score (see 5.6.2)
      attempts_used: int          # required
```

Required and optional fields are as annotated in the schema. The asset URIs and `quality_score` are optional (absent for a shot that never produced a passing asset); every other field is required.

## Provenance & Immutability

Produced by the Rendering Platform as the **final outcome roll-up** consumed by Composition. Where the manifest is the process record, `render_results.json` is the settled conclusion: for each shot, what was ultimately produced and whether it passed. `quality_score` reflects the visual quality validation of **5.6.2**, whose `minimum_score` is `0.90`. It is sealed on build completion and immutable thereafter (RIO-306). A shot that could not meet the quality threshold after `max_retry` (3) attempts is recorded as `outcome: manual_review` or `failed`, honouring Fail Closed — no publishable trailer is fabricated from a substandard shot.

---

# Output — `metadata.json`

## Canonical Schema

```yaml id="rio-metadata"
metadata:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  sealed: bool                    # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required
  producer:                       # required; rendering provenance
    component: "RenderingPlatform"
    version: string               # required; rendering platform version
  input_lineage:                  # required; exact inputs consumed
    - artifact: string            # e.g. "shot-list.json"
      content_hash: sha256        # required; verified at the precondition gate
      producer: string            # required; upstream engine
      approval_ref: string        # required
  provider_versions:              # required; provider + model versions used
    - provider: string
      provider_version: string
  output_index:                   # required; the sealed output set
    render_manifest: sha256
    render_results: sha256
    render_queue: sha256
    asset_directories: [string]   # video_assets/ preview_assets/ thumbnail_assets/
```

## Provenance & Immutability

Produced by the Rendering Platform as the **lineage document** that binds every output to the exact input set (by verified `content_hash`) and to the provider versions used. It is the artifact an auditor reads to answer "what produced this trailer, from which approved plan, with which provider versions." It is sealed on build completion and immutable thereafter (RIO-306). The `input_lineage[].content_hash` values are copied from the precondition gate's admission record, making the input→output binding non-repudiable (RIO-304, RIO-308).

---

# Output — Asset Directories

Rendering writes three asset directories. Each holds binary render products, content-addressed for fixity and reuse.

```yaml id="rio-asset-dirs"
video_assets:
  purpose: "final-quality rendered video assets"
  naming: "<shot_id>.<content_hash>.<ext>"    # content-addressed
  written_by: "download + validation stage"
  referenced_from: "render_manifest.asset_uri, render_results.chosen_asset_uri"

preview_assets:
  purpose: "preview-profile assets (runway_preview and equivalents)"
  naming: "<shot_id>.<content_hash>.<ext>"
  written_by: "preview render stage (see 5.6.4)"
  referenced_from: "render_results.preview_asset_uri"

thumbnail_assets:
  purpose: "extracted thumbnails / poster frames per shot"
  naming: "<shot_id>.<content_hash>.<ext>"
  written_by: "post-validation thumbnail extraction"
  referenced_from: "render_results.thumbnail_uri"
```

Rules for all asset directories:

* Asset filenames are **content-addressed**: the `content_hash` component is the sha256 of the asset bytes, so two identical renders yield the same filename, enabling cache reuse (**5.6.3**) and deduplication.
* An asset file is write-once and immutable thereafter (RIO-306); a re-render produces a new content-addressed file and never overwrites an existing one.
* Asset directories are referenced from the document outputs by path only — documents never embed asset bytes (RIO-310) — and assets are written only after passing technical validation (**5.6.1**); a failing asset is never linked from a sealed document.

---

# Provenance & Lineage

The contract carries an unbroken lineage chain from approved plan to sealed asset:

```text
Upstream approval_ref  →  Input artifact + content_hash (named engine)
   →  Precondition Gate admission record (verifies every hash)
   →  metadata.json.input_lineage (copies verified hashes)
   →  render_manifest / render_results (reference content-addressed assets)
   →  Content-addressed asset files (hash == filename component)
```

Every link is machine-verifiable. From `metadata.json` alone an auditor can recover the exact inputs (by hash and producer), confirm they were approved (by `approval_ref`), and confirm the outputs were derived from those inputs (by the copied hashes and content-addressed asset paths). This satisfies RIO-304 and makes the input→output relationship non-repudiable.

---

# Consolidated Validation-on-Ingest Rules

Across all six inputs, ingest applies the following rule families before any input is trusted: **presence** (all six available), **schema** (canonical schema conformance), **types** (field types and enumerations match), **approval** (`approved == true` and `approval_ref` resolves to a passed gate), **fixity** (declared `content_hash` matches recomputed hash), **build_scope** (`build_id` identical across all six), **coverage** (storyboard/camera/strategy each cover exactly the shot-list shot set), **referential** (every `character_ref`/`location_ref` resolves in its bible), **acyclic_deps** (shot dependency graph is acyclic), **strategy_closed** (every strategy ∈ `allowed_strategies`, one per shot), **camera_closed** (lens/movement/angle/framing ∈ approved enumerations), and **secret_free** (no credentials or signed-secret URLs).

A failure of any rule blocks the build at the precondition gate (RIO-307). Ingest validation never silently repairs or coerces an input; it either passes it unchanged or rejects it (RIO-305).

---

# Consolidated Immutability Guarantees

```yaml id="rio-immutability"
immutability:
  inputs:
    access: read_only                # rendering opens inputs read-only
    mutation_by_rendering: prohibited # RIO-305
    fixity_checked: true             # content_hash verified at ingest
    on_change_during_build: fatal    # observed input mutation aborts the build
  outputs:
    write_semantics: write_once
    seal: true                       # sealed on build completion
    edit_after_seal: prohibited      # RIO-306
    correction_model: new_version    # a fix is a new versioned artifact
  assets:
    naming: content_addressed
    overwrite: prohibited            # new render ⇒ new content-addressed file
```

Input immutability makes "rendering never modifies the approved plan" mechanical; output immutability makes the historical record tamper-evident; content-addressing makes asset identity and reuse deterministic.

---

# Events

The contract layer emits events for every material boundary transition.

```yaml
rio_events:
  - input_artifact_received
  - input_fixity_verified
  - input_schema_validated
  - input_referential_integrity_checked
  - precondition_gate_passed
  - precondition_gate_failed
  - input_admission_record_written
  - output_document_written
  - output_asset_written
  - output_sealed
  - output_seal_violation_detected
  - input_mutation_detected
```

Every event references the `build_id` and artifact name; gate events additionally carry the pass/fail predicate breakdown and verified input hashes. All events are durably recorded for audit and replay.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml
rio_metrics:
  inputs_received_count
  precondition_gate_pass_count
  precondition_gate_fail_count
  gate_fail_reason_breakdown       # per failing predicate
  input_fixity_verification_time
  schema_validation_time
  referential_integrity_failures
  outputs_sealed_count
  seal_violation_attempts
  input_mutation_detections
  asset_files_written
  asset_dedup_hit_count            # identical content_hash reused
```

Gate-failure reason breakdown and referential-integrity failure counts feed the upstream authoring engines' quality review; seal-violation and input-mutation counts must be zero, and any nonzero value is a release-blocking integrity alarm.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The Inputs & Outputs Contract operates under least privilege.

Required controls:

* inputs opened **read-only** (no write access to input storage); outputs written to a **build-scoped namespace** with no cross-build write access; asset directories served downstream read-only;
* **no secrets in contract artifacts** — no provider API keys, tokens, or signed-secret URLs in any input or output (RIO-309); secrets are resolved by workers from a secret store at execution time;
* **fixity verification** on every input at ingest and every output at seal, and **provenance signing** of the admission record and `metadata.json`, so lineage cannot be forged;
* **audit events** for every gate decision, seal, and any detected mutation or seal violation.

A worker receives only the references it needs to resolve authoritative payloads; it never obtains credentials or bulk descriptions through a contract artifact.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="rio-config"
inputs_outputs_contract:
  inputs:
    required:
      - shot-list.json
      - storyboard.json
      - camera-plan.json
      - render-strategy.json
      - character-bible.yaml
      - location-bible.yaml
    fixity_algorithm: sha256
    fail_closed_on_missing: true
    fail_closed_on_schema_invalid: true
    fail_closed_on_unapproved: true
  precondition_gate:
    evaluation: once_per_build
    mode: all_or_nothing
    on_fail: route_to_manual_review
    emit_admission_record: true
  outputs:
    documents:
      - render_manifest.json
      - render_queue.json
      - render_results.json
      - metadata.json
    directories:
      - video_assets/
      - preview_assets/
      - thumbnail_assets/
    seal_on_build_complete: true
    asset_naming: content_addressed
    overwrite_sealed: prohibited
  provenance:
    sign_admission_record: true
    sign_metadata: true
```

All values are environment-overridable. Configuration is validated at startup; an invalid contract configuration fails closed and blocks admission rather than admitting inputs or sealing outputs under unsafe defaults.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* schema conformance and required-vs-optional enforcement for each of the six inputs;
* enumeration closure for `strategy` and camera fields;
* content-hash fixity verification (match and mismatch);
* referential integrity (character/location ref resolution and failure);
* bijective shot coverage across shot-list / storyboard / camera / strategy;
* acyclic dependency validation and cycle rejection;
* content-addressed asset filename derivation.

## Integration Tests

* full precondition gate pass over a complete, approved input set, and gate failure on each individual missing/unapproved/malformed input;
* admission record generation and its copy into `metadata.json.input_lineage`;
* end-to-end lineage: input hash → admission record → metadata → asset path;
* output document → asset directory reference resolution.

## Concurrency Tests

* concurrent reads of inputs by multiple workers with no input mutation;
* manifest update concurrent with a queue snapshot write;
* two builds writing to disjoint build-scoped namespaces without interference.

## Failure Injection Tests

* tampered input (hash mismatch), unapproved input (`approved: false`), and extra/missing shot vs shot-list all fail the gate closed;
* attempt to overwrite a sealed output → rejected and alarmed;
* attempt to write a secret into a contract artifact → rejected;
* attempt to mutate an input during a build → fatal integrity abort.

## Recovery Tests

* rebuild lineage and verify outputs from `metadata.json` alone;
* reconstruct queue state after loss of `render_queue.json` snapshot (via manifest);
* re-run over identical gate-passing inputs yields identical derived identities (determinism, RIO-308).

---

# Quality Gate

```yaml
inputs_outputs_contract_quality_gate:
  all_six_inputs_present: true
  all_inputs_schema_valid: true
  all_inputs_approved: true
  all_input_hashes_verified: true
  shot_coverage_bijective: true
  all_refs_resolve: true
  dependency_graph_acyclic: true
  strategy_set_closed: true
  camera_enumerations_closed: true
  no_secrets_in_any_artifact: true
  precondition_gate_fail_closed_verified: true
  admission_record_signed: true
  all_outputs_sealed_on_completion: true
  no_sealed_output_edited_in_place: true
  assets_content_addressed: true
  metadata_lineage_binds_inputs_to_outputs: true
  input_immutability_verified: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RIO-401

The admissible input set is closed to exactly six artifacts; no other artifact is a source of rendering truth.

## Invariant RIO-402

The permitted output set is closed to exactly four documents and three asset directories; rendering emits nothing else across its boundary.

## Invariant RIO-403

Every input is opened read-only and is never mutated, reordered, or deleted by rendering.

## Invariant RIO-404

Rendering issues no provider request until the input precondition gate has passed in full; a failing gate blocks the entire build.

## Invariant RIO-405

Every input carries verifiable provenance (producer, approval, content hash) and is rejected if any of these cannot be established.

## Invariant RIO-406

Shot coverage across shot-list, storyboard, camera-plan, and render-strategy is bijective; no shot is missing from or extra in any of them.

## Invariant RIO-407

Every character and location reference used anywhere resolves against its bible; unresolved references block the gate.

## Invariant RIO-408

Every strategy value belongs to the fixed strategy set and exactly one strategy is assigned per shot.

## Invariant RIO-409

No contract artifact, input or output, contains provider credentials or signed-secret URLs.

## Invariant RIO-410

A sealed output artifact is immutable; corrections are new versions, never in-place edits.

## Invariant RIO-411

Asset files are content-addressed and write-once; a re-render produces a new file and never overwrites an existing asset.

## Invariant RIO-412

`metadata.json` binds every output to the exact, hash-verified input set, making the input→output derivation non-repudiable.

## Invariant RIO-413

Given an identical gate-passing input set, derived render tasks, manifest identity, and cache keys are reproducible.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR | Decision |
| --- | --- |
| ADR-415 | The Rendering Platform admits exactly six input artifacts and emits exactly four documents plus three asset directories; both sets are closed by contract. |
| ADR-416 | Rendering begins only after a conjunctive, fail-closed input precondition gate (presence, schema, approval, coverage, referential integrity, fixity, secret exclusion) has passed for the build. |
| ADR-417 | Inputs are read-only upstream truth verified by content hash; outputs are write-once and sealed on build completion, with corrections expressed as new versions rather than in-place edits. |
| ADR-418 | Binary render products are content-addressed and referenced by path from lightweight document outputs; documents never embed asset bytes, and identical renders deduplicate by hash. |
| ADR-419 | A signed `metadata.json` lineage record binds every output to the exact hash-verified input set and provider versions, making the input→output derivation auditable and non-repudiable. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.1.4** is complete when:

* The complete, closed set of six rendering inputs and seven rendering outputs is defined, each with a canonical schema, required/optional fields, provenance, validation-on-ingest rules, and immutability guarantees.
* Each input's producing upstream engine is named and its approval, coverage, referential integrity, and fixity checks are specified.
* The input precondition gate is defined as a conjunctive, fail-closed admission condition that blocks all rendering until every input is present, schema-valid, approved, complete, consistent, hash-verified, and secret-free.
* Input immutability (never mutated by rendering) and output immutability (sealed on emission, corrected only by new versions) are specified and testable.
* Asset outputs are content-addressed, write-once, and referenced from lightweight document outputs that never embed asset bytes or secrets.
* A signed lineage record binds every output to the exact input set it was derived from, making the boundary of **5.1.1** mechanically enforceable and the derivation auditable.
* Events, metrics, security boundaries, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the Inputs & Outputs Contract as a strict precondition gate and an immutable, provenance-bearing output record, without ever allowing rendering to edit the approved plan or rewrite its own sealed outputs.


---

# Part 5.1.5 — Rendering Pipeline Overview

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Rendering Pipeline** of the Rendering Platform: the ordered sequence of stages through which an approved Storyboard becomes a set of validated, composable cinematic assets.

It is an **orientation section**. Its job is to give every reader — and every implementer — a single, authoritative mental model of *how a shot becomes a rendered asset* before the later sections of Part 5 specify each stage in exhaustive detail.

The Rendering Pipeline is defined as the following ordered stages:

* **Storyboard** — the approved creative input, immutable to rendering;
* **Render Planner** — resolves per-shot render strategy and build profile;
* **Task Generation** — materializes one render task per shot;
* **Queue Submission** — admits render tasks into the render queue system;
* **Provider Execution** — dispatches, submits, and monitors provider jobs;
* **Asset Download** — retrieves produced media from the provider;
* **Validation** — enforces technical and visual quality gates;
* **Asset Cache** — records reusable, validated results by cache key;
* **Composition** — hands validated assets to the FFmpeg Composer.

This section specifies, **for each stage**:

* its single operational purpose;
* its authoritative inputs;
* its guaranteed outputs;
* the queue class it maps to (forward-referencing **5.4.1**);
* the render-task states it advances (forward-referencing **5.4.3**);
* its failure and escalation behavior.

The pipeline is a **contract of stages**, not an implementation. Each stage is owned in depth by a later section. This overview binds those sections together and guarantees that the stages compose into one deterministic, fail-closed execution flow.

The Rendering Pipeline never modifies the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard. It is an **execution layer**. It executes a plan; it does not author one.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the canonical ordering of the nine rendering stages;
* the purpose, input contract, and output contract of each stage;
* the mapping from each stage to its render queue class (**5.4.1**);
* the mapping from each stage to render-task lifecycle states (**5.4.3**);
* the stage-boundary handoff rules (what must be true before a task advances);
* the cache short-circuit that removes work from the pipeline before it begins;
* the failure, retry, and escalation edges between stages;
* the pipeline-level invariants that hold across all stages;
* the acceptance criteria that make this overview implementable.

This section does **not** specify:

* render strategy selection logic (defined in **5.2.1**, **5.2.2**, **5.2.3**);
* provider interface, submission payloads, or the Runway adapter (defined in **5.3.1**–**5.3.4**);
* queue topology, leasing, ordering, or backpressure (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the parallel execution model (defined in **5.4.2**);
* the render-task state machine and its transitions (defined in **5.4.3**);
* the Render Manifest schema and runtime state ownership (defined in **5.4.4**);
* retry classification, backoff, and failure taxonomy (defined in **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline values (defined in **5.4.6**);
* deterministic prompt assembly and injection (defined in **5.5.1**–**5.5.5**);
* asset validation and visual quality scoring logic (defined in **5.6.1**, **5.6.2**);
* render cache key construction and reuse policy (defined in **5.6.3**);
* preview and final rendering profiles (defined in **5.6.4**, **5.6.5**);
* observability and public interfaces (defined in **5.7.1**, **5.7.2**).

This section is the **map**; the sections above are the **territory**. Where this overview states a value, count, or rule, the owning section is authoritative and this overview must not contradict it.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the pipeline as a whole.

> **Every shot travels the same one-way pipeline; no stage may be skipped, reordered, or bypassed except by a validated cache hit.**

The rendering pipeline is a **directed, acyclic progression** from approved Storyboard to composed asset. A render task advances only forward, one stage at a time, and only when the previous stage's output contract has been satisfied and persisted.

There is exactly **one** legitimate way to remove work from the pipeline early: a **validated cache hit** (defined in **5.6.3**) proves the identical asset already exists, so the task is satisfied without provider execution. Every other exit — failure, timeout, budget exhaustion, sub-threshold quality — routes the task to **Retry**, **Manual Review**, or **Dead Letter**; none of them silently drop a shot or emit an unvalidated asset.

A corollary follows directly:

> **No asset reaches Composition without passing Validation.**

Composition consumes only assets that Validation has marked `READY`. An asset that fails technical or visual validation (**5.6.1**, **5.6.2**) never advances to Composition; it is retried or escalated. This preserves the Fail-Closed guarantee (Principle 5): if a shot cannot be rendered to standard, the platform escalates rather than publishing a substandard trailer.

---

# Architectural Position

The Rendering Pipeline sits between the approved Storyboard (the boundary defined in **5.1.1**) and the FFmpeg Composer (the downstream consumer). It is the internal spine of the Rendering Platform.

```text
        Approved Storyboard
        (immutable creative plan)
                │
                ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │      RENDERING PIPELINE      │
   │                              │
   │  Render Planner              │
   │        │                     │
   │        ▼                     │
   │  Task Generation             │
   │        │                     │
   │        ▼                     │
   │  Queue Submission ───────────┼──► Render Queue System (5.4.1)
   │        │                     │
   │        ▼                     │
   │  Provider Execution ─────────┼──► Provider Adapters (5.3)
   │        │                     │
   │        ▼                     │
   │  Asset Download              │
   │        │                     │
   │        ▼                     │
   │  Validation ─────────────────┼──► Quality Gates (5.6)
   │        │                     │
   │        ▼                     │
   │  Asset Cache ────────────────┼──► Render Cache (5.6.3)
   │        │                     │
   └────────┼─────────────────────┘
            ▼
     FFmpeg Composer (Composition)
            │
            ▼
     Trailer Assembly (Part 6+)
```

The pipeline is downstream of all creative approval and upstream of all composition. Every stage inside the box is an execution stage; nothing inside the box may alter the creative plan flowing into it.

The pipeline's runtime state is not owned by any stage. It is owned by the **Render Manifest** (**5.4.4**). Each stage reads its inputs from authoritative stores, produces outputs, persists the new state to the Manifest, and only then allows the task to advance. The queue system (**5.4.1**) transports tasks between stages; it never owns their truth.

---

# Responsibilities

The Rendering Pipeline (as an orchestrated whole) is responsible for:

* defining the fixed, ordered sequence of rendering stages;
* guaranteeing that each shot passes through every mandatory stage exactly once (or is satisfied by cache);
* guaranteeing forward-only progression with persisted stage boundaries;
* mapping every stage to a queue class and a lifecycle state;
* ensuring the cache short-circuit is evaluated before any provider cost is incurred;
* ensuring no asset reaches Composition without validation;
* ensuring every early exit is an explicit, recorded escalation, never a silent drop;
* preserving determinism and reproducibility across the whole flow.

The Rendering Pipeline is **not** responsible for:

* choosing *what* to render or *which* strategy to use (see **5.2**);
* the API mechanics of any provider (see **5.3**);
* the durable transport, ordering, or leasing of tasks (see **5.4.1**);
* owning render-task state (see **5.4.4**);
* deciding retry eligibility or backoff timing (see **5.4.5**);
* setting timeout values (see **5.4.6**);
* assembling prompts (see **5.5**);
* computing validation scores (see **5.6**);
* the internal mechanics of FFmpeg composition (Composition is the pipeline's terminal handoff, not an internal render stage).

The pipeline **coordinates** these owners into an ordered contract. It does not re-implement them.

---

# Pipeline Design Objectives

The Rendering Pipeline shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RPL-301 — Fixed Stage Order

The nine-stage order (Storyboard → Render Planner → Task Generation → Queue Submission → Provider Execution → Asset Download → Validation → Asset Cache → Composition) is fixed.

No configuration, provider, or build profile may reorder stages. A stage may be short-circuited only by a validated cache hit; it may never be permuted.

---

## RPL-302 — One-Way Progression

A render task advances forward through stages only.

Backward movement is expressed exclusively as a **retry** (a new attempt re-entering an earlier stage, defined in **5.4.5**), never as an in-place regression of the current attempt.

---

## RPL-303 — Persisted Stage Boundaries

A task advances to the next stage only after the current stage's output has been persisted to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) and the queue item acknowledged (**5.4.1**).

Stage boundaries are durable checkpoints, not in-memory transitions. A crash between stages resumes from the last persisted boundary, never re-executes a persisted stage.

---

## RPL-304 — Cache Precedes Cost

The cache short-circuit (**5.6.3**) is evaluated before Queue Submission and therefore before any provider cost is incurred.

A shot whose validated result already exists must never reach Provider Execution. Cache elimination precedes all provider work (consistent with the cost-priority order in **5.2.3**).

---

## RPL-305 — Validation Precedes Composition

No asset advances from Validation to Asset Cache or Composition unless it has passed both technical validation (**5.6.1**) and visual quality validation (**5.6.2**).

The minimum visual quality score is **0.90** (owned by **5.6.2**). Assets below the gate are retried or escalated, never composed.

---

## RPL-306 — Explicit Escalation Only

Every stage exit that is not "advance forward" or "cache-satisfied" is an explicit, recorded escalation:

* recoverable failure → **Retry** (RETRY queue);
* non-retryable or exhausted failure → **Dead Letter** (DEAD_LETTER queue);
* human decision required → **Manual Review** (MANUAL_REVIEW queue).

No stage may silently discard, ignore, or invisibly downgrade a render task (RPL-401, Fail Closed).

---

## RPL-307 — Stage Isolation

A backlog in one stage must not block unrelated stages of other tasks.

The pipeline is parallel across shots (**5.4.2**) and class-isolated across stages (**5.4.1**, RQ-307): a Download backlog for one shot must not prevent Provider Execution monitoring of another.

---

## RPL-308 — Reproducibility

Given identical approved inputs (Storyboard, strategy assignment, provider version, render profile), the pipeline must produce identical stage transitions and identical cache keys.

The pipeline is deterministic end to end (consistent with Determinism, Performance Constraint 2). Randomness is confined to bounded, deterministically-seeded jitter for provider polling (**5.4.1**).

---

# The Nine Stages

The pipeline consists of nine stages. Stages 1 and 9 are **boundary stages** (the immutable input and the downstream handoff); stages 2 through 8 are **execution stages** owned in depth by later sections.

Each stage below is specified by: **Purpose**, **Inputs**, **Outputs**, **Queue Class** (**5.4.1**), **Lifecycle State** (**5.4.3**), and **Failure Behavior**.

---

## Stage 1 — Storyboard (Boundary: Input)

**Purpose.** The Storyboard is the approved creative plan that the pipeline executes. It is the input boundary of rendering, defined in **5.1.1** and detailed as a contract in **5.1.4**. Rendering treats it as **immutable**: read-only, versioned, and never mutated by any downstream stage.

**Inputs.** The approved artifact set (canonical files from original 5.4, contract-detailed in **5.1.4**):

```yaml
storyboard_inputs:
  shot_list:        shot-list.json        # the enumerated shots to render
  storyboard:       storyboard.json       # per-shot visual plan
  camera_plan:      camera-plan.json      # per-shot camera language (5.5.4)
  render_strategy:  render-strategy.json  # per-shot strategy assignment (5.2)
  character_bible:  character-bible.yaml   # character refs (5.5.2)
  location_bible:   location-bible.yaml    # location refs (5.5.3)
```

All six artifacts must be present and their governing Quality Gates passed before rendering may begin (Principle 1 — Planning Before Rendering).

**Outputs.** None produced; the Storyboard is consumed, not produced, by the pipeline. Its outputs are the guaranteed presence and approval of the artifact set.

**Queue Class.** None. The Storyboard predates queueing.

**Lifecycle State.** Pre-`NEW`. No render task exists yet.

**Failure Behavior.** If any required artifact is missing or unapproved, the pipeline **does not start**. This is a precondition failure surfaced at the rendering boundary (**5.1.1**), not a per-task failure. Rendering never begins on an unapproved plan.

---

## Stage 2 — Render Planner

**Purpose.** The Render Planner resolves, for the build as a whole, *how* each shot will be rendered: which render strategy applies to each shot and which build profile (preview vs final) governs the run. It is the bridge between the approved plan and materialized work. Its selection logic is owned by **5.2** (**5.2.1** strategy model, **5.2.2** decision matrix, **5.2.3** cost-aware selection).

**Inputs.**

```yaml
render_planner_inputs:
  shot_list:           shot-list.json
  render_strategy:     render-strategy.json   # authoritative per-shot strategy
  build_profile:       preview | final        # 5.6.4 / 5.6.5
  budget_reservation:  build_render_budget     # cost envelope (5.2.3)
```

**Outputs.** A resolved render plan: for every shot, exactly one strategy from the canonical set and the render profile that will be used.

```yaml
render_plan_entry:
  shot_id:
  strategy:      runway_video | animated_still | motion_graphics | typography | svg | cached_asset
  render_profile: runway_preview | runway_final | motion_* | svg_* | typography_*
  provider:      runway | motion | svg | typography | cache
  estimated_cost:
```

Each shot has **exactly one** render strategy; mixed strategies inside one shot are prohibited (original 5.7). The cost-priority order Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway (**5.2.3**) governs how strategies are ranked for admission.

**Queue Class.** None yet. Planning produces the input to Task Generation.

**Lifecycle State.** Pre-`NEW`. The plan exists; render tasks do not.

**Failure Behavior.** If a shot cannot be assigned a valid strategy (unsupported input, unresolvable reference), the shot is routed to **Manual Review** before any task is generated. The Planner never guesses a strategy.

---

## Stage 3 — Task Generation

**Purpose.** Task Generation materializes the render plan into concrete, persisted **render tasks** — exactly one per shot (original 5.11: "Each shot creates one task"). This is the moment a render task acquires identity and enters the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**). From here forward the task is a first-class, durable, trackable entity.

**Inputs.**

```yaml
task_generation_inputs:
  render_plan:     [render_plan_entry]     # from Stage 2
  shot_hash:       per-shot content hash     # feeds cache key (5.6.3)
  provider_version: resolved per provider     # feeds cache key (5.6.3)
```

**Outputs.** One persisted render task per shot, written to the Render Manifest (canonical schema, original 5.14, owned by **5.4.4**):

```yaml
render_task:
  render_task_id:   # deterministic per shot + build
  shot_id:
  build_id:
  provider:
  render_profile:
  strategy:
  attempt:          0
  status:           NEW
  cache_key:        # sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile) — 5.6.3
  asset_uri:        null
  validation:       null
```

Task Generation also performs the **cache lookup** (**5.6.3**): it computes each task's cache key and checks whether a validated asset already exists.

**Queue Class.** None yet — but this stage decides the *next* class. A **cache hit** marks the task `READY→CACHED` in the Manifest and it bypasses queueing entirely (**5.4.1** admission contract, RQ-305 / Invariant RQM-305). A **cache miss** makes the task eligible for the READY queue.

**Lifecycle State.** `NEW` (original 5.15). The render task now exists in the Manifest.

**Failure Behavior.** If a render task cannot be persisted, no queue admission occurs; the pipeline fails closed for that shot rather than dispatching an unpersisted task (consistent with **5.4.1** RQ-301, Durable Intent).

---

## Stage 4 — Queue Submission

**Purpose.** Queue Submission admits eligible (cache-miss) render tasks into the render queue system and moves them toward provider dispatch. It is the entry into the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**), which owns admission, deterministic ordering, concurrency limits, provider rate limiting, and leasing. This stage is where cost-aware admission and backpressure (**5.4.1**) apply.

**Inputs.**

```yaml
queue_submission_inputs:
  render_task:       # persisted NEW task, cache miss
  priority_score:    # composite ordering key (5.4.1)
  budget_reservation:
  provider_availability:
```

Admission preconditions (owned by **5.4.1**) must all hold: storyboard approved, strategy assigned, task persisted, cache lookup completed, not a cache hit, budget reservation available, provider available.

**Outputs.** The task placed on the **READY** queue, then promoted to the **SUBMISSION** queue when concurrency and rate-limit headroom exist. Deterministic ordering (cost rank, scene/shot index, enqueue timestamp) governs dequeue order.

**Queue Class.** `READY`, then `SUBMISSION` (**5.4.1**, the fixed stage→class mapping).

**Lifecycle State.** Still `NEW` while `READY`; transitions toward `SUBMITTED` as it leaves `SUBMISSION` (owned by **5.4.3**).

**Failure Behavior.** No headroom is **not** a failure: the task waits, never dropped (**5.4.1**, RQ-305). Budget exhaustion **defers** expensive work and, if the build's budget is spent, routes the build to **Manual Review** rather than producing a partial trailer (RQ-306 / Invariant RQM-310, Fail Closed).

---

## Stage 5 — Provider Execution

**Purpose.** Provider Execution submits the render task to the assigned provider and monitors the provider job to completion. It is asynchronous and non-blocking (Principle 3): submission returns immediately and progress is tracked by delayed polling, never busy-waiting. The provider acts **only** as a rendering backend (**5.3.1**): it renders; it never creates story, pacing, dialogue, or camera language. Submission mechanics are owned by **5.3.3**; the Runway adapter by **5.3.4**; the interface contract by **5.3.2**.

**Inputs.**

```yaml
provider_execution_inputs:
  render_task:       # SUBMISSION-leased task
  assembled_prompt:   # deterministic prompt (5.5.1) — resolved by worker, not carried in queue
  render_profile:     # runway_preview | runway_final | ...
  provider_task_ref:  # created on submit, recorded in Manifest
```

The prompt is assembled deterministically at execution time from Character + Location + Camera + Lighting + Blocking + Emotion + Shot Purpose + Visual Style (original 5.18, owned by **5.5.1**). The provider never receives article text.

**Outputs.** A completed provider job with a reference to produced media, recorded in the Manifest:

```yaml
provider_execution_output:
  provider_task_id:
  provider_status:   completed
  asset_ref:          # provider-side asset locator (not yet downloaded)
```

**Queue Class.** `SUBMISSION` (dispatch) then `PROVIDER_MONITOR` (delayed polling to completion) (**5.4.1**). Polling uses `poll_interval` 20s with bounded jitter and an overall render deadline of 45m (original 5.17, owned by **5.4.6**).

**Lifecycle State.** `SUBMITTED` → `RUNNING` (original 5.15, owned by **5.4.3**).

**Failure Behavior.** Provider throttling (HTTP 429) is normal flow control, not failure (**5.4.1**, RQ-305). Recoverable provider failures (provider timeout, temporary API failure) route to **Retry** (original 5.16; classification owned by **5.4.5**). Non-retryable provider rejections (invalid prompt, unsupported input, permanent rejection) route to **Dead Letter**. Maximum attempts is **3** (original 5.16).

---

## Stage 6 — Asset Download

**Purpose.** Asset Download retrieves the produced media from the provider into the platform's asset repository, converting a provider-side reference into a locally durable, addressable asset. Download is bounded by `download_timeout` 120s (original 5.17, owned by **5.4.6**).

**Inputs.**

```yaml
asset_download_inputs:
  render_task:
  asset_ref:          # provider-side locator from Stage 5
  download_timeout:   120s   # 5.4.6
```

**Outputs.** A locally stored asset with a stable URI, recorded in the Manifest:

```yaml
asset_download_output:
  asset_uri:          # durable local/repository URI
  bytes:
  content_hash:       # integrity anchor (5.6.1)
```

**Queue Class.** `DOWNLOAD` (**5.4.1**), which is concurrency-isolated so a download backlog does not stall provider monitoring (RQ-307).

**Lifecycle State.** `DOWNLOADING` (original 5.15).

**Failure Behavior.** A corrupted or incomplete download is a recoverable failure and routes to **Retry** (original 5.16: "corrupted download" is a retry reason). Repeated download failure past max attempts routes to **Dead Letter**.

---

## Stage 7 — Validation

**Purpose.** Validation is the quality gate of the pipeline. It enforces that a downloaded asset is both **technically valid** (**5.6.1**) and **visually acceptable** (**5.6.2**) before it may be reused or composed. This is where Fail Closed (Principle 5) is operationally enforced: an asset that cannot meet standard is never composed.

**Inputs.**

```yaml
validation_inputs:
  render_task:
  asset_uri:
  content_hash:
```

**Outputs.** A validation verdict recorded in the Manifest.

Technical checks (original 5.23, owned by **5.6.1**):

```yaml
technical_validation:
  resolution:       pass | fail
  duration:         pass | fail
  codec:            pass | fail
  frame_rate:       pass | fail
  decode_success:   pass | fail
  file_integrity:   pass | fail
```

Visual quality checks (original 5.24, owned by **5.6.2**): human consistency, continuity, framing, lighting, camera movement, motion artifacts, visual coherence — combined into a score with `minimum_score: 0.90`.

```yaml
visual_validation:
  score:            # >= 0.90 to pass
  verdict:          READY | REJECTED
```

**Queue Class.** `VALIDATION` (**5.4.1**), concurrency-isolated from Submission and Download (RQ-307).

**Lifecycle State.** `VALIDATING` → `READY` on pass (original 5.15).

**Failure Behavior.** Technical invalidity is treated per **5.6.1** (typically retry if the asset itself is corrupt). A **failed visual quality validation is not retried by default** (original 5.16: "No retry for failed quality validation") — a sub-threshold asset after the allowed attempts routes to **Manual Review** (continuity risk, quality below threshold), never to Composition. This is the concrete enforcement of RPL-305.

---

## Stage 8 — Asset Cache

**Purpose.** Asset Cache records a validated asset against its cache key so that an identical future shot is satisfied without re-rendering (Principle 4 — Incremental Rendering: only modified shots are rendered; previously approved assets are reused). Caching happens **only after** Validation passes: the cache never stores an unvalidated asset. Cache key construction and reuse policy are owned by **5.6.3**.

**Inputs.**

```yaml
asset_cache_inputs:
  render_task:
  asset_uri:          # validated, READY
  cache_key:          # sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile) — 5.6.3
  validation:         READY
```

**Outputs.** A durable cache entry mapping `cache_key → validated asset`, and the Manifest updated to reflect cache eligibility:

```yaml
asset_cache_output:
  cache_key:
  asset_uri:
  cached_at:
  reusable:           true
```

**Queue Class.** None dedicated; caching is a write performed at the Validation→Composition boundary. (The cache *lookup* occurs earlier, in Stage 3; the cache *write* occurs here.)

**Lifecycle State.** `READY` (asset is validated and now cacheable). The lookup counterpart at Stage 3 produces `READY→CACHED` for hits.

**Failure Behavior.** A cache write failure is **non-blocking** to the current build: the validated asset still advances to Composition. A failed cache write only forfeits future reuse; it never invalidates a validated asset or fails the shot. Only **validated** assets are ever cached (RPL-402).

---

## Stage 9 — Composition (Boundary: Output)

**Purpose.** Composition is the pipeline's terminal handoff: validated assets are delivered to the **FFmpeg Composer**, which assembles them into the trailer per the approved plan. Composition is the output boundary of rendering; the internal mechanics of the Composer belong to downstream Parts, not to the render pipeline. The pipeline's responsibility ends when it has delivered a complete set of validated, ordered assets.

**Inputs.**

```yaml
composition_inputs:
  validated_assets:   # every shot's READY asset
  ordering:            scene_index, shot_index   # narrative order
  metadata:            metadata.json
```

**Outputs.** The rendering deliverables handed to the Composer (canonical outputs from original 5.5, contract-detailed in **5.1.4**):

```yaml
composition_handoff:
  render_manifest:  render_manifest.json
  render_results:   render_results.json
  video_assets:     video_assets/
  preview_assets:   preview_assets/
  thumbnail_assets: thumbnail_assets/
  metadata:         metadata.json
```

**Queue Class.** None. Composition is the exit of the pipeline.

**Lifecycle State.** `COMPOSED` (original 5.15), the terminal success state.

**Failure Behavior.** Composition consumes only `READY` assets (RPL-401 / the governing corollary). If any mandatory shot lacks a validated asset, the build **cannot** compose a complete trailer; it is held for **Manual Review** rather than composing an incomplete or substandard trailer (Fail Closed). Composition never renders, retries, or repairs a shot — it only assembles what Validation has approved.

---

# Stage Flow Diagram

The following is the canonical, forward-only pipeline flow, including the cache short-circuit and the three escalation edges. This diagram is the normative reference for stage ordering (RPL-301).

```text
                    ┌───────────────────────┐
                    │   Storyboard          │  (approved, immutable)
                    │   [Stage 1]           │
                    └──────────┬────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
                    ┌───────────────────────┐
                    │   Render Planner      │  strategy + profile per shot
                    │   [Stage 2]           │  (5.2)
                    └──────────┬────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
                    ┌───────────────────────┐
                    │   Task Generation     │  one render task per shot
                    │   [Stage 3]           │  → Manifest (5.4.4)
                    │   + cache lookup      │  (5.6.3)
                    └──────────┬────────────┘
                     cache hit │ cache miss
                    ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                    ▼                     ▼
            ┌──────────────┐   ┌───────────────────────┐
            │ READY→CACHED │   │  Queue Submission     │  READY → SUBMISSION
            │ (bypass)     │   │  [Stage 4]  (5.4.1)   │
            └──────┬───────┘   └──────────┬────────────┘
                   │                      │
                   │                      ▼
                   │           ┌───────────────────────┐
                   │           │  Provider Execution   │  SUBMISSION → MONITOR
                   │           │  [Stage 5]  (5.3)     │  SUBMITTED → RUNNING
                   │           └──────────┬────────────┘
                   │                      │
                   │                      ▼
                   │           ┌───────────────────────┐
                   │           │  Asset Download       │  DOWNLOAD
                   │           │  [Stage 6]            │  DOWNLOADING
                   │           └──────────┬────────────┘
                   │                      │
                   │                      ▼
                   │           ┌───────────────────────┐
                   │           │  Validation           │  VALIDATION
                   │           │  [Stage 7]  (5.6)     │  VALIDATING
                   │           └──────────┬────────────┘
                   │              pass    │
                   │                      ▼
                   │           ┌───────────────────────┐
                   │           │  Asset Cache          │  cache write
                   │           │  [Stage 8]  (5.6.3)   │  READY
                   │           └──────────┬────────────┘
                   │                      │
                   └──────────┬───────────┘
                              ▼
                    ┌───────────────────────┐
                    │   Composition         │  FFmpeg Composer
                    │   [Stage 9]           │  COMPOSED
                    └───────────────────────┘

        ── Escalation edges (from Stages 5, 6, 7) ──────────────

        recoverable failure ──► RETRY queue ──► re-enter at
                                                submission/download
                                                (attempt+1, max 3)

        exhausted / non-retryable ──► DEAD_LETTER queue
                                       (immutable evidence)

        quality < 0.90 / budget spent / continuity risk
                                   ──► MANUAL_REVIEW queue
```

Key reading rules for the diagram:

* Forward edges are solid vertical flow; there is exactly one forward path.
* The only branch before Composition is the **cache short-circuit** at Stage 3.
* Every failure edge lands in exactly one of three named queues; none loops silently.
* A **Retry** re-enters an earlier stage as a *new attempt* (RPL-302), it does not rewind the current attempt.

---

# Stage Contract Table

The following table is the normative summary binding every stage to its input, output, owning section, queue class (**5.4.1**), and lifecycle state (**5.4.3**). Where a value appears here and in an owning section, the owning section is authoritative.

| # | Stage | Primary Input | Primary Output | Queue Class (5.4.1) | Lifecycle State (5.4.3) | Owned By |
|---|-------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------------------|----------|
| 1 | Storyboard | approved artifact set | (consumed) | — | pre-`NEW` | 5.1.1 / 5.1.4 |
| 2 | Render Planner | shot-list + render-strategy | per-shot strategy + profile | — | pre-`NEW` | 5.2 |
| 3 | Task Generation | render plan + shot hash | persisted render task + cache lookup | — (READY or CACHED next) | `NEW` | 5.4.4 / 5.6.3 |
| 4 | Queue Submission | persisted task (cache miss) | admitted, ordered, dispatch-ready | READY → SUBMISSION | `NEW` → (toward) `SUBMITTED` | 5.4.1 |
| 5 | Provider Execution | task + assembled prompt | completed provider job + asset ref | SUBMISSION → PROVIDER_MONITOR | `SUBMITTED` → `RUNNING` | 5.3 |
| 6 | Asset Download | asset ref | durable local asset + hash | DOWNLOAD | `DOWNLOADING` | 5.6.1 (integrity) |
| 7 | Validation | downloaded asset | verdict (READY / REJECTED) | VALIDATION | `VALIDATING` → `READY` | 5.6.1 / 5.6.2 |
| 8 | Asset Cache | validated asset + cache key | cache entry (reusable) | — (cache write) | `READY` | 5.6.3 |
| 9 | Composition | all validated assets | Composer handoff bundle | — | `COMPOSED` | 5.1.4 (outputs) |

**Escalation contract (applies to Stages 5–7):**

| Exit Condition | Queue Class (5.4.1) | Owned By | Result |
|----------------|---------------------|----------|--------|
| Recoverable failure (provider/API timeout, corrupted download) | RETRY | 5.4.5 | new attempt, `attempt+1`, max 3 |
| Non-retryable / attempts exhausted | DEAD_LETTER | 5.4.5 / 5.4.1 | immutable terminal evidence |
| Quality < 0.90 / budget exhausted / continuity risk | MANUAL_REVIEW | 5.4.1 / 5.6.2 | human decision, no partial trailer |
| Operator pause / cancel / reprioritize | CONTROL | 5.4.1 | reserved-capacity control op |

---

# Stage Boundary Handoff Rules

A render task crosses a stage boundary only when the previous stage's contract is satisfied and persisted. The handoff protocol is uniform (mirroring the acknowledgement protocol in **5.4.1**):

```text
1. Stage worker completes its effect (plan / generate / submit / poll / download / validate / cache).
2. Worker persists the new render-task state and outputs to the Render Manifest (5.4.4).
3. Worker acknowledges the current queue item (5.4.1).
4. Queue Manager routes the task to the next stage's queue class.
```

Handoff rules, normatively:

* **Persist-before-advance.** No task advances before its stage output is durable (RPL-303). A crash between steps 2 and 3 causes at-least-once re-delivery; the idempotent stage worker observes the already-persisted state and safely re-acknowledges (consistent with **5.4.1**, RQ-303).
* **One active class.** A task occupies exactly one workload queue class at a time (**5.4.1**, RQ-302). Being "in Download" and "in Validation" simultaneously is prohibited.
* **Cache gate before submission.** The cache lookup at Stage 3 must complete before Stage 4 admission (RPL-304). A cache hit must never reach Stage 4.
* **Validation gate before cache and composition.** Stages 8 and 9 consume only `READY` assets (RPL-305). An unvalidated asset must never be cached or composed.
* **Escalation is terminal-for-the-attempt.** Routing to RETRY, MANUAL_REVIEW, or DEAD_LETTER ends the current attempt's forward progress; it never lets the attempt silently continue.

---

# Cache Short-Circuit

The single legitimate early exit from the pipeline is a **validated cache hit**, evaluated during Task Generation (Stage 3) and owned by **5.6.3**.

```text
Task Generation computes:
  cache_key = sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile)

Cache lookup(cache_key):
  ├── HIT  → mark render task READY→CACHED in Manifest
  │         → task bypasses Queue Submission, Provider Execution,
  │           Asset Download, and Validation entirely
  │         → validated cached asset flows directly to Composition
  │
  └── MISS → task eligible for Queue Submission (Stage 4)
```

Rules:

* Cache elimination **precedes all provider cost** (RPL-304 / **5.2.3** cost-priority order Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway).
* A cache hit reuses an asset that was **already validated** before it was cached (RPL-402), so bypassing Validation on a hit does not weaken the Fail-Closed guarantee.
* A cache hit is the **only** stage bypass; no other condition may skip Provider Execution, Download, or Validation.
* Cache hits are **never enqueued** for provider submission (consistent with **5.4.1**, Invariant RQM-305).

This short-circuit is why the pipeline is efficient without being unsafe: it removes redundant work while preserving the guarantee that every composed asset has, at some point, passed Validation.

---

# Failure & Escalation Flow

The pipeline has exactly three escalation destinations. Every non-forward, non-cache exit lands in one of them; there is no fourth outcome and no silent drop (RPL-306, RPL-401).

```text
Stage 5/6 recoverable failure
   (provider timeout, temp API failure, corrupted download)
        │
        ▼
   RETRY queue ──► delayed re-admission (backoff, 5.4.5)
        │           re-enter submission/download as attempt+1
        ▼
   attempts < max (3)?  ── yes ──► retry
        │
        no
        ▼
   DEAD_LETTER queue (immutable evidence, 5.4.1)


Stage 5 non-retryable failure
   (invalid prompt, unsupported input, provider permanent rejection)
        │
        ▼
   DEAD_LETTER queue (no retry — original 5.16)


Stage 7 quality gate
   (visual score < 0.90 after allowed attempts, continuity risk)
        │
        ▼
   MANUAL_REVIEW queue (human decision — never composed)


Stage 4 / build-level budget exhaustion
        │
        ▼
   MANUAL_REVIEW queue (no partial trailer — Fail Closed)
```

Normative escalation rules:

* **Retry ownership.** The pipeline does not decide *whether* a failure is retryable; that classification is owned by **5.4.5**. The pipeline only routes to the RETRY queue once a retry is authorized and recorded.
* **Max attempts.** `max_retry: 3` (original 5.16). A task exceeding it is dead-lettered, never retried indefinitely.
* **No-retry set.** Failed quality validation, invalid prompt, and unsupported input are **not** retried (original 5.16); they escalate (Manual Review or Dead Letter).
* **Dead Letter is evidence.** Dead-lettered tasks preserve full attempt history and are never silently mutated (consistent with **5.4.1**, Invariant RQM-312).
* **Fail Closed.** Budget exhaustion, unresolvable quality failure, and missing validated assets escalate to Manual Review rather than producing a partial or substandard trailer (Principle 5).

---

# Events

The pipeline, as an orchestration, emits a coarse-grained event at every stage boundary crossing. These complement the fine-grained queue events in **5.4.1** and the state-machine events in **5.4.3**; they exist so an operator can watch a shot progress stage by stage.

```yaml
pipeline_stage_events:
  - shot_planned                 # Stage 2 complete
  - render_task_generated        # Stage 3 complete (task persisted)
  - cache_hit_bypass             # Stage 3 short-circuit
  - render_task_submitted_stage  # Stage 4 → provider dispatch
  - provider_execution_complete  # Stage 5 complete
  - asset_downloaded             # Stage 6 complete
  - asset_validated              # Stage 7 pass
  - asset_cached                 # Stage 8 complete
  - shot_composed                # Stage 9 complete
  - shot_retry_scheduled         # escalation → RETRY
  - shot_manual_review           # escalation → MANUAL_REVIEW
  - shot_dead_lettered           # escalation → DEAD_LETTER
```

Every event references the `render_task_id`, `shot_id`, `build_id`, `stage`, `attempt`, and originating `queue_class`, and is durably recorded for audit and replay. Stage events must never be the authoritative record of state; the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) is.

---

# Metrics

Pipeline-level metrics express stage throughput and stage-boundary health. They aggregate the per-stage timings recorded by observability (**5.7.1**, original 5.29) into a stage view.

```yaml
pipeline_metrics:
  stage_dwell_time:              # time a task spends in each stage
    - render_planner
    - task_generation
    - queue_submission
    - provider_execution
    - asset_download
    - validation
    - asset_cache
    - composition
  stage_throughput:              # tasks advancing per stage per interval
  cache_hit_rate                 # SLO target >= 70%
  retry_rate                     # SLO target <= 5%
  manual_review_rate
  dead_letter_rate
  pipeline_completion_time       # NEW -> COMPOSED end-to-end
  shots_awaiting_composition
```

These roll up to the platform SLOs referenced throughout Part 5 (cache hit rate ≥ 70%, render retry rate ≤ 5%). Any stage whose dwell time or escalation rate breaches its threshold is surfaced for review; the pipeline never masks a stalled stage by silently dropping its backlog (RPL-307, RPL-401).

---

# Configuration Model

The pipeline itself is not richly configurable — its **order is fixed** (RPL-301). What is configurable is *which profile* the pipeline runs under and the deadlines that bound its stages; those values are owned by the sections named below and merely referenced here.

```yaml id="rpl-config"
rendering_pipeline:
  stage_order:                   # FIXED — not overridable (RPL-301)
    - storyboard
    - render_planner
    - task_generation
    - queue_submission
    - provider_execution
    - asset_download
    - validation
    - asset_cache
    - composition
  build_profile: preview | final       # 5.6.4 / 5.6.5
  gates:
    visual_quality_minimum_score: 0.90 # authoritative in 5.6.2
    max_retry: 3                        # authoritative in 5.4.5
  deadlines:                            # authoritative in 5.4.6
    submission_timeout: 30s
    poll_interval: 20s
    download_timeout: 120s
    overall_timeout: 45m
  cache:
    lookup_before_submission: true      # RPL-304 (5.6.3)
    store_only_validated: true          # RPL-402 (5.6.3)
```

The `stage_order` list is **immutable** and present only for documentation and validation; a configuration that attempts to reorder, remove, or duplicate stages fails closed at startup and blocks rendering rather than running a malformed pipeline. All threshold and deadline values are overridable only within the ranges permitted by their owning sections.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* each stage's input contract is enforced (missing input rejected);
* each stage's output contract is produced and persisted before advance;
* stage order is fixed and cannot be reordered by configuration;
* cache lookup occurs at Stage 3 before Stage 4 admission;
* a cache hit bypasses Stages 4–7 and flows to Composition;
* only `READY` assets are cached (Stage 8) and composed (Stage 9);
* each stage maps to the correct queue class and lifecycle state;
* deterministic cache-key computation for identical inputs.

## Integration Tests

* full happy path Storyboard → COMPOSED for a single cache-miss shot;
* full happy path for a cache-hit shot (bypass verified end to end);
* mixed build: some shots hit cache, some render, all compose;
* preview profile and final profile both traverse the same stage order;
* stage-boundary handoff persists state before acknowledgement (**5.4.1**);
* parallel shots progress through stages independently (**5.4.2**, RPL-307).

## Concurrency Tests

* multiple shots occupy different stages simultaneously without cross-blocking;
* a Download backlog for one shot does not stall Provider monitoring of another;
* a Validation backlog does not stall new Submissions within budget;
* stage-boundary handoff is safe under at-least-once re-delivery.

## Failure Injection Tests

* provider timeout at Stage 5 → RETRY, attempt increments, capped at 3;
* corrupted download at Stage 6 → RETRY;
* visual score < 0.90 at Stage 7 → MANUAL_REVIEW, never composed;
* non-retryable provider rejection at Stage 5 → DEAD_LETTER;
* budget exhaustion at Stage 4 → MANUAL_REVIEW, no partial trailer;
* crash between stage-output persist and acknowledgement → resume, no re-render.

## Recovery Tests

* resume the pipeline from the last persisted stage boundary after a crash;
* reconstruct in-flight stage membership from the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**);
* verify no persisted stage is re-executed on resume (RPL-303);
* verify a dead-lettered shot's evidence survives recovery unchanged.

---

# Pipeline Quality Gate

```yaml
rendering_pipeline_quality_gate:
  stage_order_fixed_and_validated: true
  every_shot_traverses_all_mandatory_stages_or_cache: true
  forward_only_progression_enforced: true
  stage_boundaries_persisted_before_advance: true
  cache_lookup_precedes_submission: true
  cache_hit_bypasses_provider_stages: true
  validation_precedes_cache_and_composition: true
  only_validated_assets_cached: true
  only_validated_assets_composed: true
  every_early_exit_is_recorded_escalation: true
  no_silent_shot_drop: true
  max_retry_enforced_at_3: true
  visual_quality_gate_enforced_at_0_90: true
  budget_exhaustion_routes_to_manual_review: true
  pipeline_resumable_from_persisted_boundary: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RPL-401

No render asset reaches Composition without a persisted `READY` validation verdict.

## Invariant RPL-402

The Asset Cache stores only validated assets; a cache hit therefore reuses a previously validated result.

## Invariant RPL-403

Stage order is fixed; no stage may be reordered, removed, or duplicated by configuration.

## Invariant RPL-404

A render task advances forward only; backward movement exists solely as a new retry attempt, never as regression of the current attempt.

## Invariant RPL-405

A task advances across a stage boundary only after the previous stage's output is persisted to the Render Manifest.

## Invariant RPL-406

The cache short-circuit is the only legitimate early exit; every other early exit is an explicit escalation to Retry, Manual Review, or Dead Letter.

## Invariant RPL-407

No shot is ever silently dropped; every shot ends in `COMPOSED`, `CACHED`, `MANUAL_REVIEW`, or `DEAD_LETTER`.

## Invariant RPL-408

The pipeline never modifies the approved Storyboard, Screenplay, or Story; every stage is read-only with respect to the creative plan.

## Invariant RPL-409

Each stage maps to exactly one queue class (or none) and one lifecycle state, consistent with **5.4.1** and **5.4.3**.

## Invariant RPL-410

Cache lookup precedes Queue Submission; a cache-hit task is never admitted for provider execution.

## Invariant RPL-411

The pipeline is resumable from its last persisted stage boundary; no persisted stage is re-executed on recovery.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                            |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-420 | The Rendering Platform executes a fixed nine-stage pipeline (Storyboard → Render Planner → Task Generation → Queue Submission → Provider Execution → Asset Download → Validation → Asset Cache → Composition); the stage order is immutable and not configuration-overridable. |
| ADR-421 | Each pipeline stage maps to exactly one render queue class (or none) and one render-task lifecycle state, binding this overview to the queue system (5.4.1) and state machine (5.4.3) without re-specifying them. |
| ADR-422 | The validated cache hit, evaluated at Task Generation, is the only legitimate early exit from the pipeline; it precedes all provider cost and bypasses Submission, Execution, Download, and Validation. |
| ADR-423 | No asset reaches the Asset Cache or Composition without a persisted `READY` validation verdict; validation always precedes reuse and composition (Fail Closed). |
| ADR-424 | Every non-forward, non-cache stage exit is an explicit recorded escalation to Retry, Manual Review, or Dead Letter; the pipeline never silently drops or downgrades a shot, and is resumable from its last persisted stage boundary. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.1.5** is complete when:

* The nine-stage rendering pipeline is defined in fixed order, with each stage's purpose, inputs, and outputs specified.
* Each stage is mapped to its render queue class (forward-referencing **5.4.1**) and its render-task lifecycle state (forward-referencing **5.4.3**) in a normative stage-contract table.
* The overview correctly forward-references the owning deep-specification sections (**5.2** strategy, **5.3** providers, **5.4** queue/state/manifest/retry/timeout, **5.5** prompt, **5.6** validation/cache) and never contradicts them.
* The stage flow diagram shows the single forward path, the cache short-circuit, and the three escalation edges (Retry, Manual Review, Dead Letter).
* The cache short-circuit is defined as the only legitimate early exit, evaluated before any provider cost is incurred.
* Validation is shown to precede both caching and composition, enforcing the Fail-Closed guarantee and the visual quality minimum score of 0.90.
* Stage-boundary handoff rules (persist-before-advance, one active class, at-least-once safe) are specified consistently with the acknowledgement protocol in **5.4.1**.
* The failure and escalation flow preserves the canonical retry policy (max 3, no retry for quality/invalid-prompt/unsupported-input) owned by **5.4.5**.
* Pipeline-level events, metrics, configuration, testing requirements, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs are provided.
* The section establishes that rendering is an execution layer that never modifies the approved story, screenplay, or storyboard.
* Together these provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the rendering pipeline as an ordered, deterministic, fail-closed orchestration of the stages whose internals are specified in the later sections of Part 5.


---

# Part 5.1.6 — Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.2

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section closes **Group 5.1 — Rendering Architecture & Foundations** and defines the formal handoff into **Group 5.2 — Render Strategy & Decisioning**.

Group 5.1 established *what the Rendering Platform is, where it sits, what it consumes and produces, and the immovable principles under which it operates.* It deliberately specified **no** decision logic — no strategy selection, no decision matrix, no cost arbitration. Those belong to 5.2.

This section performs four jobs:

* it **summarises** what sections 5.1.1 through 5.1.5 collectively established;
* it **consolidates** the five foundational invariants of the Rendering Platform into a single normative reference — the execution-only boundary, provider independence, asynchronous execution, incremental rendering, and fail-closed behaviour;
* it **defines the interface** — the exact artifacts, guarantees, and obligations that Group 5.1 hands to Group 5.2;
* it **states the acceptance criteria** under which Group 5.1 is considered complete and rendering foundations are frozen for downstream authoring.

This section is a **synthesis and interface contract**. It introduces no new mechanism. Where it appears to state a rule, that rule is a consolidation of a rule already established in 5.1.1–5.1.5, restated here so that 5.2 and every later group can reference one authoritative boundary.

Group 5.1 is the **foundation layer**. Everything downstream — strategy, providers, execution, prompt assembly, validation, observability — builds on the guarantees frozen here. Nothing downstream may weaken them.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* a consolidated summary of the artifacts and rules established by 5.1.1–5.1.5;
* the five foundational invariants of the Rendering Platform, restated as one normative set;
* the **5.1 → 5.2 interface contract**: the handoff artifacts and their guarantees;
* the obligations 5.2 inherits and must never violate;
* the pre-conditions 5.2 may safely assume as already satisfied;
* the compliance checklist for Group 5.1;
* the Group 5.1 quality gate;
* the invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria that freeze the foundation layer.

This section does **not** specify:

* render strategy definitions (defined in **5.2.1**);
* the rendering decision matrix (defined in **5.2.2**);
* cost-aware strategy selection (defined in **5.2.3**);
* provider interface contracts (defined in **5.3**);
* execution, queueing, retry, or timeout mechanics (defined in **5.4**);
* prompt assembly (defined in **5.5**);
* validation logic (defined in **5.6**).

This section consumes the outputs of 5.1.1–5.1.5; it re-derives nothing and contradicts nothing. Where a fact was fixed upstream, this section cites it rather than restating a variant of it.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the foundation handoff.

> **Group 5.1 hands 5.2 a fixed frame, not a suggestion: the boundary, the principles, and the contract are settled before any decision is made.**

Strategy selection (5.2) is a decision *inside* a frame that 5.1 has already drawn. The frame is non-negotiable. 5.2 chooses **how** a shot is rendered; it may never redraw **where** rendering begins and ends, **whether** the platform may alter the creative plan, or **which** guarantees survive a failure.

A corollary follows directly:

> **A decision made in 5.2 that violates a 5.1 invariant is not a strategy — it is a defect.**

The decisioning layer operates within the foundation, never above it. If a strategy choice would require the platform to author creative content, bind to a specific provider, block the pipeline, re-render an unchanged shot, or publish an unvalidated trailer, the choice is rejected at the boundary — not accommodated.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        GROUP 5.1 — Rendering Architecture & Foundations
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.1.1  Purpose & Rendering Boundary           │
        │  5.1.2  Design Philosophy & Rendering Principles│
        │  5.1.3  Position in Architecture               │
        │  5.1.4  Inputs & Outputs Contract              │
        │  5.1.5  Rendering Pipeline Overview            │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                 ┌─────────────────────┐
                 │   5.1.6  INTERFACE   │   ◄── this section
                 │   (frozen frame)     │
                 └─────────────────────┘
                            │
             handoff artifacts + guarantees
                            │
                            ▼
        GROUP 5.2 — Render Strategy & Decisioning
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.2.1  Render Strategy Model                  │
        │  5.2.2  Rendering Decision Matrix              │
        │  5.2.3  Cost-Aware Strategy Selection          │
        │  5.2.4  Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.3  │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Group 5.1 is upstream of every rendering decision. The interface defined here is the sole channel through which foundation guarantees reach the decisioning layer. 5.2 receives a **frozen frame**; it does not participate in drawing it.

---

# Summary of Group 5.1

Group 5.1 established the following, section by section. Each item is authoritative in its origin section; this summary is a consolidated index, not a restatement.

## From 5.1.1 — Purpose & Rendering Boundary

* The Rendering Platform converts **approved Storyboard artifacts** into cinematic visual assets.
* Rendering is an **execution layer**, not a creative layer.
* The platform **faithfully executes an already approved visual plan**; it does not create stories, pacing, dialogue, or camera language.
* The boundary between *creative authoring* (upstream) and *execution* (rendering) is fixed and one-directional.

## From 5.1.2 — Design Philosophy & Rendering Principles

The five founding principles, established as normative:

* **Principle 1 — Planning Before Rendering.** Rendering begins only after all upstream Quality Gates have passed (Story → Screenplay → Storyboard ✓ Approved → Rendering).
* **Principle 2 — Provider Independence.** Rendering providers are interchangeable; upstream engines never depend on a specific provider. Runway is current; Veo, Luma, Pika, OpenAI Video, and Hunyuan are candidate futures.
* **Principle 3 — Asynchronous Rendering.** Rendering never blocks the pipeline; all providers execute asynchronously.
* **Principle 4 — Incremental Rendering.** Only modified shots are rendered; previously approved assets are reused.
* **Principle 5 — Fail Closed.** If rendering quality cannot satisfy validation, no publishable trailer is produced.

## From 5.1.3 — Position in Architecture

* The platform sits **downstream of the Storyboard Engine and Render Strategy Planner** and **upstream of the Asset Repository and FFmpeg Composer**.
* Providers (Runway, Motion Assets, and future adapters) are fan-out execution backends, not decision-makers.
* Composition consumes rendered and validated assets; it never receives unvalidated output.

## From 5.1.4 — Inputs & Outputs Contract

* Canonical **inputs**: `shot-list.json`, `storyboard.json`, `camera-plan.json`, `render-strategy.json`, `character-bible.yaml`, `location-bible.yaml`.
* Canonical **outputs**: `render_manifest.json`, `render_queue.json`, `render_results.json`, `video_assets/`, `preview_assets/`, `thumbnail_assets/`, `metadata.json`.
* Inputs are **read-only** to the platform; outputs are the platform's exclusive product.

## From 5.1.5 — Rendering Pipeline Overview

* The end-to-end pipeline: **Storyboard → Render Planner → Task Generation → Queue Submission → Provider Execution → Asset Download → Validation → Asset Cache → Composition.**
* Each stage has a single responsibility and a defined successor.
* Strategy assignment (Render Planner) precedes task generation; task generation precedes any provider contact.

Together, sections 5.1.1–5.1.5 fix the platform's **identity, principles, position, contract, and flow**. Group 5.2 begins exactly where the Render Planner makes its first decision.

---

# Consolidated Foundational Invariants

The five founding principles of 5.1.2 are restated here as a single normative set, so that every downstream section references one boundary. These are the invariants the entire Part 5 rests on.

## FOUND-1 — Execution-Only Boundary

The Rendering Platform **shall never** modify the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard. It executes an approved visual plan and produces assets; it authors no creative content.

Prohibited platform actions, at every layer:

* generating or altering story, plot, or narrative;
* determining or re-timing pacing;
* creating or editing dialogue;
* designing or overriding camera language.

Providers inherit this prohibition without exception (**5.3.1**).

## FOUND-2 — Provider Independence

No component upstream of the provider adapter boundary **shall** depend on a specific rendering provider. Providers are interchangeable behind a common contract (**5.3.2**). Adding, removing, or substituting a provider **shall not** require any change to strategy, planning, prompt assembly, or validation logic.

## FOUND-3 — Asynchronous Execution

Rendering **shall never** block the pipeline. All provider execution is asynchronous; submission returns immediately, and completion is observed through polling and manifest updates (mechanics in **5.4**).

## FOUND-4 — Incremental Rendering

Only modified shots **shall** be rendered. Any shot whose render cache key matches an existing validated asset **shall** reuse that asset and **shall not** be re-rendered (cache mechanics in **5.6.3**). Re-rendering an unchanged, already-validated shot is prohibited.

## FOUND-5 — Fail Closed

If rendering quality cannot satisfy validation, **no publishable trailer is produced**. The platform escalates to Manual Review rather than emitting a partial or substandard result. Silent degradation is prohibited.

These five invariants are **jointly binding**. No strategy, provider, optimisation, or configuration may satisfy one by breaking another. They are the acceptance floor for every subsequent section of Part 5.

---

# The 5.1 → 5.2 Interface Contract

Group 5.1 hands Group 5.2 a **frozen frame**. The interface is defined as three explicit sets: the **handoff artifacts** 5.2 receives, the **guarantees** attached to them, and the **obligations** 5.2 inherits.

## Handoff Artifacts

5.2 receives, as read-only inputs, exactly the canonical inputs fixed in 5.1.4, plus the frozen principle set from 5.1.2.

```yaml id="r1a-handoff"
handoff_to_5_2:
  approved_inputs:            # read-only, produced upstream, validated by 5.1
    - shot-list.json
    - storyboard.json
    - camera-plan.json
    - character-bible.yaml
    - location-bible.yaml
  strategy_input_slot:
    - render-strategy.json    # to be POPULATED by 5.2, not by 5.1
  foundational_invariants:    # frozen, non-negotiable
    - FOUND-1  # execution-only boundary
    - FOUND-2  # provider independence
    - FOUND-3  # asynchronous execution
    - FOUND-4  # incremental rendering
    - FOUND-5  # fail closed
  pipeline_contract:
    stages: [render_planner, task_generation, queue_submission,
             provider_execution, asset_download, validation,
             asset_cache, composition]
    strategy_stage: render_planner   # 5.2 owns this stage
```

The `render-strategy.json` slot is the **single output surface** 5.2 is responsible for populating. 5.1 defines the slot's existence and read-only-input surroundings; 5.1 does **not** define its contents. Every other input in the handoff is read-only to 5.2 exactly as it is read-only to the whole platform.

## Guarantees Provided to 5.2

When 5.2 begins, it may assume the following are already true. These are guarantees, not aspirations; they are enforced by the Group 5.1 quality gate before the frame is frozen.

```yaml id="r1a-guarantees"
guarantees_to_5_2:
  storyboard_approved: true          # Principle 1 satisfied upstream
  inputs_are_read_only: true         # 5.1.4 contract
  input_set_is_canonical: true       # exactly the 5.1.4 inputs, no more
  boundary_is_fixed: true            # FOUND-1: no creative authoring downstream
  providers_are_abstracted: true     # FOUND-2: no provider name required to decide
  execution_is_async: true           # FOUND-3: decisions need not block on render
```
