```yaml id="r1a-guarantees"
  incremental_reuse_available: true  # FOUND-4: cache reuse is a valid outcome
  fail_closed_is_terminal_policy: true # FOUND-5: no partial trailers
  pipeline_positions_fixed: true     # 5.1.5: planner precedes task generation
  one_task_per_shot_expected: true   # each shot yields exactly one render task
```

The most consequential guarantee for 5.2 is `boundary_is_fixed`: 5.2 selects a strategy for an **already-approved** shot. It never adds, removes, reorders, or re-times shots; it never edits camera, character, or location content. It reads the approved plan and assigns an execution strategy to each shot.

## Obligations 5.2 Inherits

5.2 accepts the frame by honouring the following obligations. Violating any of them is a boundary defect, not a design choice.

* **Every shot shall be assigned exactly one render strategy.** Mixed rendering strategies inside one shot are prohibited (fixed in 5.1's flow; enumerated in **5.2.1**).
* **Strategy selection shall not author creative content** (FOUND-1). A strategy chooses an execution path; it never changes what is depicted.
* **Strategy selection shall be provider-independent at the decision surface** (FOUND-2). A strategy may name a *strategy class* (e.g. `runway_video`) but the decision logic shall not embed provider-specific API behaviour; that resolves at the adapter boundary in **5.3**.
* **Strategy selection shall preserve incremental reuse** (FOUND-4). A cache-eligible shot shall be routed to reuse, not to fresh provider work (cache elimination precedes queueing, per **5.4.1** and **5.6.3**).
* **Strategy selection shall respect fail-closed** (FOUND-5). No strategy may be chosen for the purpose of forcing a publishable result at the expense of validation.
* **Strategy selection shall write only `render-strategy.json`.** It mutates no approved input and produces no asset.

The result 5.2 hands forward — a fully populated `render-strategy.json` with exactly one strategy per shot — is the pre-condition for Task Generation (the first stage after the Render Planner) and for Group 5.3.

---

# Interface Sequence

The handoff is a strict, one-directional sequence. No step reads back into a prior group.

```text
5.1 freezes frame
        │
        ▼
approved inputs (read-only) ──┐
foundational invariants ──────┤
pipeline contract ────────────┤
                              ▼
                    5.2 Render Planner
                              │
             assigns one strategy per shot
                              │
                              ▼
                  render-strategy.json (populated)
                              │
                              ▼
                    Task Generation (post-5.2)
                              │
                              ▼
                 Group 5.3 Provider execution
```

If 5.2 cannot assign a valid, invariant-respecting strategy to a shot, it does **not** relax the frame. It escalates — consistent with FOUND-5 — rather than producing a strategy that violates the boundary.

---

# What 5.2 Must Not Assume

The interface is generous with guarantees but strict about non-guarantees. 5.2 must **not** assume any of the following, because 5.1 deliberately did not fix them:

* **That a specific provider is available.** 5.1 guarantees provider *independence*, not provider *presence*. Provider availability, health, and rate limits are resolved in **5.3** and enforced at execution time (**5.4.1**); 5.2 reasons in strategy classes, not in live provider state.
* **That cost budgets are known at strategy definition time.** The cost-priority order (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway) is a foundation-level ordering fact; concrete budget reservations and thresholds are owned by cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**) and enforced at admission (**5.4.1**).
* **That a cache hit has been computed.** 5.1 guarantees that incremental reuse is a *valid outcome*, not that the cache lookup has already run. Cache elimination occurs before queueing (**5.6.3**); 5.2 must route cache-eligible shots to reuse, not presume the lookup result.
* **That inputs may be edited to make a strategy fit.** The read-only guarantee is absolute. If no strategy fits an approved shot, the shot is escalated; the shot is never edited to accommodate a strategy.

Confusing a non-guarantee for a guarantee is the most common way a decisioning layer silently breaches the frame. The interface names these explicitly so 5.2 cannot drift across the boundary by assumption.

---

# Interface Verification

The handoff is verified — not merely asserted — before the frame is frozen. The following checks constitute the interface test surface for Group 5.1.

## Boundary Tests

* an approved input file is unmodified after a full strategy pass (read-only enforcement);
* a strategy pass produces no asset, no queue entry, and no manifest mutation;
* an attempt to author or edit creative content through a strategy is rejected (FOUND-1).

## Contract Tests

* the input set presented to 5.2 is exactly the 5.1.4 canonical set — no more, no fewer;
* `render-strategy.json` is the only writable surface exposed to 5.2;
* every shot in `shot-list.json` maps to exactly one strategy after the pass (R1A-404).

## Invariant Tests

* a strategy that names a provider-specific API behaviour at the decision surface is rejected (FOUND-2);
* a cache-eligible shot is routed to reuse rather than fresh provider work (FOUND-4);
* a shot with no invariant-respecting strategy escalates rather than forcing a result (FOUND-5).

## Gate Tests

* the compliance checklist fails closed on any single unmet field (R1A-406);
* the quality gate fails when a downstream section could satisfy its goals by relaxing a foundational invariant (R1A-401, R1A-402).

These tests give Codex an executable definition of "the frame is frozen": the interface opens only when all of them pass.

---

# Compliance Checklist

Before Group 5.1 is frozen and the interface to 5.2 is declared open, every item below must hold.

```yaml
group_5_1_compliance:
  # Boundary (5.1.1 / FOUND-1)
  execution_only_boundary_stated: true
  no_creative_authoring_downstream: true
  # Principles (5.1.2 / FOUND-1..5)
  all_five_principles_normative: true
  planning_before_rendering: true
  provider_independence: true
  asynchronous_execution: true
  incremental_rendering: true
  fail_closed: true
  # Position (5.1.3)
  upstream_downstream_position_fixed: true
  providers_are_execution_backends_only: true
  # Contract (5.1.4)
  canonical_inputs_defined: true
  canonical_outputs_defined: true
  inputs_read_only: true
  # Pipeline (5.1.5)
  pipeline_stages_fixed: true
  planner_precedes_task_generation: true
  # Interface (5.1.6)
  handoff_artifacts_enumerated: true
  guarantees_to_5_2_enumerated: true
  obligations_of_5_2_enumerated: true
  render_strategy_slot_is_5_2_output: true
  one_strategy_per_shot_required: true
```

Every field must be `true`. A single `false` blocks the freeze: the interface to 5.2 does not open until the foundation is whole. This mirrors the fail-closed posture of FOUND-5 applied to the specification itself.

---

# Group 5.1 Quality Gate

```yaml
group_5_1_quality_gate:
  boundary_is_execution_only: true
  five_foundational_invariants_consolidated: true
  foundational_invariants_jointly_binding: true
  handoff_artifacts_are_read_only_except_strategy_slot: true
  guarantees_to_5_2_enforced_before_freeze: true
  obligations_of_5_2_cannot_weaken_invariants: true
  strategy_stage_owned_by_5_2: true
  one_render_task_per_shot_preserved: true
  incremental_reuse_path_preserved: true
  fail_closed_escalation_preserved: true
  no_downstream_group_may_relax_foundation: true
```

The gate passes only when the frame is complete **and** demonstrably non-relaxable by any downstream group. If any downstream section could satisfy its own goals by weakening a foundational invariant, the gate fails and the invariant is re-hardened here before proceeding.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant R1A-401

Group 5.1 is a **frozen frame**: no downstream group may modify the execution-only boundary, the provider-independence guarantee, the asynchronous-execution guarantee, the incremental-rendering guarantee, or the fail-closed guarantee.

## Invariant R1A-402

The five foundational invariants (FOUND-1..5) are **jointly binding**; no strategy, provider, optimisation, or configuration may satisfy one by breaking another.

## Invariant R1A-403

The 5.1 → 5.2 interface is **one-directional**: 5.2 consumes the frozen frame and populates only `render-strategy.json`; it mutates no approved input and produces no asset.

## Invariant R1A-404

Every shot handed to 5.2 must receive **exactly one** render strategy; mixed strategies within a shot are prohibited.

## Invariant R1A-405

Any decision in 5.2 (or any later group) that would require creative authoring, provider binding at the decision surface, pipeline blocking, re-rendering of an unchanged validated shot, or a partial published trailer is a **boundary defect** and must be rejected, not accommodated.

## Invariant R1A-406

The Group 5.1 compliance checklist and quality gate must both fully pass before the interface to 5.2 is declared open; a single failing field **fails closed** and blocks the freeze.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                                                 |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-425 | Group 5.1 is frozen as a non-negotiable foundation frame; the five founding principles are consolidated into a single jointly-binding invariant set (FOUND-1..5) that no downstream group may relax. |
| ADR-426 | The 5.1 → 5.2 handoff is a one-directional interface contract in which 5.2 receives read-only approved inputs plus the frozen invariants and is responsible for populating exactly one artifact, `render-strategy.json`, with exactly one strategy per shot. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.1.6** is complete when:

* Group 5.1 (sections 5.1.1 through 5.1.5) is summarised into a single authoritative index of established facts, contradicting none of its origin sections.
* The five founding principles are consolidated into one normative, jointly-binding invariant set — execution-only boundary, provider independence, asynchronous execution, incremental rendering, and fail-closed — restated as FOUND-1 through FOUND-5.
* The 5.1 → 5.2 interface is defined as three explicit sets: handoff artifacts, guarantees provided, and obligations inherited.
* The handoff enumerates the canonical read-only inputs and identifies `render-strategy.json` as the single output surface that Group 5.2 is responsible for populating.
* The guarantees 5.2 may assume — approved storyboard, read-only canonical inputs, fixed boundary, abstracted providers, async execution, available incremental reuse, and terminal fail-closed policy — are enumerated and enforced by the Group 5.1 quality gate before the frame is frozen.
* The obligations 5.2 inherits — one strategy per shot, no creative authoring, provider-independent decisions, preserved incremental reuse, preserved fail-closed, and mutation of only `render-strategy.json` — are stated as boundary conditions rather than suggestions.
* The compliance checklist and quality gate fully pass, and both fail closed on any single unmet field so the interface to 5.2 cannot open over an incomplete foundation.
* Invariants R1A-401 through R1A-406 and decision records ADR-425 and ADR-426 fix the foundation as non-relaxable by any downstream group.
* Together these provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the foundation-to-decisioning handoff — enforcing the frozen frame, validating the read-only input surface, and gating strategy assignment against the foundational invariants — without permitting any downstream layer to weaken the rendering boundary.


---

# Part 5.2.1 — Render Strategy Model

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Render Strategy Model** of the Rendering Platform.

A **render strategy** is the single, declared method by which one shot is turned into a visual asset. It is the answer to exactly one question:

> *By what mechanism is this shot rendered?*

The Render Strategy Model specifies:

* the closed set of render strategies the platform recognises;
* the canonical schema of each strategy;
* how a strategy is assigned to a shot and where that assignment comes from;
* the **single-strategy invariant** — every shot carries exactly one strategy, never zero and never more than one;
* the metadata and parameters each strategy binds to the shot;
* how a strategy binds durably to a shot inside the Render Strategy artifact;
* how strategy assignment is validated before rendering may begin;
* how the assigned strategy feeds render task generation, the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**), and deterministic prompt assembly (**5.5**).

The render strategy is the **root routing decision** of the entire Rendering Platform. Every downstream component — task generation, provider selection, queue class, prompt template, validation profile, and cache key composition — is a function of the strategy bound to the shot.

Strategy assignment is a **planning** act. Strategy execution is a **rendering** act. This section owns the *model* that connects the two: it defines what a strategy *is*, how it is *recorded*, and how it is *consumed*. It does not decide *which* strategy a shot should receive (that is the decision matrix, **5.2.2**) nor *when* a cheaper substitute is chosen under budget pressure (that is cost-aware selection, **5.2.3**).

Rendering is an execution layer. The Render Strategy Model is the contract that tells that execution layer, per shot, exactly how to execute — and forbids it from ever executing in more than one way for a single shot.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the render strategy enumeration and its closed-set property;
* a canonical, per-strategy schema for all six strategies;
* the provenance of a strategy assignment (the Render Strategy Planner);
* the single-strategy invariant and its enforcement;
* strategy metadata, parameters, and their typing;
* the binding of a strategy to a shot within the `render-strategy.json` artifact;
* the strategy assignment record and its identity;
* validation of strategy assignment (completeness, closedness, uniqueness, referential integrity);
* the forward contract to render task generation and the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**);
* the forward contract to deterministic prompt assembly (**5.5**);
* strategy events, metrics, configuration, testing, invariants, and decision records.

This section does **not** specify:

* the **decision matrix** mapping shot semantics to strategy (defined in **5.2.2**);
* **cost-aware strategy selection** and budget-driven downgrade (defined in **5.2.3**);
* the provider interface contract each strategy's provider must implement (defined in **5.3.2**);
* the Runway provider adapter (defined in **5.3.4**);
* render task queueing, leasing, and dispatch (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the render task state machine (defined in **5.4.3**);
* prompt template internals for character, location, camera, and style (defined in **5.5.2**–**5.5.5**);
* asset and visual quality validation of the produced output (defined in **5.6.1**–**5.6.2**);
* the render cache key and reuse logic (defined in **5.6.3**).

The Render Strategy Model produces the strategy assignment that every one of those sections consumes; it does not own their internal behaviour.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **Every shot is rendered exactly one way. One shot, one strategy — declared before rendering, never mixed, never inferred at render time.**

The render strategy is a **declared property of the shot**, fixed by planning and recorded in the Render Strategy artifact. It is never guessed by a provider, never chosen by a worker, and never blended within a single shot.

A corollary follows directly:

> **A shot with no strategy, an unknown strategy, or two strategies is not renderable. It is a planning defect, and it fails closed.**

Rendering may begin for a shot only once a valid, single, closed-set strategy is bound to it. The absence of a strategy, or the presence of an out-of-enum or duplicated strategy, is not a runtime fallback condition — it is a hard validation failure that blocks the build (Fail Closed, Principle 5). The Rendering Platform never invents a strategy to make an unassigned shot renderable, and never silently renders one shot two ways and composites the results.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Storyboard (Approved)
        │
        ▼
Shot Semantics  (shot type, motion class, content class)
        │
        ▼
Render Strategy Planner   ── consults ──►  Decision Matrix (5.2.2)
        │                                  Cost Selection (5.2.3)
        ▼
render-strategy.json      ◄── THIS SECTION defines the model of ──┐
        │                                                          │
        │  one StrategyAssignment per shot                         │
        │  (exactly one strategy each)                             │
        ▼                                                          │
Strategy Assignment Validation  ──────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ├── valid ──► Render Task Generation
        │                    │
        │                    ├──► Render Queue Manager (5.4.1)
        │                    └──► Deterministic Prompt Assembly (5.5)
        │
        └── invalid ─► Fail Closed → build blocked (no rendering)
```

The Render Strategy Model sits **downstream** of the approved Storyboard and the Render Strategy Planner, and **upstream** of render task generation. It is the point at which the abstract shot becomes a concretely routable render task. No render task may be generated for a shot whose strategy assignment has not been validated by this model.

---

# Responsibilities

The Render Strategy Model is responsible for:

* defining the closed enumeration of render strategies;
* defining the canonical schema of each strategy and its parameters;
* defining the StrategyAssignment record that binds one strategy to one shot;
* defining the provenance contract with the Render Strategy Planner;
* enforcing the single-strategy invariant per shot;
* enforcing closed-set membership (no unknown strategies);
* enforcing referential integrity (assignment references a real shot; parameters reference real character/location/camera IDs);
* defining the validation gate that strategy assignment must pass before rendering;
* defining the forward contract to render task generation, queueing (**5.4.1**), and prompt assembly (**5.5**);
* emitting strategy assignment events and metrics.

It is **not** responsible for:

* choosing which strategy a shot receives (see **5.2.2**);
* substituting a cheaper strategy under budget pressure (see **5.2.3**);
* implementing any provider (see **5.3**);
* generating or ordering render tasks (see **5.4.1**);
* assembling prompt text (see **5.5**);
* validating the produced asset or its visual quality (see **5.6.1**, **5.6.2**);
* computing the render cache key (see **5.6.3**).

The model provides a validated, single-strategy assignment per shot. What each consumer does with it is owned by that consumer.

---

# Design Objectives

The Render Strategy Model shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RSM-301 — Closed Strategy Set

The set of render strategies is **closed and finite**. Exactly six strategies exist:

`runway_video`, `animated_still`, `motion_graphics`, `typography`, `svg`, `cached_asset`.

Any strategy value outside this set is invalid. New strategies may be added only by amending this section and the strategy enum; they may never be introduced ad hoc by a planner, worker, or provider.

---

## RSM-302 — Exactly One Strategy Per Shot

Every renderable shot carries **exactly one** render strategy. Zero strategies is a defect (unassigned). Two or more strategies is a defect (mixed). This is the single-strategy invariant and it is non-negotiable.

---

## RSM-303 — No Mixing Within a Shot

Mixed rendering strategies **inside one shot** are prohibited. A shot is never rendered partly by Runway and partly by motion graphics, or composited from two strategies. Composition of multiple *shots* is a downstream concern (FFmpeg Composer); composition of multiple *strategies within one shot* is forbidden.

---

## RSM-304 — Declared, Not Inferred

The strategy is **declared** by planning and **recorded** in the Render Strategy artifact before rendering. It is never inferred at render time by a worker or provider from prompt content, shot metadata, or heuristics.

---

## RSM-305 — Deterministic Assignment Identity

Given the same shot and the same assignment inputs, the StrategyAssignment record — including its identity hash — is byte-for-byte reproducible. Strategy assignment is deterministic (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism).

---

## RSM-306 — Provenance Preserved

Every strategy assignment records **where it came from**: the planner version, the matrix rule that fired (**5.2.2**), and whether cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**) downgraded it from a preferred strategy. Assignment provenance is auditable and never discarded.

---

## RSM-307 — Self-Describing Routing

The assigned strategy fully determines the shot's downstream routing: its provider (or lack thereof), its queue class, its prompt template family, its validation profile, and its cache-key participation. A consumer never needs external context beyond the StrategyAssignment to route the shot.

---

## RSM-308 — Fail Closed on Invalid Assignment

An unassigned, unknown, mixed, or referentially broken strategy assignment blocks rendering for the affected build. The platform never renders around a defective assignment and never produces a partial trailer to work past it (Principle 5).

---

# The Render Strategy Enumeration

The Rendering Platform recognises exactly six render strategies. This enumeration is the closed set required by **RSM-301**.

```yaml id="rsm-enum"
render_strategy_enum:
  - runway_video      # generative motion video via an external provider (Runway et al.)
  - animated_still    # a still image given subtle programmatic motion (Ken Burns, parallax)
  - motion_graphics   # designed, animated graphical elements (counters, UI, lower-thirds)
  - typography        # animated text / title cards
  - svg               # vector-rendered diagrams and schematics, optionally animated
  - cached_asset      # a previously rendered, validated asset reused verbatim
```

Each strategy has a distinct **execution mechanism**, a distinct **provider class**, and a distinct **parameter shape**. The strategy value is the primary routing key of the Rendering Platform.

| Strategy          | Execution Mechanism                     | Provider Class | Deterministic Output | External Cost |
| ----------------- | --------------------------------------- | -------------- | -------------------- | ------------- |
| `runway_video`    | Generative video model (async provider) | `runway`       | No (model-sampled)   | High          |
| `animated_still`  | Programmatic motion over a still image  | `motion`       | Yes                  | Low           |
| `motion_graphics` | Rendered animated graphics              | `motion`       | Yes                  | Low           |
| `typography`      | Animated text composition               | `typography`   | Yes                  | Very low      |
| `svg`             | Vector render / rasterise               | `svg`          | Yes                  | Very low      |
| `cached_asset`    | Verbatim reuse of a prior asset         | `cache`        | Yes (byte-identical) | None          |

The rationale for *which* shot receives *which* strategy is the **decision matrix** in **5.2.2**; the cost ordering `Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway` that governs downgrade is owned by **5.2.3**. This section fixes only the enumeration and its schemas.

---

# Canonical Per-Strategy Schema

Every strategy declares a canonical parameter schema. The `parameters` object of a StrategyAssignment (defined below) must conform to exactly one of these schemas — the one matching its `strategy` value.

## Common Strategy Envelope

All strategy parameter blocks share a common envelope. Strategy-specific fields extend it.

```yaml id="rsm-common"
strategy_parameters_common:
  render_profile:        # profile selector, e.g. runway_preview | runway_final | still_v1
  aspect_ratio:          # 16:9 | 9:16 | 1:1 — from storyboard, never provider-chosen
  duration_ms:           # target shot duration in milliseconds (from storyboard pacing)
  style_ref:             # reference to the enforced visual style bundle (see 5.5.5)
  deterministic_seed:    # required for non-cache strategies to stabilise output where the
                         # provider supports seeding; null where not applicable
```

## `runway_video`

```yaml id="rsm-runway"
runway_video_parameters:
  <<: strategy_parameters_common
  provider: runway
  render_profile: runway_preview | runway_final
  motion_class:          # push | walk | facial_emotion | ambient — semantic motion label
  character_refs: []     # list of character IDs (resolved by prompt assembly, 5.5.2)
  location_ref:          # single location ID (resolved by 5.5.3)
  camera_ref:            # single camera plan ID (resolved by 5.5.4)
  motion_intensity:      # low | medium | high
  frames:                # target frame count (derived from duration_ms and fps)
  fps:                   # target frame rate
```

`runway_video` is the only strategy whose output is **not deterministic** at the pixel level; it is model-sampled. It is also the only strategy that submits to an external, rate-limited, cost-bearing provider. For these reasons it is the most expensive strategy and, per **5.2.3**, the last resort in the cost order. It is reserved for shots where genuine motion contributes to storytelling (human facial emotion, walking, camera push — see the matrix in **5.2.2**).

## `animated_still`

```yaml id="rsm-animated-still"
animated_still_parameters:
  <<: strategy_parameters_common
  provider: motion
  render_profile: still_v1
  source_still_ref:      # reference to the still image asset or its generation spec
  motion_type:           # ken_burns | parallax | slow_zoom | slow_pan
  motion_path:           # start/end framing rectangle (deterministic)
  location_ref:          # single location ID (optional)
```

Produces subtle, deterministic motion over a still image. Cheaper than `runway_video` and preferred for shots that read as motion but do not require generative video (e.g. an enterprise dashboard establishing shot).

## `motion_graphics`

```yaml id="rsm-motion-graphics"
motion_graphics_parameters:
  <<: strategy_parameters_common
  provider: motion
  render_profile: mgfx_v1
  template_id:           # designed motion-graphics template (counter, UI panel, lower-third)
  data_bindings: {}      # key→value data bound into the template (e.g. countdown target)
  animation_curve:       # ease | linear | spring — deterministic timing
```

Produces designed, animated graphical elements such as countdown UI, animated statistics, and interface panels. Fully deterministic. Used for shots that are informational or interface-like (see **5.2.2**).

## `typography`

```yaml id="rsm-typography"
typography_parameters:
  <<: strategy_parameters_common
  provider: typography
  render_profile: type_v1
  text:                  # the literal title/label text (NOT article body text)
  typeface_ref:          # reference to an approved typeface in the style bundle
  layout:                # centered | lower_third | full_card
  animation:             # fade | rise | typewriter — deterministic
```

Produces animated text and title cards. Fully deterministic. The `text` field carries only titles and labels; article body text is never rendered (consistent with the prompt-assembly boundary in **5.5.1**).

## `svg`

```yaml id="rsm-svg"
svg_parameters:
  <<: strategy_parameters_common
  provider: svg
  render_profile: svg_v1
  diagram_ref:           # reference to the vector diagram source (architecture, schematic)
  animate:               # true | false — optional deterministic reveal animation
  rasterise:             # true | false — rasterise to video frames vs vector overlay
```

Produces vector-rendered diagrams and schematics (architecture diagrams, flow charts). Fully deterministic. Used for structural, illustrative shots (see **5.2.2**).

## `cached_asset`

```yaml id="rsm-cached"
cached_asset_parameters:
  <<: strategy_parameters_common
  provider: cache
  render_profile:        # the profile of the ORIGINAL render that produced the cached asset
  cache_key:             # the render cache key of the reused asset (see 5.6.3)
  source_render_task_id: # the render task that originally produced the asset
  origin_strategy:       # the strategy that ORIGINALLY produced the cached asset
```

`cached_asset` is not a generative mechanism; it is a **reuse declaration**. It states that a previously rendered, validated asset — identified by its render cache key (**5.6.3**) — is to be reused verbatim for this shot. Its `origin_strategy` records which of the other five strategies originally produced the asset, preserving provenance (**RSM-306**). A `cached_asset` shot never submits to any generative provider; it bypasses render task submission entirely and is routed as a cache hit (see the admission contract in **5.4.1**).

Note that `cached_asset` is the *strategy declaration* of intent-to-reuse recorded at planning time. The *runtime* cache lookup and reuse mechanics — key composition, hit/miss detection, and invalidation — are owned by **5.6.3**. This section fixes only how the strategy is declared and bound.

---

# Strategy Assignment Provenance

Strategy assignments originate from exactly one authority: the **Render Strategy Planner**. No other component may create, alter, or override a strategy assignment.

```text
Approved Storyboard  ──►  Render Strategy Planner
                              │
                              ├── reads shot semantics (type, motion, content class)
                              ├── applies the Decision Matrix (5.2.2)
                              ├── applies Cost-Aware Selection (5.2.3)
                              │
                              ▼
                    StrategyAssignment  (one per shot)
                              │
                              ▼
                    render-strategy.json   (the durable artifact)
```

The planner is the **sole author** of `render-strategy.json`. The Rendering Platform is a strict **reader** of that artifact. Workers, providers, and the queue never write strategy assignments; they only consume them.

Each StrategyAssignment records its provenance so that any assignment can be explained and audited (**RSM-306**):

```yaml id="rsm-provenance"
assignment_provenance:
  planner_version:       # semantic version of the Render Strategy Planner that assigned
  matrix_rule_id:        # the decision-matrix rule that fired (see 5.2.2)
  preferred_strategy:    # the strategy the matrix preferred BEFORE cost selection
  selected_strategy:     # the strategy actually assigned AFTER cost selection (5.2.3)
  downgraded:            # true if selected_strategy is cheaper than preferred_strategy
  downgrade_reason:      # budget_pressure | cache_available | null
  assigned_at:           # timestamp of assignment
```

When `downgraded` is true, `preferred_strategy` differs from `selected_strategy`; the reason (owned by **5.2.3**) is recorded but not decided here. The Render Strategy Model records the outcome of that decision faithfully and never re-derives it.

Provenance is **immutable** once recorded. A re-plan produces a new StrategyAssignment with a new identity; it does not mutate the prior record in place.

---

# The Single-Strategy Invariant

The single-strategy invariant is the defining constraint of this model.

> **For every shot `s` in the approved storyboard, `render-strategy.json` contains exactly one StrategyAssignment whose `strategy ∈ render_strategy_enum`.**

Three distinct defects violate this invariant, and each is a hard failure:

| Defect             | Condition                                                        | Outcome         |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------- |
| **Unassigned**     | A storyboard shot has zero StrategyAssignment entries.          | Fail Closed     |
| **Mixed**          | A shot has two or more StrategyAssignment entries.              | Fail Closed     |
| **Unknown**        | A shot's `strategy` value is not in `render_strategy_enum`.     | Fail Closed     |

The invariant is enforced at two points:

1. **Planning-time** — the Render Strategy Planner must emit exactly one assignment per shot. This is the planner's contract.
2. **Rendering-time** — the strategy assignment validation gate (below) re-checks the invariant before any render task is generated, refusing to trust the artifact blindly.

Enforcement at rendering-time is mandatory even though the planner is the sole author: the Rendering Platform never assumes the artifact is well-formed. Defence in depth against a malformed `render-strategy.json` is required by **RSM-308**.

The prohibition on **mixing** deserves emphasis. It is not merely "at most one entry per shot"; it is that a single shot is never internally composited from multiple render mechanisms. A shot that appears to need both generative video *and* an overlaid animated counter is a **storyboard modelling error** to be resolved upstream (by splitting into two shots, each with one strategy) — never by assigning two strategies to one shot. The Rendering Platform is an execution layer and must never restructure the approved storyboard to accommodate a mixed shot (Principle: rendering never modifies the approved plan).

---

# StrategyAssignment Record

The StrategyAssignment is the record that binds exactly one strategy to exactly one shot. It is the unit stored in `render-strategy.json`.

```yaml id="rsm-assignment"
strategy_assignment:
  assignment_id:         # deterministic: sha256(shot_id + strategy + parameters_hash)
  shot_id:               # references exactly one shot in the approved storyboard
  scene_index:
  shot_index:
  build_id:
  strategy:              # one value from render_strategy_enum — the routing key
  parameters:            # conforms to the canonical schema for `strategy`
  parameters_hash:       # sha256 of the canonical-serialised parameters
  provenance:            # assignment_provenance block (see above)
  created_at:
```

Constraints:

* `strategy` **must** be a member of `render_strategy_enum` (**RSM-301**).
* `parameters` **must** validate against the canonical schema selected by `strategy`, and against **no other** schema.
* `shot_id` **must** reference exactly one shot in the approved storyboard.
* There **must** be exactly one StrategyAssignment per `shot_id` in a given `build_id` (**RSM-302**).
* All referenced IDs (`character_refs`, `location_ref`, `camera_ref`, `cache_key`, `source_still_ref`, `diagram_ref`) **must** resolve to real entities in their authoritative stores.

The record carries **references and parameters only**. It never carries prompt text, character descriptions, provider secrets, binary assets, or article content — those are resolved downstream by prompt assembly (**5.5**) and workers, exactly as queue items are (**5.4.1**).

---

# Strategy Assignment Identity

Each StrategyAssignment has a deterministic identity.

```text id="rsm-identity"
assignment_id = sha256(
  shot_id +
  strategy +
  parameters_hash
)

parameters_hash = sha256( canonical_serialize(parameters) )
```

Deterministic identity (**RSM-305**) guarantees that:

* re-planning an unchanged shot yields the **same** `assignment_id`, enabling stable diffs and incremental rendering (only changed shots re-render, Principle 4);
* the identity changes if and only if the shot, the strategy, or any parameter changes;
* the identity participates in change detection without a central lock or version counter.

The `parameters_hash` is computed over a **canonical serialisation** (stable key order, normalised numeric and string forms) so that semantically identical parameter blocks always hash identically, regardless of incidental formatting.

---

# The render-strategy.json Artifact

Strategy assignments are persisted as the `render-strategy.json` artifact, one of the declared inputs to the Rendering Platform (per the original inputs contract). It is authored solely by the Render Strategy Planner.

```yaml id="rsm-artifact"
render-strategy.json:
  build_id:
  storyboard_ref:        # the approved storyboard this plan renders
  planner_version:
  assignments: []        # list of strategy_assignment records, one per shot
  coverage:
    storyboard_shot_count:   # total shots in the approved storyboard
    assigned_shot_count:     # total shots with a StrategyAssignment
    # invariant: assigned_shot_count == storyboard_shot_count (RSM-302)
  strategy_histogram:        # count of assignments per strategy value (for metrics)
```

The artifact is **complete** only when `assigned_shot_count == storyboard_shot_count` and every storyboard shot is covered by exactly one assignment. A coverage shortfall (some shot unassigned) or overage (some shot assigned twice) is a hard validation failure.

The artifact binds a strategy to a shot **durably**: once written and validated, the binding is the authoritative statement of how each shot renders for that build. Render task generation reads this binding and never re-decides it.

---

# Binding: How a Strategy Attaches to a Shot

A strategy binds to a shot through the StrategyAssignment record, and that binding flows deterministically into the render task.

```text
Storyboard Shot (shot_id)
        │
        │  Render Strategy Planner assigns
        ▼
StrategyAssignment  { shot_id, strategy, parameters, provenance }
        │
        │  validated (single-strategy gate)
        ▼
Render Task  { render_task_id, shot_id, strategy, provider, render_profile, cache_key ... }
        │
        ├──►  Render Queue Manager (5.4.1)   — routes by provider/profile derived from strategy
        └──►  Prompt Assembly (5.5)          — selects template family from strategy
```

The binding is **one-directional and immutable within a build**:

* a shot has exactly one bound strategy for the life of a build;
* the bound strategy determines the `provider` and `render_profile` of the generated render task;
* the binding is never re-selected by a worker, the queue, or a provider;
* changing a shot's strategy requires a **re-plan** (a new StrategyAssignment with a new identity), performed by the planner, not by the Rendering Platform.

The render task carries the strategy forward as a first-class field. Downstream, the Render Queue Manager derives the queue class, provider concurrency bucket, and rate-limit bucket from the strategy's provider (**5.4.1**), and prompt assembly derives the template family from the strategy (**5.5.1**). The strategy is thus the single value from which all shot-level routing is a pure function (**RSM-307**).

---

# Validation of Strategy Assignment

Before any render task is generated, every StrategyAssignment passes a **validation gate**. The gate is fail-closed: a single defect blocks rendering for the build (**RSM-308**).

```yaml id="rsm-validation"
strategy_assignment_validation:
  coverage_complete:        true   # every storyboard shot has an assignment
  single_per_shot:          true   # no shot has two or more assignments (RSM-302)
  strategy_in_enum:         true   # every strategy ∈ render_strategy_enum (RSM-301)
  parameters_conform:       true   # parameters validate against the strategy's schema
  no_cross_schema_leak:     true   # parameters match exactly one schema, not several
  refs_resolve:             true   # all referenced IDs resolve in authoritative stores
  aspect_ratio_from_board:  true   # aspect_ratio/duration originate from storyboard
  provenance_present:       true   # assignment_provenance is recorded (RSM-306)
  identity_deterministic:   true   # assignment_id reproduces (RSM-305)
  cached_origin_valid:      true   # cached_asset.origin_strategy ∈ enum \ {cached_asset}
```

The validation checks, in order:

1. **Coverage** — every shot in the approved storyboard has exactly one assignment. A missing shot is *unassigned*; a duplicated shot is *mixed*. Both fail.
2. **Closed set** — every `strategy` value is a member of the enumeration. An unknown value fails (**RSM-301**).
3. **Schema conformance** — each `parameters` block validates against the canonical schema selected by its `strategy`, and against no other. A `runway_video` assignment carrying `svg` parameters fails.
4. **Referential integrity** — all referenced IDs resolve to real entities: characters in the character bible, locations in the location bible, cameras in the camera plan, cache keys in the cache index (**5.6.3**), still/diagram sources in their stores.
5. **Provenance** — the assignment records its planner version, matrix rule, and any downgrade (**RSM-306**).
6. **Cached-asset origin** — a `cached_asset` assignment's `origin_strategy` must be one of the five generative strategies, never `cached_asset` itself (a cache of a cache is meaningless).
7. **Determinism** — recomputing `assignment_id` from `shot_id`, `strategy`, and `parameters_hash` reproduces the stored identity.

A build whose `render-strategy.json` fails any check does **not** proceed to render task generation. The failure is surfaced as a planning defect, not a rendering error, and the build fails closed (no partial trailer, Principle 5). This is defence in depth: even though the planner is the sole trusted author, the Rendering Platform independently verifies the artifact before acting on it.

---

# Feeding the Queue (Forward Contract to 5.4.1)

The validated strategy assignment is the primary input to render task generation, which produces the render tasks consumed by the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**).

The strategy determines the queue-relevant fields of each render task:

```yaml id="rsm-to-queue"
strategy_to_render_task:
  strategy:              # carried forward verbatim as the render task's strategy
  provider:              # derived from strategy → provider class (runway|motion|svg|typography|cache)
  render_profile:        # from parameters.render_profile
  cost_estimate:         # strategy cost class (input to cost-aware admission, 5.2.3 / 5.4.1)
  cache_key:             # present iff strategy == cached_asset (drives cache-hit bypass)
```

The Render Queue Manager consumes these fields to:

* select the **queue class** and **provider concurrency bucket** from the derived `provider` (**5.4.1** concurrency configuration);
* apply the correct **provider rate-limit bucket** (only `runway_video` submits to a rate-limited external provider);
* order admission by **cost rank**, mirroring `Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway` (the ordering is owned by **5.2.3**; the queue applies it);
* **bypass the queue entirely** for `cached_asset` assignments, routing them as cache hits that are never submitted to any provider (the admission contract in **5.4.1** requires `not_cache_hit: true` before queueing).

The mapping from strategy to provider is fixed:

| Strategy          | Derived `provider` | Rate-limited | Enqueued for submission |
| ----------------- | ------------------ | ------------ | ----------------------- |
| `runway_video`    | `runway`           | Yes          | Yes                     |
| `animated_still`  | `motion`           | No           | Yes                     |
| `motion_graphics` | `motion`           | No           | Yes                     |
| `typography`      | `typography`       | No           | Yes                     |
| `svg`             | `svg`              | No           | Yes                     |
| `cached_asset`    | `cache`            | No           | **No — cache bypass**   |

This section fixes the *derivation*; the queue mechanics (leasing, admission, ordering, backpressure) are owned by **5.4.1**.

---

# Feeding Prompt Assembly (Forward Contract to 5.5)

The assigned strategy selects the **prompt template family** used by deterministic prompt assembly (**5.5.1**). Not every strategy assembles a generative prompt, and the strategy determines which injections apply.

```yaml id="rsm-to-prompt"
strategy_to_prompt_family:
  runway_video:      full_cinematic     # character + location + camera + style + emotion
  animated_still:    still_composition  # location + style + motion path (no camera language)
  motion_graphics:   graphics_template  # template + data bindings + style (no character/camera)
  typography:        text_composition   # text + typeface + layout + style
  svg:               vector_render      # diagram source + style (no prompt to a video model)
  cached_asset:      none               # no prompt assembled; asset reused verbatim
```

Consequences for prompt assembly (**5.5**):

* Only `runway_video` receives the **full cinematic prompt** with character injection (**5.5.2**), location injection (**5.5.3**), camera injection (**5.5.4**), and visual style enforcement (**5.5.5**). It is the only strategy that submits natural-language prompts to a generative video model.
* `animated_still` and `motion_graphics` receive **compositional** parameters, not natural-language camera language; camera injection (**5.5.4**) does not apply to them.
* `typography` and `svg` assemble **deterministic composition specs**, not model prompts.
* `cached_asset` assembles **no prompt at all** — its output already exists and is reused verbatim.

In all cases, the provider (where one exists) receives only the assembled prompt and resolved references — never article text (consistent with **5.5.1**). Visual style enforcement (**5.5.5**) applies to every strategy that emits pixels, forbidding anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms, humanoid robots, and exaggerated sci-fi interfaces regardless of strategy.

The strategy is thus the branch point of prompt assembly: **5.5** selects its template family purely from the strategy value, and never needs to inspect the shot to decide how to build the prompt (**RSM-307**).

---

# Events

The Render Strategy Model emits events for every material assignment transition.

```yaml id="rsm-events"
render_strategy_events:
  - strategy_assignment_created        # planner recorded a StrategyAssignment
  - strategy_assignment_validated      # assignment passed the validation gate
  - strategy_assignment_rejected       # assignment failed (unassigned|mixed|unknown|ref)
  - strategy_downgraded                # cost selection changed preferred→selected (5.2.3)
  - strategy_bound_to_task             # render task generated from the assignment
  - strategy_cache_declared            # cached_asset assignment recorded (bypass path)
  - strategy_coverage_incomplete       # storyboard shot without an assignment detected
```

Every event references `build_id`, `shot_id`, `assignment_id`, and `strategy`, and is durably recorded for audit and replay. A `strategy_assignment_rejected` event carries the specific defect class so the planning defect is diagnosable.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml id="rsm-metrics"
render_strategy_metrics:
  assignments_total
  assignments_per_strategy          # histogram over the six strategies
  coverage_ratio                    # assigned_shot_count / storyboard_shot_count (target 1.0)
  validation_failures               # by defect class: unassigned | mixed | unknown | ref
  downgrade_count                   # assignments where downgraded == true
  downgrade_ratio_by_strategy       # how often each preferred strategy was downgraded
  cached_asset_ratio                # share of shots resolved by cached_asset (reuse rate)
  runway_video_ratio                # share of shots requiring the expensive strategy
  parameters_schema_reject_count    # cross-schema conformance failures
```

These metrics feed the cost and reuse review: a high `cached_asset_ratio` and low `runway_video_ratio` indicate healthy cost behaviour (consistent with the cache-hit and cost objectives in **5.2.3** and **5.4.1**), while any nonzero `validation_failures` indicates a planning defect requiring correction before the build can render.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The Render Strategy Model operates under least privilege and carries no secrets.

Required controls:

* StrategyAssignment records contain **no provider secrets** — providers are referenced by class, and credentials are resolved by workers from a secret store at execution time (as in **5.4.1**);
* StrategyAssignment records contain **no article content** and **no prompt text** — only references and parameters;
* assignment identity is tamper-evident via the deterministic `assignment_id` and `parameters_hash`;
* the `render-strategy.json` artifact is writable only by the Render Strategy Planner; the Rendering Platform has read-only access;
* every assignment creation, validation, and rejection emits an audit event;
* namespace isolation between concurrent builds prevents one build's assignments from resolving against another's stores.

Because the strategy value routes to a provider, tampering with a strategy could redirect work to an unintended provider. The tamper-evident identity and the planner-only write boundary together prevent unauthorised strategy substitution.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="rsm-config"
render_strategy_model:
  enum:
    - runway_video
    - animated_still
    - motion_graphics
    - typography
    - svg
    - cached_asset
  provider_map:
    runway_video: runway
    animated_still: motion
    motion_graphics: motion
    typography: typography
    svg: svg
    cached_asset: cache
  validation:
    fail_closed_on_defect: true
    require_full_coverage: true
    require_single_per_shot: true
    require_provenance: true
    reject_cross_schema_parameters: true
  identity:
    hash: sha256
    canonical_serialisation: true
  cache_bypass_strategy: cached_asset   # the sole strategy that bypasses provider submission
```

All values are validated at startup. An invalid configuration — for example an enum with a strategy absent from `provider_map`, or `fail_closed_on_defect: false` — fails closed and blocks rendering rather than proceeding with an unsafe model. The enumeration is closed (**RSM-301**); extending it requires amending both this configuration and this section.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* `strategy` value accepted only when in `render_strategy_enum`;
* each strategy's `parameters` block validates against its own schema and **fails** against every other schema (no cross-schema leak);
* deterministic `assignment_id` and `parameters_hash` reproduction under identical inputs;
* `cached_asset.origin_strategy` rejected when equal to `cached_asset`;
* provider derivation from strategy is exhaustive and total over the enum;
* prompt-template-family selection from strategy is total over the enum.

## Integration Tests

* full `render-strategy.json` with one assignment per shot passes validation and generates render tasks;
* strategy-to-queue derivation routes `runway_video` to the rate-limited provider bucket and `cached_asset` to cache bypass (**5.4.1**);
* strategy-to-prompt derivation selects `full_cinematic` only for `runway_video` (**5.5**);
* provenance with `downgraded: true` is preserved end-to-end from planner to render task.

## Failure Injection Tests

* **unassigned** shot (storyboard shot with no StrategyAssignment) → validation fails, build blocked;
* **mixed** shot (two StrategyAssignments for one shot) → validation fails, build blocked;
* **unknown** strategy value → validation fails, build blocked;
* broken reference (character/location/camera/cache ID does not resolve) → validation fails;
* parameters block for the wrong strategy → schema conformance fails;
* coverage shortfall (assigned_shot_count < storyboard_shot_count) → validation fails.

## Determinism Tests

* re-plan of an unchanged shot yields an identical `assignment_id` (supports incremental rendering, Principle 4);
* any single parameter change alters `parameters_hash` and therefore `assignment_id`;
* canonical serialisation makes reordered-but-equivalent parameter blocks hash identically.

## Recovery Tests

* rebuild the strategy binding of every shot from `render-strategy.json` alone;
* detect and reject a tampered `assignment_id` that does not match recomputation;
* reconcile a stale assignment against a re-planned storyboard shot.

---

# Render Strategy Quality Gate

```yaml id="rsm-quality-gate"
render_strategy_quality_gate:
  every_shot_has_exactly_one_strategy: true
  every_strategy_in_closed_enum: true
  no_shot_has_mixed_strategies: true
  parameters_conform_to_strategy_schema: true
  no_cross_schema_parameter_leak: true
  all_references_resolve: true
  full_storyboard_coverage: true
  assignment_identity_deterministic: true
  provenance_recorded_for_every_assignment: true
  cached_asset_origin_is_generative: true
  no_secrets_or_article_text_in_assignments: true
  provider_map_total_over_enum: true
  prompt_family_map_total_over_enum: true
  fail_closed_on_any_defect: true
```

The gate must pass for the entire `render-strategy.json` before any render task is generated. A single failing shot fails the whole gate.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RSM-401

The render strategy set is closed: exactly six strategies exist, and no value outside the enumeration is ever valid.

## Invariant RSM-402

Every renderable shot carries exactly one render strategy — never zero, never more than one.

## Invariant RSM-403

No shot is ever rendered by more than one strategy; mixing strategies within a single shot is prohibited, and a shot needing two mechanisms is split upstream, never assigned two strategies.

## Invariant RSM-404

The render strategy is declared by the Render Strategy Planner and recorded before rendering; it is never inferred at render time by a worker or provider.

## Invariant RSM-405

A StrategyAssignment's `parameters` conform to exactly one canonical schema — the one selected by its `strategy` value — and to no other.

## Invariant RSM-406

Strategy assignment identity is deterministic: identical shot, strategy, and parameters yield an identical `assignment_id`.

## Invariant RSM-407

Every strategy assignment records its provenance — planner version, matrix rule, preferred strategy, and any downgrade — and that provenance is immutable.

## Invariant RSM-408

The assigned strategy fully determines the shot's downstream routing: provider, queue class, prompt template family, and cache participation are pure functions of the strategy.

## Invariant RSM-409

A `cached_asset` strategy declares reuse of a previously validated asset and never submits to a generative provider; its origin strategy is always one of the five generative strategies.

## Invariant RSM-410

The Rendering Platform is a strict reader of `render-strategy.json`; only the Render Strategy Planner may author or alter a strategy assignment.

## Invariant RSM-411

An unassigned, mixed, unknown, or referentially broken strategy assignment fails closed and blocks rendering for the build; the platform never renders around a defective assignment or produces a partial trailer.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                          |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-427 | The Rendering Platform recognises a closed set of exactly six render strategies; new strategies require amending the specification. |
| ADR-428 | Every shot is bound to exactly one render strategy; zero, multiple, or mixed strategies per shot are prohibited and fail closed. |
| ADR-429 | Render strategy is declared by the Render Strategy Planner and recorded in `render-strategy.json` before rendering — never inferred at render time. |
| ADR-430 | Each strategy has a canonical parameter schema; a StrategyAssignment's parameters must conform to exactly one schema, matching its strategy. |
| ADR-431 | Strategy assignment identity is deterministic (`sha256(shot_id + strategy + parameters_hash)`), enabling stable diffs and incremental rendering. |
| ADR-432 | Every assignment carries immutable provenance recording the matrix rule and any cost-driven downgrade; the model records but never re-decides selection. |
| ADR-433 | The assigned strategy is the single routing key from which provider, queue class, prompt family, and cache participation are derived downstream. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.2.1** is complete when:

* The render strategy enumeration is fixed as a closed set of exactly six strategies — `runway_video`, `animated_still`, `motion_graphics`, `typography`, `svg`, `cached_asset` — with no valid value outside it.
* Each strategy has a canonical parameter schema, and a StrategyAssignment's parameters are required to conform to exactly one schema selected by its strategy value.
* The single-strategy invariant is defined and enforced at both planning-time and rendering-time: every shot carries exactly one strategy, and unassigned, mixed, and unknown assignments each fail closed.
* The prohibition on mixing strategies within one shot is explicit, with shots needing two mechanisms split upstream rather than assigned two strategies.
* Strategy assignment provenance from the Render Strategy Planner is defined, including the preferred strategy, any cost-driven downgrade, and its immutability — while explicitly deferring the decision matrix to **5.2.2** and cost selection to **5.2.3**.
* The StrategyAssignment record, its deterministic identity, and the `render-strategy.json` artifact are specified, binding one strategy durably to one shot.
* The strategy assignment validation gate is defined with completeness, closed-set, schema-conformance, referential-integrity, provenance, and determinism checks, all fail-closed.
* The forward contract feeds render task generation and the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**) — deriving provider, queue class, rate-limit bucket, cost rank, and cache bypass — and feeds deterministic prompt assembly (**5.5**) by selecting the prompt template family from the strategy.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a render strategy model in which every shot is rendered exactly one way, declared before rendering, validated fail-closed, and never mixed.


---

# Part 5.2.2 — Rendering Decision Matrix

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Rendering Decision Matrix** used by the Rendering Platform.

The Rendering Decision Matrix is the deterministic procedure that maps a single approved shot to exactly **one** render strategy from the strategy vocabulary defined in **5.2.1**.

It exists to answer one question, and only one question, per shot:

> Given this shot, which render strategy shall execute it?

The Rendering Decision Matrix converts the informal mapping table originally expressed as "Human facial emotion → Runway, Countdown UI → Motion Graphics, Architecture diagram → SVG, Title → Typography, Existing asset → Cache" into a **complete, total, reproducible decision function** over a closed shot-type taxonomy.

Its output is the `render_strategy` field consumed by every downstream rendering component:

* render task generation (**5.1.5**);
* the render queue admission contract (**5.4.1**);
* prompt assembly (**5.5.1**);
* provider selection and adapter routing (**5.3**);
* cost accounting and budget reservation (**5.2.3**).

The Rendering Decision Matrix does **not** decide *what* a shot depicts, *how* it should be paced, or *what motion* it should contain. Those facts are already fixed in the approved Storyboard, Shot List, and Camera Plan. The Matrix reads those facts; it never authors them.

The Rendering Decision Matrix does **not** decide the final *cost trade-off* between two otherwise-eligible strategies. When more than one strategy is admissible for a shot, cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**) resolves the choice under budget. The Matrix produces the **candidate set** and the **default within taxonomy**; **5.2.3** applies economic weighting. This boundary is strict and is restated throughout this section.

Selection is an **execution planning** act. The Rendering Decision Matrix is the classifier at the head of that act.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the closed **shot-type taxonomy** the Matrix classifies against;
* the canonical **decision table** mapping shot type to strategy;
* the **decision inputs** the Matrix consumes (shot metadata, motion requirement, continuity needs);
* the **motion requirement classification** that separates motion-bearing from static shots;
* the **precedence and tie-break rules** that make a multi-eligible shot resolve to one strategy deterministically;
* the **deterministic decision algorithm** (total function, no randomness);
* the **default and fallback rules** for unknown, ambiguous, or under-specified shots;
* the **decision record** written for every shot, and how the decision is made **reproducible**;
* how the Matrix hands its candidate set to cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**).

This section does **not** specify:

* the strategy vocabulary, strategy semantics, or strategy capability envelopes (defined in **5.2.1**);
* cost weighting, budget reservation, or economic tie-break resolution (defined in **5.2.3**);
* provider interface contracts or adapter behavior (defined in **5.3**);
* prompt construction for the selected strategy (defined in **5.5**);
* render queue scheduling of the resulting task (defined in **5.4.1**);
* asset or visual-quality validation of the produced render (defined in **5.6**).

The Rendering Decision Matrix consumes the strategy model of **5.2.1** and produces the candidate input to **5.2.3**. It sits precisely between them.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **The same shot must always resolve to the same strategy. The Matrix classifies; it never guesses.**

The Rendering Decision Matrix is a **pure, total, deterministic function** of a shot's declared attributes. Given identical shot attributes, taxonomy version, and matrix version, it must produce byte-for-byte identical output, on every machine, on every run, forever.

It must never:

* consult a random number generator;
* consult wall-clock time;
* consult provider availability, load, or price at classification time;
* consult a model, an LLM, or any non-deterministic inference at classification time;
* produce a different strategy for two shots whose classified attributes are equal.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Ambiguity is resolved by rule, not by chance.**

When a shot could be served by more than one strategy, the outcome is fixed by the published precedence order and tie-break keys in this section — never by evaluation order, hash-map iteration order, or "whichever matched first." Two implementers following this section must arrive at the same strategy for the same shot.

A second corollary governs completeness:

> **Every shot resolves. There is no unclassified shot.**

The taxonomy is closed and the decision function is total. A shot that matches no specific rule resolves to the published default (Animated Still) or, where its motion requirement forbids the default, to the published fallback chain. A shot is never left without a strategy, and classification never fails "open" into an arbitrary provider.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Approved Storyboard  ──►  Shot List / Camera Plan / Motion Annotations
        │
        ▼
Render Strategy Model (5.2.1)
   strategy vocabulary + capability envelopes
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        Rendering Decision Matrix (5.2.2)     │
│                                              │
│   shot attributes ─► shot-type classifier    │
│                          │                   │
│                          ▼                   │
│                 candidate strategy set        │
│                          │                   │
│                 precedence + tie-break        │
│                          │                   │
│                          ▼                   │
│              default-within-taxonomy          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │                         │
        │ candidate set           │ decision record
        ▼                         ▼
Cost-Aware Selection (5.2.3)   Render Manifest (5.4.4)
        │
        ▼
Final render_strategy per shot
        │
        ▼
Render Task Generation ──► Render Queue (5.4.1)
```

The Rendering Decision Matrix sits **downstream** of the strategy model (**5.2.1**), which gives it the legal vocabulary, and **upstream** of cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**), which resolves economic trade-offs. Every render task's strategy field originates here.

The Matrix runs once per shot per plan version. It is a planning-time classifier, not a runtime scheduler.

---

# Responsibilities

The Rendering Decision Matrix is responsible for:

* classifying each shot into exactly one **shot-type class** from the closed taxonomy;
* mapping that class to its canonical strategy via the decision table;
* deriving the **motion requirement** of the shot from its declared attributes;
* deriving the **continuity requirement** of the shot (character/location identity persistence);
* computing the **candidate strategy set** when a shot is eligible for more than one strategy;
* applying the fixed **precedence order** and deterministic **tie-break keys**;
* applying the **default** strategy for unmatched shots and the **fallback chain** where the default is inadmissible;
* validating that the chosen strategy is capability-compatible per **5.2.1**;
* emitting a durable, reproducible **decision record** per shot.

It is **not** responsible for:

* defining strategy semantics or capability envelopes (see **5.2.1**);
* choosing between admissible candidates on cost grounds (see **5.2.3**);
* provider selection beyond the strategy→provider binding fixed in **5.2.1**;
* assembling the prompt for the selected strategy (see **5.5**);
* scheduling, retrying, or timing out the resulting render task (see **5.4**);
* validating the produced asset (see **5.6**);
* modifying the shot, its motion annotation, or any approved creative fact (prohibited).

The Matrix is a read-only consumer of the approved plan. It **shall never** mutate shot attributes to force a strategy. If a shot's attributes are inconsistent, it records a classification fault and routes the shot to Manual Review (see **Default & Fallback Rules**), never a silent rewrite.

---

# Decision Objectives

The Rendering Decision Matrix shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RDM-301 — Totality

The decision function is **total** over the shot-type taxonomy. Every well-formed shot resolves to exactly one strategy. No input produces "no decision."

## RDM-302 — Determinism

Given identical shot attributes, taxonomy version, and matrix version, the output strategy and candidate set are byte-for-byte identical across machines and runs (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism, and with the ordering guarantees of **5.4.1**).

## RDM-303 — Single Strategy Per Shot

The Matrix assigns exactly one strategy to a shot. Mixed strategies within one shot are prohibited (consistent with **5.2.1**: "Mixed rendering strategies inside one shot are prohibited").

## RDM-304 — Motion Fidelity

A shot that declares genuine narrative motion must never be assigned a static strategy that cannot express that motion. Motion requirement is a hard constraint, not a preference.

## RDM-305 — Continuity Safety

A shot that must preserve character or location identity across the trailer must never be assigned a strategy whose capability envelope (per **5.2.1**) cannot guarantee that identity.

## RDM-306 — Cost Neutrality of Classification

The Matrix classifies without consulting live price, budget, or provider load. It produces the candidate set; **5.2.3** applies cost. Classification output must not vary with budget state.

## RDM-307 — Explicit Ambiguity Resolution

Where more than one strategy is eligible, the outcome is fixed by the published precedence order and tie-break keys. Evaluation order, map iteration order, and "first match wins" are prohibited as tie-breakers.

## RDM-308 — Reproducible Record

Every decision is recorded with enough provenance (shot attribute digest, taxonomy version, matrix version, matched rule id, candidate set, tie-break trace) to reproduce and audit it without re-running upstream engines.

## RDM-309 — Fail-Safe Default

An unmatched or under-specified shot resolves to the published default or fallback, never to the most expensive provider by accident, and never to an inadmissible strategy. Genuine unresolvability routes to Manual Review, not to a guess.

---

# Decision Inputs

The Matrix consumes a fixed, declared input record per shot. All inputs are read from approved upstream artifacts (Shot List, Storyboard, Camera Plan, Motion Annotations). The Matrix reads nothing else.

```yaml id="rdm-inputs"
decision_input:
  shot_id:                    # stable shot identifier
  scene_index:
  shot_index:

  # --- content classification signals ---
  subject_class:              # human | ui | data_viz | diagram | title_card | environment | existing_asset
  subject_detail:             # e.g. facial_emotion | walking | dashboard | architecture | logline
  depicts_human_performance:  # bool — face, body, gesture, gaze
  depicts_data_surface:       # bool — dashboard, chart, metric wall
  depicts_diagram:            # bool — nodes/edges, architecture, flow
  depicts_pure_text:          # bool — title, quote, chapter card

  # --- motion signals ---
  motion_requirement:         # NONE | LOOP_UI | SUBTLE | NARRATIVE   (see Motion Requirement)
  camera_movement:            # static | slow_push | pan | tilt | dolly | handheld   (from Camera Plan)
  subject_movement:           # none | micro | locomotion | interaction
  temporal_animation:         # none | countdown | reveal | transition

  # --- continuity signals ---
  continuity_requirement:     # NONE | STYLE | LOCATION | CHARACTER   (see Continuity Requirement)
  character_refs:             # [] of character ids that must remain consistent
  location_ref:               # location id that must remain consistent (nullable)

  # --- reuse signals ---
  prior_asset_ref:            # nullable — approved reusable asset for this shot
  shot_hash:                  # deterministic content hash of the shot (feeds cache key, 5.6.3)

  # --- provenance / versioning ---
  taxonomy_version:           # closed taxonomy version this input was classified under
  plan_version:               # approved plan version
```

Input rules:

* Every field is derived from an **approved** artifact. The Matrix never infers a field from raw article text or from the LLM at decision time (RDM-302).
* `subject_class`, `motion_requirement`, and `continuity_requirement` are the three **primary axes**. The taxonomy is defined over these three axes; all other fields are refinements or provenance.
* If a primary axis is missing or malformed, the shot is treated as **under-specified** and follows the Default & Fallback rules — it is never classified on a guess.
* Inputs are immutable to the Matrix. The Matrix shall never write back a corrected input.

---

# Motion Requirement Classification

Motion requirement is the single most important discriminator between motion-bearing strategies (Runway) and static/animated strategies (Animated Still, Motion Graphics, SVG, Typography). It is derived deterministically from declared motion signals.

```text
motion_requirement is derived, in priority order, as:

  NARRATIVE   if subject_movement ∈ {locomotion, interaction}
              OR depicts_human_performance = true with facial_emotion
              OR camera_movement ∈ {dolly, handheld}
              OR (camera_movement = slow_push AND subject_class = human)

  LOOP_UI     else if temporal_animation ∈ {countdown, transition}
              OR (subject_class = ui AND subject_movement = micro)

  SUBTLE      else if camera_movement ∈ {slow_push, pan, tilt}
              OR subject_movement = micro
              OR temporal_animation = reveal

  NONE        else
```

| Motion Class | Meaning | Requires genuine video motion? | Admissible strategies |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `NARRATIVE` | Human performance, locomotion, or camera motion that carries story meaning | Yes — hard requirement | `runway_video` only |
| `LOOP_UI` | Deterministic, self-contained UI animation (countdown, transition, reveal loop) | No — synthetic motion | `motion_graphics` |
| `SUBTLE` | Minor camera drift or micro-motion that can be faked from a still | No — parallax/pan from still | `animated_still`, `motion_graphics`, or `runway_video` (if continuity demands) |
| `NONE` | Fully static content | No | `svg`, `typography`, `animated_still` |

Motion fidelity is a **hard constraint** (RDM-304). A `NARRATIVE` shot shall never be down-assigned to a static strategy to save cost. Cost pressure on a `NARRATIVE` shot is resolved by deferral or Manual Review in **5.2.3**/**5.4.1**, never by silently degrading motion.

The `slow_push AND human` case is deliberately promoted to `NARRATIVE`: a camera push on a human face is the canonical "Human facial emotion / Camera push → Runway" case from the original matrix, and human identity under motion is the strongest driver of Runway selection.

---

# Continuity Requirement Classification

Continuity requirement expresses which identities must survive unchanged across the trailer. It gates strategies by capability, using the capability envelopes defined in **5.2.1**.

```text
continuity_requirement is derived, in priority order, as:

  CHARACTER   if character_refs is non-empty
  LOCATION    else if location_ref is not null AND subject_class = environment
  STYLE       else if subject_class ∈ {ui, data_viz, diagram, title_card}
  NONE        else
```

| Continuity Class | Meaning | Capability gate (per 5.2.1) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `CHARACTER` | A named character's face/body must remain identical | Requires character-injection–capable strategy: `runway_video` (via injection, **5.5.2**) or `cached_asset` reuse of an already-approved character render |
| `LOCATION` | A named location must remain identical | Requires location-injection–capable strategy: `runway_video`, `animated_still`, or `cached_asset` |
| `STYLE` | Only the enterprise visual style must be consistent | Any strategy; enforced by Visual Style Enforcement (**5.5.5**) |
| `NONE` | No cross-shot identity constraint | Any strategy |

Continuity safety is a **hard constraint** (RDM-305). A shot with `continuity_requirement = CHARACTER` shall never be assigned a strategy that cannot carry that character's identity. This is why an SVG or Typography strategy can never be selected for a human-performance shot, regardless of motion.

---

# Shot-Type Taxonomy

The taxonomy is **closed**. Every shot resolves to exactly one taxonomy class. The taxonomy is defined over the primary axes `(subject_class, motion_requirement, continuity_requirement)` with `subject_detail` as a refinement.

```yaml id="rdm-taxonomy"
shot_type_taxonomy:
  version: 1

  classes:
    - HUMAN_PERFORMANCE       # face, gaze, gesture, emotion — motion-bearing
    - HUMAN_LOCOMOTION        # walking, entering, moving through space
    - CAMERA_MOVE_ON_SUBJECT  # push / dolly / handheld on a real subject
    - UI_ANIMATED             # countdown, live UI, transition, reveal loop
    - DATA_SURFACE_STATIC     # enterprise dashboard, metric wall, chart, KPI board
    - DIAGRAM_STRUCTURAL      # architecture diagram, node/edge, system flow
    - TITLE_TEXT              # title card, logline, chapter card, quote
    - ENVIRONMENT_STATIC      # establishing location still, set, backdrop
    - ENVIRONMENT_SUBTLE      # location still with slow drift / parallax
    - EXISTING_ASSET          # an already-approved reusable asset
    - UNCLASSIFIED            # well-formed shot matching no specific class → default
    - INVALID                 # malformed / contradictory attributes → Manual Review
```

Class definitions:

* **HUMAN_PERFORMANCE** — the shot depicts a human whose facial emotion, gaze, or gesture is the point. `depicts_human_performance = true`, `motion_requirement = NARRATIVE`, `continuity_requirement = CHARACTER`. This is the original "Human facial emotion → Runway" case.
* **HUMAN_LOCOMOTION** — a human is walking, entering, or traversing space. `subject_movement = locomotion`, `motion_requirement = NARRATIVE`. The original "Walking → Runway" case.
* **CAMERA_MOVE_ON_SUBJECT** — the camera pushes, dollies, or handhelds across a real subject/scene where the motion carries meaning. `camera_movement ∈ {slow_push, dolly, handheld}` with a real (non-diagram, non-text) subject. The original "Camera push → Runway" case.
* **UI_ANIMATED** — a user-interface element animates deterministically: countdown, live counter, transition, reveal. `subject_class = ui`, `motion_requirement = LOOP_UI`. The original "Countdown UI → Motion Graphics" case.
* **DATA_SURFACE_STATIC** — an enterprise dashboard, metric wall, or chart presented as a (near-)static surface, optionally with `SUBTLE` motion. `subject_class = data_viz`. The original "Enterprise dashboard → Animated Still" case.
* **DIAGRAM_STRUCTURAL** — a structural diagram: architecture, node/edge graph, system flow, rendered crisply as vector. `subject_class = diagram`, `motion_requirement = NONE`. The original "Architecture diagram → SVG" case.
* **TITLE_TEXT** — pure text: title card, logline, chapter card, pull-quote. `depicts_pure_text = true`. The original "Title → Typography" case.
* **ENVIRONMENT_STATIC** — an establishing location shown as a fully static still. `subject_class = environment`, `motion_requirement = NONE`.
* **ENVIRONMENT_SUBTLE** — a location still with slow camera drift or parallax that can be produced from a still. `subject_class = environment`, `motion_requirement = SUBTLE`.
* **EXISTING_ASSET** — a shot for which an approved reusable asset already exists (`prior_asset_ref` present, or a render cache hit per **5.6.3**). The original "Existing asset → Cache" case.
* **UNCLASSIFIED** — a well-formed shot that matches no specific class. Resolves to the published default. Not an error.
* **INVALID** — a shot whose attributes are internally contradictory (e.g. `depicts_pure_text = true` but `subject_movement = locomotion`). Routes to Manual Review. Never guessed.

---
