# The Decision Table

The canonical decision table maps each taxonomy class to its **canonical strategy** and its **candidate set**. The canonical strategy is the default within the taxonomy; the candidate set is the full admissible set passed to **5.2.3** for cost-aware resolution.

| # | Shot-Type Class | Primary Axes `(subject, motion, continuity)` | Canonical Strategy | Candidate Set (→ 5.2.3) | Origin (5.8) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| R1 | `EXISTING_ASSET` | any, any, any | `cached_asset` | `{cached_asset}` | Existing asset → Cache |
| R2 | `HUMAN_PERFORMANCE` | human, NARRATIVE, CHARACTER | `runway_video` | `{runway_video}` | Human facial emotion → Runway |
| R3 | `HUMAN_LOCOMOTION` | human, NARRATIVE, CHARACTER | `runway_video` | `{runway_video}` | Walking → Runway |
| R4 | `CAMERA_MOVE_ON_SUBJECT` | real subject, NARRATIVE, ≥LOCATION | `runway_video` | `{runway_video}` | Camera push → Runway |
| R5 | `UI_ANIMATED` | ui, LOOP_UI, STYLE | `motion_graphics` | `{motion_graphics}` | Countdown UI → Motion Graphics |
| R6 | `DATA_SURFACE_STATIC` | data_viz, NONE\|SUBTLE, STYLE | `animated_still` | `{animated_still, motion_graphics}` | Enterprise dashboard → Animated Still |
| R7 | `DIAGRAM_STRUCTURAL` | diagram, NONE, STYLE | `svg` | `{svg, motion_graphics}` | Architecture diagram → SVG |
| R8 | `TITLE_TEXT` | title_card, NONE, STYLE | `typography` | `{typography, svg}` | Title → Typography |
| R9 | `ENVIRONMENT_STATIC` | environment, NONE, LOCATION | `animated_still` | `{animated_still, svg}` | (derived) |
| R10 | `ENVIRONMENT_SUBTLE` | environment, SUBTLE, LOCATION | `animated_still` | `{animated_still, runway_video}` | (derived) |
| R11 | `UNCLASSIFIED` | any, NONE\|SUBTLE, ≤STYLE | `animated_still` | `{animated_still}` | (default) |
| R12 | `INVALID` | contradictory | — (Manual Review) | `{}` | (fail-safe) |

Table rules:

* The `Canonical Strategy` is the **default within the taxonomy** and is the value the Matrix records as `default_strategy`.
* The `Candidate Set` is the strategy set forwarded to cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**). When the candidate set is a singleton, **5.2.3** has nothing to optimize and the canonical strategy stands.
* Rules R2–R4 have **singleton** candidate sets: a genuine narrative-motion human/subject shot is Runway or nothing. Cost pressure cannot down-assign it (RDM-304). It is deferred or escalated, never degraded.
* Rule R1 (`EXISTING_ASSET`) has the **highest match precedence** (see Precedence) and always yields `cached_asset`, mirroring "Cache elimination precedes all queueing" (**5.4.1**) and the cost-priority head (**5.2.3**).
* All strategy identifiers are drawn from the closed vocabulary of **5.2.1**: `runway_video`, `animated_still`, `motion_graphics`, `typography`, `svg`, `cached_asset`. No other value is legal.

---

# Precedence & Tie-Break Rules

A shot may satisfy the predicates of more than one class (for example, an environment still that also has a slow push, or a data surface that also has a subtle reveal). Precedence and tie-break rules make the outcome deterministic (RDM-307).

## Rule-Match Precedence

Rules are evaluated in a **fixed precedence order**, independent of source-code layout or map iteration order. The first rule in precedence order whose predicate holds selects the class.

```text
match precedence (highest first):

  1. R1   EXISTING_ASSET            (reuse dominates everything)
  2. R12  INVALID                   (contradiction dominates classification)
  3. R2   HUMAN_PERFORMANCE
  4. R3   HUMAN_LOCOMOTION
  5. R4   CAMERA_MOVE_ON_SUBJECT
  6. R5   UI_ANIMATED
  7. R7   DIAGRAM_STRUCTURAL
  8. R8   TITLE_TEXT
  9. R6   DATA_SURFACE_STATIC
 10. R10  ENVIRONMENT_SUBTLE
 11. R9   ENVIRONMENT_STATIC
 12. R11  UNCLASSIFIED              (default; always matches)
```

Precedence rationale:

* **Reuse first (R1).** If an approved asset exists, nothing else matters — reuse is free and deterministic. This mirrors the cost order head (Cache → …) in **5.2.3** and the cache-bypass rule in **5.4.1**.
* **Contradiction second (R12).** A malformed shot is caught before any content rule can accidentally classify it.
* **Human/motion before static (R2–R4 before R5–R11).** Motion fidelity and character continuity are hard constraints; a shot that qualifies for Runway on human/motion grounds is never captured by a cheaper static rule.
* **Specific static before generic static (R5/R7/R8/R6 before R9–R11).** A shot that is clearly UI, diagram, title, or data surface is classified as such before falling to environment or the default.
* **Default last (R11).** `UNCLASSIFIED` always matches, guaranteeing totality (RDM-301).

## Tie-Break Keys (within a class / within a candidate set)

Precedence resolves *which class*. When two shots or two candidate strategies remain equal after precedence, the following **deterministic tie-break keys** apply, in order. These are the *only* legal tie-breakers:

```yaml id="rdm-tiebreak"
tie_break_order:
  1: motion_fidelity        # a strategy that satisfies motion_requirement outranks one that does not
  2: continuity_capability  # a strategy that satisfies continuity_requirement outranks one that does not
  3: cost_rank              # cheaper strategy first — DELEGATED to 5.2.3, not decided here
  4: strategy_ordinal       # fixed vocabulary ordinal (cache<svg<typography<motion<animated<runway)
  5: shot_hash              # final deterministic tiebreak on shot content hash
```

Tie-break rules:

* Keys 1 and 2 are **hard capability filters** applied *inside* the Matrix: a candidate that fails motion or continuity is removed from the candidate set before it ever reaches **5.2.3**.
* Key 3 (`cost_rank`) is **delegated to 5.2.3** — the Matrix does not read price. The Matrix hands **5.2.3** the surviving candidate set and lets cost weighting choose among admissible options.
* Keys 4 and 5 exist only to guarantee a total order for reproducibility if **5.2.3** ever returns two equally-weighted candidates; they are decided by fixed ordinal and content hash, never by chance.
* No tie-break key is permitted to consult time, load, or randomness (RDM-302).

The `strategy_ordinal` order is fixed and published:

```yaml
strategy_ordinal:
  cached_asset:     0
  svg:              1
  typography:       2
  motion_graphics:  3
  animated_still:   4
  runway_video:     5
```

This ordinal ascends with expected cost, consistent with the cost-priority order in **5.2.3** (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway) and the Render Queue's `strategy_cost_rank` ordering in **5.4.1**.

---

# Deterministic Decision Algorithm

The decision procedure is a total, pure function. The following pseudo-code is normative; an implementation must be observably equivalent.

```text
function decide_strategy(shot) -> DecisionRecord:

  # ---- 0. provenance capture (reproducibility) ----
  attr_digest   = sha256(canonical_serialize(shot.attributes))
  tax_version   = shot.taxonomy_version
  matrix_version = MATRIX_VERSION

  # ---- 1. validity gate ----
  if is_contradictory(shot):                       # e.g. pure_text AND locomotion
      return record(shot, class=INVALID,
                    strategy=NONE, candidates={},
                    route=MANUAL_REVIEW,
                    reason="contradictory attributes",
                    attr_digest, tax_version, matrix_version)

  # ---- 2. reuse gate (highest precedence) ----
  if shot.prior_asset_ref != null OR cache_hit(shot.shot_hash):   # cache lookup per 5.6.3
      return record(shot, class=EXISTING_ASSET,
                    strategy=cached_asset, candidates={cached_asset},
                    route=NORMAL,
                    matched_rule="R1",
                    attr_digest, tax_version, matrix_version)

  # ---- 3. derive primary axes (deterministic) ----
  motion     = derive_motion_requirement(shot)        # NONE|SUBTLE|LOOP_UI|NARRATIVE
  continuity = derive_continuity_requirement(shot)    # NONE|STYLE|LOCATION|CHARACTER
  subject    = shot.subject_class

  # ---- 4. classify by fixed precedence ----
  class = UNCLASSIFIED
  for rule in PRECEDENCE_ORDER:                       # R1..R11 fixed order
      if rule.predicate(subject, motion, continuity, shot):
          class = rule.class
          matched_rule = rule.id
          break
  # R11 (UNCLASSIFIED) predicate is always-true → totality guaranteed

  # ---- 5. look up canonical strategy + candidate set ----
  canonical  = DECISION_TABLE[class].canonical_strategy
  candidates = DECISION_TABLE[class].candidate_set

  # ---- 6. hard capability filtering (motion + continuity) ----
  candidates = filter(candidates, s => satisfies_motion(s, motion))
  candidates = filter(candidates, s => satisfies_continuity(s, continuity))

  if candidates is empty:                             # no admissible strategy
      return record(shot, class,
                    strategy=NONE, candidates={},
                    route=MANUAL_REVIEW,
                    reason="no capability-admissible strategy",
                    attr_digest, tax_version, matrix_version)

  # canonical must remain admissible; if filtered out, replace with
  # the lowest strategy_ordinal survivor that satisfies constraints
  if canonical not in candidates:
      canonical = min_by_strategy_ordinal(candidates)

  # ---- 7. emit decision record (cost NOT applied here) ----
  return record(shot, class,
                default_strategy = canonical,
                candidate_set    = candidates,       # → forwarded to 5.2.3
                route            = NORMAL,
                matched_rule     = matched_rule,
                tie_break_trace  = capability_filter_trace,
                attr_digest, tax_version, matrix_version)
```

Algorithm invariants:

* **Purity.** `decide_strategy` reads only `shot`, the fixed tables, and the cache-existence oracle (`cache_hit`, which is itself a deterministic content-hash lookup — see **5.6.3**). It reads no clock, no RNG, no price, no load.
* **Total order of rules.** `PRECEDENCE_ORDER` is a fixed list, never derived from map iteration. Two implementations iterating the same list produce the same class.
* **Cost deferred.** Step 6 filters on *capability*, never cost. The candidate set (possibly plural) is forwarded to **5.2.3**, which applies cost weighting to pick the final strategy. When the candidate set is a singleton, **5.2.3** is a pass-through.
* **No open failure.** The only exits are a valid `DecisionRecord` with a strategy, or a `MANUAL_REVIEW` route with `strategy=NONE`. There is no path that returns an arbitrary provider.

`derive_motion_requirement` and `derive_continuity_requirement` implement exactly the priority ladders in **Motion Requirement Classification** and **Continuity Requirement Classification**. Both are pure functions of declared attributes.

---

# Default & Fallback Rules

The Matrix is total; these rules define its behavior at the edges.

## Default

A well-formed shot that matches no specific rule (`UNCLASSIFIED`, R11) resolves to `animated_still`.

Rationale: `animated_still` is the safest general-purpose static strategy — it carries location continuity, satisfies `NONE` and `SUBTLE` motion, honors the enterprise visual style (**5.5.5**), and sits mid-cost (never defaulting into the most expensive Runway path by accident, satisfying RDM-309).

The default is **never** `runway_video`. Runway is selected only by an affirmative motion/continuity rule (R2–R4) or when it is the only continuity-admissible survivor for a `SUBTLE` environment (R10). This preserves the cost discipline of **5.2.3**: "Runway is the most expensive resource and should be used only when genuine motion contributes to storytelling."

## Fallback chain (default inadmissible)

If a shot's derived motion or continuity requirement removes the class's canonical strategy from the candidate set, the Matrix substitutes the **lowest-strategy_ordinal survivor** that satisfies both hard constraints (algorithm step 6). The substitution order is therefore fixed:

```text
cached_asset → svg → typography → motion_graphics → animated_still → runway_video
```

Fallback stops at the first survivor. Example: a `DATA_SURFACE_STATIC` shot (R6, candidates `{animated_still, motion_graphics}`) that declares `LOOP_UI` motion loses `animated_still` (cannot express loop UI) and falls to `motion_graphics`.

## Unresolvable shots → Manual Review

Two cases route to Manual Review (**5.4.1** Manual Review Queue) with `strategy=NONE`:

* **INVALID** — contradictory attributes (validity gate, step 1).
* **Empty candidate set** — no capability-admissible strategy survives filtering (step 6). This can only happen for a genuinely impossible request (e.g. `NARRATIVE` motion required with a strategy vocabulary that forbids the only motion-capable provider for this build).

Manual Review is a **fail-closed** escalation, consistent with Principle 5. The Matrix never invents a strategy to avoid escalation. A human resolves the shot (or corrects the plan), and the Matrix re-runs deterministically on the corrected input.

---

# Decision Record & Reproducibility (Determinism)

Every shot's decision is recorded durably. The record is the reproducibility contract.

```yaml id="rdm-record"
render_decision_record:
  shot_id:
  scene_index:
  shot_index:

  # --- inputs provenance ---
  attribute_digest:        # sha256 of canonical-serialized shot attributes
  taxonomy_version:        # closed taxonomy version used
  matrix_version:          # Matrix ruleset version used
  plan_version:

  # --- derived axes ---
  subject_class:
  motion_requirement:      # NONE | SUBTLE | LOOP_UI | NARRATIVE
  continuity_requirement:  # NONE | STYLE | LOCATION | CHARACTER

  # --- decision ---
  matched_rule:            # R1..R12
  shot_type_class:         # taxonomy class
  default_strategy:        # canonical strategy (default within taxonomy)
  candidate_set:           # [] forwarded to 5.2.3
  capability_filter_trace: # which candidates were dropped and why
  route:                   # NORMAL | MANUAL_REVIEW

  # --- linkage ---
  cost_selected_strategy:  # filled by 5.2.3 (null until cost selection runs)
  final_strategy:          # authoritative strategy after 5.2.3
```

Reproducibility rules (Determinism):

* **Content-addressed inputs.** `attribute_digest` is a hash of the canonically-serialized shot attributes. Re-running the Matrix on the same digest, taxonomy version, and matrix version must reproduce the same `matched_rule`, `shot_type_class`, `default_strategy`, and `candidate_set` exactly (RDM-302, RDM-308).
* **Versioned rules.** Both `taxonomy_version` and `matrix_version` are recorded. A change to the taxonomy or the decision table bumps the version; historical decisions remain reproducible against the version under which they were made. A decision is only comparable to another decision at the same versions.
* **Deterministic serialization.** `canonical_serialize` uses fixed key ordering and normalized value formatting so that logically-equal shots produce identical digests on any platform.
* **Separation of classification and cost.** `default_strategy` and `candidate_set` are owned by this section and are immutable once written. `cost_selected_strategy` and `final_strategy` are written by **5.2.3**. This separation lets an auditor see *what the Matrix decided* independently of *what cost chose*, and lets classification be re-verified without re-running cost.
* **No hidden state.** The record contains everything needed to reproduce the decision without re-reading upstream engines: inputs digest, versions, derived axes, matched rule, and outputs. The Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) stores this record per shot.
* **Audit replay.** Given a stored record, an implementation can re-execute `decide_strategy` against the recorded attribute digest and assert equality with the stored outputs. Any divergence is a determinism defect and fails the Quality Gate.

---

# Events

The Matrix emits an event for every classified shot and every escalation.

```yaml
render_decision_events:
  - shot_classified              # normal decision, includes matched_rule + candidate_set
  - shot_cache_reuse_selected    # R1 fired; cached_asset chosen
  - shot_motion_forced_runway    # R2..R4/R10 forced runway_video on motion fidelity
  - shot_capability_fallback     # canonical filtered out; fallback survivor chosen
  - shot_default_applied         # R11 default (animated_still) applied
  - shot_routed_manual_review    # INVALID or empty candidate set
  - taxonomy_version_mismatch    # input classified under a superseded taxonomy version
```

Every event references `shot_id`, `matched_rule`, `shot_type_class`, `taxonomy_version`, `matrix_version`, and `attribute_digest`, and is durably recorded for audit and deterministic replay.

---

# Metrics

Planning dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml
render_decision_metrics:
  shots_classified_total
  strategy_distribution:          # count per strategy: runway/animated/motion/svg/typography/cache
  cache_reuse_rate                # fraction resolved to cached_asset (feeds cache-hit SLO ≥ 70%)
  runway_selection_rate           # fraction forced to runway_video (cost driver, feeds 5.2.3)
  capability_fallback_rate        # fraction where canonical was filtered out
  default_applied_rate            # fraction resolved via R11 default
  manual_review_rate              # fraction escalated (INVALID + empty candidate)
  taxonomy_version_mismatch_count
  determinism_replay_pass_rate    # re-decide vs stored record equality
```

A rising `runway_selection_rate` is a direct cost signal consumed by **5.2.3**. A rising `manual_review_rate` indicates upstream plan-quality drift. `determinism_replay_pass_rate` must remain 1.0; any value below 1.0 is a hard defect.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="rdm-config"
render_decision_matrix:
  taxonomy_version: 1
  matrix_version: 1

  default_strategy: animated_still         # R11 default; never runway_video

  strategy_ordinal:                        # fixed cost-ascending ordinal
    cached_asset: 0
    svg: 1
    typography: 2
    motion_graphics: 3
    animated_still: 4
    runway_video: 5

  motion_rules:
    narrative_triggers: [locomotion, interaction, facial_emotion, dolly, handheld]
    loop_ui_triggers:   [countdown, transition, micro_ui]
    subtle_triggers:    [slow_push, pan, tilt, micro, reveal]

  continuity_gates:
    character_requires: [runway_video, cached_asset]
    location_requires:  [runway_video, animated_still, cached_asset]

  precedence_order: [R1, R12, R2, R3, R4, R5, R7, R8, R6, R10, R9, R11]

  fail_closed: true                        # unresolvable → Manual Review, never a guess
```

All values are configurable and environment-overridable. Any change to `taxonomy_version`, `matrix_version`, `precedence_order`, or `default_strategy` bumps the recorded version so prior decisions remain reproducible. Configuration is validated at startup; an invalid configuration (e.g. `default_strategy: runway_video`, a strategy not in the **5.2.1** vocabulary, or a non-total precedence list missing R11) **fails closed** and blocks classification rather than classifying with unsafe rules.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* every taxonomy class resolves to its canonical strategy per the decision table;
* motion requirement derivation for each trigger combination (NONE/SUBTLE/LOOP_UI/NARRATIVE);
* continuity requirement derivation for each subject/ref combination;
* precedence ordering: a shot satisfying multiple rules resolves to the highest-precedence rule;
* R1 reuse dominates all other rules;
* R2–R4 singleton candidate sets are never reduced to a static strategy;
* default (R11) resolves to `animated_still`, never `runway_video`;
* fallback substitution picks the lowest strategy_ordinal survivor;
* INVALID and empty-candidate shots route to Manual Review with `strategy=NONE`;
* strategy identifiers are always drawn from the **5.2.1** vocabulary.

## Integration Tests

* Matrix output candidate set feeds **5.2.3**; cost selection stays within the candidate set;
* singleton candidate sets pass through **5.2.3** unchanged;
* selected strategy binds to the correct provider per **5.2.1**;
* decision record persists to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**);
* cache-hit shots (R1) bypass rendering, consistent with **5.4.1** and **5.6.3**;
* Manual Review escalation routes to the Manual Review Queue (**5.4.1**).

## Determinism Tests

* re-decide 10,000 shots and assert byte-for-byte equality of every decision record;
* decide the same shot on multiple platforms/architectures → identical output;
* shuffle rule-map insertion order in the implementation → identical classification (proves precedence is list-driven, not map-iteration-driven);
* attribute digest stability across serialization runs;
* version isolation: a taxonomy/matrix version bump does not alter decisions recorded under the prior version.

## Failure / Edge Tests

* contradictory attributes (pure_text + locomotion) → INVALID → Manual Review;
* under-specified shot (missing primary axis) → default or Manual Review, never a guess;
* NARRATIVE motion with a build that forbids the motion provider → empty candidate → Manual Review;
* unknown `subject_class` value → UNCLASSIFIED default, not a crash;
* strategy vocabulary mismatch in config → fail-closed at startup.

## Cost-Boundary Tests

* confirm the Matrix never reads price or budget (inject a poisoned price oracle; output must not change);
* confirm cost pressure cannot down-assign a NARRATIVE shot within the Matrix (that resolution belongs to **5.2.3**/**5.4.1**).

---

# Rendering Decision Quality Gate

```yaml
render_decision_quality_gate:
  every_shot_resolves: true                     # totality (RDM-301)
  single_strategy_per_shot: true                # (RDM-303)
  determinism_replay_pass_rate: 1.0             # (RDM-302, RDM-308)
  precedence_is_list_driven: true               # not map-iteration order (RDM-307)
  narrative_motion_never_static: true           # motion fidelity (RDM-304)
  continuity_never_violated: true               # continuity safety (RDM-305)
  classification_ignores_cost: true             # cost neutrality (RDM-306)
  default_is_not_runway: true                   # fail-safe default (RDM-309)
  candidate_sets_within_5_2_1_vocabulary: true
  unresolvable_shots_route_manual_review: true  # fail closed
  decision_record_persisted_per_shot: true
  config_invalid_fails_closed: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RDM-401

Every well-formed shot resolves to exactly one strategy from the **5.2.1** vocabulary; there is no unclassified shot and no null strategy on the NORMAL route.

## Invariant RDM-402

The decision function is pure and deterministic: identical shot attributes, taxonomy version, and matrix version yield byte-for-byte identical decision records on any machine.

## Invariant RDM-403

Rule-match precedence is driven by a fixed, published list — never by source layout, map iteration order, or "first match wins" by accident.

## Invariant RDM-404

A shot whose motion requirement is `NARRATIVE` is never assigned a static strategy; motion fidelity is a hard constraint the Matrix cannot trade away.

## Invariant RDM-405

A shot whose continuity requirement is `CHARACTER` or `LOCATION` is never assigned a strategy whose **5.2.1** capability envelope cannot preserve that identity.

## Invariant RDM-406

Classification never reads price, budget, provider load, or wall-clock time; the Matrix emits a candidate set and delegates cost resolution to **5.2.3**.

## Invariant RDM-407

The default strategy is `animated_still` and never `runway_video`; Runway is selected only by an affirmative motion/continuity rule.

## Invariant RDM-408

An unresolvable shot (INVALID or empty candidate set) routes to Manual Review with `strategy=NONE`; the Matrix never invents a strategy to avoid escalation (Fail Closed).

## Invariant RDM-409

Every decision is recorded with sufficient provenance (attribute digest, taxonomy version, matrix version, matched rule, candidate set, capability trace) to reproduce and audit it without re-running upstream engines.

## Invariant RDM-410

The Matrix is read-only over the approved plan; it never mutates shot attributes, motion annotations, or any creative fact to force a strategy.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR | Decision |
| --- | --- |
| ADR-434 | Strategy selection is a deterministic, total classification over a closed shot-type taxonomy — never a heuristic or model inference at decision time. |
| ADR-435 | The Matrix produces a capability-filtered candidate set and delegates the final cost trade-off to cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**); classification never reads price or budget. |
| ADR-436 | Motion fidelity and continuity are hard capability constraints; a NARRATIVE or CHARACTER shot is never down-assigned to a cheaper incapable strategy. |
| ADR-437 | Rule-match precedence and tie-breaks are fixed and published (reuse → invalid → human/motion → specific static → environment → default); ambiguity is resolved by rule, never by evaluation order or chance. |
| ADR-438 | Every decision is recorded with versioned, content-addressed provenance so it is reproducible and auditable; unresolvable shots fail closed to Manual Review rather than defaulting to a guess. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.2.2** is complete when:

* The shot-type taxonomy is closed, and every well-formed shot resolves to exactly one taxonomy class and exactly one strategy from the **5.2.1** vocabulary.
* The decision table canonically maps the original shot types (human facial emotion, walking, camera push → Runway; countdown UI → Motion Graphics; enterprise dashboard → Animated Still; architecture diagram → SVG; title → Typography; existing asset → Cache) and extends them to a total function.
* Decision inputs are precisely defined and drawn only from approved upstream artifacts, with motion requirement and continuity requirement as first-class derived axes.
* Precedence and tie-break rules are fixed and published so a multi-eligible shot resolves deterministically, independent of implementation evaluation order.
* The deterministic decision algorithm is specified as a pure, total function that reads no clock, no RNG, no price, and no provider load.
* Default and fallback rules guarantee totality, keep the default off the most expensive provider, and route genuinely unresolvable shots to Manual Review (Fail Closed).
* The Matrix emits a candidate set to cost-aware selection (**5.2.3**) and records the boundary between classification and cost explicitly.
* Every decision is captured in a versioned, content-addressed decision record that is reproducible and auditable without re-running upstream engines.
* Events, metrics, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a deterministic rendering decision classifier that assigns exactly one reproducible strategy per shot without ever consulting cost or randomness at classification time.


---

# Part 5.2.3 — Cost-Aware Strategy Selection

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Cost-Aware Strategy Selection** policy of the Rendering Platform.

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection is the discipline by which the platform accounts for, reserves, estimates, and constrains the monetary and compute cost of rendering — **without ever allowing cost to degrade the approved story**.

The Rendering Platform executes an already-approved creative plan. Every shot arriving at rendering has already been assigned exactly one render strategy by the Render Strategy Model (defined in **5.2.1**) and resolved through the Rendering Decision Matrix (defined in **5.2.2**). This section does not choose *which* strategy a shot uses on aesthetic grounds; that decision belongs to **5.2.2**. This section defines:

* how the cost of each render strategy is modelled;
* the fixed, normative cost-priority order that biases selection toward the cheapest strategy **that still satisfies the shot's storytelling requirement**;
* how a per-build render budget is reserved, drawn down, and enforced;
* how cost estimation is computed before any provider work is dispatched;
* how the queue's cost-aware admission (defined in **5.4.1**, objective **RQ-306**) consumes this policy;
* what happens when the budget is exhausted (escalation to Manual Review — Fail Closed);
* the cost telemetry emitted for every estimate, reservation, and settlement.

The governing tension of this section is explicit and non-negotiable:

Cost optimization is a **preference among adequate strategies**, never a licence to render an inadequate one, and never a licence to cut shots. Cost is a tie-breaker and a budget guardrail. It is never a story editor.

This is the expansion of the original topic *5.28 Cost Optimization* into a full selection and enforcement policy.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the per-strategy cost model and its cost-class taxonomy;
* the normative cost-priority order (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway);
* the "adequacy-first, cost-second" selection rule that binds cost preference to storytelling sufficiency;
* the **Story First** prohibition on reducing shots or downgrading strategies purely to save cost;
* the cost estimation schema and estimation timing;
* the per-build render budget: reservation, draw-down, headroom, and settlement;
* the interaction with queue admission and budget reservation (forward-ref **5.4.1** / **RQ-306**);
* budget-exhaustion behaviour and escalation to Manual Review (Fail Closed, consistent with Principle 5);
* cost telemetry, events, and metrics;
* the cost-selection quality gate, invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* the enumeration and semantics of render strategies (defined in **5.2.1**);
* the shot-type → strategy mapping and its precedence rules (defined in **5.2.2**);
* how expensive work is physically deferred, leased, and dispatched within budget (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the render cache key and cache-hit detection (defined in **5.6.3**);
* provider submission payloads and provider pricing negotiation (defined in **5.3.3** and **5.3.4**);
* retry cost accounting formulas beyond attribution (retry classification defined in **5.4.5**);
* preview vs final render profiles (defined in **5.6.4** and **5.6.5**).

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection consumes the strategy set from **5.2.1** and the decision outcome from **5.2.2**; it produces a cost estimate, a budget reservation, and an admission signal. It owns none of the creative decisions upstream of it.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **Cost chooses between adequate strategies; it never makes an inadequate strategy acceptable, and it never removes a shot the story requires.**

Cost-awareness lives entirely inside the space of strategies that already satisfy the storytelling requirement of a shot. Within that space, the platform prefers the cheapest option. Outside that space — where a cheaper strategy would fail to convey what the shot must convey — cost has no vote.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Reducing the number of shots, or downgrading a shot's strategy, purely to lower cost is prohibited without explicit Story Quality Gate approval.**

The budget constrains *how much rendering the platform may attempt*, not *how much story the platform may tell*. When the budget cannot pay for the approved plan, the platform does not silently trim the plan. It **Fails Closed**: it halts and routes the build to Manual Review with a complete cost account, so a human — through the Story Quality Gate — decides whether to raise the budget, re-plan the story, or accept a documented reduction. The platform never makes that trade autonomously.

This is the rendering-layer expression of the whole system's **Story First** posture: the story is approved upstream, and rendering is an execution layer (Principle 1, Principle 5). Cost is an execution constraint, not a creative authority.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Storyboard (Approved)
        │
        ▼
Render Strategy Model (5.2.1)        ── enumerates strategies per shot
        │
        ▼
Rendering Decision Matrix (5.2.2)    ── assigns ONE strategy per shot (adequacy)
        │
        ▼
Cost-Aware Strategy Selection (5.2.3)  ◄── THIS SECTION
        │
        ├── Cost Model            (per-strategy cost class + estimate)
        ├── Cost Estimator        (build-level cost projection)
        ├── Budget Reservation    (per-build render budget)
        ├── Adequacy Guard        (Story First: no cost-driven downgrade)
        └── Admission Signal      (budget headroom → RQ-306)
        │
        ▼
Render Task Generation
        │
        ▼
Render Queue Manager (5.4.1)         ── cost-aware admission, RQ-306
        │
        ▼
Provider Workers ──► Runway / Motion / SVG / Typography / Cache
```

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection sits **after** strategy assignment and **before** task generation and queueing. By the time a shot reaches this section, its strategy is fixed on adequacy grounds. This section attaches a cost estimate and a budget verdict to that already-adequate plan, and hands the queue a budget-reservation context that **5.4.1** enforces at admission time.

Cost never reaches back up the pipeline to change an assigned strategy. The single, tightly-controlled exception is the **Story Quality Gate** escalation path, which is a human decision, not an automatic downgrade.

---

# Responsibilities

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection is responsible for:

* maintaining the per-strategy cost model and cost-class taxonomy;
* computing a deterministic cost estimate per shot and per build before dispatch;
* enforcing the normative cost-priority order as a tie-breaker among adequate strategies (via **5.2.2**);
* reserving a per-build render budget and tracking its draw-down and headroom;
* providing the queue with the budget-reservation context consumed by **RQ-306**;
* attributing actual settled cost back to each render attempt (including retries);
* detecting budget exhaustion and routing the build to Manual Review (Fail Closed);
* enforcing the **Story First** prohibition on cost-driven shot reduction or downgrade;
* emitting cost telemetry, events, and metrics for every estimate, reservation, and settlement.

It is **not** responsible for:

* choosing a shot's strategy on creative or adequacy grounds (see **5.2.2**);
* enumerating or defining strategy semantics (see **5.2.1**);
* physically deferring or dispatching work under budget pressure (see **5.4.1**);
* cache-hit detection (see **5.6.3**);
* approving a reduced story plan (that is a human Story Quality Gate decision);
* negotiating or discovering provider prices at runtime (prices are configured, see Configuration Model);
* deciding retry eligibility (see **5.4.5**) — it only attributes retry cost.

---

# Cost Selection Objectives

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## CAS-301 — Adequacy Before Cost

Cost preference applies **only** among strategies that already satisfy the shot's storytelling requirement, as assigned in **5.2.2**.

The platform must never select a cheaper strategy that fails the shot's requirement. Adequacy is a precondition; cost is a preference within it.

---

## CAS-302 — Normative Cost-Priority Order

Where two or more strategies are equally adequate for a shot, the platform shall prefer them in the fixed order:

```text
Cache  <  Animated Still  <  Motion Graphics  <  Runway
```

(cheaper on the left). This order is normative and is the same order used by the queue's cost-rank admission (**5.4.1**) and reverse-order backpressure. It must never be reordered by configuration.

---

## CAS-303 — Estimate Before Dispatch

Every render task shall carry a cost estimate computed **before** any provider work is dispatched.

No provider submission may occur for a task whose cost has not been estimated and reserved. Dispatch-then-price is prohibited.

---

## CAS-304 — Bounded Build Budget

Every build shall have an explicit, finite render budget reserved before rendering begins.

Rendering must never proceed on an unbounded or unset budget. An absent budget fails closed and blocks admission.

---

## CAS-305 — Reservation Governs Admission

Admission of expensive provider work shall respect the build's remaining budget reservation.

The queue defers expensive work when budget headroom is insufficient (**RQ-306**); it never dispatches work it cannot pay for, and never silently drops admissible work.

---

## CAS-306 — Fail Closed on Exhaustion

Budget exhaustion shall route the build to Manual Review with a complete cost account.

Exhaustion must never yield a partial trailer, a silently downgraded shot, or a silently dropped shot. Fail Closed is mandatory (Principle 5).

---

## CAS-307 — Story First (No Cost-Driven Reduction)

The platform shall never reduce the shot count, merge shots, or downgrade a shot's assigned strategy for the sole purpose of lowering cost.

Any such reduction requires explicit **Story Quality Gate** approval, recorded as an auditable decision. Autonomous cost-driven story reduction is prohibited.

---

## CAS-308 — Deterministic Estimation

Given identical inputs — the same shots, strategies, profiles, durations, and configured prices — the cost estimate shall be byte-for-byte reproducible.

Estimation must not depend on wall-clock time, random sampling, or live price discovery. Determinism is required (consistent with the platform determinism constraint).

---

## CAS-309 — Truthful Settlement

Actual settled cost shall be attributed to each render attempt, including retries and failed attempts that consumed provider work.

The build's cost account must reflect real spend, not only estimates. Estimates guard admission; settlements record truth.

---

# Cost Model

The Rendering Platform models render cost as a small set of **cost classes**, one per strategy family. Every strategy defined in **5.2.1** maps to exactly one cost class.

```yaml id="cas-cost-classes"
cost_classes:

  CACHE:
    strategies: [ cached_asset ]
    basis: none                 # reuse of an existing validated asset
    marginal_provider_cost: 0
    compute_cost: negligible
    determinism: exact
    latency_class: instant

  DETERMINISTIC_VECTOR:
    strategies: [ svg, typography ]
    basis: local_render          # rendered locally, no external provider
    marginal_provider_cost: 0
    compute_cost: low
    determinism: exact
    latency_class: fast

  ANIMATED_STILL:
    strategies: [ animated_still ]
    basis: single_generation + light_motion
    marginal_provider_cost: low
    compute_cost: low_medium
    determinism: seeded
    latency_class: medium

  MOTION_GRAPHICS:
    strategies: [ motion_graphics ]
    basis: templated_animation
    marginal_provider_cost: low_medium
    compute_cost: medium
    determinism: template_exact
    latency_class: medium

  GENERATIVE_MOTION:
    strategies: [ runway_video ]
    basis: per_second_generative_video
    marginal_provider_cost: high
    compute_cost: high
    determinism: seeded_approximate
    latency_class: slow
```

Note that `svg` and `typography` are deterministic, locally-rendered, and effectively free of marginal provider cost. For cost-priority purposes they rank alongside or below Animated Still (they are cheaper than any generative strategy) but they are only *eligible* where **5.2.2** assigns them; the cost model never promotes a vector strategy into a shot that requires motion. The four-tier order named in **CAS-302** (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway) is the canonical spine expanded from original topic 5.28; `svg`/`typography` are the near-zero-cost deterministic floor beneath Animated Still.

## Per-Strategy Cost Factors

Each strategy's estimate is computed from a small, configured set of cost factors.

```yaml id="cas-cost-factors"
cost_factor_model:

  cached_asset:
    formula: 0                              # reuse only
    factors: []

  svg:
    formula: fixed_local_unit
    factors: [ complexity_tier ]

  typography:
    formula: fixed_local_unit
    factors: [ complexity_tier ]

  animated_still:
    formula: base_generation + motion_pass
    factors: [ resolution_tier, motion_seconds ]

  motion_graphics:
    formula: template_base * duration_seconds
    factors: [ template_class, duration_seconds ]

  runway_video:
    formula: per_second_rate * duration_seconds * profile_multiplier
    factors: [ duration_seconds, render_profile, resolution_tier ]
```

The `runway_video` estimate is the dominant term in almost every build. It is the only strategy whose cost scales linearly and steeply with shot duration, which is precisely why the cost-priority order and the **RQ-306** budget reservation focus their pressure on it.

## Cost Ordering as a Partial Order

The cost model induces a strict, total order over cost classes for tie-breaking:

```text
CACHE  <  DETERMINISTIC_VECTOR  <  ANIMATED_STILL  <  MOTION_GRAPHICS  <  GENERATIVE_MOTION
```

This order is **fixed and normative**. It must never be reordered by configuration. Prices within a class are configurable; the *ranking* of classes is not. This guarantees that the cost-rank used by the queue's deterministic ordering (**5.4.1**) is stable across environments and builds.

---

# Cost-Priority Order (Normative)

The normative cost-priority order, expanded directly from original topic 5.28, is:

```text
Cache
  ↓   cheapest — reuse of an already-validated asset, zero provider cost
Animated Still
  ↓   single generation plus light motion, low provider cost
Motion Graphics
  ↓   templated animation, medium cost, fully deterministic
Runway
      generative per-second video, highest cost
```

> **Runway is the most expensive rendering resource and shall be used only when genuine motion contributes to storytelling.**

This sentence is preserved verbatim in intent from the original specification and is elevated here to a normative rule. Runway (`runway_video`) is admitted only where **5.2.2** has assigned it because the shot genuinely requires generative motion — human facial emotion, walking, a camera push, or equivalent motion that carries narrative meaning. Runway must never be selected as a default, a convenience, or an aesthetic upgrade over an adequate cheaper strategy.

## How the Order Is Applied

The cost-priority order is applied in exactly two places, and nowhere else:

1. **As a tie-breaker within 5.2.2.** When the Rendering Decision Matrix finds that more than one strategy is adequate for a shot, it selects the cheapest adequate strategy using this order. This is a *preference*, applied only after adequacy is established (**CAS-301**).

2. **As the queue's cost-rank and reverse-backpressure order (5.4.1).** The queue admits cheaper strategies first within a budget window and, under budget pressure, pauses the most expensive class first (Runway), then progressively cheaper classes, in reverse of this order. Cheap deterministic work (Cache, SVG, typography) continues longest.

The order is **never** applied as a reason to override a shot's assigned strategy after the fact. A shot that **5.2.2** assigned to Runway on genuine-motion grounds stays on Runway unless a human, through the Story Quality Gate, decides otherwise.

## What the Order Does Not Authorize

The cost-priority order explicitly does **not** authorize any of the following:

| Prohibited cost-driven action | Why prohibited | Correct path |
| ----------------------------- | -------------- | ------------ |
| Downgrade a Runway shot to Animated Still to save money | Violates adequacy (CAS-301) and Story First (CAS-307) | Story Quality Gate |
| Drop a shot because the budget is tight | Silently reduces the story | Manual Review (Fail Closed) |
| Merge two shots into one to halve generative seconds | Alters approved pacing | Story Quality Gate |
| Shorten a shot's duration to reduce per-second cost | Alters approved timing | Story Quality Gate |
| Prefer a lower-quality render profile to fit budget | Trades quality for cost | Story Quality Gate |

Each prohibited action is a *story* change dressed as a *cost* change. The platform treats it as a story change and refuses to make it autonomously.

---

# Story First — The Cost/Quality Boundary

This is the load-bearing rule of the section. It is stated once, precisely, and enforced by invariant.

> **Cost may never override story quality. Reducing shots, merging shots, shortening shots, downgrading a shot's strategy, or lowering a render profile — for the purpose of saving cost — is prohibited unless the Story Quality Gate explicitly approves it.**

The platform distinguishes two categories of decision:

* **Cost decisions** (autonomous): choosing the cheapest *adequate* strategy for a shot; ordering admission by cost rank; deferring expensive work under budget pressure; estimating and reserving budget; settling actual spend. These are all within-adequacy and touch no story.

* **Story decisions** (never autonomous): changing how many shots exist, how long they are, what they depict, or how well they are rendered. These are creative decisions. The Rendering Platform is an execution layer and has no authority to make them.

When budget pressure would force a story decision, the platform does not make it. It stops and escalates:

```text
Budget cannot pay for the approved plan
        │
        ▼
Cost-Aware Strategy Selection detects insufficiency
        │
        ├── does NOT drop shots
        ├── does NOT downgrade strategies
        ├── does NOT shorten shots
        │
        ▼
Route build → Manual Review  (Fail Closed, Principle 5)
        │
        ▼
Story Quality Gate (human decision):
        ├── raise the render budget, OR
        ├── re-plan the story upstream, OR
        └── approve a documented reduction (recorded, auditable)
```

The Story Quality Gate is the *only* authority that may approve a cost-driven reduction, and its approval is always an explicit, recorded, auditable decision — never an inference, never a silent default.

---

# Cost Estimation Schema

Every build produces a deterministic cost projection before rendering begins. The estimate is computed per shot, aggregated per strategy class, and rolled up to a build total.

```yaml id="cas-estimate"
render_cost_estimate:
  build_id:
  estimate_id:                 # deterministic: sha256(build_id + plan_hash + price_table_version)
  plan_hash:                   # hash of the approved shot list + strategy assignment
  price_table_version:         # which configured price table was used
  currency: USD

  per_shot:
    - shot_id:
      strategy:                # runway_video | animated_still | motion_graphics | typography | svg | cached_asset
      cost_class:              # CACHE | DETERMINISTIC_VECTOR | ANIMATED_STILL | MOTION_GRAPHICS | GENERATIVE_MOTION
      render_profile:          # runway_preview | runway_final | ...
      cache_hit_expected:      # true → estimated cost 0 (see 5.6.3)
      duration_seconds:
      resolution_tier:
      estimated_units:         # provider-billable units (e.g. generative seconds)
      estimated_cost:          # units * configured price
      estimate_basis:          # formula id from cas-cost-factors

  per_class_subtotal:
    CACHE:
    DETERMINISTIC_VECTOR:
    ANIMATED_STILL:
    MOTION_GRAPHICS:
    GENERATIVE_MOTION:

  build_estimate_total:
  retry_reserve:               # provisioned headroom for authorized retries
  estimate_total_with_reserve: # build_estimate_total + retry_reserve
  computed_at:
```

## Estimation Rules

* The estimate is computed **once per plan** and re-computed only when the `plan_hash` or `price_table_version` changes. A change in either invalidates the prior `estimate_id`.
* Cache-eligible shots (`cache_hit_expected: true`) contribute **zero** estimated provider cost. Cache elimination precedes costing, mirroring the queue's cache-bypass rule in **5.4.1**.
* The `retry_reserve` provisions headroom for the maximum authorized retries (`max_retry: 3`, from **5.4.5**), so that a build does not exhaust its budget on first-attempt work and then fail closed on a routine retry. The reserve is a fraction of generative-class subtotal, configured, never unbounded.
* Estimation is **deterministic** (**CAS-308**): it reads only the approved plan and a versioned, configured price table. It never calls a provider to discover a live price.
* The estimate is durably recorded and referenced by the budget reservation below.

---

# Per-Build Render Budget

Every build reserves a finite render budget before any render task is admitted.

```yaml id="cas-budget"
render_budget:
  build_id:
  budget_id:
  budget_total:                # hard ceiling for this build's render spend
  currency: USD
  source: config | override    # how the ceiling was set
  reserved_at:

  reservation:
    estimate_total_with_reserve:  # from cas-estimate
    reserved_amount:              # amount fenced for this build
    headroom:                     # budget_total - reserved_amount
    status: reserved | admitting | exhausted | settled

  draw_down:
    committed:                    # sum of estimated cost of admitted tasks
    settled:                      # sum of actual settled cost (CAS-309)
    outstanding:                  # committed - settled (in-flight)
    remaining:                    # budget_total - committed

  thresholds:
    pause_expensive_at: 0.90      # fraction of budget_total; aligns with 5.4.1 backpressure
    manual_review_at: 1.00        # exhaustion → Fail Closed
```

## Budget Lifecycle

```text
reserved   → budget fenced; estimate computed; no dispatch yet
   │
   ▼
admitting  → tasks admitted within headroom; committed draws down remaining
   │
   ├── committed < pause_expensive_at * total  → normal admission
   │
   ├── committed ≥ pause_expensive_at * total  → pause expensive (Runway) first
   │                                             (queue backpressure, reverse cost order)
   │
   ▼
exhausted  → committed ≥ manual_review_at * total  → route to Manual Review (Fail Closed)
   │
   ▼
settled    → all attempts settled; actual spend recorded; account closed
```

## Budget Rules

* A build with **no** reserved budget must not be admitted. An unset or unbounded budget fails closed (**CAS-304**) and blocks admission — the queue's `budget_reservation_available` admission precondition (**5.4.1**) evaluates false.
* The budget is a **ceiling on spend**, not a target. Coming in under budget is normal and desirable; it is never a reason to add generative work the story did not require.
* At `pause_expensive_at` (default 0.90 of `budget_total`), the queue pauses admission of the most expensive class first (Runway), then progressively cheaper classes, in reverse of the cost-priority order (**5.4.1** backpressure). Deterministic vector and cache work continue longest.
* At `manual_review_at` (1.00), the budget is **exhausted**: the build routes to Manual Review. The platform does not spend past the ceiling and does not trim the plan to fit (**CAS-306**, **CAS-307**).
* `settled` draw-down reflects actual provider spend, including retries and failed attempts that consumed provider work (**CAS-309**). Settlement may exceed the estimate for a given task; when cumulative settlement approaches the ceiling, the same exhaustion path applies.

---

# Interaction with Queue Admission (RQ-306)

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection produces the budget-reservation context that the Render Queue Manager consumes at admission. The division of responsibility is strict.

| Concern | Owner | Reference |
| ------- | ----- | --------- |
| Compute cost estimate | This section (5.2.3) | `cas-estimate` |
| Reserve per-build budget | This section (5.2.3) | `cas-budget` |
| Provide remaining-budget signal | This section (5.2.3) | `draw_down.remaining` |
| Decide whether a task fits headroom at admission | Queue (5.4.1) | RQ-306 |
| Physically defer expensive work | Queue (5.4.1) | backpressure |
| Route exhausted build to Manual Review | Queue (5.4.1) + this section | RQ-306 / CAS-306 |

The queue's admission precondition `budget_reservation_available` (defined in **5.4.1**) is satisfied **only** when this section reports sufficient headroom for the task's estimated cost. The flow is:

```text
Task ready (strategy assigned by 5.2.2, cost estimated by 5.2.3)
        │
        ▼
Queue admission (5.4.1) evaluates budget_reservation_available
        │
        ├── remaining ≥ task.estimated_cost  → admit; commit draw-down
        │
        ├── remaining < task.estimated_cost  → defer (RQ-306), do not drop
        │        └── expensive class paused first (reverse cost order)
        │
        └── budget exhausted (committed ≥ ceiling) → Manual Review (Fail Closed)
```

Deferral is not failure. A task deferred for budget is held, not dropped — consistent with the queue's rule that backpressure defers and never fails admissible work (**5.4.1**). Only genuine exhaustion (the whole build cannot complete within its ceiling) escalates to Manual Review.

The commit/settle split matters for correctness: admission **commits** the estimated cost (reducing `remaining`), while completion **settles** the actual cost. If an attempt is cheaper than estimated, headroom is released back; if it is more expensive (or a retry consumed additional provider work), the additional draw-down is recorded and may push the build toward the exhaustion threshold.

---

# Retry & Failed-Attempt Cost Attribution

Retries and failed attempts that consumed provider work carry real cost and must be accounted for (**CAS-309**).

```yaml id="cas-attribution"
cost_attribution:
  render_task_id:
  attempt:                      # 1..max_retry (max_retry = 3, from 5.4.5)
  outcome: completed | failed | cancelled
  provider_work_consumed:       # true if the provider billed for this attempt
  estimated_cost:
  settled_cost:
  variance:                     # settled_cost - estimated_cost
  attributed_to:
    build_id:
    shot_id:
    strategy:
```

Attribution rules:

* Every attempt that consumed provider work draws down the budget, whether or not it produced a valid asset. A failed Runway generation that the provider billed for is real spend.
* The `retry_reserve` in the estimate (`cas-estimate`) provisions headroom for authorized retries so a routine retry does not itself trigger exhaustion. Retries beyond `max_retry` do not occur (**5.4.5**), so the reserve is bounded.
* This section only **attributes** retry cost. Whether a failure is retryable at all is owned by **5.4.5**; this section never authorizes a retry, it only reserves and settles its cost.
* Cancelled attempts (build/scene/shot cancellation via the Control Queue, **5.4.1**) settle only the provider work actually consumed before the cancel took effect.

---

# Budget Exhaustion → Manual Review (Fail Closed)

Budget exhaustion is a terminal budget condition, not an error to be worked around. Its handling is the concrete expression of Fail Closed (Principle 5) at the cost layer.

A build is **exhausted** when the committed draw-down reaches the ceiling (`manual_review_at`, default 1.00) and unrendered, non-cache, story-required shots remain.

On exhaustion the platform shall:

```yaml id="cas-exhaustion"
on_budget_exhaustion:
  stop_admitting_new_expensive_work: true
  do_not_drop_shots: true              # CAS-307
  do_not_downgrade_strategies: true    # CAS-307
  do_not_shorten_shots: true           # CAS-307
  preserve_completed_assets: true      # already-rendered work is retained
  route_build: MANUAL_REVIEW           # via 5.4.1 Manual Review Queue
  attach_cost_account:
    budget_total:
    committed:
    settled:
    remaining:
    unrendered_shots: [ shot_id, ... ]
    unrendered_estimated_cost:
    reason: BUDGET_EXHAUSTED
  emit_event: render_budget_exhausted
```

The Manual Review payload carries a **complete cost account**: what the ceiling was, what was spent, what remains, which shots are unrendered, and what completing them would cost. This is what the Story Quality Gate needs to decide between raising the budget, re-planning the story, or approving a documented reduction. The platform presents the facts; the human makes the story decision.

A partial trailer is **never** produced from an exhausted build. Consistent with **5.4.1** invariant RQM-310 and Principle 5, budget exhaustion routes to Manual Review; it never yields a partial published trailer.

---

# Events

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection emits events for every material cost transition.

```yaml id="cas-events"
cost_selection_events:
  - render_cost_estimated           # per-build estimate computed
  - render_budget_reserved          # ceiling fenced for a build
  - render_cost_committed           # task admitted; estimated cost drawn down
  - render_cost_settled             # attempt completed; actual cost recorded
  - render_cost_variance_recorded   # settled differs from estimated
  - render_expensive_work_paused    # pause_expensive_at threshold reached
  - render_budget_exhausted         # ceiling reached → Manual Review
  - story_first_block               # a cost-driven downgrade/drop was refused
  - story_quality_gate_reduction_approved  # human-approved documented reduction
```

Every event references the `build_id`, and where applicable the `shot_id`, `render_task_id`, `attempt`, `strategy`, and `cost_class`. Events are durably recorded for audit, cost reconciliation, and replay. The `story_first_block` event in particular is an audit artifact: it records that the platform declined to make a cost-driven story change, which is exactly the behaviour **CAS-307** mandates.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards and the cost SLO review shall report, at minimum:

```yaml id="cas-metrics"
cost_selection_metrics:
  build_estimated_cost
  build_committed_cost
  build_settled_cost
  build_cost_variance                # settled - estimated
  budget_utilization                 # committed / budget_total
  headroom_remaining
  cost_by_class:                      # spend attributed per cost class
    CACHE
    DETERMINISTIC_VECTOR
    ANIMATED_STILL
    MOTION_GRAPHICS
    GENERATIVE_MOTION
  runway_cost_share                   # generative spend / total spend
  cache_avoided_cost                  # estimated cost avoided by cache hits
  expensive_work_pause_count
  budget_exhaustion_count
  story_first_block_count
  retry_attributed_cost
  cost_per_completed_shot
```

`cache_avoided_cost` and `runway_cost_share` are the two headline signals of cost health. A high `cache_avoided_cost` (backed by the cache hit-rate SLO of ≥ 70% from **5.4.1**) and a `runway_cost_share` bounded to genuinely motion-bearing shots together demonstrate that the cost-priority order is working as designed. A rising `story_first_block_count` or `budget_exhaustion_count` signals that budgets are set below the cost of the approved stories — a planning problem to be solved upstream, never by silently trimming shots.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

Cost-Aware Strategy Selection operates under least privilege and touches no secrets.

Required controls:

* the configured price table is versioned and integrity-checked; a build records which `price_table_version` it costed against;
* budget ceilings and overrides are authorized and audited; an override that raises a build's ceiling is an auditable action attributable to a principal;
* cost estimates, reservations, and settlements are scoped to the build namespace, with isolation between concurrent builds;
* no provider API secrets appear in estimates, budgets, or cost telemetry — cost records carry references and amounts only;
* cost telemetry is tamper-evident and durably recorded for reconciliation;
* the `story_quality_gate_reduction_approved` event is attributable to the human principal who approved it.

Cost records are financial evidence. They must be as durable and as tamper-evident as the Dead Letter records in **5.4.1**.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="cas-config"
cost_aware_selection:

  price_table:
    version: "2026.07"
    currency: USD
    runway_video:
      per_second_rate: { runway_preview: <n>, runway_final: <n> }
      profile_multiplier: { runway_preview: 1.0, runway_final: <n> }
    animated_still:
      base_generation: <n>
      motion_pass: <n>
    motion_graphics:
      template_base: <n>
    svg:        { fixed_local_unit: <n> }
    typography: { fixed_local_unit: <n> }
    cached_asset: 0                       # always zero; not overridable above 0

  cost_priority_order:                    # FIXED — informational only, NOT reorderable
    - CACHE
    - DETERMINISTIC_VECTOR
    - ANIMATED_STILL
    - MOTION_GRAPHICS
    - GENERATIVE_MOTION

  budget:
    default_budget_total: <n>
    require_explicit_budget: true         # unset budget fails closed
    retry_reserve_fraction: 0.15          # headroom for authorized retries
    pause_expensive_at: 0.90              # aligns with 5.4.1 backpressure
    manual_review_at: 1.00                # exhaustion threshold

  story_first:
    autonomous_shot_reduction: prohibited # never true
    autonomous_strategy_downgrade: prohibited
    reduction_requires: story_quality_gate_approval
```

All prices and budgets are configurable and environment-overridable. The **cost-priority order is not configurable** — it is fixed and normative (**CAS-302**), listed here for reference only. `cached_asset` cost is always zero and cannot be configured above zero. `require_explicit_budget` and the `story_first` prohibitions cannot be relaxed by configuration. Configuration is validated at startup; invalid configuration (missing price table version, unset required budget, attempt to reorder the cost priority, attempt to enable autonomous reduction) **fails closed** and blocks admission rather than proceeding with unsafe defaults.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* deterministic `estimate_id` generation from `plan_hash` + `price_table_version`;
* per-strategy cost formula correctness against the configured price table;
* cache-eligible shots contribute zero estimated cost;
* cost-class ordering is fixed and not affected by configuration order;
* cheapest-adequate tie-break selects the correct strategy given multiple adequate options;
* `retry_reserve` computed from `max_retry` and configured fraction;
* budget reservation, commit, settle, and headroom arithmetic;
* variance computation (settled − estimated);
* unset/unbounded budget fails closed.

## Integration Tests

* estimate → reserve → admit within headroom, with commit draw-down (with **5.4.1**);
* deferral of expensive work when headroom is insufficient (RQ-306), without dropping the task;
* pause of Runway class first at `pause_expensive_at`, cheaper classes continuing;
* settlement releasing headroom when actual < estimated;
* additional draw-down when retry consumes provider work;
* build completing under budget with correct final cost account.

## Concurrency Tests

* concurrent commits from multiple admitted tasks against one budget;
* settle concurrent with a new admission's commit;
* two builds' budgets isolated under concurrent draw-down;
* exhaustion detected atomically when concurrent commits cross the ceiling.

## Failure Injection Tests

* provider bills for a failed attempt → settled cost still attributed;
* price table version mismatch between estimate and settlement → flagged, not silently reconciled;
* budget override raising the ceiling mid-build → audited, admission resumes;
* attempt to configure a reordered cost-priority order → startup fails closed;
* attempt to enable autonomous shot reduction → configuration rejected.

## Story First Tests

* budget too small for the approved plan → build routes to Manual Review with full cost account, no shots dropped;
* verify no code path downgrades an assigned strategy for cost;
* verify no code path shortens or merges shots for cost;
* `story_first_block` event emitted when a cost-driven reduction is attempted and refused;
* Story Quality Gate approval required and recorded before any reduction is applied.

## Recovery Tests

* reconstruct the build cost account (committed/settled/remaining) from recorded events;
* re-derive the estimate from `plan_hash` + `price_table_version` and confirm it matches the stored estimate;
* recover reservation status after restart mid-build;
* reconcile in-flight (`outstanding`) draw-down after a worker loss.

---

# Cost Selection Quality Gate

```yaml id="cas-quality-gate"
cost_selection_quality_gate:
  every_task_has_cost_estimate: true
  estimate_is_deterministic: true
  every_build_has_reserved_budget: true
  budget_never_unbounded: true
  cost_priority_order_is_fixed: true
  cheapest_adequate_selected_within_5_2_2: true
  runway_only_for_genuine_motion: true
  cache_eligible_costed_zero: true
  admission_respects_headroom: true          # RQ-306
  expensive_work_paused_before_cheap: true
  no_autonomous_shot_reduction: true         # CAS-307
  no_autonomous_strategy_downgrade: true     # CAS-307
  budget_exhaustion_routes_manual_review: true   # CAS-306, Fail Closed
  no_partial_trailer_on_exhaustion: true
  actual_cost_settled_and_attributed: true   # CAS-309
  retry_cost_attributed: true
  story_quality_gate_required_for_reduction: true
  cost_telemetry_emitted_and_durable: true
```

Every field must hold for the section's implementation to be accepted.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant CAS-401

Cost preference is applied only among strategies already adequate per **5.2.2**; a cheaper inadequate strategy is never selected.

## Invariant CAS-402

The cost-priority order (Cache < Animated Still < Motion Graphics < Runway) is fixed and normative and is never reordered by configuration.

## Invariant CAS-403

Runway (`runway_video`) is admitted only where **5.2.2** assigned it on genuine-motion grounds; it is never chosen as a cheaper-path default or an aesthetic upgrade.

## Invariant CAS-404

Every render task carries a deterministic cost estimate computed before any provider work is dispatched.

## Invariant CAS-405

Every build has a finite, explicitly reserved render budget; rendering never proceeds on an unset or unbounded budget.

## Invariant CAS-406

Admission of expensive work respects the build's remaining budget headroom; work that does not fit is deferred, never silently dropped (RQ-306).

## Invariant CAS-407

Budget exhaustion routes the build to Manual Review with a complete cost account; it never yields a partial trailer, a downgraded shot, or a dropped shot.

## Invariant CAS-408

The platform never reduces, merges, shortens, or downgrades shots for cost; any such reduction requires explicit, recorded Story Quality Gate approval.

## Invariant CAS-409

Actual settled cost — including retries and provider-billed failed attempts — is attributed to each render attempt and drawn down against the build budget.

## Invariant CAS-410

Cache-eligible shots contribute zero estimated and settled provider cost; cache elimination precedes costing.

## Invariant CAS-411

Cost estimation is deterministic: identical plan and configured price table yield a byte-for-byte identical estimate; no live price discovery or random sampling is used.

## Invariant CAS-412

Cost never reaches back up the pipeline to alter an assigned strategy except through the human Story Quality Gate escalation path.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                     |
| ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-439 | Cost optimization is expressed as a preference among adequate strategies only; adequacy (5.2.2) is always evaluated first.  |
| ADR-440 | The cost-priority order Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway is fixed and normative and is never configurable.  |
| ADR-441 | Every render task is priced by a deterministic, versioned cost model before dispatch; live price discovery is prohibited.    |
| ADR-442 | Every build reserves a finite render budget; an unset or unbounded budget fails closed and blocks admission.                 |
| ADR-443 | Budget reservation governs queue admission via RQ-306; insufficient headroom defers expensive work, it never drops it.       |
| ADR-444 | Budget exhaustion escalates the build to Manual Review with a full cost account (Fail Closed); no partial trailer is produced.|
| ADR-445 | Reducing, merging, shortening, or downgrading shots for cost is prohibited without explicit, recorded Story Quality Gate approval. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.2.3** is complete when:

* Cost-Aware Strategy Selection is formally separated from strategy enumeration (**5.2.1**) and strategy assignment (**5.2.2**), consuming their outputs and adding cost only.
* A per-strategy cost model and cost-class taxonomy map every strategy to exactly one cost class with a deterministic cost formula.
* The cost-priority order (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway) is specified as fixed and normative, applied only as a tie-break among adequate strategies and as the queue's cost-rank and reverse-backpressure order.
* Runway is restricted to shots that genuinely require generative motion, never selected as a cheaper-path default or aesthetic upgrade.
* A deterministic cost estimation schema prices each shot and build before any dispatch, with cache-eligible shots costed at zero.
* A per-build render budget is reserved, drawn down (commit/settle), and enforced, with an explicit exhaustion threshold and a pause-expensive threshold aligned to **5.4.1** backpressure.
* Budget reservation is shown to govern queue admission through **RQ-306**, deferring rather than dropping expensive work that does not fit headroom.
* Budget exhaustion routes the build to Manual Review with a complete cost account and never produces a partial trailer, a downgraded shot, or a dropped shot (Fail Closed, Principle 5).
* The **Story First** rule is enforced by invariant: no autonomous cost-driven reduction, merge, shortening, or downgrade of shots, and any such reduction requires explicit, recorded Story Quality Gate approval.
* Actual settled cost, including retries and provider-billed failed attempts, is attributed to each attempt and reconciled against the budget.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a deterministic, budget-bounded, Story-First cost-aware selection subsystem without ever letting cost override story quality.


---

# Part 5.2.4 — Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.3

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section closes **Group 5.2 — Render Strategy & Decisioning** and defines the formal handoff into **Group 5.3 — Provider Abstraction & Runway Integration**.

Group 5.2 established *how every approved shot is rendered* — the strategy model, the decision matrix that maps shot intent to a strategy class, and the cost-aware selection policy that chooses the cheapest strategy that still serves the story. It deliberately specified **no** execution mechanism — no provider API, no submission protocol, no Runway adapter, no queue. Those belong to 5.3 and 5.4.

This section performs four jobs:

* it **summarises** what sections 5.2.1 through 5.2.3 collectively established;
* it **consolidates** the strategy invariants of the decisioning layer into a single normative reference — one strategy per shot, deterministic decision, the cost-priority order, story-first-over-cost, and a provider-independent decision surface;
* it **defines the interface** — the exact per-shot strategy-plus-provider assignment, the guarantees, and the obligations that Group 5.2 hands to Group 5.3;
* it **states the acceptance criteria** under which Group 5.2 is considered complete and the decisioning layer is frozen for downstream execution.

This section is a **synthesis and interface contract**. It introduces no new decision rule. Where it appears to state a rule, that rule is a consolidation of a rule already established in 5.2.1–5.2.3, restated here so that 5.3 and every later group can reference one authoritative decisioning boundary.

Group 5.2 is the **decisioning layer**. It sits inside the frozen frame handed down by Group 5.1 (see **5.1.6**) and hands a settled per-shot execution plan to Group 5.3. Nothing downstream of this interface may re-open a strategy decision.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* a consolidated summary of the strategy artifacts and rules established by 5.2.1–5.2.3;
* the strategy invariants of the decisioning layer, restated as one normative set (STRAT-1..5);
* the **5.2 → 5.3 interface contract**: the per-shot strategy-plus-provider assignment and its guarantees;
* the obligations 5.3 inherits and must never violate;
* the pre-conditions 5.3 may safely assume as already decided;
* the assumptions 5.3 must **not** make;
* the compliance checklist for Group 5.2;
* the Group 5.2 quality gate;
* the invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria that freeze the decisioning layer.

This section does **not** specify:

* the render strategy model or the six strategy classes (defined in **5.2.1**);
* the rendering decision matrix (defined in **5.2.2**);
* cost-aware strategy selection and budget arbitration (defined in **5.2.3**);
* provider independence and the provider boundary (defined in **5.3.1**);
* the provider interface contract (defined in **5.3.2**);
* task submission and the Runway adapter (defined in **5.3.3** and **5.3.4**);
* execution, queueing, retry, or timeout mechanics (defined in **5.4**).

This section consumes the outputs of 5.2.1–5.2.3; it re-derives nothing and contradicts nothing. Where a decision was fixed upstream, this section cites it rather than restating a variant of it. It also inherits the foundational invariants FOUND-1..5 from **5.1.6** and never weakens them.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the decisioning handoff.

> **Group 5.2 decides how every shot is rendered; Group 5.3 decides only who renders it — never the reverse.**

Strategy is settled before any provider is contacted. A provider is an execution backend that carries out an assignment it did not make and may not change. 5.3 resolves *which live provider instance* fulfils a strategy class and *how the API is spoken*; it never re-selects the strategy, re-orders cost, or re-judges whether motion serves the story.

A corollary follows directly:

> **A provider that alters the chosen strategy is not an adapter — it is a defect.**

The provider boundary is one-directional. If executing a shot would require substituting a different strategy, upgrading or downgrading the render profile for cost reasons, merging shots, or re-running the cost decision, the action is rejected at the boundary — not accommodated. Providers only render (consistent with **5.9** of the original specification and **5.3.1**); they never make or unmake a decision that 5.2 has already frozen.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        GROUP 5.2 — Render Strategy & Decisioning
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.2.1  Render Strategy Model                  │
        │  5.2.2  Rendering Decision Matrix              │
        │  5.2.3  Cost-Aware Strategy Selection          │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                 ┌─────────────────────┐
                 │   5.2.4  INTERFACE   │   ◄── this section
                 │  (settled plan)      │
                 └─────────────────────┘
                            │
          per-shot strategy + provider assignment
                   + guarantees
                            │
                            ▼
        GROUP 5.3 — Provider Abstraction & Runway Integration
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.3.1  Provider Independence & Boundary       │
        │  5.3.2  Provider Interface Contract            │
        │  5.3.3  Task Submission Protocol               │
        │  5.3.4  Runway Provider Adapter                │
        │  5.3.5  Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.4  │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Group 5.2 is upstream of every provider contact. The interface defined here is the sole channel through which strategy decisions reach the execution layer. 5.3 receives a **settled plan**; it executes the plan, it does not re-decide it.

---

# Summary of Group 5.2

Group 5.2 established the following, section by section. Each item is authoritative in its origin section; this summary is a consolidated index, not a restatement.

## From 5.2.1 — Render Strategy Model

* Every shot has **exactly one** render strategy; mixed rendering strategies inside one shot are prohibited (original **5.7**).
* The strategy classes are fixed: `runway_video`, `animated_still`, `motion_graphics`, `typography`, `svg`, `cached_asset`.
* A strategy is an **execution class**, not a provider name and not creative content. It names *how* a shot is realised, never *what* is depicted.
* The strategy is written into `render-strategy.json` — the single output surface Group 5.2 is responsible for populating (per the 5.1 → 5.2 handoff in **5.1.6**).

## From 5.2.2 — Rendering Decision Matrix

* Shot intent maps deterministically to a strategy class through a fixed matrix (original **5.8**):

| Shot Type            | Strategy        |
| -------------------- | --------------- |
| Human facial emotion | Runway          |
| Walking              | Runway          |
| Camera push          | Runway          |
| Countdown UI         | Motion Graphics |
| Enterprise dashboard | Animated Still  |
| Architecture diagram | SVG             |
| Title                | Typography      |
| Existing asset       | Cache           |

* Shots requiring **genuine human or camera motion** map to Runway; synthetic, diagrammatic, or textual shots map to deterministic non-Runway strategies; unchanged shots map to Cache.
* The mapping is **deterministic**: identical shot intent yields identical strategy, with no randomness (consistent with the platform determinism requirement).

## From 5.2.3 — Cost-Aware Strategy Selection

* The cost-priority order is fixed: **Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway** (original **5.28**).
* Runway is the most expensive resource and **shall be used only when genuine motion contributes to storytelling**; it is never selected for cost reasons and never avoided when the story requires it.
* Deterministic renders (`svg`, `typography`) and `cached_asset` are the cheapest classes; expensive provider work (`runway_video`) is selected last and only when justified.
* Cost-aware selection chooses the **cheapest strategy that still serves the story**; it never downgrades a story-required strategy to save budget. Budget pressure defers or escalates work — it does not silently substitute a cheaper strategy (fail-closed, FOUND-5).

Together, sections 5.2.1–5.2.3 fix the platform's **strategy vocabulary, decision mapping, and cost policy**. Group 5.3 begins exactly where a settled strategy must be carried out by a provider.

---
