* Submission failure classes are enumerated and mapped to disposition for **5.4.5**, preserving the original retry rules (retry transient; no retry for invalid prompt or unsupported input).
* Rate-limit and concurrency interaction with the Render Queue Manager (RQ-305) treats throttling as deferral, never failure.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gates, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a durable, idempotent, asynchronous task submission protocol that creates exactly one provider job per render task attempt without ever awaiting render completion.


---

# Part 5.3.4 — Runway Provider Adapter

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Runway Provider Adapter** used by the Rendering Platform.

The Runway Provider Adapter is the single concrete implementation that binds the abstract **Provider Interface Contract** (defined in **5.3.2**) to the Runway generative-video service. It is the one and only component in the entire platform that is permitted to know that Runway exists, that Runway speaks a particular HTTP dialect, that Runway returns particular status strings, and that Runway emits particular error codes.

The adapter exists to answer one narrow question, repeatedly and reliably:

* given a fully assembled render request (produced by prompt assembly in **5.5** and dispatched through the submission protocol in **5.3.3**), how is a single shot rendered on Runway, monitored to completion, downloaded, verified for transport integrity, and — when necessary — cancelled?

The Runway Provider Adapter is responsible for:

* task submission to Runway;
* status polling of in-flight Runway jobs;
* downloading completed Runway assets;
* cancellation of Runway jobs;
* bounded, idempotent retry of transient Runway failures;
* translating Runway-specific status and error vocabulary into the canonical vocabulary the rest of the platform understands.

The Runway Provider Adapter is emphatically **not** responsible for deciding *what* to render, *how fast* the trailer should move, *what characters say*, or *how the camera behaves*. Those are creative decisions owned entirely upstream. Runway — and therefore this adapter — acts **only** as a rendering backend.

Rendering is an execution layer. The Runway Provider Adapter is the execution *driver* for exactly one backend within that layer, and it is fully interchangeable with future adapters (Google Veo, Luma, Pika, OpenAI Video, Tencent Hunyuan) behind the same interface.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* how the Runway Provider Adapter realizes each method of the Provider Interface Contract — `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, `cancel()`, `health_check()`;
* the Runway render profiles (`runway_preview`, `runway_final`) and the underlying Runway model profile (`gen4.5`);
* the deterministic mapping from an assembled render request into a Runway submission payload;
* Runway status polling behavior (poll interval, overall timeout, deadline discipline);
* Runway asset download and transport-integrity verification;
* the Runway job status → canonical render state mapping;
* the Runway error → canonical error class mapping consumed by retry classification;
* Runway rate-limit and concurrency compliance at the adapter boundary;
* idempotent submission and safe retry semantics specific to Runway;
* strict containment of all Runway-specific logic inside the adapter;
* adapter events, metrics, security posture, configuration, testing, invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* the abstract Provider Interface Contract itself (defined in **5.3.2**);
* provider independence and the provider boundary rationale (defined in **5.3.1**);
* the generic task-submission protocol and its idempotency envelope (defined in **5.3.3**);
* how render tasks are queued, leased, and dispatched (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the render task state machine and legal transitions (defined in **5.4.3**);
* render manifest ownership and authoritative state (defined in **5.4.4**);
* retry classification, backoff formulas, and attempt accounting (defined in **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline *policy* values as platform contracts (defined in **5.4.6**);
* prompt assembly, character/location/camera injection, and style enforcement (defined in **5.5.1**–**5.5.5**);
* asset technical validation and visual quality validation (defined in **5.6.1** and **5.6.2**).

The adapter **consumes** all of those contracts. It owns only the Runway-specific realization of the provider interface.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the Runway Provider Adapter.

> **Runway renders pixels on request; it never gets a vote on the film.**

The adapter is a translator and a courier. It converts an already-decided render request into the exact bytes Runway expects, carries the result back, and reports what happened in the platform's own words. It introduces no creative intent of its own and lets none leak from Runway into the platform.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Every fact that is true only because the provider is Runway lives inside this adapter and nowhere else.**

Runway endpoint URLs, Runway model identifiers, Runway status enumerations, Runway error codes, Runway payload field names, and Runway authentication headers are all confined to the adapter. If Runway were replaced tomorrow, only files under the Runway adapter would change; the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**), the state machine (**5.4.3**), the manifest (**5.4.4**), retry classification (**5.4.5**), prompt assembly (**5.5**), and validation (**5.6**) would remain byte-for-byte unchanged. That property is the test of whether this section has been implemented correctly.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Prompt Assembly (5.5)
        │
        ▼  assembled render request (no article text)
Task Submission Protocol (5.3.3)
        │
        ▼  submit_request(render_task_id, cache_key, profile, idempotency_key)
Provider Interface Contract (5.3.2)
        │
        ▼  dispatch to the adapter bound for provider = "runway"
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Runway Provider Adapter (5.3.4)      │
│                                                │
│   submit()        ── map → POST Runway job     │
│   get_status()    ── GET  → Runway status      │
│   download()      ── GET  → Runway asset bytes │
│   cancel()        ── POST → Runway cancel       │
│   health_check()  ── GET  → Runway reachability │
│                                                │
│   request mapper   status mapper   error mapper │
│   rate governor    retry shim      secret load  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │                         ▲
        ▼                         │
   Runway API (HTTPS)      canonical status / error
        │                         │
        ▼                         │
  Runway asset URL ──► download ──► integrity check
        │
        ▼
  Asset Repository ──► Asset Validation (5.6.1)
```

The Runway Provider Adapter sits **below** the Provider Interface Contract and **above** the Runway API. Nothing above the interface line may reach around the adapter to touch Runway directly. Nothing below the interface line (Runway's own vocabulary) may pass upward except through the adapter's mappers.

The adapter is invoked by provider workers that have already leased a render task from the queue (**5.4.1**). It is never invoked with raw article text, storyboards, or screenplays; it receives only the assembled render request and the references needed to resolve secrets and destinations.

---

# Responsibilities

The Runway Provider Adapter is responsible for:

* implementing every method of the Provider Interface Contract for `provider = "runway"`;
* mapping an assembled render request into a valid Runway submission payload;
* selecting the correct Runway model and quality settings from the render profile;
* issuing idempotent submissions so a re-delivered task never double-charges Runway;
* polling Runway job status on the configured interval until a terminal outcome or the overall deadline;
* downloading the completed Runway asset and verifying transport integrity (size, checksum, decodability handshake);
* cancelling in-flight Runway jobs on request, best-effort, and reporting the observed outcome;
* probing Runway reachability and readiness for `health_check()`;
* translating Runway status strings into canonical render states;
* translating Runway error responses into canonical error classes for retry classification;
* honoring Runway rate limits and concurrency ceilings before every call;
* emitting adapter-scoped events and metrics for observability;
* resolving Runway credentials from the secret store at call time and never persisting them.

It is **not** responsible for:

* generating, altering, or reinterpreting story, screenplay, pacing, dialogue, or camera language (prohibited — see **5.3.1** and the boundary below);
* choosing which render strategy or provider a shot uses (see **5.2**);
* assembling or editing prompts (see **5.5**);
* deciding whether a failed render should be retried (classification is owned by **5.4.5**);
* owning render-task state (the Render Manifest is authoritative — see **5.4.4**);
* enqueuing, leasing, or ordering work (see **5.4.1**);
* judging visual quality or technical validity of the downloaded asset (see **5.6.1** and **5.6.2**);
* composing or publishing the trailer.

The adapter performs I/O and translation. Every decision of consequence is made elsewhere and handed to it.

---

# The Runway Non-Authority Boundary

Because Runway is a generative model capable of far more than the platform asks of it, the boundary must be stated as a hard prohibition, not a convention. This restates and specializes the provider boundary of **5.3.1**.

Runway — and therefore this adapter — **must never**:

* generate stories, narrative beats, or scene ordering;
* determine pacing, shot duration intent, or edit rhythm;
* create, rewrite, or infer dialogue, captions, or on-screen text content;
* design, invent, or override camera language (lens, movement, angle, framing);
* introduce characters, locations, or props not present in the assembled request;
* alter visual style away from the enforced style contract (see **5.5.5**);
* substitute its own creative "improvement" of an approved shot.

The adapter enforces this structurally: the only creative information Runway ever receives is the assembled prompt produced by **5.5**, transmitted verbatim through the request mapper. The adapter has no code path that generates, edits, extends, or "enhances" prompt content. Any Runway feature that would let the model act autonomously beyond a single deterministic text/image-conditioned render is not wired into the adapter.

If a future Runway API surface tempts callers to delegate creative decisions to the model, that surface is out of scope for this adapter and its use is prohibited (see Invariant **RWA-401**).

---

# Adapter Design Objectives

The Runway Provider Adapter shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RWA-301 — Faithful Interface Realization

The adapter shall implement `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, `cancel()`, and `health_check()` exactly as specified by the Provider Interface Contract in **5.3.2**, with identical signatures, return shapes, and error semantics. Callers must be unable to tell Runway apart from any other conformant provider by inspecting the interface.

---

## RWA-302 — Total Runway Containment

All Runway-specific knowledge — endpoints, model IDs, payload fields, status strings, error codes, headers, quirks — shall reside within the adapter. No such knowledge may appear in any module above the interface line. A grep for the string `runway` outside the adapter package must return only configuration keys and the provider identifier, never Runway API semantics.

---

## RWA-303 — Deterministic Request Mapping

Given an identical assembled render request and render profile, the adapter shall produce a byte-identical Runway submission payload (modulo the idempotency key and timestamp). Mapping is a pure function of its inputs; it introduces no randomness, no time-of-day behavior, and no hidden creative transformation (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism).

---

## RWA-304 — Idempotent Submission

A re-delivered render task (at-least-once transport, **5.4.1**) must never cause a second Runway job to be created for the same attempt. The adapter shall key submission idempotency on the render `cache_key` and attempt, so that re-submission returns the existing Runway job reference rather than starting new billable work.

---

## RWA-305 — Rate and Concurrency Compliance

The adapter shall never issue a Runway API call that would violate the configured Runway rate limits or concurrency ceilings (RQ-305, **5.4.1**). Throttle responses are treated as normal flow control and fed back into the adapter's rate governor, not surfaced as render failures.

---

## RWA-306 — Deadline Discipline

Every adapter operation shall be bounded by an explicit deadline derived from the timeout policy in **5.4.6**: submission within `submission_timeout`, each status poll on `poll_interval`, download within `download_timeout`, and the overall render within `overall_timeout`. No adapter call may block indefinitely.

---

## RWA-307 — Canonical Translation

The adapter shall translate every Runway status into a canonical render state and every Runway error into a canonical error class before returning. The rest of the platform must never see a raw Runway status string or error code. Unknown Runway statuses map conservatively (to a running or retryable-unknown outcome), never optimistically to success.

---

## RWA-308 — Transport Integrity, Not Judgment

The adapter shall verify that a downloaded asset arrived intact — expected byte length, checksum match where provided, and a successful decode handshake — but shall make no judgment about technical adequacy or visual quality. A corrupt transfer is an adapter error; an intact-but-poor render is a valid delivery that the validators in **5.6** will judge.

---

## RWA-309 — Credential Hygiene

The adapter shall resolve Runway credentials from the secret store at call time, use them only for the duration of the call, and never write them to logs, events, manifests, queue items, or downloaded asset metadata. Credentials never leave the adapter's execution frame.

---

# Interface Realization Overview

The adapter binds to the Provider Interface Contract of **5.3.2**. The canonical interface is:

```python
class VideoProvider:
    def submit(request: RenderRequest) -> ProviderSubmitResult: ...
    def get_status(job_ref: ProviderJobRef) -> ProviderStatus: ...
    def download(job_ref: ProviderJobRef, dest: AssetDestination) -> DownloadResult: ...
    def cancel(job_ref: ProviderJobRef) -> CancelResult: ...
    def health_check() -> HealthStatus: ...
```

The Runway adapter provides `RunwayProvider`, a concrete `VideoProvider`. The remainder of this section specifies each method's Runway realization. Return types are the canonical types defined in **5.3.2**; the adapter never widens or narrows them.

```python
class RunwayProvider(VideoProvider):
    provider_id = "runway"

    def __init__(self, config: RunwayAdapterConfig,
                 secrets: SecretResolver,
                 clock: Clock,
                 http: HttpClient): ...
```

The adapter is constructed with configuration (endpoints, profiles, limits), a secret resolver, an injectable clock (for deterministic tests and deadline math), and an HTTP client with enforced timeouts. No global state is used; a build may run multiple adapter instances without interference.

---

# Runway Profiles

Render profiles decouple the platform's intent (preview vs final) from Runway's concrete model and quality parameters. Two profiles are defined, mirroring **5.6.4** (Preview) and **5.6.5** (Final).

```yaml id="rwa-profiles"
runway_profiles:

  runway_preview:
    purpose: validate pacing, continuity, framing at low cost
    model_profile: gen4.5
    quality_tier: draft
    resolution: 1280x720
    frame_rate: 24
    max_duration_seconds: 5
    seed_policy: fixed_from_cache_key   # reproducible previews
    upscale: false
    watermark_free: true
    cost_class: preview

  runway_final:
    purpose: publish-grade motion after preview approval
    model_profile: gen4.5
    quality_tier: high
    resolution: 1920x1080
    frame_rate: 24
    max_duration_seconds: 10
    seed_policy: fixed_from_cache_key
    upscale: true
    watermark_free: true
    cost_class: final
```

The underlying **model profile** is Runway's generation model, addressed abstractly as `gen4.5`. The concrete Runway model identifier is resolved from configuration inside the adapter; callers pass only the profile name (`runway_preview` or `runway_final`), never a Runway model string.

Profile rules:

* The profile is chosen upstream (preview vs final build) and arrives in the render request. The adapter selects, it does not decide.
* `seed_policy: fixed_from_cache_key` derives Runway's seed deterministically from the render `cache_key`, so a re-render of an identical shot is reproducible and cache-eligible (see **5.6.3**).
* Quality tier, resolution, and frame rate are provider settings, not creative settings; they never alter story, pacing, dialogue, or camera intent.
* `runway_final` shall be selected only after preview approval, consistent with **5.6.5**; the adapter does not enforce that gate but records the profile used for audit.
* Adding a new Runway model or tier is a configuration change confined to this section's config model — never a change to the interface or to callers.

---

# Runway Request Mapping

The request mapper is the heart of the adapter. It converts a canonical `RenderRequest` into a Runway submission payload. It is a pure, deterministic function (RWA-303).

The canonical input (produced by **5.5** and carried by **5.3.3**) contains:

```yaml id="rwa-render-request"
render_request:
  render_task_id:
  shot_id:
  build_id:
  cache_key:               # idempotency + seed anchor
  attempt:
  render_profile:          # runway_preview | runway_final
  assembled_prompt:        # deterministic text from 5.5.1 — NEVER article text
  conditioning_image_ref:  # optional keyframe/still reference (asset store URI)
  camera_parameters:       # lens, movement, angle — from Shot Planner (5.5.4), read-only
  duration_seconds:        # from storyboard timing, clamped to profile max
  style_tokens:            # enforced style contract (5.5.5)
  negative_style_tokens:   # forbidden style set (5.5.5)
```

The mapper produces the Runway payload:

```json id="rwa-runway-payload"
{
  "model": "<resolved from model_profile>",
  "prompt_text": "<assembled_prompt verbatim>",
  "init_image": "<resolved bytes/URL from conditioning_image_ref, if present>",
  "seed": "<derived from cache_key>",
  "duration": "<min(duration_seconds, profile.max_duration_seconds)>",
  "resolution": "<from profile>",
  "fps": "<from profile>",
  "quality": "<from profile.quality_tier>",
  "negative_prompt": "<from negative_style_tokens>",
  "idempotency_key": "<sha256(cache_key + attempt)>"
}
```

Mapping rules (all normative):

* **Prompt is passed verbatim.** The `assembled_prompt` from **5.5.1** is copied into `prompt_text` unchanged. The adapter shall not append, prepend, edit, summarize, translate, or "improve" it. The prompt already encodes character, location, camera, lighting, blocking, emotion, shot purpose, and visual style; the adapter adds nothing creative.
* **Article text is impossible here.** The request contract carries `assembled_prompt` only. There is no field through which article body, screenplay lines, or story prose could reach Runway. The adapter shall reject a request whose prompt fails the assembled-prompt provenance check from **5.5.1** (fail closed).
* **Camera is read-only.** `camera_parameters` inform the prompt (already baked in upstream) and are echoed into audit, but the adapter shall not translate them into any Runway control that would let the model reinterpret camera language. Runway may not override camera values (consistent with **5.5.4**).
* **Duration is clamped, not invented.** The requested duration is clamped to the profile maximum. The adapter never extends duration or infers pacing.
* **Seed is deterministic.** Derived from `cache_key`, guaranteeing reproducibility and cache alignment.
* **Idempotency key is deterministic.** `sha256(cache_key + attempt)` (see RWA-304).
* **Style constraints are forwarded, not authored.** `negative_style_tokens` (anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms, humanoid robots, exaggerated sci-fi interfaces — from **5.5.5**) are forwarded to Runway's negative-prompt channel to reinforce the enforced style, but authorship of the style contract remains upstream.

The mapper produces no side effects. It reads the request and returns bytes. All network activity happens in `submit()`.

---

# submit()

`submit()` creates (or idempotently re-attaches to) a Runway job for one shot attempt.

```text
submit(request):

  1. validate request provenance (assembled_prompt from 5.5, profile known)
  2. acquire a rate token from the Runway rate governor (RWA-305)
  3. resolve Runway credentials from the secret store (RWA-309)
  4. map request → Runway payload (deterministic, RWA-303)
  5. POST job to Runway with idempotency_key, bounded by submission_timeout (30s)
  6. on success:
        parse Runway job id
        return ProviderSubmitResult{ job_ref, provider="runway", accepted_at }
  7. on Runway 4xx (permanent): map → canonical error → return non-retryable failure
  8. on Runway 429 / 5xx / timeout: map → canonical error → return retryable failure
  9. never create a second job for an already-submitted idempotency_key
```

Behavioral requirements:

* **Returns immediately.** `submit()` initiates asynchronous work and returns a `ProviderJobRef`; it never waits for the render to complete (Principle 3 — Asynchronous Rendering; consistent with **5.3.3**).
* **Idempotent (RWA-304).** The `idempotency_key = sha256(cache_key + attempt)` is sent to Runway and also cached adapter-side. If Runway (or the adapter cache) already has a job for that key, `submit()` returns that job's reference and records `idempotent_replay = true`. No second billable job is created even under at-least-once re-delivery.
* **Rate-governed (RWA-305).** A submission consumes a token from the Runway token bucket. If no token is available, submission is not attempted; the caller receives a deferred/retryable signal so the queue can reschedule via delayed visibility. This is flow control, not failure.
* **Deadline-bounded (RWA-306).** The POST is bounded by `submission_timeout: 30s`. A submission that does not confirm within the deadline is treated as a transient failure and mapped to a retryable canonical error; because the idempotency key was already sent, a subsequent retry safely re-attaches rather than duplicating.
* **No state ownership.** `submit()` returns the job reference to the caller, which persists it to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**). The adapter does not own or store render-task state; its idempotency cache is a performance optimization, not a source of truth, and is reconstructable from the manifest.

```yaml id="rwa-submit-result"
ProviderSubmitResult:
  job_ref:
    provider: runway
    provider_job_id:        # Runway's job identifier
    idempotency_key:
  accepted_at:
  idempotent_replay:        # true if re-attached to existing job
```

---

# get_status() and Status Polling

`get_status()` reports the canonical state of a Runway job. It is called by the provider-monitor worker on the queue's delayed-poll cadence (**5.4.1**), not in a busy loop.

```text
get_status(job_ref):

  1. acquire a rate token (status polls are rate-governed too)
  2. resolve credentials
  3. GET Runway job status, bounded by a short poll deadline
  4. map Runway status string → canonical render state (RWA-307)
  5. return ProviderStatus{ canonical_state, progress?, provider_message?, terminal? }
```

Polling discipline (values from the timeout policy, **5.4.6**):

```yaml id="rwa-poll"
runway_polling:
  poll_interval: 20s          # cadence between status checks
  overall_timeout: 45m        # hard deadline for the whole render
  jitter: 0-3s                # deterministic per-task jitter (task_id hash)
  unknown_status: treat_as_running   # conservative; never optimistic
```

Rules:

* **The queue drives cadence.** The adapter exposes `get_status()`; the Render Queue Manager schedules it every `poll_interval` via delayed visibility. The adapter does not spin its own polling loop for monitoring, avoiding synchronized polling storms (jitter is derived deterministically from the task id).
* **Overall deadline (RWA-306).** If a job has not reached a terminal Runway status within `overall_timeout: 45m` from submission, the adapter returns a canonical `TIMED_OUT` outcome. The caller then routes to retry classification (**5.4.5**). The adapter measures elapsed time from the manifest-recorded submission time, not from adapter memory, so restarts do not reset the deadline.
* **Conservative unknowns (RWA-307).** An unrecognized Runway status maps to `RUNNING` (keep polling) until the overall deadline, never to `COMPLETED`. Optimistic mapping of an unknown status to success is prohibited.
* **No side effects on judgment.** `get_status()` reports progress; it does not download, validate, or mutate manifest state. The worker persists the reported state.

## Runway Status → Canonical State Mapping

The status mapper is the only place Runway status strings are interpreted.

| Runway job status (illustrative)     | Canonical render state | Terminal | Notes |
| ------------------------------------ | ---------------------- | -------- | ----- |
| `PENDING` / `QUEUED`                 | `SUBMITTED`            | no  | accepted, not yet running |
| `RUNNING` / `PROCESSING`             | `RUNNING`              | no  | in progress; report progress if given |
| `SUCCEEDED` / `COMPLETE`             | `COMPLETED`            | yes | asset ready for download |
| `FAILED`                             | `FAILED`               | yes | map error detail → canonical error class |
| `CANCELLED`                          | `CANCELLED`            | yes | acknowledges a prior cancel |
| `THROTTLED` / rate signal           | `RUNNING` (deferred)   | no  | feed rate governor; not a failure |
| `EXPIRED` / provider-side timeout    | `TIMED_OUT`            | yes | route to retry classification |
| *(unrecognized)*                     | `RUNNING`              | no  | conservative; poll until overall_timeout |

The canonical states align with the render task lifecycle (`NEW → SUBMITTED → RUNNING → DOWNLOADING → VALIDATING → READY → COMPOSED`, with `FAILED → RETRY → MANUAL REVIEW`) owned by the state machine in **5.4.3**. The adapter reports states; the state machine owns transitions.

---

# download()

`download()` retrieves the completed Runway asset and verifies transport integrity. It is called only after `get_status()` reports `COMPLETED`.

```text
download(job_ref, dest):

  1. resolve the Runway asset URL for the completed job
  2. acquire a rate token
  3. resolve credentials
  4. stream bytes to dest, bounded by download_timeout (120s)
  5. verify transport integrity:
        - byte length matches Runway-reported size (if provided)
        - checksum matches Runway-reported checksum (if provided)
        - decode handshake: container/codec/frame-count probe succeeds
  6. on integrity pass: return DownloadResult{ asset_uri, bytes, checksum, verified=true }
  7. on integrity fail: map → canonical CORRUPT_DOWNLOAD (retryable) error
```

Rules:

* **Deadline-bounded (RWA-306).** Download is bounded by `download_timeout: 120s`. A stalled transfer is aborted and mapped to a retryable canonical error (a corrupted/partial download is a documented retry reason in **5.4.5** / original 5.16).
* **Transport integrity only (RWA-308).** The adapter confirms the bytes arrived intact and are decodable *as a container* — it does not judge resolution adequacy, duration correctness, or visual quality. Those are the province of Asset Validation (**5.6.1**) and Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**). The decode handshake is a shallow probe (open, read header, confirm frame count > 0), not full validation.
* **Idempotent.** Re-downloading the same completed job to the same destination is safe; a verified existing asset short-circuits the transfer. The download does not consume the render attempt.
* **Destination is provided.** The caller supplies the asset destination (repository URI). The adapter writes bytes and returns provenance; it does not choose storage location or cache eligibility (see **5.6.3**).

```yaml id="rwa-download-result"
DownloadResult:
  asset_uri:
  bytes:
  checksum:                 # sha256 of transferred bytes
  content_type:
  verified: true|false
  provider_reported_size:   # for audit, if Runway supplied it
```

---

# cancel()

`cancel()` requests best-effort cancellation of an in-flight Runway job.

```text
cancel(job_ref):

  1. acquire a rate token
  2. resolve credentials
  3. POST cancel to Runway for provider_job_id, bounded by submission_timeout
  4. map Runway response → canonical CancelResult
  5. report observed outcome; do NOT assume success
```

Rules:

* **Best-effort, honestly reported.** Runway may already have completed, may cancel promptly, or may not support mid-flight cancellation for a given state. The adapter reports the *observed* outcome (`CANCELLED`, `ALREADY_TERMINAL`, or `CANCEL_UNSUPPORTED`), never a fabricated success.
* **Triggered by the platform.** Cancellation originates from the Control path of the queue (**5.4.1**) or a build-level cancel. The adapter never cancels on its own initiative.
* **Idempotent.** Cancelling an already-cancelled or already-terminal job is a no-op that returns the terminal state.
* **State remains authoritative upstream.** The manifest records the cancellation outcome; the adapter only performs and reports the API action.

```yaml id="rwa-cancel-result"
CancelResult:
  requested_at:
  outcome: CANCELLED | ALREADY_TERMINAL | CANCEL_UNSUPPORTED | CANCEL_FAILED
  observed_state:
```

---

# health_check()

`health_check()` probes Runway reachability and readiness so the platform can route around an unavailable provider before committing expensive work.

```text
health_check():

  1. resolve credentials (read-only scope if available)
  2. GET a lightweight Runway readiness/quota endpoint, short deadline
  3. classify: HEALTHY | DEGRADED | UNAVAILABLE
  4. return HealthStatus{ status, checked_at, detail? }
```

Rules:

* **Cheap and side-effect-free.** `health_check()` shall not submit renders, consume meaningful quota, or mutate state. It is a reachability and credential probe.
* **Feeds admission, not creativity.** A `DEGRADED` or `UNAVAILABLE` Runway result informs the queue's admission and the strategy layer's fallback (Runway unavailable with no fallback → Manual Review, per **5.4.1**). It never changes what is rendered, only whether/when Runway is used.
* **Distinguishes auth from outage.** Credential failure maps to `UNAVAILABLE` with an auth detail (so operators fix secrets), distinct from a Runway outage.

```yaml id="rwa-health"
HealthStatus:
  status: HEALTHY | DEGRADED | UNAVAILABLE
  checked_at:
  detail:                   # e.g. "auth_failed", "quota_low", "timeout"
```

---

# Runway Error → Canonical Error Mapping

The error mapper is the only place Runway error codes are interpreted (RWA-307). It converts Runway failures into the platform's canonical error classes, which retry classification (**5.4.5**) then acts on. The adapter classifies transport-level retryability; it does **not** decide whether the *render attempt* is retried — that authority belongs to **5.4.5**.

| Runway condition (illustrative)                | Canonical error class    | Retryable | Rationale |
| ---------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | --------- | --------- |
| HTTP 429 / explicit throttle                   | `PROVIDER_RATE_LIMITED`  | yes (flow control) | feed rate governor; defer, do not fail |
| HTTP 500/502/503/504                           | `PROVIDER_TRANSIENT`     | yes | temporary API failure (retry reason, 5.16) |
| Submission timeout (30s)                       | `PROVIDER_TIMEOUT`       | yes | provider timeout (retry reason) |
| Overall render timeout (45m)                   | `RENDER_TIMED_OUT`       | yes | route to retry classification |
| Download stall / partial / checksum mismatch   | `CORRUPT_DOWNLOAD`       | yes | corrupted download (retry reason) |
| HTTP 401/403 (auth)                            | `PROVIDER_AUTH`          | no  | credential fault; operator action, not retry |
| HTTP 400 malformed payload                     | `PROVIDER_BAD_REQUEST`   | no  | invalid/unsupported input (no retry, 5.16) |
| HTTP 404 unknown job                           | `PROVIDER_NOT_FOUND`     | no  | reconcile against manifest; non-retryable |
| Runway job `FAILED` with content rejection     | `PROVIDER_REJECTED`      | no  | permanent provider rejection |
| Runway job `FAILED`, unspecified               | `PROVIDER_TRANSIENT`     | yes (bounded) | conservative; bounded by max_retry |
| Unmapped Runway error code                     | `PROVIDER_UNKNOWN`       | yes (bounded) | conservative retry within max_retry: 3 |

Mapping rules:

* **Rate limiting is not failure.** `PROVIDER_RATE_LIMITED` is fed back into the adapter's token bucket to reduce the refill rate temporarily and is surfaced as a deferral, consistent with RQ-305.
* **Auth and bad-request are terminal.** These are non-retryable; blindly retrying wastes budget and quota. They route toward Dead Letter or Manual Review per **5.4.1**/**5.4.5**.
* **Conservative unknowns are bounded.** `PROVIDER_UNKNOWN` and unspecified `FAILED` are retryable but only within `max_retry: 3` (original 5.16); the adapter never grants unlimited retries.
* **The adapter tags, the classifier decides.** The adapter attaches the canonical error class and a redacted provider detail. Whether a retry actually occurs, and with what backoff, is owned by **5.4.5**. Retry counting is owned by the state machine (**5.4.3**), never incremented by the adapter.

Every canonical error carries a redacted `provider_detail` for diagnostics; no credentials or full request bodies appear in it (RWA-309).

---

# Rate Limiting and Concurrency at the Adapter Boundary

The adapter enforces Runway limits before every outbound call, complementing the queue-level governor of **5.4.1** (RQ-305). Defense in depth: even if a caller bypasses queue rate control, the adapter refuses to exceed Runway limits.

```yaml id="rwa-rate"
runway_rate_limits:
  requests_per_minute: 20
  burst: 5
  min_submission_interval: 3s
concurrency:
  max_concurrent_submissions: 4
  max_inflight_jobs: 6
```

Mechanism:

* The adapter maintains a per-instance token bucket sized to `requests_per_minute` with `burst` capacity and a `min_submission_interval` floor between submissions.
* `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, and `cancel()` each acquire a token before their HTTP call. When no token is available, the call is not made; the adapter returns a deferred/retryable signal and the queue reschedules.
* Runway `429`/throttle responses reduce the refill rate temporarily (adaptive backoff of the bucket), then recover. This is flow control, not an error path (RWA-305).
* Concurrency ceilings (`max_concurrent_submissions`, `max_inflight_jobs`) bound simultaneous Runway work; excess is deferred, never failed for lack of headroom.

These values are the Runway realization of the provider rate limits declared in **5.4.1**; they are configuration, confined to this adapter (RWA-302).

---

# Idempotency and Retry Interaction

The adapter's job is to make retries *safe*, not to *decide* them.

* **Safe re-submission (RWA-304).** Because `idempotency_key = sha256(cache_key + attempt)` is deterministic and sent to Runway, a re-delivered task re-attaches to the existing job. Exactly-once *effect* is achieved even under at-least-once transport (consistent with **5.4.1**).
* **Attempt ownership.** The render attempt counter lives in the manifest/state machine (**5.4.3**, **5.4.4**). The adapter reads `attempt` from the request; it never increments it. A new attempt (post-classification by **5.4.5**) arrives with a new `attempt` value and therefore a new idempotency key, allowing a genuinely fresh render.
* **Bounded by policy.** All retryable adapter errors are ultimately bounded by `max_retry: 3` (original 5.16 / **5.4.5**). The adapter surfaces retryability; it does not extend the ceiling.
* **No hidden retries.** The adapter shall not silently retry a failed render internally in a way that hides attempts from the state machine. Internal, sub-attempt retries are permitted only for pure transport hiccups within a single call's deadline (e.g. a socket reset during download) and must never create a second Runway job.

---

# Events

The adapter emits events for every material Runway interaction. Events reference `render_task_id`, `provider="runway"`, `attempt`, and `build_id`, and never contain credentials or full prompts.

```yaml id="rwa-events"
runway_adapter_events:
  - runway_submit_requested
  - runway_submit_accepted
  - runway_submit_idempotent_replay
  - runway_submit_deferred_rate_limit
  - runway_status_polled
  - runway_status_terminal
  - runway_download_started
  - runway_download_verified
  - runway_download_integrity_failed
  - runway_cancel_requested
  - runway_cancel_observed
  - runway_health_checked
  - runway_error_mapped          # includes canonical error class + redacted detail
```

Events are durably recorded for audit and replay, consistent with the observability model of **5.7.1**.

---

# Metrics

The adapter reports Runway-scoped metrics that feed the platform dashboards (**5.7.1**) and SLO review.

```yaml id="rwa-metrics"
runway_adapter_metrics:
  runway_submission_time
  runway_submission_success_rate
  runway_idempotent_replay_rate
  runway_status_poll_count
  runway_render_time              # submit → terminal
  runway_download_time
  runway_download_integrity_failure_rate
  runway_rate_limit_deferral_count
  runway_error_class_distribution
  runway_cancel_outcome_distribution
  runway_health_status
  runway_overall_timeout_count
```

These specialize the general render metrics of the original 5.29 (`submission_time`, `render_time`, `download_time`, `provider_latency`, `retry_count`) for Runway, and support the retry-rate SLO (render retry rate ≤ 5%).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The adapter operates under least privilege (RWA-309).

Required controls:

* Runway credentials resolved from the secret store at call time, scoped to the adapter's execution frame;
* credentials never persisted to manifests, queue items, events, logs, or asset metadata;
* redaction of prompts and credentials in all diagnostics (`provider_detail` carries only a safe error summary);
* TLS for all Runway calls; certificate verification never disabled;
* the assembled prompt treated as data, never as an instruction to the adapter;
* egress limited to configured Runway endpoints;
* audit events for every submission, cancellation, and error mapping;
* no Runway API surface wired that would permit autonomous creative generation (enforces the non-authority boundary).

A provider worker hands the adapter only references and the assembled request; the adapter resolves secrets itself and returns provenance without ever exposing them.

---

# Configuration Model

All Runway-specific configuration is confined here (RWA-302) and environment-overridable.

```yaml id="rwa-config"
runway_adapter:
  provider_id: runway
  endpoints:
    base_url: ${RUNWAY_BASE_URL}
    submit_path: /v1/jobs
    status_path: /v1/jobs/{job_id}
    asset_path: /v1/jobs/{job_id}/asset
    cancel_path: /v1/jobs/{job_id}/cancel
    health_path: /v1/health
  auth:
    secret_ref: secret://runway/api_key      # resolved at call time
  model_profiles:
    gen4.5:
      model_id: ${RUNWAY_MODEL_GEN45}
  profiles:
    runway_preview:
      model_profile: gen4.5
      quality_tier: draft
      resolution: 1280x720
      frame_rate: 24
      max_duration_seconds: 5
      upscale: false
    runway_final:
      model_profile: gen4.5
      quality_tier: high
      resolution: 1920x1080
      frame_rate: 24
      max_duration_seconds: 10
      upscale: true
  timeouts:
    submission_timeout: 30s
    poll_interval: 20s
    download_timeout: 120s
    overall_timeout: 45m
  rate_limits:
    requests_per_minute: 20
    burst: 5
    min_submission_interval: 3s
  concurrency:
    max_concurrent_submissions: 4
    max_inflight_jobs: 6
  retry:
    max_retry: 3                # policy owned by 5.4.5; mirrored for adapter bounds
  seed_policy: fixed_from_cache_key
  integrity:
    verify_size: true
    verify_checksum: true
    decode_handshake: true
```

Configuration is validated at startup. Invalid configuration fails closed: the adapter refuses to submit rather than dispatching with unsafe defaults. Timeout and retry values here mirror the authoritative policy of **5.4.6** and **5.4.5**; on conflict, the platform policy governs and the adapter is reconfigured, never the reverse.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* deterministic request mapping: identical request + profile → byte-identical payload (modulo idempotency key/timestamp);
* prompt passed verbatim; no creative mutation, append, or edit;
* rejection of a request whose prompt fails assembled-prompt provenance (fail closed);
* deterministic seed and idempotency key derivation from `cache_key`/`attempt`;
* duration clamped to profile maximum;
* profile resolution (`runway_preview`/`runway_final` → model, resolution, fps, quality);
* status mapping for every Runway status, including unknown → `RUNNING`;
* error mapping for every Runway condition, including unmapped → bounded retryable;
* integrity checks: size mismatch, checksum mismatch, decode-handshake failure → `CORRUPT_DOWNLOAD`;
* credential redaction in events and error details.

## Integration Tests

* `submit()` → `get_status()` polling → `download()` → integrity pass against a Runway sandbox/mock;
* `runway_preview` vs `runway_final` produce the configured resolution/quality;
* cancel of an in-flight job yields an honest observed outcome;
* `health_check()` distinguishes HEALTHY / DEGRADED / UNAVAILABLE and auth failure;
* download to repository destination returns correct provenance.

## Concurrency Tests

* concurrent submissions respect `max_concurrent_submissions` and `max_inflight_jobs`;
* two workers re-deliver the same attempt → single Runway job (idempotent replay);
* poll and download proceed without exceeding the rate bucket.

## Failure Injection Tests

* Runway `429` burst during submission → deferral, not failure; bucket refill adapts;
* Runway `5xx`/timeout on submit → retryable; retry re-attaches via idempotency key (no double job);
* submission timeout at 30s → `PROVIDER_TIMEOUT`, safe re-submit;
* download stall past 120s and partial/corrupt transfer → `CORRUPT_DOWNLOAD`;
* Runway `401/403` → `PROVIDER_AUTH`, non-retryable;
* Runway `400` malformed → `PROVIDER_BAD_REQUEST`, non-retryable;
* overall render exceeds 45m → `RENDER_TIMED_OUT`;
* unmapped Runway error code → conservative bounded retry.

## Recovery Tests

* deadline computed from manifest submission time survives adapter restart (no reset);
* re-submit after crash-before-manifest-persist re-attaches to existing Runway job;
* idempotency cache loss falls back to the Runway-side idempotency key (still no double job);
* reconcile a `PROVIDER_NOT_FOUND` job against the manifest.

## Containment Tests

* static check: no Runway status string, model id, endpoint, or error code appears outside the adapter package;
* replacing the adapter with a fake provider requires zero changes above the interface line.

---

# Runway Adapter Quality Gate

```yaml id="rwa-quality-gate"
runway_adapter_quality_gate:
  implements_full_provider_interface: true
  all_runway_specifics_contained: true
  request_mapping_deterministic: true
  prompt_forwarded_verbatim_no_mutation: true
  no_article_text_path_exists: true
  submission_idempotent_no_double_job: true
  rate_and_concurrency_enforced: true
  all_operations_deadline_bounded: true
  status_mapping_complete_and_conservative: true
  error_mapping_complete_and_conservative: true
  download_integrity_verified_not_judged: true
  credentials_never_persisted_or_logged: true
  overall_timeout_45m_enforced: true
  max_retry_bound_respected: true
  containment_static_check_passed: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RWA-401

The adapter renders only what it is given; it never generates, alters, or reinterprets story, pacing, dialogue, or camera language.

## Invariant RWA-402

All Runway-specific knowledge is contained within the adapter; no Runway API semantics appear above the interface line.

## Invariant RWA-403

The adapter implements the Provider Interface Contract of **5.3.2** exactly, indistinguishably from any other conformant provider.

## Invariant RWA-404

A re-delivered attempt never creates a second billable Runway job; submission idempotency is keyed on `cache_key` and `attempt`.

## Invariant RWA-405

No adapter call is issued in violation of Runway rate limits or concurrency ceilings; throttling is flow control, not failure.

## Invariant RWA-406

Every adapter operation is bounded by an explicit deadline; nothing blocks indefinitely, and the overall render deadline is 45 minutes.

## Invariant RWA-407

Every Runway status and error is translated to a canonical value before returning; unknown inputs map conservatively, never optimistically to success.

## Invariant RWA-408

Download verifies transport integrity only; the adapter never judges technical adequacy or visual quality (owned by **5.6**).

## Invariant RWA-409

The adapter never owns render-task state or increments the render attempt count; the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) and state machine (**5.4.3**) are authoritative.

## Invariant RWA-410

Runway credentials are resolved at call time and never persisted to logs, events, manifests, queue items, or asset metadata.

## Invariant RWA-411

The adapter surfaces retryability but never decides retries or extends the `max_retry: 3` ceiling; retry authority belongs to **5.4.5**.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                     |
| ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-467 | Runway is integrated solely as a concrete adapter implementing the Provider Interface Contract; it is never a creative authority. |
| ADR-468 | All Runway-specific knowledge — endpoints, model ids, status strings, error codes, headers — is contained within the adapter package. |
| ADR-469 | The request mapper forwards the assembled prompt (5.5) verbatim; the adapter has no code path that generates or edits prompt content, and no field can carry article text. |
| ADR-470 | Runway submissions are idempotent, keyed on `sha256(cache_key + attempt)`, so at-least-once re-delivery never creates a second billable job. |
| ADR-471 | Runway status and error vocabularies are translated to canonical states/error classes at the adapter boundary; unknowns map conservatively. |
| ADR-472 | Runway rate limits (20 rpm, burst 5, 3s min interval) and concurrency ceilings are enforced inside the adapter as defense in depth, with throttling treated as flow control. |
| ADR-473 | Download verifies transport integrity only (size, checksum, decode handshake); adequacy and visual quality judgments are deferred to 5.6. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.3.4** is complete when:

* The Runway Provider Adapter implements `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, `cancel()`, and `health_check()` exactly as the Provider Interface Contract (**5.3.2**) specifies, indistinguishably from any other provider.
* Runway acts only as a rendering backend; the adapter contains no path by which Runway could generate stories, determine pacing, create dialogue, or design camera language.
* The `runway_preview` and `runway_final` profiles and the `gen4.5` model profile are defined, with all Runway model, resolution, and quality settings confined to adapter configuration.
* Request mapping from the assembled prompt (**5.5**) is deterministic, forwards the prompt verbatim, clamps duration, derives seed and idempotency key from the `cache_key`, and provably cannot carry article text.
* Submission is idempotent, so at-least-once re-delivery re-attaches to the existing Runway job rather than double-charging.
* Status polling honors `poll_interval: 20s` and `overall_timeout: 45m`, and Runway statuses map conservatively to canonical render states aligned with the state machine of **5.4.3**.
* Download verifies transport integrity (size, checksum, decode handshake) without judging quality, deferring all adequacy decisions to **5.6.1** and **5.6.2**.
* Runway errors map completely to canonical error classes for retry classification (**5.4.5**), with rate limiting treated as flow control and auth/bad-request as non-retryable.
* Runway rate limits (RQ-305) and concurrency ceilings are enforced at the adapter boundary, and every operation is deadline-bounded.
* Credentials are resolved at call time and never persisted or logged, and the adapter never owns render-task state or increments the attempt count.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the Runway Provider Adapter as a fully contained, interchangeable rendering backend that renders on request and never influences the film.


---

# Part 5.3.5 — Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.4

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section closes **Group 5.3 — Provider Abstraction & Runway Integration** and defines the formal handoff into **Group 5.4 — Render Queue & Execution Control**.

Group 5.3 established *how the Rendering Platform talks to a rendering provider without ever depending on one.* It fixed the provider boundary, the common provider contract, the submission protocol, and one concrete adapter (Runway). It deliberately specified **no** execution control — no queueing, no state machine, no manifest, no retry classification, no timeout policy. Those belong to 5.4.

This section performs four jobs:

* it **summarises** what sections 5.3.1 through 5.3.4 collectively established;
* it **consolidates** the provider invariants — interchangeable providers, no provider-logic leakage, providers never make creative decisions, one task per shot, and asynchronous submission — into a single normative reference;
* it **defines the interface** — the exact artifacts, guarantees, and obligations that Group 5.3 hands to Group 5.4, centred on **submitted provider tasks and their `provider_task_id`s**;
* it **states the acceptance criteria** under which Group 5.3 is considered complete and the provider abstraction is frozen for downstream execution control.

This section is a **synthesis and interface contract**. It introduces no new mechanism. Where it appears to state a rule, that rule is a consolidation of a rule already established in 5.3.1–5.3.4, restated here so that 5.4 and every later group can reference one authoritative provider boundary.

Group 5.3 is the **abstraction layer**. Everything downstream — queueing, parallel execution, the render task state machine, the manifest, retry, and timeout — coordinates provider work through the contract frozen here. Nothing downstream may reach around it.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* a consolidated summary of the artifacts and rules established by 5.3.1–5.3.4;
* the provider invariants, restated as one normative set (PROV-1..5);
* the **5.3 → 5.4 interface contract**: the submitted-task handoff and its guarantees;
* the obligations 5.4 inherits and must never violate;
* the pre-conditions 5.4 may safely assume as already satisfied;
* the non-guarantees 5.4 must never assume;
* the compliance checklist for Group 5.3;
* the Group 5.3 quality gate;
* the invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria that freeze the provider-abstraction layer.

This section does **not** specify:

* the Render Queue Manager (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the parallel execution model (defined in **5.4.2**);
* the render task state machine (defined in **5.4.3**);
* the Render Manifest and runtime state (defined in **5.4.4**);
* retry, backoff, and failure classification (defined in **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline policy (defined in **5.4.6**);
* prompt assembly (defined in **5.5**);
* validation logic (defined in **5.6**).

This section consumes the outputs of 5.3.1–5.3.4; it re-derives nothing and contradicts nothing. Where a fact was fixed upstream, this section cites it rather than restating a variant of it.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the provider handoff.

> **Group 5.3 hands 5.4 a set of already-submitted provider tasks and their opaque task ids — never a provider. Execution control tracks tasks; it never learns to speak a provider's API.**

Execution control (5.4) coordinates work that has already crossed the provider boundary. The boundary is closed behind it. 5.4 decides **when** a task is dispatched, monitored, retried, or timed out; it may never decide **how** to submit to, poll, or download from a specific provider — that resolves inside the adapter (5.3.4) behind the common contract (5.3.2).

A corollary follows directly:

> **A `provider_task_id` is a handle, not an API. If any execution-control component branches on which provider produced it, the boundary has leaked and that branch is a defect.**

The queue, the state machine, and the manifest carry `provider_task_id`s as opaque references. They call `get_status()`, `download()`, and `cancel()` through the interface; they never construct a provider-specific request. If a scheduling decision would require knowledge of a provider's endpoints, payloads, or error codes, the decision is rejected at the boundary — not accommodated.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        GROUP 5.3 — Provider Abstraction & Runway Integration
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.3.1  Provider Independence & Boundary        │
        │  5.3.2  Provider Interface Contract             │
        │  5.3.3  Task Submission Protocol                │
        │  5.3.4  Runway Provider Adapter                 │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                 ┌─────────────────────┐
                 │   5.3.5  INTERFACE   │   ◄── this section
                 │  (closed boundary)   │
                 └─────────────────────┘
                            │
          submitted provider tasks + provider_task_ids
                            │
                            ▼
        GROUP 5.4 — Render Queue & Execution Control
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.4.1  Render Queue Manager                    │
        │  5.4.2  Parallel Execution Model                │
        │  5.4.3  Render Task State Machine               │
        │  5.4.4  Render Manifest & Runtime State         │
        │  5.4.5  Retry, Backoff & Failure Classification │
        │  5.4.6  Timeout & Deadline Policy               │
        │  5.4.7  Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.5   │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Group 5.3 is upstream of every execution-control decision. The interface defined here is the sole channel through which provider work reaches the execution layer. 5.4 receives **submitted tasks and opaque handles**; it does not participate in submitting them and does not hold provider credentials or endpoints.

---

# Summary of Group 5.3

Group 5.3 established the following, section by section. Each item is authoritative in its origin section; this summary is a consolidated index, not a restatement.

## From 5.3.1 — Provider Independence & Boundary

* Rendering providers are **interchangeable backends**; the Story Engine, Screenplay Engine, and every layer upstream of the adapter boundary must never depend on a specific provider.
* Runway is the **current** provider; Google Veo, Luma Dream Machine, Pika, OpenAI Video, and Tencent Hunyuan Video are candidate futures.
* A provider **acts only as a rendering backend**. Its responsibilities are limited to: task submission, status polling, downloading, cancellation, and retries.
* A provider **must never** generate stories, determine pacing, create dialogue, or design camera language.

## From 5.3.2 — Provider Interface Contract

* Every provider implements the same contract: `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, `cancel()`, `health_check()`.
* **No provider-specific logic may leak outside this interface.** Endpoints, payload shapes, authentication, and error codes are private to the adapter.
* The contract is the only surface any other layer may call; there is no provider-specific escape hatch.

## From 5.3.3 — Task Submission Protocol

* Each shot creates **exactly one** render task; the submitted task carries `shot_id`, `provider`, `profile`, and `status`.
* **Submission returns immediately.** `submit()` is asynchronous: it hands work to the provider and returns a provider task handle without blocking on completion.
* Submission yields a **provider task identifier** (`provider_task_id`) that is the sole means of subsequently polling, downloading, or cancelling that provider job.

## From 5.3.4 — Runway Provider Adapter

* Runway is one concrete implementation of the 5.3.2 contract; it maps the five contract methods onto Runway's API (e.g. profiles `gen4.5`, `runway_preview`, `runway_final`).
* All Runway-specific behaviour — request shaping, polling cadence translation, download URLs, throttle signals, error mapping — lives **inside** the adapter and is invisible above the boundary.
* Substituting Runway for another provider requires a new adapter only; no change to strategy, queueing, state machine, manifest, or validation.

Together, sections 5.3.1–5.3.4 fix the platform's **provider boundary, common contract, submission protocol, and one working adapter**. Group 5.4 begins exactly where a task has been submitted and a `provider_task_id` exists.

---

# Consolidated Provider Invariants

The provider rules of 5.3.1–5.3.4 are restated here as a single normative set, so that every downstream section references one boundary. These are the invariants execution control rests on.

## PROV-1 — Interchangeable Providers

Providers **shall** be interchangeable behind the common contract (**5.3.2**). No component upstream of the adapter boundary — strategy, queue, state machine, manifest, retry, timeout, prompt assembly, or validation — **shall** depend on which provider executes a task. Adding, removing, or substituting a provider **shall** require a new adapter only, with no change to execution-control logic.

## PROV-2 — No Provider Logic Leakage

Provider-specific behaviour — endpoints, payload shapes, authentication, polling translation, download mechanics, throttle signals, and error codes — **shall** remain private to the adapter. Nothing above the boundary **shall** branch on provider identity or construct a provider-specific request. The interface (`submit`, `get_status`, `download`, `cancel`, `health_check`) is the only surface any other layer may call.

## PROV-3 — Providers Never Make Creative Decisions

A provider **shall never** generate stories, determine or re-time pacing, create or edit dialogue, or design or override camera language. A provider executes an approved visual plan and returns an asset; it authors no creative content. This inherits FOUND-1 (**5.1.6**) and applies it at the adapter boundary without exception.

## PROV-4 — One Task Per Shot

Each shot **shall** produce **exactly one** render task submitted to exactly one provider (**5.3.3**). Mixed rendering strategies within a shot are prohibited (**5.2.1**), and a shot **shall never** be fanned out to multiple concurrent provider tasks for the same attempt. One shot, one task, one `provider_task_id` per attempt.

## PROV-5 — Asynchronous Submission

`submit()` **shall** return immediately with a `provider_task_id`; it **shall never** block on render completion (**5.3.3**, Principle 3 / FOUND-3). Completion is observed only through subsequent `get_status()` polling. No caller above the boundary may treat submission as synchronous or await a rendered asset inline.

These five invariants are **jointly binding**. No queue policy, execution model, retry rule, or timeout may satisfy one by breaking another. They are the acceptance floor for every section of Group 5.4 and beyond.

---

# The 5.3 → 5.4 Interface Contract

Group 5.3 hands Group 5.4 a **set of submitted provider tasks and their opaque handles**. The interface is defined as three explicit sets: the **handoff artifacts** 5.4 receives, the **guarantees** attached to them, and the **obligations** 5.4 inherits.

## Handoff Artifacts

5.4 receives, per shot, one submitted provider task. Each task is a reference record — a handle plus routing metadata — not a payload container.

```yaml id="r3a-handoff"
handoff_to_5_4:
  submitted_render_task:
    render_task_id:            # stable identity, one per shot per attempt
    shot_id:                   # references the approved shot
    provider:                  # runway | motion | svg | typography | cache
    provider_task_id:          # OPAQUE handle returned by submit(); poll/cancel key
    render_profile:            # e.g. runway_preview | runway_final | gen4.5
    submission_status:         # submitted (post-5.3.3)
    cache_key:                 # render cache key; idempotency anchor (5.6.3)
    submitted_at:              # submission timestamp
  provider_contract_handle:
    methods: [submit, get_status, download, cancel, health_check]  # 5.3.2
  invariants:                  # frozen, non-negotiable
    - PROV-1  # interchangeable providers
    - PROV-2  # no provider logic leakage
    - PROV-3  # providers never make creative decisions
    - PROV-4  # one task per shot
    - PROV-5  # asynchronous submission
```

The `provider_task_id` is the **single most important field** in the handoff. It is the only key through which 5.4 may observe, download, or cancel provider work — and it is **opaque**: its internal format is a provider detail, meaningful only to the adapter that produced it. 5.4 stores it, passes it back through `get_status(provider_task_id)`, and never parses or interprets it.

## Guarantees Provided to 5.4

When 5.4 begins, it may assume the following are already true. These are guarantees, not aspirations; they are enforced by the Group 5.3 quality gate before the boundary is closed.

```yaml id="r3a-guarantees"
guarantees_to_5_4:
  one_task_per_shot: true              # PROV-4: exactly one task per shot per attempt
  provider_abstracted: true            # PROV-1/2: no provider API visible above boundary
  submission_is_async: true            # PROV-5: submit() already returned, non-blocking
  provider_task_id_present: true       # every submitted task carries an opaque handle
  contract_is_uniform: true            # 5.3.2: same five methods for every provider
  no_creative_leakage: true            # PROV-3: provider authored no creative content
  provider_substitutable_by_adapter: true  # swapping providers is an adapter-only change
  cache_key_bound: true                # each task carries its idempotency anchor
  no_secrets_in_handoff: true          # credentials stay in the adapter, not the task
```

The most consequential guarantee for 5.4 is `provider_task_id_present`: every task handed forward is already submitted and already has a handle. 5.4 never has to invent, wait for, or reconstruct a `provider_task_id`; it schedules, monitors, and reconciles tasks that already exist on the provider side.

## Obligations 5.4 Inherits

5.4 accepts the boundary by honouring the following obligations. Violating any of them is a boundary defect, not a design choice.

* **Every `provider_task_id` shall be tracked through the queue, the state machine, and the manifest** (**5.4.1**, **5.4.3**, **5.4.4**). The handle is recorded and carried; it is never dropped or regenerated.
* **All provider interaction shall go through the contract** (PROV-2). Monitoring calls `get_status()`, retrieval calls `download()`, cancellation calls `cancel()`; no execution-control component may branch on provider identity or issue a provider-specific request.
* **Execution control shall author no creative content** (PROV-3, FOUND-1). Scheduling, retry, and timeout decisions change *when* work runs, never *what* is depicted.
* **The one-task-per-shot mapping shall be preserved** (PROV-4). A retry re-submits **one** replacement task for the shot; it never produces concurrent duplicate provider tasks for the same attempt.
* **Submission shall be treated as asynchronous** (PROV-5). The queue observes completion by polling (poll_interval 20s, defined in **5.4.6**); it never blocks a worker awaiting a provider result inline.
* **Provider substitution shall remain an adapter-only change** (PROV-1). No queue, state-machine, or manifest field may be keyed to a specific provider's behaviour.

The state 5.4 receives — a `submitted` task with a live `provider_task_id` — is the entry condition for the render task state machine, which begins at **NEW** and advances to **SUBMITTED** on receipt of the handle, then **RUNNING → DOWNLOADING → VALIDATING → READY → COMPOSED** as monitoring proceeds (**5.4.3**).

---

# Interface Sequence

The handoff is a strict, one-directional sequence. No execution-control step reaches back across the provider boundary except through the contract.

```text
5.3.3 submit()  (async, returns immediately)
        │
        ▼
provider_task_id issued (opaque handle)
        │
        ▼
submitted render task ──┐
provider contract handle┤
provider invariants ────┤
                        ▼
              5.4.1 Render Queue Manager
                        │
        record handle → 5.4.4 Render Manifest
                        │
        advance NEW → SUBMITTED → 5.4.3 State Machine
                        │
     poll get_status(provider_task_id)  (5.4.6 poll_interval 20s)
                        │
        ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
        ▼               ▼               ▼
   still running     completed        failed
   (re-defer)     download()      classify (5.4.5)
                        │
                        ▼
               VALIDATING (5.6)
```

Every arrow that touches a provider passes through one of the five contract methods. The `provider_task_id` travels the full length of the sequence unchanged and uninterpreted. If a task cannot advance — provider unavailable, non-retryable failure, attempts exhausted — 5.4 escalates through Manual Review or Dead Letter (**5.4.1**, **5.4.5**), consistent with Fail Closed (FOUND-5); it never relaxes the provider boundary to force progress.

---

# What 5.4 Must Not Assume

The interface is generous with guarantees but strict about non-guarantees. 5.4 must **not** assume any of the following, because 5.3 deliberately did not fix them:

* **That a submitted task has completed, or will complete.** Submission is asynchronous (PROV-5); a `provider_task_id` proves a job was accepted, not that an asset exists. 5.4 must poll `get_status()` and reconcile RUNNING, completion, and failure through the state machine (**5.4.3**), never presume a result.
* **That the `provider_task_id` is parseable or portable.** The handle is opaque and provider-private (PROV-2). It carries no schema 5.4 may read, and it is meaningful only to the adapter that issued it. 5.4 stores and returns it; it never decodes it or reuses it against a different provider.
* **That the submitted task is authoritative state.** A submitted task is execution *intent* with a handle; the authoritative render-task state lives in the **Render Manifest** (**5.4.4**), consistent with the queue's non-authoritative posture (**5.4.1**). If the handoff record and the manifest disagree, the manifest wins.
* **That the provider remains available for the task's lifetime.** 5.3 guarantees provider *independence* and *substitutability*, not continuous *availability*. Health, rate limits, and throttling are observed via `health_check()` and enforced at execution time (**5.4.1**, **5.4.6**); 5.4 must handle a provider becoming unavailable after submission.
* **That a failed task may be re-submitted without a new handle.** A retry produces a **new** `provider_task_id` for a new attempt (PROV-4); the old handle is not reactivated. Retry authorization and classification are owned by **5.4.5**, not inferred from the handoff.

Confusing a non-guarantee for a guarantee is the most common way an execution layer silently breaches the provider boundary. The interface names these explicitly so 5.4 cannot drift across the boundary by assumption.

---

# Interface Verification

The handoff is verified — not merely asserted — before the boundary is declared closed. The following checks constitute the interface test surface for Group 5.3.

## Boundary Tests

* no component above the adapter constructs a provider-specific request or branches on provider identity (PROV-2);
* substituting the Runway adapter for a stub provider requires no change to queue, state-machine, or manifest code (PROV-1);
* an attempt by a provider to alter shot, camera, character, or pacing content is rejected (PROV-3).

## Contract Tests

* every provider adapter exposes exactly `submit`, `get_status`, `download`, `cancel`, `health_check` (**5.3.2**);
* `submit()` returns a non-null `provider_task_id` and returns before render completion (PROV-5);
* each shot yields exactly one submitted task and one handle per attempt (PROV-4).

## Handoff Tests

* every submitted task presented to 5.4 carries a `provider_task_id`, `render_task_id`, `shot_id`, `provider`, `render_profile`, and `cache_key`;
* no submitted task carries provider credentials, endpoints, or asset payloads (`no_secrets_in_handoff`);
* `get_status(provider_task_id)` is the only path by which 5.4 observes completion.

## Gate Tests

* the compliance checklist fails closed on any single unmet field (R3A-405);
* the quality gate fails when a downstream section could satisfy its goals by reaching around the provider contract (R3A-401, R3A-402).

These tests give Codex an executable definition of "the boundary is closed": the interface opens only when all of them pass.

---

# Compliance Checklist

Before Group 5.3 is frozen and the interface to 5.4 is declared open, every item below must hold.

```yaml
group_5_3_compliance:
  # Independence & boundary (5.3.1 / PROV-1, PROV-3)
  providers_interchangeable: true
  provider_is_backend_only: true
  provider_makes_no_creative_decisions: true
  # Contract (5.3.2 / PROV-2)
  common_interface_defined: true
  five_contract_methods_present: true
  no_provider_logic_leaks_outside_interface: true
  # Submission (5.3.3 / PROV-4, PROV-5)
  one_task_per_shot: true
  submission_is_asynchronous: true
  provider_task_id_returned: true
  # Adapter (5.3.4)
  runway_adapter_implements_contract: true
  provider_specifics_private_to_adapter: true
  substitution_is_adapter_only: true
  # Interface (5.3.5)
  submitted_task_handoff_enumerated: true
  provider_task_id_is_opaque_handle: true
  guarantees_to_5_4_enumerated: true
  obligations_of_5_4_enumerated: true
  no_secrets_in_handoff: true
  handle_tracked_through_queue_state_manifest: true
```

Every field must be `true`. A single `false` blocks the freeze: the interface to 5.4 does not open until the provider abstraction is whole. This mirrors the Fail-Closed posture of FOUND-5 applied to the specification itself.

---

# Group 5.3 Quality Gate

```yaml
group_5_3_quality_gate:
  providers_interchangeable_behind_contract: true
  provider_invariants_consolidated: true      # PROV-1..5
  provider_invariants_jointly_binding: true
  no_provider_logic_above_boundary: true
  provider_makes_no_creative_decisions: true
  one_task_and_one_handle_per_shot: true
  submission_async_with_immediate_handle: true
  provider_task_id_opaque_and_tracked: true
  handoff_carries_no_secrets_or_payloads: true
  manifest_remains_authoritative_over_handoff: true
  no_downstream_group_may_bypass_provider_contract: true
```

The gate passes only when the boundary is complete **and** demonstrably non-bypassable by any downstream group. If any section of 5.4 could satisfy its own goals by calling a provider outside the contract or by depending on provider identity, the gate fails and the boundary is re-hardened here before proceeding.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant R3A-401

Group 5.3 is a **closed provider boundary**: no downstream group may reach around the common contract (`submit`, `get_status`, `download`, `cancel`, `health_check`) to interact with a provider directly, and no downstream logic may depend on provider identity.

## Invariant R3A-402

The provider invariants (PROV-1..5) are **jointly binding**; no queue policy, execution model, retry rule, timeout, or configuration may satisfy one by breaking another.

## Invariant R3A-403

The 5.3 → 5.4 handoff is a set of **submitted render tasks each carrying exactly one opaque `provider_task_id`**; execution control tracks the handle through the queue (**5.4.1**), the state machine (**5.4.3**), and the manifest (**5.4.4**) without parsing, regenerating, or reusing it across providers.

## Invariant R3A-404

Submission is **asynchronous and one-per-shot**: a `provider_task_id` attests acceptance, not completion; each shot yields exactly one task per attempt; and a retry produces a new handle rather than reactivating an old one (classification owned by **5.4.5**).

## Invariant R3A-405

The Group 5.3 compliance checklist and quality gate must both fully pass before the interface to 5.4 is declared open; a single failing field **fails closed** and blocks the freeze.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                                                 |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-474 | Group 5.3 is frozen as a closed provider boundary; the provider rules of 5.3.1–5.3.4 are consolidated into a single jointly-binding invariant set (PROV-1..5) — interchangeable providers, no provider-logic leakage, no creative decisions, one task per shot, and asynchronous submission — that no downstream group may bypass. |
| ADR-475 | The 5.3 → 5.4 handoff is a one-directional interface of already-submitted render tasks, each carrying one opaque `provider_task_id`; execution control tracks that handle through the queue, state machine, and manifest and interacts with providers only through the common contract, never by provider identity. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.3.5** is complete when:

* Group 5.3 (sections 5.3.1 through 5.3.4) is summarised into a single authoritative index of established facts — provider independence, the common contract, the submission protocol, and the Runway adapter — contradicting none of its origin sections.
* The provider rules are consolidated into one normative, jointly-binding invariant set — interchangeable providers, no provider-logic leakage, no creative decisions, one task per shot, and asynchronous submission — restated as PROV-1 through PROV-5.
* The 5.3 → 5.4 interface is defined as three explicit sets: the submitted-task handoff artifacts, the guarantees provided, and the obligations inherited.
* The handoff enumerates the submitted render task and identifies the opaque `provider_task_id` as the single handle through which execution control observes, downloads, and cancels provider work.
* The guarantees 5.4 may assume — one task per shot, abstracted providers, asynchronous submission, a present `provider_task_id`, a uniform contract, no creative leakage, adapter-only substitutability, a bound cache key, and no secrets in the handoff — are enumerated and enforced by the Group 5.3 quality gate before the boundary is closed.
* The obligations 5.4 inherits — tracking the handle through queue, state machine, and manifest; interacting with providers only through the contract; authoring no creative content; preserving one task per shot; treating submission as asynchronous; and keeping provider substitution adapter-only — are stated as boundary conditions rather than suggestions.
* The non-guarantees are named explicitly, so 5.4 cannot presume completion, parse the opaque handle, treat the handoff as authoritative over the manifest, assume continuous provider availability, or re-use a failed handle.
* The compliance checklist and quality gate fully pass, and both fail closed on any single unmet field so the interface to 5.4 cannot open over an incomplete provider abstraction.
* Invariants R3A-401 through R3A-405 and decision records ADR-474 and ADR-475 fix the provider boundary as closed and non-bypassable by any downstream group.
* Together these provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the abstraction-to-execution handoff — passing already-submitted tasks and their opaque `provider_task_id`s into the queue, state machine, and manifest, and enforcing that all provider interaction flows through the common contract — without permitting any downstream layer to depend on provider identity or reach around the provider boundary.


---

# Part 5.4.1 — Render Queue Manager

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Render Queue Manager** used by the Rendering Platform.

The Render Queue Manager is responsible for holding, ordering, and dispatching **render tasks** between:

* render task generation;
* provider submission;
* provider status monitoring;
* asset download;
* asset validation;
* retry scheduling;
* manual review.

Its purpose is to ensure that render work moves through the Rendering Platform in a manner that is:

* deterministic;
* durable;
* observable;
* idempotent;
* concurrency-bounded;
* provider-rate-aware;
* cost-aware;
* failure-tolerant.

The Render Queue Manager does **not** decide *what* should be rendered.

It receives already materialized Render Tasks — one per shot, produced from the approved Storyboard and Render Strategy — and coordinates their safe, ordered transfer between rendering components without ever modifying the approved creative plan.

Rendering is an execution layer. The Render Queue Manager is the scheduling core of that execution layer.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:
