* the logical queue topology of the Rendering Platform;
* queue classes and their operational purpose;
* the render task admission contract;
* deterministic ordering and priority;
* concurrency and provider rate limiting;
* leasing, visibility timeout, and acknowledgement;
* retry scheduling through delayed visibility;
* cancellation and drain behavior;
* backpressure and cost-aware admission;
* dead-letter and manual-review routing;
* queue observability, security, and configuration;
* queue invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* the Render Task State Machine (defined in **5.4.3**);
* retry classification and backoff formulas (defined in **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline values (defined in **5.4.6**);
* provider submission payloads (defined in **5.3.3**);
* asset validation logic (defined in **5.6.1** and **5.6.2**).

The Render Queue Manager consumes those contracts; it does not own them.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **The render queue stores execution intent temporarily. It never owns render truth.**

The authoritative state of every render task remains in the **Render Manifest** (defined in **5.4.4**).

The Render Queue Manager is an execution transport and coordination mechanism.

It must never become the only source of:

* render task identity;
* render task state;
* provider task identity;
* attempt or retry history;
* asset provenance;
* cache eligibility;
* build status.

If all queue data is lost, rendering must be fully reconstructable from the Render Manifest, the approved Render Strategy, and provider task records.

A corollary follows directly:

> **A render is never issued twice for the same validated result.**

Duplicate provider submissions are prevented by idempotent admission keyed on the render cache key (defined in **5.6.3**), not by trusting queue contents.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Storyboard (Approved)
        │
        ▼
Render Strategy Planner
        │
        ▼
Render Task Generation
        │
        ▼
Render Queue Manager
        │
        ├── Ready Queue
        ├── Submission Queue
        ├── Provider Monitor Queue
        ├── Download Queue
        ├── Validation Queue
        ├── Retry Queue
        ├── Control Queue
        ├── Manual Review Queue
        └── Dead Letter Queue
        │
        ▼
Provider Workers  ──►  Runway / Motion / SVG / Typography Adapters
        │
        ▼
Asset Repository ──► FFmpeg Composer
```

Render tasks enter queues only **after** their identity and eligibility have been persisted to the Render Manifest.

The Render Queue Manager sits downstream of planning and upstream of provider execution. It is the sole admission point through which any provider request may be issued.

---

# Responsibilities

The Render Queue Manager is responsible for:

* durable render-task enqueuing;
* queue classification and routing by pipeline stage;
* deterministic ordering within each queue;
* concurrency limiting per provider and per class;
* provider rate-limit compliance;
* task leasing and visibility timeout;
* acknowledgement after persisted state and outputs;
* release and requeue on worker loss;
* delayed visibility for retry and provider polling;
* duplicate active-entry prevention;
* cost-aware admission and backpressure integration;
* cancellation and graceful drain;
* dead-letter and manual-review routing;
* queue metrics emission;
* crash recovery and queue reconstruction.

It is **not** responsible for:

* selecting render strategy (see **5.2**);
* assembling prompts (see **5.5**);
* provider API invocation semantics (see **5.3**);
* render task state ownership (see **5.4.4**);
* retry eligibility classification (see **5.4.5**);
* asset validation outcomes (see **5.6**);
* creative fallback decisions (escalated to Manual Review).

---

# Queue Design Objectives

The Render Queue Manager shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RQ-301 — Durable Intent

A queued render task must survive:

* worker crash;
* provider adapter restart;
* GitHub Actions runner termination;
* transient network failure;
* Orchestrator restart.

Queued intent is persisted, not held only in memory.

---

## RQ-302 — Single Active Delivery

One render-task attempt may have at most one active queue delivery or lease per queue class at any moment.

Parallel duplicate delivery of the same attempt is prohibited.

---

## RQ-303 — At-Least-Once Transport

Under failure conditions the queue may deliver a render task more than once.

Therefore every consumer — submission, monitoring, download, validation — must be idempotent.

Exactly-once delivery is **not** assumed. Exactly-once *effect* is achieved through idempotent provider submission keyed on the render cache key.

---

## RQ-304 — Deterministic Ordering

Given identical queue contents, priority scores, scene ordering, and timestamps, dequeue order must be deterministic.

Random scheduling is prohibited (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism).

---

## RQ-305 — Provider Rate Safety

The queue must never dispatch render submissions faster than the configured provider concurrency and rate limits allow.

Provider throttling is a first-class scheduling input, not an error path.

---

## RQ-306 — Cost Awareness

Admission of expensive provider work (e.g. Runway final renders) must respect the build's budget reservation and the cost-priority order defined in **5.2.3**.

The queue defers, not silently drops, work that exceeds available budget.

---

## RQ-307 — Class Isolation

Different render workload classes must not block one another unnecessarily.

A download backlog must not prevent provider monitoring. A validation backlog must not prevent new submissions within budget.

---

## RQ-308 — Recoverability

Queue state must be reconstructable from:

* the Render Manifest;
* render task state;
* the retry schedule (`next_retry_at`);
* active lease records;
* provider task records.

---

# Queue Topology

The Rendering Platform defines multiple logical queues rather than one global queue.

```text
Ready Queue          → tasks eligible for provider submission
Submission Queue     → tasks accepted for dispatch within rate limits
Provider Monitor Q   → submitted tasks awaiting provider completion (delayed poll)
Download Queue       → completed provider jobs awaiting asset download
Validation Queue     → downloaded assets awaiting technical + visual validation
Retry Queue          → failed attempts awaiting delayed re-admission
Control Queue        → pause / cancel / reprioritize operations (reserved capacity)
Manual Review Queue  → tasks requiring human approval before continuation
Dead Letter Queue    → tasks exhausted or non-retryable (immutable evidence)
```

Each queue serves exactly one operational purpose. No task may occupy two workload queues simultaneously.

---

# Queue Classes

```yaml
render_queue_classes:
  - READY
  - SUBMISSION
  - PROVIDER_MONITOR
  - DOWNLOAD
  - VALIDATION
  - RETRY
  - CONTROL
  - MANUAL_REVIEW
  - DEAD_LETTER
```

Every queued item must belong to exactly one queue class.

The mapping from render pipeline stage to queue class is fixed:

| Pipeline Stage        | Queue Class      |
| --------------------- | ---------------- |
| Task ready            | READY            |
| Awaiting dispatch     | SUBMISSION       |
| Provider running      | PROVIDER_MONITOR |
| Asset ready to fetch  | DOWNLOAD         |
| Asset fetched         | VALIDATION       |
| Recoverable failure   | RETRY            |
| Operator command      | CONTROL          |
| Human decision needed | MANUAL_REVIEW    |
| Terminal failure      | DEAD_LETTER      |

---

# Render Queue Item Schema

A queue item is a lightweight reference, never a payload container.

```yaml id="rq-item"
render_queue_item:
  queue_item_id:          # deterministic: sha256(task_id + queue_class + attempt)
  render_task_id:         # references Render Manifest entry
  shot_id:
  build_id:
  queue_class:
  provider:               # runway | motion | svg | typography | cache
  render_profile:         # runway_preview | runway_final | ...
  priority_score:
  scene_index:
  shot_index:
  cache_key:              # render cache key; idempotency anchor
  attempt:
  enqueued_at:
  visible_at:             # delayed visibility timestamp
  lease_id:               # null until leased
  lease_expires_at:
  cost_estimate:
```

Queue items **must not** contain:

* prompt text;
* character or location descriptions;
* provider API secrets;
* binary asset data;
* article content.

All heavy or sensitive data is resolved by the worker from authoritative stores using the references above.

---

# Queue Item Identity

Each queue item has a deterministic identity.

```text id="rq-id"
queue_item_id = sha256(
  render_task_id +
  queue_class +
  attempt
)
```

Deterministic identity guarantees that a re-enqueue after a process retry produces the **same** item id, enabling duplicate-active detection (RQ-302) without a central lock.

---

# Admission Contract

A render task is admitted to the Ready Queue only when **all** of the following hold:

```yaml
admission_preconditions:
  storyboard_approved: true
  render_strategy_assigned: true
  render_task_persisted: true      # exists in Render Manifest
  cache_lookup_completed: true
  not_cache_hit: true              # cache hits skip rendering entirely
  budget_reservation_available: true
  provider_available: true
```

If `not_cache_hit` is false — i.e. a matching render cache entry exists — the task bypasses the queue entirely and is marked `READY→CACHED` in the manifest (see **5.6.3**). Cache elimination precedes all queueing.

Admission is **idempotent**: re-admitting a task whose `queue_item_id` is already active is a no-op.

---

# Deterministic Ordering & Priority

Within the Ready and Submission queues, ordering is computed from a composite priority score.

```yaml id="rq-priority"
priority_score:
  build_priority:        # preview build vs final build vs backfill
  strategy_cost_rank:    # cheaper strategies admitted first within budget
  scene_index:           # earlier scenes first for pipelined composition
  shot_index:
  enqueued_at:           # FIFO tiebreak, deterministic
```

Ordering rule (highest precedence first):

1. **Control operations** always precede workload (reserved capacity, RQ-307).
2. **Build priority** — an approved final build outranks a speculative preview backfill.
3. **Cost rank** — within a build and budget window, cheaper strategies (Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway) are admitted first, mirroring the cost-optimization order in **5.2.3**.
4. **Scene/shot index** — earlier narrative position first, so downstream composition can begin pipelined.
5. **Enqueue timestamp** — deterministic FIFO tiebreak.

Given identical inputs the resulting order is byte-for-byte reproducible.

---

# Concurrency Configuration

Concurrency is bounded globally, per provider, and per class.

```yaml
concurrency:
  global_max_active_renders: 8

  per_provider:
    runway:
      max_concurrent_submissions: 4
      max_inflight_jobs: 6
    motion:
      max_concurrent: 4
    svg:
      max_concurrent: 8
    typography:
      max_concurrent: 8

  per_class:
    DOWNLOAD:
      max_concurrent: 4
    VALIDATION:
      max_concurrent: 4
```

The Submission Queue may promote a task to a worker only when **every** applicable limit has available headroom. If any limit is saturated the task remains queued; it is never failed for lack of headroom.

---

# Provider Rate Limiting

Provider rate limits are enforced at the queue boundary before any provider API call.

```yaml
provider_rate_limits:
  runway:
    requests_per_minute: 20
    burst: 5
    min_submission_interval: 3s
```

The Render Queue Manager maintains a per-provider token bucket. Submission dequeue consumes a token; when the bucket is empty, submission is deferred via delayed visibility rather than rejected.

Rate-limit responses (HTTP 429 or provider-specific throttle signals) feed back into the bucket, temporarily reducing the refill rate. This is normal flow control, not a failure (RQ-305).

---

# Leasing & Visibility Timeout

Workers acquire **time-bounded leases**, never permanent ownership (consistent with the orchestrator lease model).

```yaml id="rq-lease"
lease:
  lease_id:
  queue_item_id:
  worker_id:
  acquired_at:
  expires_at:
  renewals:
```

Lease rules:

* A dequeued item becomes invisible for the lease duration.
* A worker must renew the lease before expiry for long provider jobs.
* On lease expiry without acknowledgement, the item becomes visible again for re-delivery.
* Re-delivery increments delivery count but **not** the render attempt count; the render attempt is owned by the state machine (**5.4.3**).

Default lease durations align with the timeout policy in **5.4.6**:

```yaml
lease_durations:
  SUBMISSION: 60s
  PROVIDER_MONITOR: 30s     # renewed each poll cycle
  DOWNLOAD: 180s
  VALIDATION: 150s
```

---

# Acknowledgement Protocol

Acknowledgement removes an item from its queue.

**Acknowledgement occurs only after the authoritative Render Manifest state and any produced outputs have been durably persisted.**

Ordering is strict:

```text
1. Worker completes stage effect (submit / poll / download / validate)
2. Worker persists new render task state + outputs to Render Manifest
3. Worker acknowledges queue item
4. Queue Manager routes task to next queue class (if any)
```

If the process crashes between steps 2 and 3, at-least-once delivery re-delivers the item; the idempotent consumer observes the already-persisted state and safely re-acknowledges. This ordering prevents lost work and prevents double provider submission (RQ-303).

---

# Provider Monitor & Delayed Polling

Submitted provider jobs are tracked in the Provider Monitor Queue using delayed visibility rather than busy polling.

```yaml
provider_monitor:
  poll_interval: 20s        # from 5.4.6 timeout policy
  jitter: 0-3s              # deterministic per-task jitter from task_id hash
  max_poll_duration: 45m    # overall render timeout
```

A monitor item's `visible_at` is set to `now + poll_interval`. On each poll the worker queries provider status, updates the manifest, and either:

* re-defers (still running) → new `visible_at`;
* routes to Download Queue (completed);
* routes to Retry Queue (recoverable failure);
* routes to Dead Letter / Manual Review (terminal).

Jitter is derived deterministically from the task id hash, preserving reproducibility while avoiding synchronized provider polling storms.

---

# Retry Scheduling

Retryable failures are re-admitted through the Retry Queue using delayed visibility.

```yaml
retry_scheduling:
  max_attempts: 3           # from 5.16 / 5.4.5
  backoff: exponential      # base and cap defined in 5.4.5
  visible_at: now + backoff(attempt)
```

The Render Queue Manager does **not** decide *whether* a failure is retryable — that classification is owned by **5.4.5**. The queue only schedules the delayed re-admission once a retry has been authorized and recorded in the manifest.

Retry authorization must be present in the manifest before a Retry Queue item is created; an unauthorized retry item is rejected.

---

# Cancellation & Drain

Cancellation flows through the Control Queue, which holds reserved capacity and precedes all workload (RQ-307).

Cancellation semantics:

```yaml
cancellation:
  scope: build | scene | shot
  in_flight_provider_jobs: cancel_via_adapter   # best-effort provider cancel
  queued_items: evict
  leased_items: mark_cancel_requested           # worker checks and aborts at next safe point
  manifest_state: CANCELLED
```

Graceful **drain** (e.g. runner shutdown) stops admitting new Ready items, allows in-flight leases to complete or checkpoint, persists all state, and leaves the queue reconstructable. No render task is silently dropped during drain.

---

# Backpressure & Cost-Aware Admission

Queue depth and budget consumption feed a backpressure signal.

```yaml
backpressure:
  ready_queue_high_watermark: 40
  download_queue_high_watermark: 20
  budget_consumed_pause_threshold: 0.90    # 90% of build render budget
```

When a high-water mark or the budget threshold is reached, admission of new **expensive** work (Runway final renders) is paused first, then progressively cheaper classes, in reverse of the cost-priority order. Cheap deterministic work (SVG, typography, cache) continues longest.

Backpressure **defers**; it never fails admissible work. Budget exhaustion routes the build to Manual Review (RQ-306) rather than producing a partial trailer (Fail Closed, Principle 5).

---

# Dead Letter & Manual Review Routing

Two terminal-adjacent queues capture work that cannot proceed automatically.

**Manual Review Queue** — the task is well-formed but requires a human decision:

* budget exhausted before completion;
* visual quality below threshold after max retries;
* provider unavailable with no fallback strategy;
* continuity risk flagged by validation.

**Dead Letter Queue** — the task is non-retryable or has exhausted retries:

* invalid prompt or unsupported input;
* provider permanent rejection;
* attempts exhausted (attempt > max_attempts).

Dead Letter records are **immutable evidence**. They preserve the full attempt history and never silently alter a task outcome. Reconciliation is explicit and human-initiated (or policy-driven), never automatic mutation.

---

# Events

The Render Queue Manager emits events for every material transition.

```yaml
render_queue_events:
  - render_task_admitted
  - render_task_deferred_rate_limit
  - render_task_deferred_budget
  - render_submission_dispatched
  - provider_job_monitored
  - asset_download_enqueued
  - asset_validation_enqueued
  - render_task_retry_scheduled
  - render_task_cancelled
  - render_task_manual_review
  - render_task_dead_lettered
  - lease_acquired
  - lease_expired
  - queue_reconstructed
```

Every event references the `render_task_id`, `queue_class`, `attempt`, and originating `build_id`, and is durably recorded for audit and replay.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml
render_queue_metrics:
  ready_queue_depth
  submission_queue_depth
  provider_monitor_depth
  download_queue_depth
  validation_queue_depth
  retry_queue_depth
  dead_letter_depth
  queue_wait_time
  submission_dispatch_rate
  provider_rate_limit_deferrals
  budget_deferrals
  active_leases
  lease_expiration_rate
  duplicate_delivery_rate
  cache_bypass_count
```

All metrics are stored for historical trend analysis and feed the SLO review (cache hit rate ≥ 70%, render retry rate ≤ 5%).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The Render Queue Manager operates under least privilege.

Required controls:

* queue access authorization scoped to build namespace;
* namespace isolation between concurrent builds;
* no provider secrets in queue items (resolved by workers from a secret store);
* encryption in transit and at rest where applicable;
* tamper detection on queue item identity;
* audit events for every admission and dead-letter action;
* least-privilege worker access to provider adapters.

A worker receives only the references it needs; it resolves prompts and credentials at execution time, never through the queue payload.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="rq-config"
render_queue:
  concurrency:
    global_max_active_renders: 8
    per_provider: { runway: { max_concurrent_submissions: 4, max_inflight_jobs: 6 } }
  rate_limits:
    runway: { requests_per_minute: 20, burst: 5, min_submission_interval: 3s }
  leases:
    submission: 60s
    provider_monitor: 30s
    download: 180s
    validation: 150s
  retry:
    max_attempts: 3
  backpressure:
    ready_queue_high_watermark: 40
    budget_consumed_pause_threshold: 0.90
  monitor:
    poll_interval: 20s
    max_poll_duration: 45m
```

All values are configurable and environment-overridable. Configuration is validated at startup; invalid configuration fails closed and blocks admission rather than dispatching with unsafe defaults.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* deterministic `queue_item_id` generation;
* routing by pipeline stage → queue class;
* duplicate active-item prevention;
* deterministic ordering under identical inputs;
* cost-rank ordering within budget;
* delayed visibility for retry and monitor;
* lease creation, renewal, expiration;
* acknowledgement only after persisted state;
* cache-hit admission bypass.

## Integration Tests

* Ready → Submission → provider dispatch within rate limits;
* Provider Monitor delayed polling to completion;
* Download and Validation queue isolation under backlog;
* Retry Queue re-admission from `next_retry_at`;
* Control Queue precedence during saturation;
* budget-threshold pause of expensive work;
* queue reconstruction from Render Manifest.

## Concurrency Tests

* two workers lease the same item;
* acknowledgement concurrent with lease expiration;
* cancellation concurrent with dequeue;
* duplicate delivery after process retry;
* worker crash after provider submission but before manifest persist.

## Failure Injection Tests

* provider 429 throttle during submission burst;
* manifest persist succeeds but acknowledgement fails;
* provider job completes but download queue unavailable;
* corrupted queue item;
* expired lease with active provider job;
* Dead Letter Queue unavailable.

## Recovery Tests

* rebuild Ready Queue from manifest;
* reconstruct Retry Queue from `next_retry_at`;
* reconstruct Provider Monitor Queue from provider task records;
* reconcile terminal tasks with stale queue entries;
* recover leased items after worker loss.

---

# Render Queue Quality Gate

```yaml
render_queue_quality_gate:
  all_items_reference_valid_render_tasks: true
  no_duplicate_active_items: true
  all_leases_have_expiration: true
  all_acknowledgements_follow_persisted_state: true
  all_delayed_items_have_visible_at: true
  all_retry_items_have_retry_authorization: true
  cache_hits_never_enqueued: true
  provider_rate_limits_respected: true
  control_queue_reserved_capacity: true
  budget_threshold_enforced: true
  dead_letter_queue_enabled: true
  queue_reconstruction_test_passed: true
  worker_crash_test_passed: true
  at_least_once_delivery_test_passed: true
  idempotent_consumer_test_passed: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RQM-301

Queue state is not authoritative render-task state; the Render Manifest is.

## Invariant RQM-302

Every queue item references exactly one persisted render task.

## Invariant RQM-303

One render-task attempt has at most one active item per queue class.

## Invariant RQM-304

Queue delivery is at least once; all render consumers are idempotent.

## Invariant RQM-305

A cache-hit render task is never enqueued for provider submission.

## Invariant RQM-306

No provider submission is dispatched in violation of provider concurrency or rate limits.

## Invariant RQM-307

Acknowledgement occurs only after authoritative state and outputs are persisted.

## Invariant RQM-308

Retry and provider monitoring are represented through delayed visibility, not busy-waiting.

## Invariant RQM-309

The Control Queue cannot be starved by render workload queues.

## Invariant RQM-310

Budget exhaustion routes work to Manual Review; it never yields a partial published trailer.

## Invariant RQM-311

Queue loss is recoverable from the Render Manifest, retry schedule, leases, and provider records.

## Invariant RQM-312

Dead-lettering preserves attempt evidence and never silently changes a render outcome.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                            |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-476 | The Rendering Platform uses multiple purpose-specific render queues rather than one global queue.                  |
| ADR-477 | Render queue transport is at-least-once; exactly-once effect is achieved through idempotent, cache-keyed submission. |
| ADR-478 | Queue items carry references and routing metadata only — never prompts, secrets, or asset payloads.               |
| ADR-479 | Worker ownership of render tasks is implemented through renewable, time-bounded leases.                            |
| ADR-480 | Render Manifest persistence always precedes queue acknowledgement.                                                 |
| ADR-481 | Provider rate limits and concurrency are enforced at the queue boundary via token buckets, before any API call.   |
| ADR-482 | Cache-hit tasks bypass the queue entirely; cache elimination precedes all queueing.                                |
| ADR-483 | Admission is cost-aware; expensive provider work is deferred under budget pressure in reverse cost-priority order. |
| ADR-484 | Budget exhaustion and sub-threshold quality escalate to Manual Review rather than producing a partial trailer.     |
| ADR-485 | Dead Letter records are immutable evidence requiring explicit reconciliation.                                       |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.4.1** is complete when:

* The Render Queue Manager is formally separated from render-task state, strategy selection, and validation.
* Ready, Submission, Provider Monitor, Download, Validation, Retry, Control, Manual Review, and Dead Letter queues are defined with explicit ownership, routing, and failure behavior.
* Queue items use deterministic identity and reference persisted render tasks without carrying payloads or secrets.
* At-least-once delivery and idempotent consumption are mandatory, with exactly-once provider effect guaranteed through cache-keyed idempotency.
* Deterministic ordering, cost-aware priority, concurrency limits, and provider rate limiting are specified and reproducible.
* Leasing, visibility timeout, renewal, acknowledgement, release, and dead-letter handling are fully defined.
* Render Manifest persistence provably precedes queue acknowledgement.
* Cache-hit tasks bypass the queue and are never submitted to a provider.
* Backpressure and budget-aware admission defer rather than drop work, and escalate to Manual Review on exhaustion.
* Cancellation and graceful drain leave the queue reconstructable with no silently dropped tasks.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gates, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a durable render queue subsystem without treating the queue as the authoritative source of render truth.


---

# Part 5.4.2 — Parallel Execution Model

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Parallel Execution Model** used by the Rendering Platform.

The Parallel Execution Model governs how many render tasks execute **at the same time**, how those tasks remain **independent** of one another, and how their independently produced results **reconcile** into a single authoritative build.

Rendering is fully parallel. Each shot is an independent render task. The pipeline never waits for one shot to finish before submitting the next. The Parallel Execution Model is the discipline that makes this concurrency **safe, bounded, deterministic, and failure-isolated**.

Its purpose is to guarantee that concurrent rendering is:

* fan-out by default — every eligible shot task is dispatched without waiting on any sibling;
* bounded — total concurrency respects the global, per-provider, and per-class limits owned by the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**);
* independent — no shot task depends on the state, success, or output of another shot task;
* failure-isolated — the failure of one shot task can never fail, block, stall, or corrupt another;
* order-preserving in *intent* — scene and shot order influence **priority**, never **blocking**;
* provider-rate-safe — parallel fan-out never exceeds provider concurrency or rate limits (RQ-305);
* reconcilable — independently produced results converge into the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) without races;
* barrier-aware — composition begins only when the manifest reports every required shot READY (the composition barrier).

The Parallel Execution Model does **not** decide *what* to render, *how many* workers to run, or *when* a task is retryable.

It receives already materialized, already ordered, already admitted render tasks from the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**) and defines the concurrency semantics under which independent workers execute them. It never modifies the approved creative plan.

Rendering is an execution layer. The Parallel Execution Model is the **concurrency contract** of that execution layer.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the parallelism model of the Rendering Platform (fan-out, per-shot task independence);
* the worker pool topology and how workers relate to queue classes;
* the concurrency envelope and how it aligns with the limits owned by **5.4.1**;
* task independence and the prohibition of inter-shot dependencies;
* failure isolation between concurrent shot tasks;
* the relationship between narrative ordering and parallelism (priority, not blocking);
* dependency-free execution and the absence of a shot dependency graph;
* provider rate-limit interaction during parallel fan-out (RQ-305);
* how independently produced results reconcile into the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**);
* the composition barrier that separates parallel rendering from serial composition;
* partial-progress semantics, straggler handling, and drain under concurrency;
* parallel-execution events, metrics, configuration, testing, invariants, and decision records.

This section does **not** specify:

* queue topology, leasing, admission, or acknowledgement (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the per-task render lifecycle and transitions (defined in **5.4.3**);
* the manifest schema, persistence, and reconciliation record format (defined in **5.4.4**);
* retry classification and backoff formulas (defined in **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline values (defined in **5.4.6**);
* provider submission payloads and adapter semantics (defined in **5.3.3** and **5.3.4**);
* FFmpeg composition mechanics downstream of the barrier (defined in Part 5.7 integration and Part 6).

The Parallel Execution Model consumes those contracts; it does not own them. It defines only the **concurrency shape** of rendering: how tasks run alongside one another without interfering.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **Every shot renders alone, together. Tasks share capacity, never fate.**

Each shot render task is an isolated unit of work. Tasks execute concurrently and draw from a shared, bounded pool of capacity, but no task's outcome is coupled to any other task's outcome.

A corollary follows directly:

> **The pipeline fans out on submission and joins only at the composition barrier.**

Between fan-out and join there is no synchronization point across shots. A task may fail, retry, or complete at any time relative to its siblings without waiting for, blocking, or being blocked by them. The single, explicit synchronization point in the entire rendering execution is the **composition barrier** — the moment the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) reports that every required shot has reached READY.

If any shot task is delayed, retried, or escalated, other shot tasks continue unaffected. Parallelism is the default; serialization exists only where a limit (concurrency, rate, budget) or the composition barrier explicitly requires it.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Render Task Generation
        │
        ▼
Render Queue Manager (5.4.1)
   admits ordered, independent render tasks
        │
        ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Parallel Execution Model           │
│                                              │
│   Fan-out (no inter-shot wait)               │
│        │                                     │
│        ├── Worker ── Shot A ──► Provider      │
│        ├── Worker ── Shot B ──► Provider      │
│        ├── Worker ── Shot C ──► Provider      │
│        ├── Worker ── Shot D ──► Provider      │
│        └── Worker ── Shot N ──► Provider      │
│        │                                     │
│   bounded by 5.4.1 concurrency envelope      │
│   (global 8 · runway 4 submit / 6 inflight)  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼  each task persists its own result
Render Manifest (5.4.4)  ── authoritative state
        │
        ▼  composition barrier: ALL shots READY
FFmpeg Composer (serial)
```

The Parallel Execution Model sits **between** the Render Queue Manager (which admits and orders tasks) and the Render Manifest (which reconciles results). It does not hold state; it defines how leased tasks run concurrently and how their independent completions converge.

Fan-out happens the instant a task is admitted and capacity exists. Join happens once — at the composition barrier — and only through the manifest, never through direct task-to-task coordination.

---

# Responsibilities

The Parallel Execution Model is responsible for:

* fanning out eligible render tasks concurrently without inter-shot waiting;
* enforcing that each shot is an independent, self-contained unit of work;
* running a bounded worker pool that draws leased tasks from the queue (**5.4.1**);
* respecting the global, per-provider, and per-class concurrency envelope owned by **5.4.1**;
* guaranteeing failure isolation — one task's failure never propagates to another;
* honoring narrative order as a **priority** input, never as a **blocking** dependency;
* ensuring dependency-free execution — no shot dependency graph exists;
* interacting correctly with provider rate limits during fan-out (RQ-305);
* driving each task to persist its own result independently to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**);
* enforcing the composition barrier so serial composition begins only when all shots are READY;
* handling stragglers, partial progress, and drain without dropping or coupling tasks.

It is **not** responsible for:

* queue admission, ordering computation, leasing, or acknowledgement (see **5.4.1**);
* the per-task state machine transitions (see **5.4.3**);
* manifest schema and persistence mechanics (see **5.4.4**);
* deciding whether a failure is retryable (see **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline values (see **5.4.6**);
* provider API call semantics (see **5.3**);
* composition, muxing, or encoding downstream of the barrier (see Part 5.7 / Part 6).

The Parallel Execution Model defines *concurrency behavior*. Every other concern is delegated to the sibling section that owns it.

---

# Parallel Execution Design Objectives

The Parallel Execution Model shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## PEX-301 — Fan-Out By Default

Every render task admitted to the Ready Queue (**5.4.1**) is eligible for immediate dispatch subject only to the concurrency envelope.

The pipeline must never hold an eligible task solely because a sibling shot is still rendering. Submission of Shot *N* does not wait on the completion of Shot *N−1*.

Serialization is permitted **only** where a concurrency limit, provider rate limit, budget threshold, or the composition barrier explicitly requires it.

---

## PEX-302 — Per-Shot Task Independence

Each shot is exactly one render task, and each render task is a self-contained unit of work.

A shot task must be executable using only:

* its own render task reference (from **5.4.4**);
* its own resolved prompt inputs (from **5.5**);
* shared read-only bibles and strategy (character, location, camera, style);
* provider capacity from the shared pool.

A shot task must never read, wait on, or depend on the runtime state or output of any other shot task.

---

## PEX-303 — Bounded Concurrency

Total concurrent rendering must never exceed the concurrency envelope owned by **5.4.1**.

The envelope is authoritative: `global_max_active_renders`, per-provider `max_concurrent_submissions` and `max_inflight_jobs`, and per-class limits. The Parallel Execution Model consumes these values; it does not define its own.

Unbounded fan-out is prohibited. Fan-out is always fan-out *within the envelope*.

---

## PEX-304 — Failure Isolation

The failure of one shot task must have **no effect** on any other shot task.

A failed, timed-out, retrying, dead-lettered, or manually-reviewed task must never:

* fail a sibling task;
* block a sibling task's submission, polling, download, or validation;
* corrupt a sibling task's manifest entry;
* release or invalidate a sibling task's provider job or lease.

Failure is a property of a single task's own lineage in the state machine (**5.4.3**), never a shared event.

---

## PEX-305 — Order Influences Priority, Not Blocking

Scene index and shot index determine **dispatch priority** (the order in which tasks are admitted when capacity is scarce), consistent with the deterministic ordering in **5.4.1**.

Narrative order must never be implemented as a blocking dependency. Shot 4 may complete before Shot 1. Earlier scenes are preferred for dispatch so downstream composition can begin pipelined, but no shot is ever *prevented* from rendering because an earlier shot has not finished.

---

## PEX-306 — Dependency-Free Execution

The Rendering Platform maintains **no shot dependency graph**.

There are no edges between shot tasks: no "render Shot B after Shot A", no shared intermediate artifact between shots, no producer/consumer relationship among concurrent renders. Every shot task is a root with no predecessors and no successors until the single composition join.

Any requirement that would introduce an inter-shot dependency is a specification defect and must be escalated, not encoded.

---

## PEX-307 — Provider Rate Safety Under Fan-Out

Parallel fan-out must never dispatch provider submissions faster than provider concurrency and rate limits allow (RQ-305, **5.4.1**).

The number of *ready* tasks is independent of the number of tasks that may be *in flight* to a provider. Fan-out width is capped by the provider envelope at the queue boundary; excess ready tasks wait via delayed visibility, not by failing.

---

## PEX-308 — Independent Result Reconciliation

Each task persists its own result independently to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**), keyed by its own `render_task_id`.

Concurrent completions must reconcile without races, lost updates, or cross-task interference. There is no shared mutable "results" object that tasks contend over; each task owns exactly one manifest entry.

---

## PEX-309 — Single Join At The Composition Barrier

Composition is the **only** point at which parallel rendering converges.

The barrier is satisfied strictly from the Render Manifest: composition may begin only when every required shot for the build reports READY (or an approved substitute per cache/incremental rules). The barrier reads manifest truth; it never coordinates tasks directly.

---

# The Parallelism Model

The Rendering Platform executes rendering as a **wide fan-out / single join** computation.

```text
                 ┌─── Shot 1 task ──┐
                 ├─── Shot 2 task ──┤
Task Generation ─┼─── Shot 3 task ──┼─► Manifest ─► Composition
                 ├─── Shot 4 task ──┤   (join)      (serial)
                 └─── Shot N task ──┘
                  independent, concurrent
```

Three properties define the model:

**1. Fan-out is immediate and unconditional (subject to capacity).**
When task generation produces the render tasks for a build, all of them are admitted to the queue at once. The Parallel Execution Model then dispatches as many as the concurrency envelope permits, immediately, in priority order. No task waits on a sibling.

**2. The middle is dependency-free.**
Between fan-out and join, there is no cross-shot coordination. Each shot task advances through its own instance of the state machine (**5.4.3**) at its own pace. Tasks neither read nor write each other's state.

**3. The join is singular and manifest-mediated.**
The only convergence is the composition barrier. It is evaluated against the manifest (**5.4.4**), not against the tasks. This keeps the concurrent region free of synchronization and confines all coordination to one well-defined boundary.

This model directly realizes original Principle 3 (Asynchronous Rendering — "Rendering must never block the pipeline") and the original Parallel Execution statement ("The pipeline never waits for one shot before submitting another").

---

# Task Independence Contract

A render task qualifies as independent only when **all** of the following hold.

```yaml
task_independence_contract:
  self_contained_inputs: true       # all inputs resolvable without sibling tasks
  no_sibling_state_reads: true      # never reads another shot task's runtime state
  no_sibling_output_reads: true     # never consumes another shot's produced asset
  own_manifest_entry: true          # writes exactly one render_task_id entry
  no_shared_mutable_state: true     # no contended results/aggregation object
  provider_job_isolation: true      # its provider job is independent of siblings'
  idempotent: true                  # re-execution yields the same effect (RQ-303)
```

Consequences of the contract:

* Any two shot tasks may run in **either order** or **simultaneously** with identical correctness.
* Re-running a single shot task (after crash re-delivery) affects only that shot's entry.
* Removing, cancelling, or dead-lettering one task changes no other task's inputs or outputs.

Shared inputs (character bible, location bible, camera plan, style guide, render strategy) are **read-only** and **immutable** for the duration of a build. Read-only sharing does not create a dependency: it creates no ordering constraint and no contention.

The one prohibited pattern is a **mixed-strategy shot**, which is already forbidden by **5.2.1** (original 5.7): because each shot has exactly one strategy, each shot maps to exactly one provider task, preserving the one-shot / one-task / one-provider-job isolation this contract requires.

---

# Worker Pool Topology

Concurrency is realized by a **bounded worker pool**. Workers are stateless executors that lease tasks from the queue (**5.4.1**), perform one stage of work, persist the result, and acknowledge.

```text
        Render Queue Manager (5.4.1)
        ├── Ready / Submission / Monitor / Download / Validation queues
                     │  (leased items)
                     ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
        │          Worker Pool             │
        │  W1  W2  W3  W4  W5  W6  W7  W8   │  ≤ global_max_active_renders
        └──────────────────────────────────┘
                     │
                     ▼
        Provider Adapters (5.3.4)  ── Runway / Motion / SVG / Typography
```

Worker properties:

* **Stateless.** A worker holds no task identity between leases. All authority lives in the manifest (**5.4.4**) and the queue lease (**5.4.1**).
* **Homogeneous by capability, specialized by lease.** Any worker can execute any stage; the queue class of the leased item determines the stage performed.
* **Bounded.** The number of workers concurrently holding render leases is capped by `global_max_active_renders` and by the per-provider and per-class limits.
* **Crash-safe.** A worker that dies mid-task loses its lease; the item becomes visible again and another worker resumes idempotently (RQ-303, **5.4.1**). No sibling task is affected (PEX-304).

Workers do not communicate with one another. Coordination is entirely mediated by the queue (for capacity and leasing) and the manifest (for state and the barrier). This is what makes the pool horizontally safe: adding or removing a worker changes throughput, never correctness.

---

# Concurrency Envelope

The Parallel Execution Model executes strictly within the concurrency envelope owned by the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**). These values are reproduced here for reference; **5.4.1** is authoritative.

```yaml
concurrency_envelope:            # authoritative source: 5.4.1
  global_max_active_renders: 8

  per_provider:
    runway:
      max_concurrent_submissions: 4    # simultaneous submit() calls in flight
      max_inflight_jobs: 6             # accepted jobs being monitored/downloaded
    motion:
      max_concurrent: 4
    svg:
      max_concurrent: 8
    typography:
      max_concurrent: 8

  per_class:
    DOWNLOAD:
      max_concurrent: 4
    VALIDATION:
      max_concurrent: 4
```

Interpretation for parallel execution:

* **`global_max_active_renders: 8`** — at most eight render tasks occupy an active render lease at once, across all providers and classes. A ninth eligible task waits; it is never failed for lack of headroom.
* **`runway.max_concurrent_submissions: 4`** — at most four Runway `submit()` calls may be in flight simultaneously. This caps the *submission* fan-out width to Runway.
* **`runway.max_inflight_jobs: 6`** — at most six accepted Runway jobs may be in the monitor/download region at once. This caps the *acceptance* fan-out width, which is larger than the submission width because accepted jobs poll cheaply via delayed visibility.

The distinction between `max_concurrent_submissions` (4) and `max_inflight_jobs` (6) is deliberate and load-bearing for parallelism: submission is the expensive, rate-limited action; monitoring is cheap. The model may therefore hold up to six Runway shots *rendering in parallel* while only ever *submitting* four at a time. The submission limit throttles the burst; the inflight limit sizes the parallel width.

The Parallel Execution Model must never widen fan-out beyond the tightest applicable limit. Effective parallelism for a provider is:

```text
effective_parallel_width(provider) =
    min( global_max_active_renders,
         provider.max_inflight_jobs,
         tasks_ready_for_provider )
```

with submission rate additionally bounded by `provider.max_concurrent_submissions` and the provider rate limits (RQ-305, **5.4.1**).

---

# Ordering Versus Parallelism

Narrative order and parallelism coexist without conflict because they operate on different axes:

* **Order** is a *priority* axis. It decides **which** tasks are dispatched first when capacity is scarce.
* **Parallelism** is a *capacity* axis. It decides **how many** tasks run at once.

```text
Scarce capacity (8 slots, 20 ready tasks):
    dispatch priority = scene_index, then shot_index   (5.4.1 ordering)
    → earlier scenes fill the 8 slots first
    → but all 8 run concurrently; none blocks another

Ample capacity (8 slots, 5 ready tasks):
    all 5 dispatch immediately, in any completion order
    → Shot 4 may finish before Shot 1
```

Rules:

1. Dispatch order follows the deterministic priority in **5.4.1** (build priority → cost rank → scene index → shot index → enqueue timestamp).
2. Once dispatched, tasks run fully in parallel; completion order is unconstrained.
3. Earlier scenes are *preferred* for early dispatch to enable pipelined composition readiness, but preference is never a barrier.
4. The composition barrier — not narrative order — determines when the serial join may begin.

This satisfies PEX-305: order shapes priority, never blocking. A late Shot 1 delays only the barrier, and only if Shot 1 is genuinely still pending; it never delays the *rendering* of Shots 2..N.

---

# Dependency-Free Execution

The Rendering Platform deliberately maintains **no dependency graph among shots**.

```text
Prohibited (never modeled):

   Shot A ──► Shot B ──► Shot C      ✗ inter-shot edges

Actual model:

   Shot A     Shot B     Shot C      ✓ independent roots
      └──────────┴──────────┘
                 ▼
          Composition (single join)
```

Why dependency-free execution is mandatory:

* **Determinism.** With no cross-shot edges, the completion of any shot is a pure function of its own inputs and provider behavior, not of scheduling accidents among siblings.
* **Failure isolation.** No edge means no failure can propagate along an edge (PEX-304).
* **Maximal parallelism.** With no predecessors, every task is dispatchable the instant capacity exists (PEX-301).
* **Trivial recovery.** Re-running one shot never invalidates another; reconstruction (RQ-308, **5.4.1**) is per-task.

Shared, immutable, read-only inputs do **not** constitute dependencies. The character bible, location bible, camera plan, render strategy, and style guide are fixed for the build; many tasks read them, none mutate them, and no ordering is implied. This is fan-out over shared constants, not a producer/consumer graph.

If a future creative requirement appears to need Shot B to consume Shot A's output, that requirement violates the rendering boundary (rendering is an execution layer that never creates cross-shot narrative structure) and must be resolved upstream in the Storyboard, not encoded as a render dependency.

---

# Provider Rate-Limit Interaction

Parallel fan-out and provider rate limiting are two controls on the same action — provider submission — and the Parallel Execution Model must reconcile them so that width never violates rate (RQ-305).

```yaml
provider_rate_limits:            # authoritative source: 5.4.1
  runway:
    requests_per_minute: 20
    burst: 5
    min_submission_interval: 3s
```

Interaction model:

1. **Fan-out proposes; the queue disposes.** The Parallel Execution Model may have many ready tasks for Runway. It does not submit them directly; it draws leases through the Submission Queue, which enforces the token bucket and concurrency limits at the boundary before any `submit()` call.
2. **Submission width ≤ `max_concurrent_submissions` (4).** No more than four Runway submissions are ever in flight at once, regardless of how many tasks are ready.
3. **Submission rate ≤ token bucket.** Even within the four-slot submission width, dequeue consumes a rate token; an empty bucket defers the next submission via delayed visibility (**5.4.1**), not by failing the task.
4. **Throttle feedback narrows fan-out, not correctness.** A provider 429 or throttle signal reduces the refill rate; the effective parallel submission width contracts smoothly. This is flow control, not an error path (RQ-305).
5. **Inflight width absorbs the difference.** Because up to six Runway jobs may be inflight while only four submit concurrently, the parallel *rendering* width stays high even while submission is rate-throttled. Submission is the choke point; monitoring is not.

The net guarantee: parallel fan-out can be arbitrarily wide in *intent*, but the realized submission concurrency and rate never exceed the provider envelope. The queue is the single enforcement point; the Parallel Execution Model never bypasses it to submit directly.

---

# Independent Result Reconciliation

Each shot task reconciles its own result into the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) independently. There is no shared results aggregator that concurrent tasks contend over.

```text
Shot A task ─► persist manifest[A] = {status, asset_uri, validation, attempt}
Shot B task ─► persist manifest[B] = {...}          (concurrent, no contention)
Shot C task ─► persist manifest[C] = {...}
                     │
                     ▼
        Render Manifest (5.4.4): one entry per render_task_id
```

Reconciliation rules:

* **One writer per entry.** Exactly one render task owns each `render_task_id` manifest entry. Concurrent tasks write disjoint entries, so there is no lost-update race across shots.
* **Persist-then-acknowledge.** Consistent with the acknowledgement protocol in **5.4.1**, a task persists its manifest result *before* acknowledging its queue item. A crash between persist and ack triggers idempotent re-delivery (RQ-303), which observes the already-persisted entry and safely re-acknowledges.
* **Monotonic per-task progress.** Within a single task's entry, state advances monotonically along the state machine (**5.4.3**); a stale re-delivery never regresses a more-advanced entry.
* **No cross-task aggregation during rendering.** Aggregate build state (how many shots are READY) is *derived* from the per-entry states by the barrier evaluator; it is never a separately maintained mutable counter that tasks race to update.

Because reconciliation is per-entry and persist-then-acknowledge, arbitrary interleavings of concurrent completions produce the same final manifest as any serial ordering — the correctness property that makes wide parallelism safe.

---

# The Composition Barrier

The composition barrier is the single synchronization point of the rendering execution. It converts the parallel render region into the serial composition region.

```text
Parallel render region                 Serial region
──────────────────────────────         ─────────────────
Shot 1 ─► READY ┐
Shot 2 ─► READY ┤
Shot 3 ─► READY ┤── barrier: ALL required shots READY ──► Composition
Shot 4 ─► READY ┤        (evaluated from 5.4.4)
Shot N ─► READY ┘
```

Barrier semantics:

```yaml
composition_barrier:
  evaluated_from: render_manifest        # 5.4.4 is the sole source of truth
  satisfied_when:
    all_required_shots_ready: true       # every required shot READY (or CACHED/substituted)
  required_shot: any shot in the approved build not eliminated by cache/incremental rules
  on_partial:
    action: wait                         # barrier holds; rendering continues in parallel
  on_unrecoverable_shot:
    action: manual_review                # Fail Closed (Principle 5); no partial trailer
  reads_tasks_directly: false            # never coordinates tasks; only reads manifest
```

Barrier rules:

1. **Manifest-mediated only.** The barrier evaluates the manifest, never the live tasks. It has no knowledge of workers, leases, or provider jobs.
2. **All-or-hold.** Composition begins only when *every* required shot is READY. If any required shot is not yet READY, the barrier holds and parallel rendering continues; siblings are not blocked (PEX-305).
3. **Fail Closed on unrecoverable shots.** If a required shot cannot reach READY after exhausting retries (**5.4.5**) and manual review, the build does not compose a partial trailer. It escalates to Manual Review (Principle 5, consistent with **5.4.1** RQ-306/RQM-310).
4. **Cache and incremental substitution honored.** A shot satisfied by a cache hit (**5.6.3**) or an unchanged previously-approved asset (Principle 4, Incremental Rendering) counts as READY for the barrier without re-rendering.
5. **Single crossing.** The barrier is crossed at most once per build; composition is serial and downstream (Part 5.7 / Part 6).

The barrier is what makes "fan out on submission, join only at composition" (the governing corollary) concrete and enforceable.

---

# Partial Progress, Stragglers & Drain

Because tasks are independent and unsynchronized, the model must define behavior when tasks progress unevenly.

**Partial progress.** At any instant a build may have shots in mixed states — some READY, some RUNNING, some RETRY. This is normal and expected. Partial progress never triggers composition (all-or-hold barrier) and never couples tasks. The manifest reflects the true mixed state at all times.

**Stragglers.** A slow shot (a long Runway job, a retry, a throttled submission) is a straggler. Stragglers delay only the barrier, and only if they are genuinely required and not yet READY. They never delay sibling *rendering*. Straggler handling is governed by the timeout and deadline policy (**5.4.6**): a straggler that exceeds `overall_timeout` (45m) is failed and re-classified (**5.4.5**), not left to stall the build indefinitely.

**Drain.** On graceful drain (e.g. GitHub Actions runner shutdown), the Parallel Execution Model:

```yaml
drain_behavior:
  stop_new_fan_out: true            # no new tasks leased for submission
  allow_inflight_to_checkpoint: true # inflight tasks persist state, then release
  persist_all_progress: true         # every task's manifest entry is durable
  no_task_coupling: true             # draining one task never fails another
  reconstructable: true              # remaining work rebuildable from 5.4.4 + queue (5.4.1)
```

Drain stops *fan-out*, lets inflight tasks checkpoint their independent progress to the manifest, and leaves the build fully reconstructable. Because tasks are independent (PEX-302) and results reconcile per-entry (PEX-308), a drained build resumes by simply re-fanning-out the not-yet-READY shots. No shot is silently dropped, and no partial trailer is produced.

---

# Events

The Parallel Execution Model emits events for every material concurrency transition.

```yaml
parallel_execution_events:
  - render_fanout_started            # build's tasks admitted for concurrent dispatch
  - render_task_dispatched           # a task acquired a render slot in the envelope
  - render_slot_saturated            # global/provider/class limit reached; tasks waiting
  - render_slot_released             # a task freed a render slot
  - provider_submission_throttled    # fan-out narrowed by rate limit (RQ-305)
  - render_task_result_reconciled    # a task's own manifest entry persisted (5.4.4)
  - straggler_detected               # a required shot exceeds expected duration window
  - composition_barrier_pending      # not all required shots READY yet
  - composition_barrier_satisfied    # all required shots READY; serial region may begin
  - composition_barrier_blocked      # unrecoverable shot; escalated to Manual Review
  - fanout_drained                   # drain complete; build reconstructable
```

Every event references the `build_id`, and where task-scoped, the `render_task_id`, `shot_id`, `provider`, and `attempt`. Events are durably recorded for audit and replay, consistent with the observability model in **5.7.1**.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml
parallel_execution_metrics:
  active_render_slots            # currently occupied slots vs global_max_active_renders
  provider_inflight_jobs         # per provider, vs max_inflight_jobs
  provider_concurrent_submissions # per provider, vs max_concurrent_submissions
  fanout_width                   # tasks dispatched concurrently for the build
  ready_but_waiting_for_slot     # eligible tasks deferred by the envelope
  submission_throttle_events     # fan-out narrowings due to rate limits
  per_shot_render_duration       # distribution across concurrent shots
  straggler_count                # required shots exceeding expected window
  barrier_wait_time              # time from first-READY to all-READY
  shots_ready / shots_required   # barrier progress ratio
  build_wall_clock_vs_serial     # parallel speedup vs hypothetical serial time
```

Metrics feed the SLO review and the performance analysis in **5.7.1**. The `build_wall_clock_vs_serial` ratio quantifies the value of parallelism: with a bounded pool of eight, a many-shot build should approach an eightfold reduction in render wall-clock relative to serial execution, subject to provider limits.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The Parallel Execution Model inherits the least-privilege posture of the queue and workers (**5.4.1**).

Required controls:

* **Build namespace isolation.** Concurrent builds run in isolated namespaces; a worker executing Build X's shot can never observe Build Y's tasks, entries, or provider jobs.
* **No cross-task secret sharing.** A worker resolves provider credentials for the single task it holds, at execution time, from the secret store — never from a shared or sibling context.
* **No shared mutable execution state.** Because there is no cross-task shared object, there is no shared-state channel for tamper or leakage between shots.
* **Independent audit lineage.** Each task's events and manifest writes carry its own `render_task_id` lineage; concurrency never merges audit trails across shots.
* **Least-privilege pool.** Workers hold only the provider-adapter access needed for the leased stage; the pool grants no ambient authority.

Failure isolation (PEX-304) is also a security property: a compromised or misbehaving single task cannot corrupt sibling tasks because it shares capacity, not state or fate.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="pex-config"
parallel_execution:
  # concurrency envelope is owned by 5.4.1; referenced here, not redefined
  envelope_source: render_queue.concurrency      # 5.4.1 authoritative
  fanout:
    mode: immediate                # dispatch on admission within the envelope
    inter_shot_wait: prohibited    # PEX-301
    dependency_graph: none         # PEX-306
  worker_pool:
    max_workers: 8                 # aligned to global_max_active_renders (5.4.1)
    stateless: true
    crash_resumable: true          # idempotent re-delivery (RQ-303)
  ordering:
    priority_source: render_queue.priority_score  # 5.4.1
    blocking_on_order: false       # PEX-305
  barrier:
    require_all_shots_ready: true  # PEX-309
    on_unrecoverable: manual_review # Fail Closed (Principle 5)
  straggler:
    overall_timeout: 45m           # from 5.4.6
  drain:
    stop_new_fanout: true
    checkpoint_inflight: true
```

All values are configurable and environment-overridable. Any value duplicated from **5.4.1** or **5.4.6** must remain consistent with those authoritative sources; configuration validation at startup fails closed on a mismatch rather than dispatching with a conflicting envelope.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* fan-out dispatches all eligible tasks up to the envelope, with no inter-shot wait;
* effective parallel width computed as `min(global, provider_inflight, ready)`;
* task independence contract holds (no sibling state/output reads);
* order affects dispatch priority but never blocks a lower-priority shot from rendering;
* no dependency graph is constructed for any build;
* barrier evaluates satisfied only when all required shots are READY;
* barrier honors cache/incremental substitutions as READY;
* drain stops fan-out and checkpoints inflight tasks.

## Integration Tests

* many-shot build fans out to eight concurrent renders and reconciles all entries;
* Runway build holds ≤4 concurrent submissions and ≤6 inflight jobs simultaneously;
* completion order differs from narrative order without affecting correctness;
* barrier holds while stragglers render, then releases on all-READY;
* mixed-provider build parallelizes SVG/typography (width 8) alongside Runway (width ≤6);
* parallel results converge to the same manifest as a serial replay.

## Concurrency Tests

* eight workers reconcile eight disjoint manifest entries with no lost updates;
* two workers re-deliver the same task after crash; only that shot's entry is touched;
* concurrent completion and barrier evaluation produce a consistent shots_ready count;
* saturating the global limit defers the ninth task without failing it;
* provider submission slots and inflight slots enforced independently.

## Failure Injection Tests

* one shot task fails permanently; all sibling tasks complete unaffected (PEX-304);
* Runway 429 throttle narrows submission fan-out; inflight rendering width preserved;
* worker crash mid-render leaves siblings and their provider jobs intact;
* a straggler exceeds overall_timeout; it is failed and re-classified without stalling siblings;
* an unrecoverable required shot blocks the barrier and escalates to Manual Review (no partial trailer).

## Recovery Tests

* drained build resumes by re-fanning-out only not-yet-READY shots;
* per-task reconstruction from the manifest yields an identical build state;
* barrier re-evaluates correctly after recovery from a persisted manifest;
* re-delivered task after persist-before-ack crash re-acknowledges idempotently.

---

# Parallel Execution Quality Gate

```yaml
parallel_execution_quality_gate:
  fanout_has_no_inter_shot_wait: true
  every_shot_is_one_independent_task: true
  no_shot_dependency_graph_exists: true
  concurrency_within_envelope: true
  runway_submission_width_le_4: true
  runway_inflight_width_le_6: true
  global_active_le_8: true
  order_affects_priority_not_blocking: true
  failure_isolation_verified: true
  results_reconcile_per_entry: true
  barrier_requires_all_shots_ready: true
  barrier_reads_manifest_only: true
  unrecoverable_shot_escalates_not_partial: true
  drain_leaves_build_reconstructable: true
  provider_rate_limits_respected_under_fanout: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant PEX-401

Rendering fans out by default; an eligible task is never held because a sibling shot is still rendering.

## Invariant PEX-402

Each shot is exactly one independent render task; a task never reads or depends on another shot task's state or output.

## Invariant PEX-403

Total concurrency never exceeds the envelope owned by **5.4.1** (global 8, Runway 4 submit / 6 inflight, per-class limits).

## Invariant PEX-404

The failure of one shot task never fails, blocks, or corrupts any other shot task.

## Invariant PEX-405

Scene and shot order influence dispatch priority only; they are never implemented as a blocking dependency.

## Invariant PEX-406

The Rendering Platform maintains no inter-shot dependency graph; every shot task is a dependency-free root.

## Invariant PEX-407

Parallel fan-out never dispatches provider submissions in violation of provider concurrency or rate limits (RQ-305).

## Invariant PEX-408

Each task reconciles its own manifest entry (**5.4.4**) independently; concurrent completions never race a shared results object.

## Invariant PEX-409

The composition barrier is the single join; it is evaluated solely from the Render Manifest and never coordinates tasks directly.

## Invariant PEX-410

Composition begins only when every required shot is READY; an unrecoverable required shot escalates to Manual Review and never yields a partial published trailer (Fail Closed).

## Invariant PEX-411

Drain stops fan-out and checkpoints inflight tasks, leaving the build reconstructable with no shot silently dropped and no task coupled to another's drain.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                    |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-486 | Rendering executes as a wide fan-out / single-join computation: shots render fully in parallel and converge only at the composition barrier. |
| ADR-487 | Each shot is one independent, dependency-free render task; the platform maintains no inter-shot dependency graph.          |
| ADR-488 | Concurrency is bounded by the envelope owned by 5.4.1; parallelism is always fan-out within that envelope, never unbounded. |
| ADR-489 | Narrative order sets dispatch priority only and is never encoded as a blocking dependency between shots.                    |
| ADR-490 | Independently produced results reconcile per-entry into the Render Manifest (5.4.4) with persist-before-acknowledge, so any interleaving equals a serial replay. |
| ADR-491 | The composition barrier is the sole synchronization point, evaluated from the manifest; an unrecoverable required shot escalates to Manual Review rather than composing a partial trailer. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.4.2** is complete when:

* Rendering is defined as fully parallel: every eligible shot task fans out without waiting on any sibling.
* Each shot is formally one independent, self-contained, dependency-free render task, with an explicit task independence contract.
* The bounded worker pool and its stateless, crash-safe workers are specified, drawing leased tasks from the queue (**5.4.1**).
* Concurrency is bounded strictly by the envelope owned by **5.4.1** — global 8, Runway 4 concurrent submissions / 6 inflight jobs, per-class limits — with effective parallel width defined against those limits.
* Failure isolation is guaranteed: one task's failure, retry, or escalation never affects a sibling.
* Narrative order is shown to influence dispatch priority only, never blocking, and the absence of any shot dependency graph is mandated.
* Provider rate-limit interaction under fan-out is specified so submission width and rate never exceed the provider envelope (RQ-305), with inflight width absorbing throttled submission.
* Independent per-entry result reconciliation into the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) is defined with persist-before-acknowledge and no shared mutable results object.
* The composition barrier is defined as the single manifest-mediated join, holding until all required shots are READY and escalating unrecoverable shots to Manual Review (Fail Closed) rather than producing a partial trailer.
* Partial progress, straggler handling, and drain are specified without coupling or dropping tasks, leaving builds reconstructable.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gates, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a bounded, failure-isolated, dependency-free parallel rendering executor that references — and never contradicts — the queue (**5.4.1**), state machine (**5.4.3**), and manifest (**5.4.4**).


---

# Part 5.4.3 — Render Task State Machine

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Render Task State Machine** used by the Rendering Platform.

The Render Task State Machine is the single, explicit finite-state model that governs the lifecycle of every **render task** from the moment it is generated out of the approved Storyboard until it is either composed into the trailer or escalated for human decision.

A render task is the unit of rendering work: exactly one render task exists per shot per render profile, produced from the approved Storyboard and the assigned Render Strategy (see **5.2.1**). The state machine describes *where in its lifecycle* each such task currently is, *how* it may move, and *what must be true* for each move to occur.

The state machine exists to make render execution:

* explicit — every task is in exactly one enumerated state at all times;
* deterministic — identical inputs and events produce identical state paths;
* guarded — every transition is gated by an evaluable precondition;
* idempotent — re-applying a transition never corrupts state;
* durable — every transition is persisted to the Render Manifest before it is acknowledged;
* recoverable — the full lifecycle is reconstructable from persisted state alone;
* fail-closed — no task reaches a published outcome unless every prior gate passed.

The Render Task State Machine does **not** move tasks by itself. It is a passive, normative model. The actors that drive transitions — the Render Queue Manager (**5.4.1**), the Parallel Execution workers (**5.4.2**), the provider adapters (**5.3.2**, **5.3.4**), the validators (**5.6.1**, **5.6.2**), and the Composer — each apply transitions **through** this model. The model defines which moves are legal; the actors decide when to attempt them.

Rendering is an execution layer. The state machine is the contract that keeps that execution layer legible, replayable, and safe. It never modifies the approved story, screenplay, or storyboard; it only records and constrains the progression of execution.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the full render task **state enum** and the meaning of every state;
* a canonical **state table** — meaning, entry conditions, exit conditions, and allowed successors for each state;
* the **transition diagram** for the success path and the failure path;
* the **events** that drive transitions and the **guards** that gate them;
* the distinction between **terminal** and **non-terminal** states;
* **idempotent transition** semantics;
* the separation of **attempt** count from **state** (attempt vs state);
* the relationship between the fine-grained state machine (NEW → COMPOSED) and the coarse **render lifecycle** projection (Pending → Validated) from **5.12**;
* the **mapping of normalized provider status** (**5.3.2**) onto render task state;
* the **persistence contract** by which every transition is written to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**).

This section does **not** specify:

* queue topology, admission, leasing, or dispatch (defined in **5.4.1**);
* the degree and bounding of parallel execution (defined in **5.4.2**);
* whether a given failure is **retryable**, the retry classification taxonomy, or backoff timing (defined in **5.4.5**);
* timeout and deadline **values** that trigger the `TIMEOUT` event (defined in **5.4.6**);
* the provider API payloads or transport used to obtain provider status (defined in **5.3.3**);
* the manifest **record layout** and storage medium (defined in **5.4.4**);
* validation **logic** or scoring thresholds (defined in **5.6.1** and **5.6.2**).

The state machine **consumes** those contracts. It classifies nothing, times nothing, and stores nothing on its own; it defines the states those subsystems move a task between.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **A render task is always in exactly one known state, and it moves only along declared, guarded, persisted transitions.**

There is no implicit state, no derived-only state, and no "in between" state. If a process crashes mid-operation, the task's state is whatever was last durably persisted — never an ambiguous middle value. The state machine has no hidden nodes and no undocumented edges.

A corollary follows directly:

> **State is what the manifest says it is — not what a worker, a queue entry, or a provider believes.**

A worker's in-memory belief, a queue item's routing class, and a provider's reported job status are all **inputs** to a transition decision. None of them **is** the task's state. The authoritative state lives in the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**), and every actor reads it there. If the three ever disagree, the manifest wins and reconciliation is explicit (see **5.4.1** Dead Letter / Manual Review routing).

This principle is what makes rendering deterministic (Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism) and fail-closed (Design Philosophy Principle 5): a task cannot be quietly declared finished, and it cannot silently regress.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Storyboard (Approved)
        │
        ▼
Render Strategy Planner (5.2)
        │
        ▼
Render Task Generation ── creates task in state NEW
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Render Task State Machine            │  ← THIS SECTION (5.4.3)
│  (normative FSM; no execution of its own)      │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        ▲            ▲            ▲            ▲
        │            │            │            │
   applies      applies      applies      applies
 transitions  transitions  transitions  transitions
        │            │            │            │
 Queue Mgr     Exec Workers  Provider      Validators
  (5.4.1)       (5.4.2)      Adapters       (5.6)
                              (5.3.2)
        │            │            │            │
        └────────────┴─────┬──────┴────────────┘
                           ▼
              Render Manifest (5.4.4)
        (single source of task-state truth;
         every transition persisted here)
                           │
                           ▼
                   FFmpeg Composer ── drives READY → COMPOSED
```

The state machine sits at the centre of the execution layer. It is downstream of planning (which produces tasks in `NEW`) and it is the shared vocabulary of every execution actor. No actor invents its own lifecycle; each applies the transitions defined here and persists the result to the manifest.

The queue (**5.4.1**) governs *when* a task is worked and *where* it is transported; the state machine governs *what the task is* at each point and *which moves are legal*. Queue class and task state are correlated but distinct concepts (see the mapping later in this section).

---

# Responsibilities

The Render Task State Machine is responsible for:

* enumerating every legal render task state exhaustively;
* defining the entry and exit conditions of each state;
* defining the complete set of allowed transitions (edges) and forbidding all others;
* naming the events that trigger transitions and the guards that gate them;
* classifying each state as terminal or non-terminal;
* defining idempotent re-application semantics for every transition;
* separating the render **attempt** counter from the render **state**;
* defining how normalized provider status (**5.3.2**) projects onto task state;
* defining how the coarse render lifecycle labels (**5.12**) project from the fine-grained states;
* mandating persistence of every transition to the manifest before acknowledgement.

It is **not** responsible for:

* deciding when to attempt a transition (owned by the execution actors — **5.4.1**, **5.4.2**);
* selecting render strategy (see **5.2**);
* determining whether a failure is retryable or computing backoff (see **5.4.5**);
* setting timeout values (see **5.4.6**);
* invoking provider APIs or interpreting raw provider payloads (see **5.3.3**);
* performing asset or visual validation (see **5.6**);
* storing the manifest record or choosing its medium (see **5.4.4**);
* composing the final trailer (Composer, Part 5.7 / downstream).

---

# State-Machine Design Objectives

The Render Task State Machine shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RTS-301 — Total Enumeration

Every render task must, at every instant, be in exactly one state drawn from a **closed, finite enum**. No task may be in zero states, two states, or an unnamed state. Adding a new state requires a specification change; it can never arise implicitly at runtime.

---

## RTS-302 — Single Authoritative State

A task's current state is a single value persisted in the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**). Queue class, worker memory, and provider job status are **not** the task's state; they are transition inputs. Where they disagree with the manifest, the manifest is authoritative.

---

## RTS-303 — Guarded Transitions Only

Every transition must be gated by an explicit, evaluable **guard**. A transition whose guard is false must never be applied. There are no unconditional edges except the initial creation of `NEW` at task generation.

---

## RTS-304 — Idempotent Transitions

Re-applying a transition that has already been applied must be a **no-op** that yields the same state, not an error and not a double effect. This is required because delivery is at-least-once (**5.4.1**, Invariant RQM-304) and workers may replay a transition after a crash.

---

## RTS-305 — Attempt / State Separation

The render **attempt** count is orthogonal to the render **state**. Retrying a failed task re-enters the state machine at a submission state and increments `attempt`; it does **not** create per-attempt state duplicates. The enum size is independent of `max_retry`.

---

## RTS-306 — Deterministic Reachability

From any given state, the set of reachable successor states under a given event and guard evaluation is fixed and reproducible. Given identical persisted state and identical events, two independent workers compute the identical next state. Random or nondeterministic transitions are prohibited (Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism).

---

## RTS-307 — Fail-Closed Terminality
