Completeness is checked *after* normalization, because normalization can reveal an effectively empty component (for example, a field that was only whitespace). A mandatory component that normalizes to empty is a failure.

Completeness failures are distinct from article-freedom failures and are reported with a typed reason so that observability can distinguish "prompt was incomplete" from "prompt was contaminated." Both fail closed; both escalate; neither ever submits.

The Assembler must never substitute a default, placeholder, or inferred value for a missing mandatory component. Fabricating a character description, a location, a camera move, or an emotional beat would violate both purity (PMA-301) and the responsibilities boundary (rendering is execution, not authorship).

---

# Component Injection Model

The four reference-resolved components enter the prompt through their owning sub-sections. The Assembler orchestrates injection but does not own the resolution logic.

| Component | Injected via | Owning section | Assembler obligation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| CHARACTER | `character.ref` → bible entry | **5.5.2** | resolve by ref at pinned version; never inline; never edit |
| LOCATION | `location.ref` → bible entry | **5.5.3** | resolve by ref at pinned version; environment immutable |
| CAMERA | camera plan params | **5.5.4** | copy verbatim; never override lens/movement/angle |
| VISUAL_STYLE | style profile | **5.5.5** | resolve + submit to enforcement; reject forbidden styles |

Injection rules:

* **Character** (forward-ref **5.5.2**): characters are referenced by ID (for example `maya_v1`). The Assembler passes the ref to the character injector, which returns the canonical description. The Assembler never accepts an inline character description that bypasses the bible, and never modifies the returned text.
* **Location** (forward-ref **5.5.3**): locations are immutable and referenced by ID (for example `operations_center_night`). Environment consistency is guaranteed by resolving the same ref to the same description at the pinned version.
* **Camera** (forward-ref **5.5.4**): camera parameters (lens, movement, angle) originate only from the Shot Planner and are authoritative. The Assembler copies them verbatim into the CAMERA component. Providers — and the Assembler itself — may not override these values.
* **Visual Style** (forward-ref **5.5.5**): the style profile is resolved and passed through style enforcement. If enforcement flags a forbidden style (anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms, humanoid robots, exaggerated sci-fi interfaces), assembly fails closed before a prompt is produced.

When any injector cannot resolve its reference — an unknown character ref, a missing location, an absent camera plan entry, or a style that fails enforcement — the Assembler fails closed (PMA-305). It does not proceed with a partial or improvised component.

---

# Prompt Identity & Hashing

Every assembled prompt has a deterministic identity: the **prompt hash**.

```text id="pma-hash"
prompt_hash = sha256( canonical_text )

where canonical_text is the exact, normalized, fixed-order template
rendering of the prompt (UTF-8, NFC, LF), and is byte-identical to the
text handed to task submission.
```

Properties:

* **Deterministic** — same components in same order ⇒ same canonical text ⇒ same hash.
* **Collision-resistant** — SHA-256 over the full canonical text.
* **Total** — computed over *all* prompt content, so any component change changes the hash.
* **Stable across time and host** — because inputs are pinned and formatting is fixed.

The prompt hash is a required input to the **render cache key** (**5.6.3**). The cache key combines the shot's identity with provider, provider version, render profile, and the prompt hash, so that a cached asset is reused only when the exact same prompt would be submitted to the exact same provider configuration. If the prompt changes in any component, the prompt hash changes, the cache key changes, and the cache correctly misses — forcing a fresh render.

This is why prompt determinism is a *correctness* property, not merely a tidiness property. If assembly were nondeterministic, the same shot would hash differently across runs, the cache would never hit, and rendering cost (Runway being the most expensive resource) would balloon. Determinism (Performance Constraint 2) makes the cache sound and cost optimization (**5.2.3**) effective.

The relationship, stated precisely:

```text
same shot artifacts (pinned)
   ⇒ same components
   ⇒ same canonical_text
   ⇒ same prompt_hash
   ⇒ same render cache key (5.6.3)
   ⇒ cache hit ⇒ no re-render (5.2 Principle 4, incremental rendering)
```

---

# Assembly Failure Model

Assembly failures are typed, and every one fails closed.

```yaml id="pma-failures"
assembly_failures:
  UNRESOLVED_CHARACTER_REF:   # character.ref not found in bible (5.5.2)
  UNRESOLVED_LOCATION_REF:    # location.ref not found in bible (5.5.3)
  MISSING_CAMERA_PARAMS:      # no camera plan entry for shot (5.5.4)
  FORBIDDEN_STYLE:            # style rejected by enforcement (5.5.5)
  MISSING_MANDATORY_COMPONENT:# completeness failure (PMA-305)
  EMPTY_AFTER_NORMALIZATION:  # component normalized to empty
  ARTICLE_CONTAMINATION:      # article-freedom check failed (PMA-303)
  PINNED_VERSION_UNAVAILABLE: # recorded bible/style version not retrievable
on_any_failure:
  emit_no_prompt: true
  route: MANUAL_REVIEW        # via 5.4.1 Manual Review Queue
  mutate_creative_plan: false # never auto-edit storyboard content
```

No failure results in a submitted prompt. No failure is repaired by the Assembler inventing content. Every failure is recorded with its type and the offending `shot_id` for audit, then escalated. This mirrors the Fail Closed principle: the platform would rather stop and ask for a human decision than ship a trailer built on a defective prompt.

Distinguishing failure types matters for triage: `UNRESOLVED_*` and `MISSING_*` indicate an upstream artifact gap; `FORBIDDEN_STYLE` indicates a style violation to be corrected in the style profile; `ARTICLE_CONTAMINATION` indicates a storyboard component authored in editorial rather than cinematic terms. Each points to a different upstream fix.

---

# Events

The Prompt Assembler emits events for every material outcome.

```yaml id="pma-events"
prompt_assembly_events:
  - prompt_assembly_started
  - references_resolved
  - components_collected
  - prompt_normalized
  - prompt_completeness_verified
  - prompt_article_freedom_verified
  - prompt_hashed
  - prompt_assembled                 # success; carries prompt_hash
  - prompt_assembly_failed           # carries typed failure reason
  - prompt_escalated_manual_review
```

Every event references `shot_id`, `build_id`, `render_profile`, `provider`, and — on success — `prompt_hash`. Events are durably recorded so that any prompt can be reconstructed and audited. Because assembly is pure, an audit can re-run assembly on the recorded pinned inputs and confirm the same `prompt_hash`, giving end-to-end reproducibility.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml id="pma-metrics"
prompt_assembly_metrics:
  prompts_assembled_total
  prompt_assembly_failures_total
  assembly_failures_by_type          # UNRESOLVED_*, ARTICLE_CONTAMINATION, ...
  article_contamination_events       # should trend to zero
  incomplete_prompt_events
  forbidden_style_rejections         # from 5.5.5 during assembly
  distinct_prompt_hashes             # supports cache-hit correlation
  prompt_hash_reuse_rate             # correlates with cache hit rate (≥70% SLO)
  assembly_duration                  # pure compute time; no network
```

`article_contamination_events` is a safety metric and should trend to zero; any nonzero value indicates an upstream authoring defect that let editorial content into a storyboard component. `prompt_hash_reuse_rate` correlates with the cache hit rate SLO (≥ 70%) tracked in **5.6.3**, because identical prompt hashes are what enable cache reuse.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The Prompt Assembler operates under least privilege and enforces the content boundary.

Required controls:

* the Assembler reads storyboard artifacts and pinned bible/style content only — never the raw article body;
* the article body is not present in, nor required by, the rendering environment; article-freedom validation uses fingerprints (hashes), not the article text;
* the canonical prompt contains no provider secrets, credentials, or API tokens (those are attached downstream by the adapter, **5.3.4**);
* provenance metadata is retained for audit but never serialized into the submitted prompt;
* every assembly is auditable and reproducible from pinned inputs;
* article-freedom validation is mandatory and cannot be disabled by configuration.

The content firewall is a security control, not merely a formatting rule: it guarantees that a third-party rendering provider never receives the confidential source text or its metadata. Combined with provider independence (**5.3.1**), it ensures no provider can reconstruct the article from render traffic.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="pma-config"
prompt_assembly:
  schema_version: pma-1
  component_order:               # fixed; changing it is a schema_version bump
    - CHARACTER
    - LOCATION
    - CAMERA
    - LIGHTING
    - BLOCKING
    - EMOTION
    - SHOT_PURPOSE
    - VISUAL_STYLE
  mandatory_components:
    [CHARACTER, LOCATION, CAMERA, LIGHTING, EMOTION, SHOT_PURPOSE, VISUAL_STYLE]
  optional_components: [BLOCKING]
  normalization:
    unicode_form: NFC
    newline: LF
    collapse_internal_whitespace: true
    numeric_policy: verbatim
  article_freedom:
    enabled: true                # cannot be disabled
    max_matching_shingles: 0
  hashing:
    algorithm: sha256
    input: canonical_text
  determinism:
    no_wall_clock: true
    no_random: true
    pinned_inputs: true
```

All values are validated at startup. Invalid configuration fails closed and blocks assembly rather than assembling with unsafe defaults. The `article_freedom.enabled` flag is fixed true and is rejected if set false, because disabling the content firewall is never a permissible configuration. Any change to `component_order` or `normalization` requires a `schema_version` bump, since it would change historical prompt hashes and therefore cache identity (**5.6.3**).

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* byte-identical output for repeated assembly of the same shot (PMA-301);
* identical prompt hash across two processes and two hosts;
* fixed component order regardless of input field order;
* `BLOCKING` omission produces a well-defined, distinct canonical text;
* normalization idempotence (normalize∘normalize = normalize);
* camera parameters copied verbatim, never reformatted (**5.5.4**);
* completeness failure on each missing mandatory component;
* empty-after-normalization detected as incomplete;
* prompt hash changes when any single component changes;
* provenance recorded for every component (PMA-308).

## Integration Tests

* character/location refs resolved via **5.5.2**/**5.5.3** at pinned versions;
* forbidden style rejected during assembly via **5.5.5**;
* prompt hash flows correctly into the render cache key (**5.6.3**);
* canonical text handed to submission equals hashed canonical text (**5.3.3**);
* pinned bible edit does not change an already-approved shot's prompt hash;
* provider adapter receives no article content (**5.3.4**).

## Determinism Tests

* same shot assembled 1000× yields one distinct hash;
* assembly output invariant under changed locale, timezone, and env vars;
* assembly output invariant under shuffled input map/dict ordering;
* CRLF vs LF source inputs converge to identical canonical text.

## Article-Freedom / Failure Injection Tests

* injected verbatim article sentence in a component ⇒ ARTICLE_CONTAMINATION, no submission;
* single-shingle article overlap fails (zero tolerance);
* headline/byline/url tokens in a component ⇒ rejection;
* unresolved character ref ⇒ UNRESOLVED_CHARACTER_REF, escalation;
* missing camera plan entry ⇒ MISSING_CAMERA_PARAMS, escalation;
* pinned bible version unavailable ⇒ PINNED_VERSION_UNAVAILABLE, no fallback to latest.

## Recovery / Reproducibility Tests

* re-assemble from recorded pinned inputs reproduces the audited prompt hash;
* audit replay confirms article-free and complete verification flags;
* escalated shot leaves no partial prompt in any downstream store.

---

# Prompt Assembly Quality Gate

```yaml id="pma-quality-gate"
prompt_assembly_quality_gate:
  assembly_is_pure_function: true
  same_inputs_same_prompt_hash: true
  component_order_fixed: true
  all_mandatory_components_present: true
  no_article_text_in_prompt: true
  article_freedom_check_enabled: true
  zero_article_shingle_tolerance: true
  camera_params_verbatim: true
  references_resolved_at_pinned_versions: true
  prompt_hash_feeds_cache_key: true
  no_partial_prompt_ever_submitted: true
  failures_escalate_never_fabricate: true
  determinism_test_passed: true
  article_freedom_test_passed: true
  reproducibility_replay_passed: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant PMA-401

Prompt assembly is a pure function of pinned storyboard artifacts; identical inputs yield byte-identical prompts and identical prompt hashes.

## Invariant PMA-402

The eight prompt components always appear in the fixed order Character, Location, Camera, Lighting, Blocking, Emotion, Shot Purpose, Visual Style.

## Invariant PMA-403

No article text, story prose, screenplay narrative, or publication metadata ever appears in a submitted prompt.

## Invariant PMA-404

Character, location, camera, and style content enters a prompt only through resolved references to authoritative artifacts, never through inline free-form text.

## Invariant PMA-405

A prompt is submitted only after it is proven complete and proven article-free; a partial or contaminated prompt is never submitted.

## Invariant PMA-406

Camera parameters are copied verbatim from the authoritative camera plan; the Assembler never overrides lens, movement, or angle.

## Invariant PMA-407

Every prompt has a deterministic prompt hash over its canonical text, and that hash is a required input to the render cache key (**5.6.3**).

## Invariant PMA-408

References are resolved at pinned artifact versions, so an approved shot's prompt and hash are stable against later bible or style edits.

## Invariant PMA-409

Assembly failures escalate to Manual Review; the Assembler never fabricates, defaults, or infers missing creative content.

## Invariant PMA-410

The canonical text used for hashing is byte-identical to the text handed to task submission; there is exactly one canonical rendering.

## Invariant PMA-411

The article-freedom check cannot be disabled by configuration and enforces zero verbatim-shingle tolerance.

## Invariant PMA-412

The Assembler produces a provider-neutral canonical prompt; provider-specific translation occurs only in the adapter (**5.3.4**).

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                              |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-520 | Prompt assembly is implemented as a pure, side-effect-free function of pinned storyboard artifacts, guaranteeing reproducibility. |
| ADR-521 | Prompts are composed from a fixed eight-component model in a fixed, immutable order (Character → Visual Style).       |
| ADR-522 | The provider boundary is a content firewall: article text, story prose, and publication metadata are categorically prohibited from any prompt. |
| ADR-523 | Article-freedom is validated against an article/story fingerprint set with zero verbatim-shingle tolerance and fails closed. |
| ADR-524 | Character, location, camera, and style enter prompts only by reference resolution through their owning sub-sections (5.5.2–5.5.5). |
| ADR-525 | Each prompt has a SHA-256 identity over one canonical text that both serves submission and feeds the render cache key (5.6.3). |
| ADR-526 | Assembly failures — unresolved refs, incomplete components, contamination — escalate to Manual Review and never trigger content fabrication. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.5.1** is complete when:

* Prompt assembly is formally defined as a pure function of pinned storyboard artifacts, producing byte-identical output for identical inputs (Determinism, Performance Constraint 2).
* The eight-component model — Character, Location, Camera, Lighting, Blocking, Emotion, Shot Purpose, Visual Style — is specified with a canonical schema, a fixed order, and clear mandatory/optional status.
* The assembly algorithm, canonical normalization, and fixed template are defined such that the same shot always yields the same canonical text and the same prompt hash.
* The prohibition on article text, story prose, screenplay narrative, and publication metadata reaching a provider is stated categorically and enforced by an article-freedom check with zero tolerance.
* Character, location, camera, and style injection are defined by reference resolution through **5.5.2**, **5.5.3**, **5.5.4**, and **5.5.5**, with camera parameters copied verbatim and forbidden styles rejected.
* Completeness and article-freedom validation are specified as mandatory preconditions of submission (composing with **5.3.3**), both failing closed to Manual Review.
* Prompt hashing and prompt identity are defined and shown to feed the render cache key in **5.6.3**, making cache reuse sound and cost optimization (**5.2.3**) effective.
* The assembly failure model never fabricates creative content and always escalates, consistent with Fail Closed (Principle 5).
* Events, metrics, security boundaries, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a deterministic, article-free prompt assembler that renders approved shots without ever authoring content or leaking article text to a provider.


---

# Part 5.5.2 — Character Injection

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Character Injection** mechanism used by the Rendering Platform during deterministic prompt assembly.

Character Injection is the process by which a shot's **character reference** — a versioned identifier such as `maya_v1` — is resolved into a full, authoritative **character description** and deterministically merged into the assembled render prompt defined in **5.5.1**.

Its purpose is to guarantee that every appearance of a character across every shot, scene, build, and provider submission carries the **same visual identity**, so that the rendered trailer presents a single, coherent human being rather than a sequence of visually inconsistent look-alikes.

Character Injection exists because generative video providers are stateless and non-deterministic with respect to identity. A provider given the free-text phrase "a female analyst" will invent a different face, build, wardrobe, and skin tone on every call. The Rendering Platform must never permit this. Instead, it references characters **by identifier only** in the storyboard, and injects the canonical description **automatically** from a single authoritative source at prompt-assembly time.

Character Injection does **not** invent, edit, or embellish character descriptions.

It receives an already-approved character reference — produced upstream by the Story and Screenplay Engines and frozen in the **Character Bible** (`character-bible.yaml`, defined in **5.1.4**) — and performs a deterministic, immutable substitution into the prompt. It never modifies the approved creative plan.

Character Injection is a resolution-and-substitution step within the execution layer. It is a load-bearing guarantee of visual continuity, not a creative act.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the character reference model and its versioned identity;
* the character reference schema carried by each shot;
* the Character Bible as the single authoritative source of character truth;
* the character record schema resolved from the Bible;
* deterministic resolution of a reference to a canonical description;
* deterministic injection of that description into the assembled prompt (**5.5.1**);
* the cross-shot consistency guarantee (same ref → same visual identity);
* versioned character identity and description immutability during a build;
* validation that every referenced character exists and is resolvable;
* the injection failure model (fail closed);
* how character consistency is verified downstream by the human consistency check (**5.6.2**);
* events, metrics, configuration, testing, invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria for Character Injection.

This section does **not** specify:

* the overall prompt assembly order and segment grammar (defined in **5.5.1**);
* location resolution and injection (defined in **5.5.3**);
* camera parameter injection (defined in **5.5.4**);
* visual style enforcement and forbidden aesthetics (defined in **5.5.5**);
* how the Character Bible is authored or approved (an upstream Story/Screenplay concern, consumed here via **5.1.4**);
* the visual quality scoring algorithm behind the human consistency check (defined in **5.6.2**);
* provider submission payload formatting (defined in **5.3.3**).

Character Injection consumes the Character Bible contract and the prompt-assembly contract; it owns neither.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **A character is referenced by identity and rendered from a single frozen description; the same reference must always produce the same face.**

The authoritative description of every character lives in exactly one place — the **Character Bible** (`character-bible.yaml`, **5.1.4**) — and is addressed only through a versioned reference such as `maya_v1`.

The Rendering Platform must never:

* let a shot carry an inline, free-text character description;
* let a provider infer a character's appearance from context;
* resolve the same reference to two different descriptions within one build;
* mutate an injected description after a build has begun;
* substitute a character that is absent from the Character Bible.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Visual identity is a function of the reference, never of the shot, the scene order, the provider, or the attempt.**

If the same `character.ref` appears in shot 3, shot 41, a retry of shot 41, and a preview build and a final build, all five renders are assembled from byte-identical character description text. Any divergence in the resulting face is a provider defect surfaced downstream by **5.6.2**, never a prompt-assembly defect.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Story Engine / Screenplay Engine
        │  (author & approve characters)
        ▼
Character Bible  (character-bible.yaml, 5.1.4)
        │  authoritative, versioned, frozen at build start
        │
        ▼
Storyboard Shot ── character.ref: maya_v1 ─┐
        │                                   │
        ▼                                   ▼
Deterministic Prompt Assembly (5.5.1)   Character Resolver
        │                                   │
        │        ┌── resolve(ref) ──────────┘
        │        ▼
        ├── Character Segment  ◄── frozen description (immutable during build)
        ├── Location Segment   (5.5.3)
        ├── Camera Segment     (5.5.4)
        ├── Lighting / Blocking / Emotion / Shot Purpose
        └── Visual Style       (5.5.5)
        │
        ▼
Assembled Prompt ──► Provider Adapter (5.3) ──► Rendered Asset
        │
        ▼
Visual Quality Validation ── Human Consistency Check (5.6.2)
```

Character Injection sits **inside** prompt assembly (**5.5.1**), between the storyboard's character reference and the character segment of the final prompt. It reads from the Character Bible and writes only into the assembled prompt. It never writes back to the Bible, the storyboard, or the manifest's creative fields.

---

# Responsibilities

Character Injection is responsible for:

* accepting a shot's `character.ref` set (zero, one, or many characters per shot);
* resolving each reference against the build-frozen Character Bible;
* validating that every referenced character exists and is complete;
* producing a canonical, deterministic character description segment;
* injecting that segment into the assembled prompt at the position fixed by **5.5.1**;
* guaranteeing identical output for identical references within and across builds;
* preserving reference-to-description immutability for the entire build lifetime;
* emitting resolution and injection events and metrics;
* failing closed when a reference is unresolvable or ambiguous;
* recording the resolved character version into the render manifest for auditability and for the downstream consistency check.

It is **not** responsible for:

* authoring or approving character descriptions (upstream Story/Screenplay);
* choosing which characters appear in a shot (owned by the approved Storyboard);
* ordering prompt segments (owned by **5.5.1**);
* location, camera, or style injection (owned by **5.5.3**, **5.5.4**, **5.5.5**);
* scoring visual consistency of the rendered output (owned by **5.6.2**);
* remediation of a failed consistency check (escalated to Manual Review per **5.4.1**).

---

# Character Injection Design Objectives

Character Injection shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## CHI-301 — Reference-Only Storyboard

A storyboard shot shall carry characters **only** as versioned references. Inline free-text character descriptions in a shot are prohibited. The description is resolved at assembly time, never stored in the shot.

---

## CHI-302 — Single Authoritative Source

Every character description shall originate from exactly one source: the build-frozen Character Bible (**5.1.4**). No secondary, cached, or provider-side description is authoritative.

---

## CHI-303 — Deterministic Resolution

Given the same reference and the same frozen Character Bible, resolution shall produce a byte-identical description segment. Resolution shall contain no randomness, no time dependence, and no provider dependence (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism).

---

## CHI-304 — Cross-Shot Identity Consistency

The same `character.ref` shall resolve to the same visual identity in every shot in which it appears, regardless of scene index, shot index, render attempt, render profile, or provider.

---

## CHI-305 — Build-Scoped Immutability

Once a build begins, the resolved description for every referenced character is frozen for the entire build. A later edit to the Character Bible must never change the description injected into an in-progress build.

---

## CHI-306 — Existence Validation

Every referenced character shall be validated to exist and be complete in the frozen Bible **before** any provider submission. An unresolvable or incomplete reference blocks the build; it is never rendered with a placeholder.

---

## CHI-307 — Version Explicitness

Character identity shall be explicitly versioned (`maya_v1`, `maya_v2`). A bare, unversioned character name is prohibited as a reference. Version is part of identity; two versions are two distinct visual identities.

---

## CHI-308 — Downstream Verifiability

The resolved character version and a stable description digest shall be recorded per shot so that the downstream human consistency check (**5.6.2**) can verify that all renders of a character share one frozen identity.

---

# The Character Reference Model

Characters are never described inline in a shot. They are **referenced by identity**.

The storyboard, produced upstream and approved before rendering begins, carries for each shot the set of characters that appear in it. Each entry is a versioned reference key into the Character Bible.

```yaml id="chi-shot-ref"
# Fragment of an approved storyboard shot
shot:
  shot_id: sc04_sh12
  characters:
    - ref: maya_v1          # versioned identity, resolvable in the Bible
      role: primary          # primary | secondary | background
      presence: on_screen    # on_screen | voice_over | referenced
    - ref: director_chen_v2
      role: secondary
      presence: on_screen
```

Rules of the reference model:

* A shot may reference **zero** characters (e.g. a pure typography title card or an architecture-diagram SVG shot). Zero-character shots inject an empty character segment, not a placeholder human.
* A shot may reference **many** characters. Each is resolved independently; the combined segment preserves the deterministic ordering defined below.
* The `ref` field is the sole identity anchor. `role` and `presence` affect blocking and emphasis in the prompt but never change the resolved appearance.
* The reference is opaque to the provider. The provider receives the resolved description text, never the identifier `maya_v1`.

---

# Versioned Character Identity

Character identity is **versioned**. The version suffix is a first-class part of the identity.

```text id="chi-identity"
character identity = <character_key>_v<version>

  maya_v1   →  the approved appearance of Maya, revision 1
  maya_v2   →  a deliberately re-approved appearance of Maya, revision 2
```

Semantics:

* `maya_v1` and `maya_v2` are **two distinct visual identities**. They are not interchangeable. A shot referencing `maya_v1` must never be rendered from the `maya_v2` description.
* Versioning is how a character's canonical appearance is intentionally changed: the Story/Screenplay layer approves a **new** version rather than editing an existing one in place. Existing shots keep their original reference until the storyboard is re-approved to point at the new version.
* A reference **must** be versioned (CHI-307). A bare `maya` is rejected at validation as an incomplete reference.
* Version is carried through to the render manifest and cache key composition (**5.6.3**), so a version change correctly invalidates cached renders of the old identity.

Because identity is versioned and immutable per version, the phrase "the same reference" in this section always means the same `character_key` **and** the same version.

---

# The Character Bible — Authoritative Source

The **Character Bible** (`character-bible.yaml`) is the single authoritative source of character truth for the Rendering Platform. It is one of the declared rendering inputs in **5.1.4**.

The Bible is authored and approved **upstream** by the Story and Screenplay Engines. The Rendering Platform treats it as **read-only**, **versioned**, and **frozen at build start**.

```yaml id="chi-bible"
# character-bible.yaml  (authoritative; consumed read-only per 5.1.4)
character_bible:
  bible_version: 2025-11-04T09:00:00Z#a1c9   # content-addressed revision id
  characters:
    maya_v1:
      display_name: Maya
      version: 1
      status: approved            # only 'approved' entries are resolvable
      identity_digest: sha256:...  # digest of the frozen description below
      description:
        age_range: "30s"
        build: "average height, composed posture"
        skin_tone: "warm medium-brown"
        hair: "dark, shoulder-length, tied back"
        face: "calm, attentive; minimal expression by default"
        wardrobe: "charcoal blazer over a slate shirt; no logos"
        distinguishing: "thin silver watch on left wrist"
        demeanor: "measured, senior operator; never theatrical"
      style_constraints:
        - realistic
        - restrained
        - enterprise
      forbidden:
        - anime
        - cartoon
        - exaggerated_sci_fi
    director_chen_v2:
      display_name: Director Chen
      version: 2
      status: approved
      identity_digest: sha256:...
      description:
        age_range: "50s"
        build: "tall, upright"
        skin_tone: "light"
        hair: "grey, short"
        face: "authoritative, weathered"
        wardrobe: "dark suit, no tie"
        distinguishing: "wire-frame glasses"
        demeanor: "decisive, spare of gesture"
      style_constraints:
        - realistic
        - restrained
        - enterprise
      forbidden:
        - anime
        - cartoon
        - exaggerated_sci_fi
```

Properties of the Character Bible as consumed here:

* **Authoritative** — no other artifact may describe a character's appearance. If a shot, prompt template, or provider default disagrees with the Bible, the Bible wins.
* **Keyed by versioned identity** — the top-level keys are exactly the reference identities (`maya_v1`), so resolution is a direct keyed lookup.
* **Approval-gated** — only entries with `status: approved` are resolvable. A `draft` or `retired` entry is treated as non-existent for injection (CHI-306).
* **Digest-bearing** — each entry carries an `identity_digest` over its frozen `description`. The digest is the stable anchor used downstream by **5.6.2** and in cache keying.
* **Content-addressed** — the whole Bible carries a `bible_version`. The build records which `bible_version` it froze, so the exact character truth used is auditable and reproducible.

Character Injection **never** writes to the Character Bible and never alters an entry. It reads, resolves, and injects.

---

# Character Record Schema (Resolved)

The record resolved from the Bible for a single reference has the following canonical shape.

```yaml id="chi-record"
resolved_character:
  ref: maya_v1               # the versioned identity requested
  character_key: maya
  version: 1
  display_name: Maya
  identity_digest: sha256:... # frozen digest of the description
  bible_version: 2025-11-04T09:00:00Z#a1c9
  description_segment: |      # deterministic, provider-ready text
    Maya — a composed operator in her 30s, average height, warm
    medium-brown skin, dark shoulder-length hair tied back, wearing a
    charcoal blazer over a slate shirt, a thin silver watch on her left
    wrist; calm and attentive, measured and senior in demeanor.
  style_constraints: [realistic, restrained, enterprise]
  forbidden: [anime, cartoon, exaggerated_sci_fi]
```

The `description_segment` is produced by a **deterministic serializer** that renders the Bible's structured `description` fields in a fixed field order into stable prose. Because the field order and formatting are fixed and the source fields are frozen, the serializer is a pure function of the resolved entry: identical entry in, identical text out (CHI-303).

The `style_constraints` and `forbidden` lists are carried forward but their **enforcement** is owned by Visual Style Enforcement (**5.5.5**); Character Injection merely surfaces the character-scoped constraints so **5.5.5** can reconcile them with the global style contract.

---

# Deterministic Resolution

Resolution converts a `character.ref` into a `resolved_character` record. It is a pure, deterministic function.

```text id="chi-resolve"
resolve(ref, frozen_bible) -> resolved_character | ResolutionError

  1. Reject if ref is unversioned            → ResolutionError(UNVERSIONED)
  2. Look up ref as a key in frozen_bible    → miss → ResolutionError(NOT_FOUND)
  3. Reject if entry.status != approved      → ResolutionError(NOT_APPROVED)
  4. Reject if any required description field
     is missing or empty                     → ResolutionError(INCOMPLETE)
  5. Serialize description (fixed field order) -> description_segment
  6. Verify sha256(description) == identity_digest
                                             → mismatch → ResolutionError(DIGEST_MISMATCH)
  7. Return resolved_character (with bible_version, digest)
```

Resolution guarantees:

* **Keyed, not fuzzy** — the reference is matched by exact key equality against the frozen Bible. There is no nearest-name matching, no case-folding, no aliasing at resolution time. Aliases, if any, are resolved upstream in the storyboard, never here.
* **No side effects** — resolution reads the frozen Bible and returns a value. It never fetches remotely, never mutates, never caches across builds in a way that could drift.
* **Deterministic serialization** — step 5 uses a fixed field order (`age_range, build, skin_tone, hair, face, wardrobe, distinguishing, demeanor`) and fixed connective grammar, so the emitted prose is byte-stable.
* **Self-checking** — step 6 confirms the frozen description still matches its digest, catching any corruption of the frozen snapshot before it can reach a provider.

Any `ResolutionError` fails the shot closed (see Failure Model). A shot with an unresolvable character is never submitted to a provider.

---

# Deterministic Injection into the Assembled Prompt

Once resolved, each character's `description_segment` is injected into the **Character** segment of the assembled prompt at the position fixed by **5.5.1**.

The prompt segment order (from **5.5.1**) is:

```text
Character  +  Location  +  Camera  +  Lighting  +  Blocking  +  Emotion  +  Shot Purpose  +  Visual Style
```

Character Injection owns only the leading **Character** segment. Injection rules:

* **Fixed position** — the character segment is always first, per **5.5.1**. Injection never reorders other segments.
* **Deterministic multi-character ordering** — when a shot references several characters, they are emitted in a deterministic order: by `role` (primary → secondary → background), then by first appearance order in the shot's `characters` list, then by `character_key` as a final lexical tiebreak. This makes multi-character prompts byte-reproducible (CHI-303).
* **Reference is stripped** — the identifier `maya_v1` never appears in the emitted prompt. Only the resolved prose reaches the provider. This preserves the boundary in **5.5.1** that the provider receives no internal identifiers and no article text.
* **Empty when absent** — a zero-character shot injects an empty character segment. No default or placeholder human is ever invented (a direct consequence of the reference-only model, CHI-301).

```text id="chi-injection-example"
shot.characters = [ {ref: maya_v1, role: primary}, {ref: director_chen_v2, role: secondary} ]

resolve(maya_v1)          → segment_A
resolve(director_chen_v2) → segment_B

Character segment  =  segment_A  " With her, "  segment_B
Assembled prompt   =  <Character: segment_A + segment_B>
                      + <Location …> + <Camera …> + <Lighting …>
                      + <Blocking …> + <Emotion …> + <Shot Purpose …>
                      + <Visual Style …>
```

The final assembled prompt is handed back to **5.5.1**, which is responsible for the overall determinism guarantee of the whole prompt. Character Injection guarantees only that its segment is a deterministic function of the references and the frozen Bible.

---

# The Cross-Shot Consistency Guarantee

The central guarantee of this section is **visual identity consistency across shots**.

> Same `character.ref` → same frozen description → same injected character segment → the strongest achievable prompt-level identity consistency.

Mechanism:

```text id="chi-consistency"
shot 3   references maya_v1 ─┐
shot 41  references maya_v1 ─┤
retry of shot 41 (attempt 2) ┤  all resolve against the SAME frozen Bible entry
preview build references maya_v1 ┤  → SAME identity_digest
final build references maya_v1  ─┘  → BYTE-IDENTICAL description_segment
```

Because:

1. Identity is versioned and immutable per version (CHI-307).
2. The Bible entry for that version is frozen for the build (CHI-305).
3. Resolution is deterministic (CHI-303).
4. The serializer is a pure function of the frozen entry.

…every render of `maya_v1` is assembled from the same character text. The Rendering Platform therefore delivers identity consistency **at the prompt layer** as a hard guarantee.

What this guarantee does **not** claim: it does not claim the *provider* will render pixel-identical faces — providers are stochastic. It claims that any inconsistency is **not** attributable to prompt drift, and it makes the frozen `identity_digest` available so that the downstream check (**5.6.2**) can detect and act on residual provider-side drift. Prompt-layer consistency is necessary; provider-layer consistency is verified separately.

---

# Build-Scoped Immutability

Injected character descriptions are **immutable for the lifetime of a build**.

```text id="chi-freeze"
Build start
   │
   ├── snapshot Character Bible  → record bible_version
   ├── for each referenced character: freeze identity_digest + description
   │
   ▼  (build proceeds: submit, poll, download, validate, retry, compose)
   │
   │   Any edit to character-bible.yaml here has NO effect on this build.
   │
   ▼
Build end
```

Rules:

* At build start, the platform snapshots the Character Bible (`bible_version`) and, for every referenced character, freezes the `identity_digest` and resolved description.
* For the remainder of the build — including all retries, the preview→final transition, and cache reuse — resolution reads only the frozen snapshot (CHI-305).
* A concurrent or subsequent edit to `character-bible.yaml` (e.g. approving `maya_v2`) is invisible to the in-flight build. It affects only builds that start **after** the edit and whose storyboards reference the new version.
* This immutability is what makes retries safe: a retried shot re-resolves to the **identical** description, so a retry can never silently change a character's face.

Immutability also underpins caching: because the frozen description and its digest participate in the render cache key (**5.6.3**), a cached render is only reused for an identical frozen identity, and a version bump correctly busts the cache.

---

# Validation — Referenced Characters Must Exist

Before any provider submission, the platform validates that every referenced character is resolvable. This is a build-blocking gate (CHI-306).

```yaml id="chi-validation"
character_reference_validation:
  every_ref_is_versioned: true          # CHI-307
  every_ref_exists_in_bible: true       # keyed lookup succeeds
  every_ref_is_approved: true           # status == approved
  every_ref_is_complete: true           # all required fields present
  every_ref_digest_matches: true        # description == identity_digest
  bible_version_frozen: true            # snapshot recorded
```

Validation timing and behavior:

* Validation runs during prompt assembly (**5.5.1**), before the render task is admitted to the provider submission path (**5.4.1**).
* A failure is **fatal to the affected shot** and, by the Fail-Closed principle, prevents that shot from producing a publishable asset.
* Validation never substitutes a default character, never downgrades to a generic human, and never lets the provider infer appearance. The only outcomes are *resolved* or *blocked*.
* A blocked shot is routed to Manual Review (**5.4.1**) with the specific `ResolutionError` reason, so a human can correct the storyboard reference or the Bible upstream and re-approve.

This makes an unresolvable character a loud, early, deterministic failure rather than a silent visual defect discovered only after rendering.

---

# Injection Failure Model (Fail Closed)

Character Injection fails closed. Every failure mode has one safe outcome: the affected shot does not reach a provider with an unknown identity.

| Failure                                   | Detected at         | Outcome                                  |
| ----------------------------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Unversioned reference (`maya`)            | Resolution step 1   | Block shot → Manual Review (UNVERSIONED) |
| Reference not in Bible                    | Resolution step 2   | Block shot → Manual Review (NOT_FOUND)   |
| Entry not `approved`                      | Resolution step 3   | Block shot → Manual Review (NOT_APPROVED)|
| Missing/empty required description field  | Resolution step 4   | Block shot → Manual Review (INCOMPLETE)  |
| Description ≠ `identity_digest`           | Resolution step 6   | Block build → integrity alert (DIGEST_MISMATCH) |
| Bible snapshot missing at build start     | Build start freeze  | Block build (no unfrozen rendering)      |

Principles:

* **No placeholder rendering.** The platform never renders "some person" in place of an unresolved character.
* **No provider inference.** A failure never degrades into letting the provider guess.
* **Early and deterministic.** Failures surface during assembly/validation, before cost is spent on provider submission.
* **Evidence preserved.** The failing reference, reason, and frozen `bible_version` are recorded for the operator.

This is the same Fail-Closed posture the platform applies throughout Part 5: if identity cannot be guaranteed, no publishable trailer is produced for that shot.

---

# Downstream Verification — Human Consistency Check (5.6.2)

Character Injection guarantees identity consistency at the **prompt** layer. Verification that the **rendered pixels** are consistent is performed downstream by the **Human Consistency Check** within Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**).

The handoff:

```text id="chi-downstream"
Character Injection            Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2)
─────────────────────          ─────────────────────────────────
records per shot:              consumes per shot:
  character.ref                  character.ref
  version                        version
  identity_digest      ───────►  identity_digest  (expected identity)
  bible_version                  bible_version

                                 for each character appearing in
                                 multiple shots:
                                   compare rendered facial/identity
                                   features across all its shots
                                   → human_consistency sub-score
                                   → must meet minimum_score: 0.90
```

Contract with **5.6.2**:

* Character Injection **records** the resolved `version`, `identity_digest`, and frozen `bible_version` into the render manifest for every shot, per character. These are the ground-truth "expected identity" anchors.
* **5.6.2** groups all shots that share a `character.ref` and scores whether the rendered outputs depict the **same** person. Because injection guarantees the prompts were identical, any failure of the human-consistency sub-score is attributable to provider stochasticity, and is handled by **5.6.2**'s scoring and the retry/Manual-Review paths — **not** by re-writing the character description.
* The `minimum_score: 0.90` visual quality floor (defined in **5.6.2**) applies. A character whose renders drift below the consistency threshold blocks the trailer (Fail Closed), consistent with the platform-wide guarantee that character consistency is automatically enforced.

This division of labor is deliberate: **injection makes identity deterministic; validation confirms the provider honored it.** Together they deliver the Part 5 acceptance guarantee that "character consistency is automatically injected" and enforced.

---

# Events

Character Injection emits events for every material step.

```yaml
character_injection_events:
  - character_bible_frozen           # bible_version snapshotted at build start
  - character_reference_resolved     # ref → resolved_character
  - character_segment_injected       # segment written into assembled prompt
  - character_reference_unversioned  # CHI-307 violation
  - character_reference_not_found    # CHI-306 violation
  - character_reference_not_approved
  - character_reference_incomplete
  - character_identity_digest_mismatch
  - character_identity_recorded      # version + digest written to manifest
```

Every event references the `build_id`, `shot_id`, `character.ref`, resolved `version`, and frozen `bible_version`, and is durably recorded for audit and for reconciliation with the downstream consistency check.

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml
character_injection_metrics:
  characters_referenced_total
  characters_resolved_total
  distinct_character_versions_used
  multi_character_shots
  zero_character_shots
  resolution_failures_total
  resolution_failures_by_reason        # UNVERSIONED | NOT_FOUND | NOT_APPROVED | INCOMPLETE | DIGEST_MISMATCH
  bible_version_in_use
  injection_determinism_check_passed   # re-resolve equals first resolve
```

These metrics feed the same performance and quality review as the rest of the Rendering Platform. A non-zero `resolution_failures_total` at submission time indicates a storyboard/Bible mismatch that must be resolved upstream before the build can complete.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

Character Injection operates under least privilege and preserves the platform's data boundaries.

Required controls:

* **read-only** access to the Character Bible; injection never writes it;
* the resolved description is **not** secret, but the boundary that **no article text** reaches the provider (**5.5.1**) is preserved — only the character prose is injected;
* internal identifiers (`maya_v1`) are stripped from the prompt and never sent to a provider;
* the frozen `bible_version` and per-shot `identity_digest` are recorded as tamper-evident anchors;
* no provider credentials are ever placed in a character segment;
* build namespace isolation: one build's frozen Bible snapshot is not visible to another build.

The provider receives resolved appearance prose and nothing else about the character — no identifier, no Bible metadata, no upstream article content.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="chi-config"
character_injection:
  require_versioned_refs: true          # CHI-307; unversioned refs rejected
  require_approved_status: true         # only status: approved resolvable
  verify_identity_digest: true          # CHI-303 / integrity
  freeze_bible_at_build_start: true     # CHI-305 immutability
  serializer:
    field_order:                        # fixed; determines byte-stable prose
      - age_range
      - build
      - skin_tone
      - hair
      - face
      - wardrobe
      - distinguishing
      - demeanor
  multi_character_ordering:
    - role                              # primary → secondary → background
    - appearance_index                  # order in shot.characters
    - character_key                     # lexical tiebreak
  on_resolution_failure: manual_review  # fail closed; never placeholder
  record_identity_to_manifest: true     # CHI-308; anchors for 5.6.2
```

All values are validated at startup. Invalid configuration fails closed and blocks admission rather than injecting with unsafe defaults, consistent with the platform-wide configuration policy in **5.4.1**. The `field_order` and `multi_character_ordering` lists are load-bearing for determinism and must never be changed mid-build.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* unversioned reference (`maya`) is rejected;
* known reference (`maya_v1`) resolves to the expected record;
* unknown reference fails NOT_FOUND;
* `status: draft` / `retired` entry fails NOT_APPROVED;
* missing required description field fails INCOMPLETE;
* description tampering triggers DIGEST_MISMATCH;
* serializer emits byte-identical prose for the same entry (determinism);
* zero-character shot injects an empty segment (no placeholder);
* `maya_v1` and `maya_v2` resolve to distinct descriptions.

## Integration Tests

* multi-character shot resolves and orders segments deterministically;
* character segment is injected first, ahead of location/camera/style (**5.5.1**);
* internal reference (`maya_v1`) never appears in the assembled prompt;
* resolved `version` + `identity_digest` recorded to the render manifest;
* validation blocks a build whose shot references an absent character (**5.4.1** Manual Review route).

## Consistency Tests

* the same `character.ref` across multiple shots yields byte-identical segments;
* a retry of a shot re-resolves to the identical segment;
* preview build and final build resolve the same reference identically;
* a mid-build edit to the Bible does not alter the in-progress build (immutability);
* a `maya_v1` → `maya_v2` version bump busts the relevant render cache (**5.6.3**).

## Failure Injection Tests

* Bible snapshot missing at build start blocks the build;
* corrupted frozen entry (digest mismatch) blocks the build;
* partially approved Bible (one character draft) blocks only the affected shot;
* unresolvable reference discovered at assembly never reaches a provider.

## Downstream Verification Tests

* per-shot identity anchors are consumable by **5.6.2**;
* a character rendered inconsistently across shots is flagged by the human consistency check against the recorded `identity_digest`;
* a sub-0.90 human-consistency score blocks the trailer (Fail Closed).

---

# Character Injection Quality Gate

```yaml
character_injection_quality_gate:
  all_shot_characters_are_versioned_refs: true
  all_refs_resolve_from_frozen_bible: true
  all_resolved_entries_approved: true
  all_resolved_entries_complete: true
  all_identity_digests_verified: true
  bible_frozen_at_build_start: true
  segments_deterministic_across_shots: true
  segments_deterministic_across_attempts: true
  no_reference_identifier_in_prompt: true
  no_placeholder_character_ever_rendered: true
  identity_anchors_recorded_to_manifest: true
  resolution_failures_route_to_manual_review: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant CHI-401

A shot carries characters only as versioned references; no shot contains an inline character description.

## Invariant CHI-402

Every character description injected into a prompt originates from the single, build-frozen Character Bible.

## Invariant CHI-403

The same `character.ref` resolves to a byte-identical description segment everywhere it appears within a build.

## Invariant CHI-404

A resolved character description is immutable for the entire lifetime of a build, including all retries and the preview→final transition.

## Invariant CHI-405

`maya_v1` and `maya_v2` are distinct visual identities and are never substituted for one another.

## Invariant CHI-406

Every referenced character is validated to exist, be approved, and be complete before any provider submission.

## Invariant CHI-407

An unresolvable character reference blocks the affected shot (or build); it is never rendered with a placeholder or provider-inferred appearance (Fail Closed).

## Invariant CHI-408

The internal reference identifier is never transmitted to a provider; only resolved appearance prose is injected.

## Invariant CHI-409

The resolved character version and identity digest are recorded per shot so the human consistency check (**5.6.2**) can verify rendered identity against a single frozen anchor.

## Invariant CHI-410

Character Injection reads the Character Bible and writes only the prompt's character segment; it never mutates the Bible, storyboard, or approved creative plan.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                        |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-527 | Characters are referenced in the storyboard by versioned identity only; inline character descriptions in shots are prohibited. |
| ADR-528 | The Character Bible (`character-bible.yaml`) is the single authoritative source of character descriptions, consumed read-only and frozen at build start. |
| ADR-529 | Character resolution and injection are deterministic pure functions of the reference and the frozen Bible, guaranteeing that the same reference yields byte-identical prompt text across shots, attempts, and builds. |
| ADR-530 | Unresolvable, unapproved, unversioned, or incomplete character references fail closed and route to Manual Review; the platform never renders a placeholder or provider-inferred character. |
| ADR-531 | The resolved character version and identity digest are recorded per shot as the ground-truth anchor for the downstream human consistency check (**5.6.2**). |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.5.2** is complete when:

* Characters are referenced exclusively by versioned identity (`maya_v1`) in the storyboard, with no inline descriptions in shots.
* The Character Bible (`character-bible.yaml`, **5.1.4**) is established as the single authoritative, read-only, build-frozen source of character truth.
* The character reference schema and the resolved character record schema are fully specified.
* Resolution from a reference to a canonical description is deterministic, keyed, self-checking, and side-effect-free.
* Injection places the character segment deterministically into the assembled prompt defined in **5.5.1**, strips internal identifiers, and orders multi-character shots reproducibly.
* The cross-shot consistency guarantee is defined: the same reference always yields byte-identical character prompt text.
* Character identity is explicitly versioned, and resolved descriptions are immutable for the entire build, including retries and the preview→final transition.
* Every referenced character is validated to exist, be approved, and be complete before any provider submission, and failures fail closed to Manual Review with no placeholder rendering.
* The downstream verification handoff to the human consistency check (**5.6.2**) is specified, with per-shot identity anchors recorded to the manifest and the `minimum_score: 0.90` floor enforced.
* Events, metrics, security boundaries, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement Character Injection as a deterministic, fail-closed component that guarantees a single frozen visual identity per character reference without ever modifying the approved creative plan.


---

# Part 5.5.3 — Location Injection

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Location Injection** mechanism used by the Rendering Platform during deterministic prompt assembly.

Location Injection is the process by which an **immutable location reference** carried on a shot — for example `location.ref: operations_center_night` — is resolved against the authoritative **Location Bible** and expanded into the environment, setting, and lighting fragment of the render prompt assembled in **5.5.1**.

Its purpose is to guarantee that every shot which claims to occur in a given location renders that location **identically**, so that a trailer cut from many independently rendered shots reads as one continuous, coherent physical world.

Location Injection is responsible for turning a **reference** into a **canonical environment description** in a way that is:

* deterministic;
* immutable within a build;
* referentially validated;
* consistent across every shot sharing a location;
* time-of-day and lighting bound;
* free of provider or creative invention;
* verifiable downstream by continuity validation.

Location Injection does **not** decide *which* location a shot occupies.

It receives an already-approved `location_ref` on each shot — chosen upstream by the Screenplay and Storyboard Engines and frozen into `shot-list.json` — and resolves that reference into descriptive text. It never selects, alters, relights, or invents a location.

Location Injection is a resolution and expansion step inside the execution layer. It mirrors, for environments, exactly what **Character Injection (5.5.2)** does for characters and what **Camera Injection (5.5.4)** does for camera language.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the location reference model carried on each shot;
* the Location Bible as the single authoritative source of environment identity (from **5.1.4**);
* the location record schema resolved during injection;
* deterministic resolution of a `location_ref` into a canonical environment fragment;
* deterministic injection of that fragment into the assembled prompt (**5.5.1**);
* the environment **consistency guarantee** across all shots sharing a location;
* location **immutability** for the full duration of a build;
* referential validation that every `location_ref` exists in the Location Bible;
* time-of-day and lighting binding derived from the location reference (`operations_center_night`);
* the boundary that prohibits providers from altering environment identity;
* handoff of injected environment provenance to downstream continuity verification (**5.6.2**);
* location-injection events, metrics, configuration, testing, invariants, and decision records.

This section does **not** specify:

* the overall prompt assembly order and envelope (defined in **5.5.1**);
* character resolution and injection (defined in **5.5.2**);
* camera parameter injection (defined in **5.5.4**);
* visual style enforcement and forbidden-aesthetic rules (defined in **5.5.5**);
* the production of `location-bible.yaml` itself (owned by the Location Engine, contracted in **5.1.4**);
* visual quality and continuity scoring logic (defined in **5.6.2**).

Location Injection consumes the Location Bible contract from **5.1.4** and produces one segment of the prompt owned by **5.5.1**. It owns neither end.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **A location is a reference, never a description a shot may reword.**

Every shot names its environment by an immutable `location_ref`. The words that describe that environment live in exactly one place — the Location Bible — and are injected identically wherever the reference appears.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Two shots that share a location ref render the same world; a shot can never quietly redecorate a room.**

Environment consistency is therefore not an emergent property to be hoped for and later measured — it is **guaranteed by construction**, because the same canonical description is deterministically injected for every occurrence of the reference. Continuity validation in **5.6.2** confirms the guarantee; it is not the source of it.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Location Engine
        │
        ▼
location-bible.yaml   (approved, environment-locked — 5.1.4)
        │
        ▼
Rendering Input Ingest (5.1.4)
        │
        ▼
shot-list.json  ──►  shot.location_ref: operations_center_night
        │
        ▼
Deterministic Prompt Assembly (5.5.1)
        │
        ├── Character Injection (5.5.2)
        ├── Location Injection (5.5.3)   ◄── THIS SECTION
        │        │
        │        ├── resolve location_ref → Location Bible record
        │        ├── expand canonical environment description
        │        ├── bind time_of_day / lighting
        │        └── emit environment fragment + provenance
        ├── Camera Injection (5.5.4)
        └── Visual Style Enforcement (5.5.5)
        │
        ▼
Assembled Render Prompt (deterministic)
        │
        ▼
Provider Adapter (renders only; 5.3)
        │
        ▼
Visual Quality / Continuity Validation (5.6.2)
```

Location Injection sits **inside** prompt assembly (**5.5.1**), between reference intake and prompt emission. It is invoked once per shot, resolves against a read-only bible, and never calls a provider or mutates a source artifact.

---

# Responsibilities

Location Injection is responsible for:

* accepting the `location_ref` carried by each shot in `shot-list.json`;
* resolving that reference against the loaded Location Bible;
* expanding the resolved record into a canonical environment fragment;
* binding the location's time-of-day and lighting semantics into the fragment;
* placing the fragment deterministically in the prompt slot owned by **5.5.1**;
* emitting environment provenance (ref id, bible content hash, record fingerprint) for downstream continuity verification;
* failing closed when a reference does not resolve;
* guaranteeing identical expansion for every shot that shares a `location_ref`.

It is **not** responsible for:

* choosing a shot's location (owned upstream; frozen in `shot-list.json`);
* authoring or editing location descriptions (owned by the Location Engine, **5.1.4**);
* the ordering of prompt segments (owned by **5.5.1**);
* character or camera injection (**5.5.2**, **5.5.4**);
* style/forbidden-aesthetic enforcement (**5.5.5**);
* scoring environment continuity (**5.6.2**);
* any provider-side relighting or set extension (prohibited — providers render only, **5.3**).

---

# Location Injection Design Objectives

Location Injection shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## LOI-301 — Reference-Only Environments

A shot references its environment solely by an immutable `location_ref`. The prompt must never carry a free-text environment description authored anywhere except the Location Bible.

Ad-hoc environment prose in a shot is prohibited.

---

## LOI-302 — Single Authoritative Source

The Location Bible (`location-bible.yaml`, **5.1.4**) is the **only** source of environment identity. No environment fact may enter a prompt from any other artifact, provider default, or heuristic.

---

## LOI-303 — Deterministic Expansion

Given the same `location_ref` and the same Location Bible content hash, injection must produce a byte-identical environment fragment on every invocation, every worker, and every retry.

Random phrasing, paraphrase, or model-generated description is prohibited (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism, and with **5.5.1**).

---

## LOI-304 — Environment Consistency Across Shots

Every shot that carries the same `location_ref` within a build must receive the **same** canonical environment fragment.

Consistency is guaranteed by construction, not achieved by post-hoc correction.

---

## LOI-305 — Immutability Within a Build

The resolved environment for a `location_ref` is fixed for the entire duration of a build. It must never change between shots, between preview and final profiles, or between retries of the same build.

A build renders exactly one version of each location.

---

## LOI-306 — Referential Integrity

Every `location_ref` used by any shot must resolve to exactly one record in the Location Bible. An unresolved or ambiguous reference is a fatal prompt-assembly error and must fail closed (Principle 5 — Fail Closed).

---

## LOI-307 — Time-of-Day & Lighting Binding

Where a location encodes a time-of-day or lighting state (e.g. `operations_center_night`), that state must be bound deterministically into the environment fragment and must remain consistent with the location's `time_of_day` field.

Lighting is a property of the location reference, not a free provider choice.

---

## LOI-308 — Verifiable Provenance

Every injected environment fragment must carry provenance — the `location_ref`, the Location Bible `content_hash`, and a record fingerprint — sufficient for downstream continuity verification (**5.6.2**) to confirm that shots sharing a location were built from identical source.

---

# The Location Reference Model

A shot never contains an environment description. It contains a **reference**.

```yaml id="loi-shot-ref"
shot:
  shot_id: sc03_sh07
  location_ref: operations_center_night   # immutable; resolves in Location Bible
  # ... character_refs, camera, strategy (owned elsewhere)
```

The reference is the atom of environment identity. It is:

* **opaque** — its string value carries no descriptive authority; the words live in the bible;
* **immutable** — frozen into `shot-list.json` upstream and never rewritten by rendering;
* **shared** — many shots may carry the same ref, and all must render identically;
* **semantic** — by convention it may encode place and state (`operations_center` + `night`), but the authority for that state is the bible record, not the string.

This mirrors the character reference model of **5.5.2** (`character.ref: maya_v1`) exactly, so that prompt assembly (**5.5.1**) treats characters and locations as parallel reference-resolved segments.

---

# Location Bible — Authoritative Source

Location Injection resolves against the **Location Bible**, contracted as an input in **5.1.4** and produced by the Location Engine. The bible is read-only, approved, and environment-locked before any shot is rendered.

```yaml id="loi-location-bible"
location_bible:
  schema_version: string          # required
  build_id: string                # required; must match the build under render
  approved: bool                  # required; must be true
  approval_ref: string            # required; resolves to a passed gate
  generated_at: iso8601           # required
  content_hash: sha256            # required; fixity anchor for provenance
  locations:                      # required; keyed by ref id
    - ref: string                 # required; e.g. operations_center_night
      display_name: string        # required
      description: string         # required; canonical environment description
      time_of_day: string         # optional; e.g. night | day
      environment_lock: bool      # required; must be true (immutable environment)
```

Contract obligations carried over from **5.1.4** and relied upon here:

* every `ref` in `locations` is unique;
* `environment_lock` is `true` for every location;
* `approved == true` and `approval_ref` resolves to a passed Quality Gate;
* `content_hash` verifies against the recomputed hash on ingest;
* every `location_ref` used in `shot-list.json` exists in `locations` (referential integrity).

Location Injection **trusts but re-verifies**: ingest (**5.1.4**) has already validated the bible, but injection independently confirms that the specific `location_ref` it is asked to inject resolves before it emits a fragment (see *Referential Validation*).

---

# Resolved Location Record

During injection, a `location_ref` resolves to exactly one **location record** — the internal, immutable projection of a bible entry used to build the fragment.

```yaml id="loi-record"
resolved_location:
  ref:                 operations_center_night
  display_name:        "Operations Center — Night"
  description:         # canonical environment prose (verbatim from bible)
  time_of_day:         night          # from bible, or derived+confirmed
  lighting_profile:    # deterministic binding derived from time_of_day
  environment_lock:    true
  bible_content_hash:  # sha256 of the source Location Bible
  record_fingerprint:  # sha256(ref + description + time_of_day)
```

The record is a **read-only value object**. It is constructed once per `(build_id, location_ref)` and cached for the build; every shot sharing the ref receives the identical record (LOI-304, LOI-305). The `record_fingerprint` is the consistency witness: two shots with the same ref must exhibit the same fingerprint.

```text id="loi-fingerprint"
record_fingerprint = sha256(
  ref +
  description +
  time_of_day
)
```

Because `description` and `time_of_day` are taken verbatim from an environment-locked bible, the fingerprint is stable for the life of the build.

---

# Deterministic Resolution & Expansion

Resolution turns a reference into a fragment through a fixed, side-effect-free pipeline.

```text id="loi-resolve"
location_ref (operations_center_night)
        │
        ▼
1. lookup ref in loaded Location Bible (exact match, unique)
        │
        ▼
2. assert environment_lock == true
        │
        ▼
3. build resolved_location record (verbatim description)
        │
        ▼
4. bind time_of_day → lighting_profile (deterministic map)
        │
        ▼
5. render environment fragment via fixed template
        │
        ▼
6. attach provenance (ref, bible_content_hash, record_fingerprint)
        │
        ▼
environment fragment  →  prompt slot owned by 5.5.1
```

The expansion **template** is fixed and version-pinned. It concatenates canonical fields in a fixed order and never paraphrases:

```text id="loi-template"
[ENVIRONMENT]
Location: {display_name}
{description}
Time of day: {time_of_day}
Lighting: {lighting_profile}
```

Rules:

* the `description` string is emitted **verbatim** — never summarized, translated, or re-ordered;
* no field is omitted when present; optional `time_of_day` absent means the lighting binding falls back to the location's declared default (see *Time-of-Day & Lighting Binding*);
* the template version is pinned per build; a template change is a new build input, never an in-build mutation (LOI-305);
* the same `resolved_location` always yields the same fragment bytes (LOI-303).

Determinism here is identical in spirit to **5.5.2**: injection is pure resolution plus fixed templating, never generative authoring.

---

# Injection Into the Assembled Prompt

Location Injection does not own the prompt. It emits one **segment** into the deterministic envelope defined by **5.5.1**.

Recall the canonical prompt structure (original 5.18, formalized in **5.5.1**):

```text
Character  +  Location  +  Camera  +  Lighting  +  Blocking  +  Emotion  +  Shot Purpose  +  Visual Style
```

Location Injection populates the **Location** segment and contributes to the **Lighting** segment via time-of-day binding. It writes only into the slots **5.5.1** assigns it; it never reorders, wraps, or edits neighbouring segments.

```yaml id="loi-slot"
prompt_segment:
  slot: location                 # fixed position owned by 5.5.1
  source: location_injection     # 5.5.3
  content:                       # the rendered environment fragment
  provenance:
    location_ref: operations_center_night
    bible_content_hash:
    record_fingerprint:
    template_version:
```

Placement rules:

* the location segment occupies the fixed ordinal position assigned by **5.5.1** — deterministic ordering is a property of the assembler, not of this section;
* the segment is emitted with its provenance block so the assembler can attach a whole-prompt provenance manifest;
* the provider never receives article text, and never receives the bible itself — only the resolved, templated environment prose (consistent with 5.18 and **5.5.1**).

---

# Environment Consistency Guarantee

The central guarantee of this section: **shots that share a `location_ref` share an environment, exactly.**

```text id="loi-consistency"
shot A  location_ref = operations_center_night ─┐
shot B  location_ref = operations_center_night ─┤
shot C  location_ref = operations_center_night ─┘
                                                 │
                       resolve once per build_id │
                                                 ▼
                           single resolved_location record
                                                 │
                       identical fragment + fingerprint
                                                 ▼
        every shot renders the SAME operations center at night
```

How the guarantee is enforced:

* **single resolution per ref per build** — the resolved record is computed once for `(build_id, operations_center_night)` and reused; there is no per-shot re-authoring path (LOI-304).
* **verbatim description** — the fragment is byte-identical because the source prose is copied, not regenerated (LOI-303).
* **fingerprint witness** — every emitted segment carries `record_fingerprint`; equal refs must carry equal fingerprints. A mismatch is a build defect, not a tolerated variation.
* **no provider drift** — providers render the supplied prompt and may not relight, redecorate, or extend the set (LOI boundary; **5.3**).

This is exactly the character-consistency guarantee of **5.5.2**, applied to environments. Together they make a multi-shot trailer feel like one physical world inhabited by one consistent cast.

---

# Location Immutability Within a Build

Locations are **immutable** (original 5.20). Immutability is scoped to the build and enforced at three boundaries.

```yaml id="loi-immutability"
immutability:
  source_lock:   environment_lock == true      # bible is environment-locked (5.1.4)
  build_lock:    resolved_location frozen per build_id
  provider_lock: provider may not alter environment identity
```

Consequences:

* the same location renders identically in **preview** (`runway_preview`) and **final** (`runway_final`) profiles — the profile changes render quality, never environment identity (see **5.6.4**/**5.6.5**);
* a **retry** of a shot resolves the same record and emits the same fragment; retries never re-author environments (consistent with the retry policy owned by **5.4.5**);
* a change to a location description requires a **new** approved Location Bible and therefore a new build input — never an in-flight edit (LOI-305);
* two builds may legitimately differ if their bibles differ; within one build, they may not.

If the Location Bible presented mid-build differs from the one ingested (a changed `content_hash`), injection must fail closed rather than silently render a mixed world.

---

# Referential Validation

Before emitting any fragment, injection validates the reference. Referential integrity is a hard precondition (LOI-306).

```yaml id="loi-validation"
reference_validation:
  ref_present_in_bible: true          # exact, unique match required
  ref_unique: true                    # no duplicate ref ids
  environment_lock_true: true         # location must be environment-locked
  bible_approved: true                # approved == true
  bible_hash_matches_build: true      # content_hash matches ingested bible
  time_of_day_consistent: true        # binding agrees with record
```

Failure handling:

* an **unresolved** `location_ref` (absent from the bible) is a fatal prompt-assembly error — the shot cannot be rendered, and the build fails closed rather than inventing an environment (Principle 5);
* an **ambiguous** ref (would indicate a duplicate bible entry, itself a **5.1.4** violation) is fatal;
* a location with `environment_lock == false` is rejected — an unlocked environment cannot guarantee consistency;
* a **hash mismatch** between the bible the shot expects and the bible loaded is fatal (immutability breach).

Fatal reference errors route the build to **Manual Review** (mirroring the escalation model of the queue in **5.4.1**); rendering never substitutes a default or nearest-match location. There is no "reasonable guess" for an environment.

---

# Time-of-Day & Lighting Binding

A location reference frequently encodes a **time-of-day / lighting state** — the canonical example being `operations_center_night`. That state is a property of the reference and the bible record, never a free provider choice (LOI-307).

```yaml id="loi-lighting"
lighting_binding:
  ref:            operations_center_night
  time_of_day:    night            # from bible record
  lighting_profile:                # deterministic map from time_of_day
    key_light:    low
    ambient:      cool_blue_screens_glow
    contrast:     high
    practical:    console_and_monitor_light
```

Binding rules:

* `time_of_day` is taken from the bible record; if the record omits it, the binding uses the location's declared default, and if none exists the binding is `unspecified` and the location prose alone governs lighting;
* the `time_of_day` embedded in the ref string (e.g. `..._night`) must **agree** with the record's `time_of_day` field; disagreement is a validation error (`time_of_day_consistent`), never a silent reconciliation;
