* the `lighting_profile` is produced by a **fixed, deterministic map** from `time_of_day`; the same input always yields the same lighting fragment (LOI-303);
* lighting derived here feeds the **Lighting** slot of the assembled prompt (**5.5.1**); it is coordinated with, but does not override, camera-driven exposure notes from **5.5.4**;
* a provider may not choose or alter lighting — `operations_center_night` renders as night, in every shot, in every profile, on every retry.

This binding is what makes a night operations center stay night: the darkness is bound to the reference, not left to the model.

---

# Boundary — What Providers May Not Do

Location Injection enforces the provider boundary for environments (consistent with 5.9 and **5.3.1**). A provider is a rendering backend; it renders the supplied environment and nothing more.

Providers must **never**:

* alter, relight, or reinterpret the environment identity;
* extend, redecorate, or add set elements not present in the canonical description;
* substitute a different time-of-day or lighting state;
* merge or blend two locations;
* invent an environment when a reference is missing (that path fails closed, not renders).

Providers **may** only:

* render the supplied environment fragment as delivered.

Any environment authority attempting to originate from a provider is a boundary violation and is out of contract.

---

# Provenance to Downstream Continuity Verification

Location Injection emits provenance so that **Visual Quality / Continuity Validation (5.6.2)** can *verify* the consistency this section *guarantees*.

```yaml id="loi-provenance"
environment_provenance:
  shot_id:
  location_ref:            operations_center_night
  bible_content_hash:
  record_fingerprint:
  template_version:
  time_of_day:             night
```

Downstream, **5.6.2** uses this provenance to:

* group shots by `location_ref` and confirm identical `record_fingerprint` across the group (source consistency);
* check that rendered frames of a shared location are visually coherent (rendered consistency), scoring against `minimum_score: 0.90`;
* flag a **continuity risk** for Manual Review when rendered frames diverge despite identical source provenance — evidence of provider drift, not of an injection defect.

The division of labour is strict: **5.5.3 guarantees the inputs are identical; 5.6.2 verifies the outputs are coherent.** Injection provenance is the bridge between the two, and it is why a continuity failure can be attributed unambiguously to rendering rather than to prompt assembly.

---

# Events

Location Injection emits events for every material action.

```yaml id="loi-events"
location_injection_events:
  - location_ref_resolved
  - location_record_built
  - location_fragment_injected
  - time_of_day_bound
  - location_consistency_confirmed        # fingerprint matched across shared ref
  - location_ref_unresolved               # fatal; routes to manual review
  - location_lock_violation               # environment_lock false or hash mismatch
  - location_time_of_day_mismatch         # ref string vs record disagreement
```

Every event references the `shot_id`, `location_ref`, `build_id`, and `bible_content_hash`, and is durably recorded for audit and for the continuity provenance trail consumed by **5.6.2**.

---

# Metrics

Injection reporting shall include, at minimum:

```yaml id="loi-metrics"
location_injection_metrics:
  locations_resolved_total
  distinct_locations_per_build
  shots_per_location
  fragment_bytes
  consistency_fingerprint_groups
  consistency_mismatch_count          # must be 0
  unresolved_ref_count                # must be 0
  lock_violation_count                # must be 0
  time_of_day_mismatch_count          # must be 0
  injection_duration
```

`consistency_mismatch_count`, `unresolved_ref_count`, `lock_violation_count`, and `time_of_day_mismatch_count` are **fail-closed gauges**: any non-zero value blocks the build and escalates to Manual Review. `shots_per_location` feeds the cache-eligibility analysis in **5.6.3** (shared locations improve reuse).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

Location Injection operates under least privilege and carries no secrets.

Required controls:

* read-only access to the loaded, approved Location Bible scoped to the build namespace;
* no provider credentials in the environment fragment or provenance;
* no article text, screenplay prose, or source URLs in any emitted segment;
* namespace isolation between concurrent builds — one build's bible can never resolve another build's ref;
* tamper detection via `bible_content_hash` on every emitted segment;
* audit events for every resolution and every fatal reference error.

The environment fragment delivered to a provider contains only canonical environment prose and lighting — never bibles, keys, or identifiers beyond the opaque `location_ref` carried in provenance.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="loi-config"
location_injection:
  template_version: v1               # pinned per build; change is a new input
  verbatim_description: true         # description copied, never paraphrased
  require_environment_lock: true     # reject unlocked locations
  fail_closed_on_unresolved: true    # missing ref → manual review, never default
  time_of_day:
    bind_from_record: true
    require_ref_record_agreement: true
    default_when_absent: unspecified
  lighting_map:
    night:   { key_light: low,  ambient: cool_blue_screens_glow, contrast: high }
    day:     { key_light: high, ambient: neutral_daylight,       contrast: medium }
    unspecified: {}                  # location prose governs
  provenance:
    emit_fingerprint: true
    emit_bible_hash: true
```

All values are validated at startup. Invalid configuration fails closed and blocks injection rather than emitting an under-specified environment. `require_environment_lock` and `fail_closed_on_unresolved` must not be disabled in a production build.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* resolve a valid `location_ref` to its unique bible record;
* verbatim emission of `description` (no paraphrase, no reorder);
* deterministic fragment bytes across repeated invocations;
* fixed template ordering and version pinning;
* time-of-day binding to the correct lighting profile;
* `record_fingerprint` stability for a given record;
* provenance block populated with ref, bible hash, and fingerprint.

## Integration Tests

* three shots sharing `operations_center_night` receive identical fragments and fingerprints;
* preview and final profiles of one shot yield identical environment identity;
* a retry of a shot re-emits the identical fragment;
* injected location segment lands in the correct **5.5.1** slot alongside character (**5.5.2**) and camera (**5.5.4**) segments;
* provenance flows to **5.6.2** and groups shots by location.

## Consistency Tests

* fingerprint equality across all shots sharing a ref (must hold for every ref);
* two distinct locations never collide to the same fragment;
* environment identity invariant across the full shot set of a build.

## Failure Injection Tests

* unresolved `location_ref` → fatal, routes to Manual Review, no fragment emitted;
* duplicate bible ref → ambiguous, fatal;
* `environment_lock == false` → rejected;
* `content_hash` mismatch between expected and loaded bible → immutability breach, fatal;
* ref string `..._night` disagreeing with record `time_of_day: day` → mismatch, fatal.

## Recovery Tests

* re-resolution after worker restart yields the identical record and fragment;
* provenance reconstructable from `location_ref` + Location Bible content hash;
* build re-run with the same bible reproduces byte-identical environment fragments.

---

# Location Injection Quality Gate

```yaml id="loi-quality-gate"
location_injection_quality_gate:
  every_shot_has_location_ref: true
  every_ref_resolves_in_bible: true
  every_location_environment_locked: true
  descriptions_emitted_verbatim: true
  fragments_deterministic: true
  shared_ref_fingerprints_identical: true
  time_of_day_bound_and_consistent: true
  provenance_attached_to_every_segment: true
  bible_hash_verified: true
  no_provider_authored_environment: true
  unresolved_refs: 0
  consistency_mismatches: 0
  lock_violations: 0
```

Any failed boolean or non-zero counter blocks the build and escalates to Manual Review (Fail Closed).

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant LOI-401

A shot references its environment only by an immutable `location_ref`; it never carries free-text environment description.

## Invariant LOI-402

The Location Bible is the single authoritative source of environment identity; no environment fact enters a prompt from any other origin.

## Invariant LOI-403

The environment fragment for a given `location_ref` and Location Bible content hash is deterministic and byte-identical across workers, profiles, and retries.

## Invariant LOI-404

Every shot sharing a `location_ref` within a build receives the identical environment fragment and `record_fingerprint`.

## Invariant LOI-405

A resolved location is immutable for the full duration of a build; changing a location requires a new approved Location Bible and a new build.

## Invariant LOI-406

Every `location_ref` must resolve to exactly one environment-locked bible record; an unresolved or ambiguous reference fails closed and is never defaulted.

## Invariant LOI-407

Time-of-day and lighting are bound from the location reference and its record; a provider may never choose or alter the environment's lighting state.

## Invariant LOI-408

Every injected environment segment carries provenance sufficient for continuity verification in **5.6.2** to attribute any inconsistency to rendering rather than to injection.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                               |
| ------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-532 | Environments are injected by immutable reference (`location_ref`); shots never carry authorable environment prose.      |
| ADR-533 | The Location Bible (`location-bible.yaml`, 5.1.4) is the sole authoritative source of environment identity; injection copies descriptions verbatim and never paraphrases. |
| ADR-534 | A `location_ref` is resolved once per build to a frozen record, guaranteeing environment consistency and immutability across all shots, profiles, and retries. |
| ADR-535 | Time-of-day and lighting are bound deterministically from the reference and its record; providers may never originate or alter environment lighting. |
| ADR-536 | Every environment segment emits provenance (ref, bible hash, record fingerprint) so continuity verification in 5.6.2 confirms — rather than creates — environment consistency; unresolved references fail closed to Manual Review. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.5.3** is complete when:

* Every shot references its environment solely by an immutable `location_ref`, and no shot carries free-text environment description.
* The Location Bible (**5.1.4**) is established as the single authoritative source of environment identity, and injection copies canonical descriptions verbatim.
* Resolution of a `location_ref` into an environment fragment is deterministic, version-pinned, and byte-identical across workers, preview/final profiles, and retries.
* Every shot sharing a `location_ref` provably receives the identical environment fragment and `record_fingerprint`, guaranteeing environment consistency by construction.
* A resolved location is immutable for the duration of a build; changing a location requires a new approved Location Bible.
* Referential validation guarantees every `location_ref` resolves to exactly one environment-locked record, and unresolved or ambiguous references fail closed to Manual Review rather than inventing an environment.
* Time-of-day and lighting (e.g. `operations_center_night`) are bound deterministically from the reference and its record, and providers can never originate or alter environment lighting.
* The location segment is injected into the exact prompt slot owned by **5.5.1**, parallel to character injection (**5.5.2**) and camera injection (**5.5.4**), and providers receive only canonical environment prose — never bibles, secrets, or article text.
* Environment provenance flows to continuity verification (**5.6.2**), which confirms rather than creates the consistency this section guarantees.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gates, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement deterministic, immutable, consistency-guaranteeing location injection without ever letting a provider or a shot re-author an environment.


---

# Part 5.5.4 — Camera Injection

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Camera Injection** stage of the Rendering Platform.

Camera Injection is the deterministic step that resolves the approved camera plan for a shot and writes its **camera language** — lens, movement, angle, framing, and related cinematography parameters — into the assembled render prompt produced by **5.5.1**.

Camera Injection exists to guarantee one property above all others:

> The camera the audience sees is the camera the **Shot Planner** designed — never the camera a rendering provider invented.

Camera language is a creative decision. It is authored upstream, during shot planning, and frozen in `camera-plan.json` (an input contract defined in **5.1.4**). By the time rendering begins, the camera for every shot is already decided. Camera Injection does not choose lenses, invent movements, or reinterpret angles. It **transcribes** an already approved camera specification into provider-consumable prompt text, deterministically and losslessly.

The Rendering Platform is an execution layer. Camera Injection is the component that carries the approved cinematographic intent across the provider boundary **without letting the provider redesign it**. Runway — or any future provider — receives fully specified camera language as an instruction to execute, never as a blank to fill.

If Camera Injection is correct, then two renders of the same approved shot request the same lens, the same movement, and the same angle, every time, on every provider. If it is incorrect, the platform has silently ceded a creative decision to a stochastic backend, and the trailer no longer reflects the approved plan.

Camera Injection prevents that.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the **camera parameter model** — lens, movement, angle, framing, height, and the related cinematography parameters that constitute camera language;
* the `camera-plan.json` input contract as the **sole authoritative source** of camera parameters;
* the controlled vocabulary and value domains for each camera parameter;
* deterministic resolution of a shot's camera specification from the camera plan;
* deterministic serialization of camera language into the render prompt (feeding **5.5.1**);
* the **prohibition** on any rendering provider altering, inferring, defaulting, or overriding camera language (ties to the provider boundary in **5.3.1**);
* validation of camera parameters before injection (fail closed on missing or invalid camera data);
* the injection block position and format within the deterministic prompt;
* downstream **camera movement verification** hooks consumed by Visual Quality Validation in **5.6.2**;
* camera-injection events, metrics, invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* the overall deterministic prompt structure and byte-exact ordering (defined in **5.5.1**);
* character description injection (defined in **5.5.2**);
* location description injection (defined in **5.5.3**);
* visual style enforcement and the forbidden-aesthetic list (defined in **5.5.5**);
* how the Shot Planner *authors* the camera plan (that is a Storyboard/Screenplay concern upstream of Part 5);
* the provider submission payload envelope (defined in **5.3.3**);
* the numeric scoring of camera movement fidelity (owned by **5.6.2**).

Camera Injection **consumes** the camera plan and **produces** a camera language block. It does not own camera authorship, and it does not own quality scoring; it owns the faithful, deterministic transport between them.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **Camera language is authored once, by the Shot Planner, and is injected verbatim — providers execute the camera, they never design it.**

Every camera parameter that reaches a provider must be traceable to a specific field in `camera-plan.json`. No camera value may be introduced, defaulted, softened, or embellished during rendering. A provider that receives "lens 50mm, movement slow_push, angle eye" must render that camera; it may not substitute a wider lens, an added dolly, or a different angle because its model "prefers" one.

A corollary follows directly:

> **A missing or invalid camera parameter is a fail-closed condition, never a provider default.**

If the camera plan does not fully specify a shot's camera, Camera Injection must **not** fabricate a value and must **not** allow the provider to fabricate one. The task is routed to Manual Review (consistent with Fail Closed, Principle 5). The platform never publishes a trailer whose camera language was silently completed by a rendering backend.

This principle is the rendering-side enforcement of the upstream rule that camera design belongs to planning. **5.3.1** forbids providers from *designing* camera language; **5.5.4** guarantees they never *need to*, because the camera is always fully injected before submission.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Shot Planner (Storyboard / Screenplay)
        │
        │  authors camera design (creative decision)
        ▼
camera-plan.json   ── input contract (5.1.4) ──┐
        │                                       │
        ▼                                       │
Render Task Generation                          │
        │                                       │
        ▼                                       │
Deterministic Prompt Assembly (5.5.1)           │
        │                                       │
        ├── Character Injection      (5.5.2)    │
        ├── Location Injection       (5.5.3)    │
        ├── Camera Injection ◄───────────────────┘  THIS SECTION
        │        │
        │        ├── resolve shot camera spec
        │        ├── validate against vocabulary
        │        ├── serialize camera language block
        │        └── emit movement verification hint
        │
        └── Visual Style Enforcement (5.5.5)
        │
        ▼
Assembled Render Prompt  (camera language frozen)
        │
        ▼
Provider Adapter (5.3.2 / 5.3.4)  ── MUST NOT alter camera language (5.3.1)
        │
        ▼
Rendered Asset
        │
        ▼
Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2) ── camera movement check
```

Camera Injection sits **inside** Deterministic Prompt Assembly (**5.5.1**), as one of the ordered injection stages. It reads only from `camera-plan.json` (the authoritative source) and writes only into the camera language block of the assembled prompt. It never reads from, or writes to, the provider; the arrow into the provider is one-way and the camera block is immutable once assembled.

---

# Responsibilities

Camera Injection is responsible for:

* resolving the exact camera specification for a given `shot_id` from `camera-plan.json`;
* validating every camera parameter against its controlled vocabulary and value domain;
* rejecting (fail closed) any shot whose camera specification is missing or invalid;
* deterministically serializing camera language into a canonical prompt block;
* placing that block at the fixed position within the assembled prompt required by **5.5.1**;
* preserving byte-for-byte reproducibility across identical inputs;
* recording camera provenance (which plan version, which fields) into the render manifest;
* emitting a normalized camera-movement descriptor consumed by **5.6.2**;
* ensuring no camera parameter reaches a provider as an inferable blank.

It is **not** responsible for:

* authoring or altering camera design (owned by the Shot Planner, upstream);
* choosing lenses, movements, or angles (a creative decision, already made);
* character or location description text (see **5.5.2**, **5.5.3**);
* the forbidden-aesthetic style constraints (see **5.5.5**);
* provider API invocation (see **5.3**);
* scoring whether the rendered camera movement matched the request (see **5.6.2**);
* modifying `camera-plan.json` — the plan is read-only at render time.

---

# Design Objectives

Camera Injection shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## CMI-301 — Single Source of Camera Truth

All camera parameters shall originate **exclusively** from `camera-plan.json`. No other artifact — not the render strategy, not the provider adapter, not a hard-coded default — may contribute a camera value. If a camera parameter is not present in the camera plan, it does not exist for rendering purposes.

---

## CMI-302 — Verbatim Injection

The camera specification resolved from the plan shall be injected without semantic modification. Normalization is limited to deterministic formatting (canonical unit strings, canonical token casing). The **meaning** of every parameter is preserved exactly: 50mm stays 50mm, slow_push stays slow_push, eye stays eye.

---

## CMI-303 — Provider Non-Authority

No rendering provider may originate, override, default, infer, or embellish any camera parameter. The provider boundary (**5.3.1**) forbids providers from designing camera language; Camera Injection guarantees the provider always receives a complete camera specification and therefore never has a reason — or an opening — to invent one.

---

## CMI-304 — Determinism

Given identical `camera-plan.json` content and an identical `shot_id`, Camera Injection shall produce a byte-identical camera language block. There is no randomness, no time dependence, and no provider dependence in the produced text (consistent with Performance Constraint 2 — Determinism).

---

## CMI-305 — Fail Closed on Incompleteness

A shot whose camera specification is missing, partial, or fails vocabulary validation shall **not** be rendered. The task is routed to Manual Review with a precise validation error, never rendered with a substituted or defaulted camera.

---

## CMI-306 — Full Provenance

Every injected camera block shall be traceable to the exact `camera-plan.json` version and the exact fields it was built from. Provenance is recorded in the render manifest so any rendered frame's camera language can be audited back to an approved planning decision.

---

## CMI-307 — Movement Verifiability

Camera Injection shall emit a normalized, machine-readable movement descriptor alongside the prompt text, so that Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**) can verify that the rendered asset actually exhibits the requested camera movement.

---

# Camera Parameter Model

Camera language is modelled as a fixed set of parameters. Three are **core** (present in the original specification — lens, movement, angle) and the remainder are **related cinematography parameters** that complete a professionally specified shot. Every parameter is authored by the Shot Planner and carried, unchanged, by Camera Injection.

| Parameter        | Field           | Required | Governs                                          |
| ---------------- | --------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Lens             | `lens`          | yes      | focal length / field of view                     |
| Movement         | `movement`      | yes      | camera motion type                               |
| Angle            | `angle`         | yes      | vertical viewpoint relative to subject           |
| Shot size        | `shot_size`     | yes      | framing scale (wide → extreme close-up)          |
| Height           | `height`        | no       | camera elevation relative to subject             |
| Movement speed   | `movement_speed`| no       | pace of the movement (still → fast)              |
| Movement easing  | `easing`        | no       | acceleration profile of the movement             |
| Depth of field   | `depth_of_field`| no       | focus falloff (deep / shallow)                   |
| Aperture         | `aperture`      | no       | f-stop, when a specific bokeh is intended        |
| Focus target     | `focus_target`  | no       | subject the lens is focused on                   |
| Composition      | `composition`   | no       | framing rule (centered, rule_of_thirds, …)       |
| Stability        | `stability`     | no       | tripod-locked vs handheld texture                |

Core parameters are **mandatory**: a shot with no lens, no movement, or no angle is incomplete and fails closed (CMI-305). Related parameters are optional in the plan; when absent they are **omitted** from the injected block — they are never filled with a rendering-side default (CMI-301). Absence means "the planner did not constrain this," not "let the provider decide."

The canonical example from the original specification:

```yaml
camera:
  lens: 50mm
  movement: slow_push
  angle: eye
```

is a complete core specification and injects cleanly.

---

# Camera Plan Schema

`camera-plan.json` is the authoritative camera source (**5.1.4**). It maps every planned `shot_id` to exactly one camera specification.

```yaml id="cmi-plan"
camera_plan:
  plan_version:            # semver; recorded in provenance (CMI-306)
  storyboard_ref:          # the approved storyboard this plan derives from
  shots:
    - shot_id:             # unique; joins to shot-list.json and the render task
      camera:
        lens:              # e.g. 24mm | 35mm | 50mm | 85mm | 135mm
        movement:          # controlled vocabulary (see Movement Vocabulary)
        angle:             # eye | low | high | overhead | dutch
        shot_size:         # wide | medium | close | extreme_close
        height:            # ground | low | eye | high        (optional)
        movement_speed:    # still | slow | medium | fast      (optional)
        easing:            # linear | ease_in | ease_out | ease_in_out  (optional)
        depth_of_field:    # deep | medium | shallow           (optional)
        aperture:          # e.g. f1.8 | f2.8 | f5.6           (optional)
        focus_target:      # subject reference, e.g. character ref  (optional)
        composition:       # centered | rule_of_thirds | leading_lines  (optional)
        stability:         # locked | handheld                 (optional)
```

The camera plan is produced upstream and is **read-only** during rendering. Camera Injection never writes to it. There is exactly one camera specification per shot; a shot with two conflicting camera entries is a plan-integrity error and fails closed.

---

# Controlled Vocabularies

Every camera parameter draws from a fixed vocabulary or a validated value domain. Free-form camera prose is prohibited, because prose invites provider reinterpretation. Constrained tokens inject deterministically and validate cleanly.

## Lens Vocabulary

Lens is expressed as a focal length with a `mm` unit, drawn from the approved set of cinematographic primes.

```yaml
lens_vocabulary:
  - 18mm      # ultra-wide, environment
  - 24mm      # wide
  - 35mm      # natural-wide, editorial
  - 50mm      # standard, human-eye perspective
  - 85mm      # portrait / emotional close
  - 135mm     # long, compression
```

Values outside the approved set fail validation. The unit is normalized to lowercase `mm` with no space (`50mm`, never `50 MM` or `50 mm`).

## Movement Vocabulary

Movement is the camera's motion path. Only these tokens are permitted.

```yaml
movement_vocabulary:
  - static          # locked frame, no camera motion
  - slow_push       # gradual dolly-in toward subject
  - slow_pull       # gradual dolly-out from subject
  - pan_left
  - pan_right
  - tilt_up
  - tilt_down
  - tracking        # lateral follow of subject
  - crane_up
  - crane_down
  - orbit           # arc around subject
  - handheld_drift  # subtle organic instability
```

`slow_push` (the canonical example) is a permitted token. Compound or free-form movements (e.g. "a sweeping push that then whips left") are prohibited; a shot needing two motions is two shots in the plan.

## Angle Vocabulary

```yaml
angle_vocabulary:
  - eye        # subject at camera eye level (neutral)
  - low        # camera below subject, looking up
  - high       # camera above subject, looking down
  - overhead   # top-down
  - dutch      # canted horizon (used sparingly, editorial)
```

## Shot Size Vocabulary

```yaml
shot_size_vocabulary:
  - wide           # establishing / full environment
  - medium         # subject waist-up
  - close          # subject face / detail
  - extreme_close  # eyes, hands, single detail
```

Optional-parameter vocabularies (`height`, `movement_speed`, `easing`, `depth_of_field`, `composition`, `stability`) follow the value domains listed in the plan schema above. `aperture` is validated as an f-stop pattern (`f` followed by a number). `focus_target`, when present, must reference a known subject (typically a character ref shared with **5.5.2**).

Any value not in its vocabulary — or any core parameter absent entirely — is a validation failure (CMI-305).

---

# Deterministic Resolution

Resolving a shot's camera specification is a pure function of the plan and the shot id.

```text id="cmi-resolve"
resolve_camera(camera_plan, shot_id):
  1. locate the single shots[] entry with matching shot_id
        └─ 0 matches → fail closed (missing camera plan for shot)
        └─ >1 match  → fail closed (ambiguous camera plan)
  2. read camera.{lens, movement, angle, shot_size}   (core, required)
        └─ any absent → fail closed (incomplete core camera)
  3. read present optional parameters, in canonical order
        └─ absent optionals are omitted, never defaulted
  4. validate every present value against its vocabulary / domain
        └─ any invalid → fail closed (invalid camera value)
  5. return CameraSpec { core + present optionals, plan_version, source_fields }
```

Resolution is deterministic (CMI-304): the same plan and the same `shot_id` always yield the same `CameraSpec`. No step consults the provider, the clock, the render strategy, or any random source. The returned `CameraSpec` carries its own provenance (`plan_version`, `source_fields`) for CMI-306.

---

# Deterministic Serialization

The resolved `CameraSpec` is serialized into a canonical camera language block. Serialization is deterministic: parameters appear in a **fixed order**, optionals are omitted (not blanked) when absent, and tokens use canonical casing and units.

Canonical ordering of the block:

```text
1. shot_size
2. lens
3. angle   (+ height, when present)
4. movement   (+ movement_speed, easing, when present)
5. depth_of_field   (+ aperture, focus_target, when present)
6. composition
7. stability
```

For the canonical example the produced block is:

```text
CAMERA:
  framing: medium shot, 50mm lens, eye-level angle
  movement: slow push toward subject
```

For a fully specified shot the block is:

```text
CAMERA:
  framing: close shot, 85mm lens, low angle from ground height
  movement: slow push toward subject, ease-in, slow speed
  focus: shallow depth of field at f1.8, focused on subject
  composition: rule of thirds, tripod-locked
```

Two rules govern serialization:

* **Fixed order, fixed template** — the same set of present parameters always renders the same string, byte for byte (CMI-304).
* **Omit, never default** — an absent optional produces no text at all; it never emits "default depth of field" or similar. The provider therefore sees only what the planner specified (CMI-301, CMI-303).

The block is emitted into the render prompt at the position that **5.5.1** reserves for camera language, between location (**5.5.3**) and lighting/blocking. Camera Injection owns the block's *content*; **5.5.1** owns its *position* and the overall prompt envelope.

---

# Injection Point within the Assembled Prompt

The original prompt structure (from **5.5.1**) is:

```text
Character  +  Location  +  Camera  +  Lighting  +  Blocking  +  Emotion  +  Shot Purpose  +  Visual Style
```

Camera Injection populates the **Camera** segment only. It does not touch adjacent segments. The assembled prompt is built by **5.5.1** in this fixed order so that the whole prompt — and therefore the provider request — is deterministic. Camera Injection's contribution to that determinism is a stable, ordered, defaults-free camera block.

The provider receives the assembled prompt as an **instruction to execute**. It never receives article text (per **5.5.1**), and — the concern of this section — it never receives an incomplete camera it is expected to complete. The camera is whole before it crosses the provider boundary.

---

# The Provider Non-Override Boundary

This is the enforcement heart of Camera Injection and the rendering-side counterpart to the provider boundary defined in **5.3.1**.

From **5.3.1 / original 5.9**, a provider (Runway or any future backend) must never:

* generate stories;
* determine pacing;
* create dialogue;
* **design camera language.**

Camera Injection operationalizes the last clause. The rules are absolute:

```yaml
provider_camera_rules:
  provider_may_originate_camera:   false
  provider_may_override_camera:    false
  provider_may_default_camera:     false
  provider_may_infer_camera:       false
  provider_may_embellish_camera:   false
  camera_source:                   camera-plan.json   # only
```

Consequences enforced by the platform:

* The provider request always carries a **complete** injected camera block; the provider is never asked to "pick a lens."
* Camera parameters are **not** exposed as tunable provider knobs. Even when a provider API offers camera controls, the adapter (**5.3.4**) sets them from the injected `CameraSpec` and from nowhere else.
* A provider that returns metadata claiming a different camera than requested does **not** get to rewrite the request; the discrepancy is surfaced to **5.6.2** as a movement-verification failure.
* No adapter may add a "camera enhancement" or "auto-framing" step. Such behavior is a boundary violation and fails the provider conformance gate in **5.3.2**.

In short: providers execute the camera; they never design it. Camera Injection removes every situation in which a provider might otherwise have to.

---

# Camera Parameter Validation

Validation runs before injection and is fail-closed (CMI-305). It is a gate, not a fixer.

```yaml id="cmi-validation"
camera_validation:
  plan_entry_present:        true    # exactly one entry for the shot
  plan_entry_unique:         true    # no ambiguous duplicates
  core_complete:                     # all four required core params present
    lens:      present
    movement:  present
    angle:     present
    shot_size: present
  vocabulary_valid:                  # every present value in its vocabulary/domain
    lens_in_set:        true
    movement_in_set:    true
    angle_in_set:       true
    shot_size_in_set:   true
    optionals_in_domain: true
  focus_target_resolvable:   true    # if present, references a known subject
  aperture_wellformed:       true    # if present, matches f<number>
```

On any failure:

* the shot is **not** rendered;
* a precise error is recorded (which parameter, which value, which rule);
* the render task is routed to **Manual Review** (never a default substitution);
* no provider submission occurs.

Validation never repairs, softens, or completes camera data. A partially specified camera is a planning defect to be resolved by a human, not a gap for the renderer to fill.

---

# Downstream Movement Verification

Camera Injection does not merely request a movement — it makes the request **verifiable**. Alongside the prompt block it emits a normalized movement descriptor into the render manifest, which **5.6.2** (Visual Quality Validation, camera movement check) consumes to confirm the rendered asset actually exhibits the requested camera behavior.

```yaml id="cmi-movement-hint"
camera_movement_hint:
  shot_id:
  expected_movement:      # e.g. slow_push
  expected_direction:     # inferred canonical direction (toward | left | up | arc | none)
  expected_magnitude:     # from movement_speed (still | slow | medium | fast)
  expected_stability:     # locked | handheld
  static_expected:        # true only when movement == static
  plan_version:           # provenance link (CMI-306)
```

**5.6.2** compares this hint against measured motion in the rendered frames:

* `static` shots must show no camera translation beyond tolerance;
* `slow_push` / `slow_pull` must show monotonic scale change in the correct direction;
* pans, tilts, tracking, crane, orbit must show motion consistent with the token;
* magnitude mismatches (a "slow" push rendered as a fast whip) are flagged.

A rendered asset whose camera movement contradicts the hint fails the camera movement check in **5.6.2**, is not accepted, and is retried or escalated per **5.4.5**. This closes the loop: the camera is injected authoritatively (this section) and verified downstream (**5.6.2**), so a provider that ignores the requested camera cannot pass silently.

---

# Provenance Recording

Every injected camera block is recorded in the render manifest with full provenance.

```yaml id="cmi-provenance"
camera_provenance:
  shot_id:
  render_task_id:
  plan_version:            # camera-plan.json version used
  storyboard_ref:          # approved storyboard the plan derives from
  source_fields:           # exact plan fields read for this shot
  injected_block_hash:     # sha256 of the serialized camera block
  movement_hint_hash:      # sha256 of the movement descriptor
```

Provenance guarantees (CMI-306) that any frame's camera language can be audited back to a specific approved planning decision. The `injected_block_hash` also lets caching (**5.6.3**) and reproduction checks confirm that two renders used identical camera language.

---

# Events

Camera Injection emits events for every material step.

```yaml
camera_injection_events:
  - camera_spec_resolved
  - camera_validation_passed
  - camera_validation_failed        # → manual review
  - camera_block_injected
  - camera_movement_hint_emitted
  - camera_provenance_recorded
  - camera_override_attempt_blocked  # provider boundary violation detected
```

Every event references `shot_id`, `render_task_id`, `plan_version`, and originating `build_id`, and is durably recorded for audit. `camera_override_attempt_blocked` is a security-relevant event (a provider or adapter tried to alter camera language) and is escalated.

---

# Metrics

```yaml
camera_injection_metrics:
  camera_specs_resolved
  camera_validation_failures
  camera_manual_review_escalations
  missing_core_parameter_count
  invalid_vocabulary_count
  optional_parameter_coverage        # share of shots specifying optionals
  movement_hint_emit_count
  movement_verification_failures      # reported back from 5.6.2
  provider_override_attempts_blocked
  injection_determinism_check_passed
```

`movement_verification_failures` and `provider_override_attempts_blocked` are the key boundary-health signals: a nonzero, rising value indicates a provider is not faithfully executing injected camera language and warrants adapter review under **5.3.2**.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

Camera Injection handles no secrets, but it enforces an integrity boundary.

Required controls:

* `camera-plan.json` is treated as **read-only** at render time; write attempts are rejected and audited;
* the injected camera block is **immutable** once assembled into the prompt; no post-assembly mutation is permitted;
* camera parameters are never exposed as provider-tunable inputs (the adapter maps them, it does not surface them for the provider to change);
* tamper detection via `injected_block_hash` — a camera block whose hash does not match its provenance is rejected;
* every `camera_override_attempt_blocked` event is retained as evidence of a boundary violation.

The threat model is specific: it is the risk that a stochastic provider (or a well-meaning adapter "improvement") quietly changes the approved camera. These controls make any such change detectable and prevent it from reaching a published trailer.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="cmi-config"
camera_injection:
  source: camera-plan.json          # authoritative; not overridable
  require_core_parameters:
    - lens
    - movement
    - angle
    - shot_size
  fail_closed_on_missing: true
  fail_closed_on_invalid: true
  allow_provider_camera_override: false   # MUST remain false
  emit_movement_hint: true
  serialization:
    fixed_order: true
    omit_absent_optionals: true
    canonical_units: true
  vocabularies:
    lens:      [18mm, 24mm, 35mm, 50mm, 85mm, 135mm]
    movement:  [static, slow_push, slow_pull, pan_left, pan_right,
                tilt_up, tilt_down, tracking, crane_up, crane_down,
                orbit, handheld_drift]
    angle:     [eye, low, high, overhead, dutch]
    shot_size: [wide, medium, close, extreme_close]
```

`allow_provider_camera_override` is a locked setting: it must remain `false`. A configuration that sets it `true`, or that supplies a camera source other than `camera-plan.json`, is invalid and fails closed at startup rather than rendering with an unsafe camera policy. Vocabularies are environment-extensible only through governed change; providers can never extend them.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* resolve a shot's camera spec from a well-formed plan;
* fail closed on a shot with no plan entry;
* fail closed on a shot with duplicate/ambiguous plan entries;
* fail closed on missing each core parameter (lens, movement, angle, shot_size);
* reject out-of-vocabulary lens, movement, angle, shot_size values;
* omit (never default) absent optional parameters;
* canonical unit and casing normalization (`50mm`);
* deterministic serialization — byte-identical block for identical input;
* fixed-order serialization across different optional subsets;
* movement hint emission for each movement token;
* `static` produces `static_expected: true`.

## Integration Tests

* camera block placed at the correct position in the 5.5.1 assembled prompt;
* camera block coexists with character (5.5.2) and location (5.5.3) blocks without interference;
* provenance recorded in the render manifest with correct hashes;
* movement hint consumed by 5.6.2 for a push, a pan, and a static shot;
* validation failure routes the task to Manual Review, not to a provider.

## Boundary / Conformance Tests

* adapter maps injected camera to provider knobs and originates none;
* provider cannot introduce a camera value absent from the plan;
* an adapter "auto-framing" step fails the 5.3.2 conformance gate;
* `camera_override_attempt_blocked` fires when an override is attempted.

## Determinism Tests

* two runs of the same plan + shot produce identical block hash;
* injection is independent of provider, clock, and render strategy;
* reordering unrelated plan fields does not change the block.

## Failure Injection Tests

* corrupted camera value in the plan;
* partial camera spec (core incomplete);
* unresolvable `focus_target`;
* malformed aperture string;
* provider returns metadata contradicting the requested movement (→ 5.6.2 failure).

---

# Camera Injection Quality Gate

```yaml
camera_injection_quality_gate:
  camera_source_is_camera_plan_only: true
  all_core_parameters_present: true
  all_values_in_vocabulary: true
  absent_optionals_omitted_not_defaulted: true
  serialization_deterministic: true
  camera_block_at_fixed_position: true
  provider_override_disabled: true
  movement_hint_emitted: true
  provenance_recorded_with_hash: true
  validation_failures_route_to_manual_review: true
  no_provider_originated_camera_values: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant CMI-401

Every camera parameter injected into a render prompt originates solely from `camera-plan.json`.

## Invariant CMI-402

No rendering provider originates, overrides, defaults, infers, or embellishes any camera parameter.

## Invariant CMI-403

Camera Injection is deterministic: identical plan and shot id yield a byte-identical camera block.

## Invariant CMI-404

A missing or invalid camera specification fails closed to Manual Review; it is never completed by a default or by a provider.

## Invariant CMI-405

Absent optional parameters are omitted from the injected block, never rendered as defaults.

## Invariant CMI-406

Every injected camera block is traceable to a specific `camera-plan.json` version and its source fields.

## Invariant CMI-407

A normalized movement descriptor is emitted for every shot and is verifiable by Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2).

## Invariant CMI-408

The camera block is immutable once assembled; no post-assembly mutation by any component or provider is permitted.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                        |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-537 | Camera parameters are sourced exclusively from `camera-plan.json`; no provider, adapter, or default may contribute a camera value. |
| ADR-538 | Camera language is drawn from controlled vocabularies (lens, movement, angle, shot size) rather than free-form prose, to prevent provider reinterpretation. |
| ADR-539 | Camera Injection fails closed on any missing or invalid camera parameter, routing the shot to Manual Review rather than substituting a default. |
| ADR-540 | Providers may never originate or override camera language; camera parameters are mapped by the adapter and are never exposed as tunable provider knobs (enforces 5.3.1). |
| ADR-541 | Camera Injection emits a normalized movement descriptor so that camera movement can be independently verified downstream by 5.6.2. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.5.4** is complete when:

* Camera language is modelled as a fixed set of parameters — lens, movement, angle, shot size, and related cinematography parameters — each drawn from a controlled vocabulary or validated domain.
* `camera-plan.json` is established as the sole authoritative source of camera parameters, consistent with the input contract in **5.1.4**.
* Resolution of a shot's camera specification is deterministic and pure, with no provider, clock, or random dependence.
* Serialization produces a byte-identical, fixed-order camera block that omits (never defaults) absent optional parameters, and is placed at the position **5.5.1** reserves for camera language.
* The prohibition on providers originating, overriding, defaulting, inferring, or embellishing camera language is specified and enforced, operationalizing the provider boundary in **5.3.1**.
* Camera parameter validation is fail-closed: missing or invalid camera data routes the shot to Manual Review and never reaches a provider with a substituted camera.
* A normalized movement descriptor is emitted for every shot and is consumed by the camera movement check in **5.6.2**, closing the injection-to-verification loop.
* Provenance links every injected camera block back to a specific approved plan version and its source fields.
* Events, metrics, security controls, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement Camera Injection such that the rendered camera is always the camera the Shot Planner approved, never one a rendering provider invented.


---

# Part 5.5.5 — Visual Style Enforcement

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Visual Style Enforcement** mechanism of the Rendering Platform.

Visual Style Enforcement is the component that guarantees every rendered asset — regardless of provider, strategy, shot, or build — conforms to a single, fixed brand style: **cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained**.

It exists because the Insynergy platform produces thought-leadership trailers whose visual register is a brand asset in its own right. A trailer that looks like anime, a cartoon, a cyberpunk scene, a fantasy render, or an exaggerated sci-fi interface is not merely off-brand; it is a defect. Visual Style Enforcement makes that register non-negotiable and non-accidental.

Visual Style Enforcement operates at two distinct points in the render lifecycle:

* as a **precondition** — the mandatory style vocabulary is deterministically injected into every assembled prompt (see **5.5.1**) before any provider submission occurs;
* as a **postcondition** — every downloaded asset is checked for style conformance during visual quality validation (see **5.6.2**), and any style violation fails closed to Manual Review.

Visual Style Enforcement does **not** decide *what* is in a shot — that is fixed by the approved Storyboard. It decides *how* every shot must look, and it rejects any render that departs from the approved visual language.

Style is not a creative choice made per shot. It is a **platform invariant** applied identically to every render. Visual Style Enforcement is the mechanism that makes that invariant deterministic, auditable, and enforced on both sides of provider execution.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the mandatory **style vocabulary** and its canonical schema;
* the **forbidden-style blocklist** expressed as normative constraints;
* the deterministic **style descriptor** and its stable hash;
* how the style descriptor is **injected** into every assembled prompt (5.5.1);
* style enforcement as an **admission precondition** (pre-render);
* style enforcement as **post-render validation** (link to 5.6.2);
* the **brand / editorial consistency guarantee** across a build and across builds;
* **deterministic style application** and its reproducibility guarantees;
* **rejection and escalation** of style violations (Fail Closed → Manual Review);
* style events, metrics, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* the full deterministic prompt assembly algorithm (defined in **5.5.1**);
* character description injection (defined in **5.5.2**);
* location description injection (defined in **5.5.3**);
* camera parameter injection (defined in **5.5.4**);
* the technical asset checks — resolution, codec, integrity (defined in **5.6.1**);
* the visual quality scoring model and the `minimum_score: 0.90` threshold (defined in **5.6.2**);
* the render strategy that selects provider or profile (defined in **5.2**).

Visual Style Enforcement contributes the style **segment** to the prompt owned by 5.5.1, and the style **conformance signal** to the validation owned by 5.6.2. It owns neither the assembler nor the scorer; it owns the style contract that both must honour.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **Every render wears the same clothes. Style is injected before the render and enforced after it — never left to the provider's imagination.**

The approved Storyboard fixes narrative and composition. Visual Style Enforcement fixes register. A provider is free to interpret motion, timing, and micro-detail within a shot; it is **never** free to choose the visual language of that shot.

A corollary follows directly:

> **A render that cannot be shown to satisfy the style contract is not publishable.**

Style conformance is a hard gate, identical in force to technical validity and visual quality. A style violation is not a stylistic preference to be argued; it is a Fail-Closed condition (consistent with Design Philosophy Principle 5) that routes the shot to Manual Review rather than into a published trailer.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
Approved Storyboard + Style Guide
        │
        ▼
Render Strategy (5.2)  ── provider, profile
        │
        ▼
Deterministic Prompt Assembly (5.5.1)
        │
        ├── Character Injection   (5.5.2)
        ├── Location Injection    (5.5.3)
        ├── Camera Injection      (5.5.4)
        └── Visual Style Enforcement (5.5.5)  ◄── injects style segment
        │
        ▼
   Assembled Prompt  ── style-stamped, style_hash recorded
        │
        ▼
Provider Submission (5.3)  ── Runway / Motion / SVG / Typography
        │
        ▼
   Rendered Asset
        │
        ▼
Asset Validation (5.6.1)   ── technical
        │
        ▼
Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2)
        └── Style Conformance Check (5.5.5)  ◄── enforces style after render
        │
        ├── conforms ──► READY
        └── violates ──► FAIL CLOSED ──► Manual Review
```

Visual Style Enforcement is the only component that appears on **both** sides of provider execution. It writes the style into the prompt before the render and reads the style out of the asset after the render. The provider sits between the two and is trusted for neither.

---

# Responsibilities

Visual Style Enforcement is responsible for:

* owning the canonical mandatory style vocabulary;
* owning the forbidden-style blocklist;
* producing the deterministic style descriptor and its `style_hash`;
* injecting the style segment into every assembled prompt (via 5.5.1);
* asserting the pre-render style precondition before any submission;
* providing the style conformance contract consumed by post-render validation (5.6.2);
* classifying a render as style-conforming or style-violating after download;
* failing closed and routing style violations to Manual Review;
* guaranteeing brand / editorial consistency across all shots of a build;
* emitting style events and metrics;
* keeping style application fully reproducible.

It is **not** responsible for:

* choosing shot content or composition (owned by Storyboard);
* assembling the full prompt string (owned by **5.5.1**);
* injecting character, location, or camera detail (owned by **5.5.2**, **5.5.3**, **5.5.4**);
* technical asset validity (owned by **5.6.1**);
* computing the visual quality score (owned by **5.6.2**);
* selecting the provider or render profile (owned by **5.2**);
* deciding the creative outcome of a Manual Review (owned by a human reviewer).

---

# Style Design Objectives

Visual Style Enforcement shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## VSE-301 — Universal Application

Every render, without exception, carries the mandatory style. There is no shot, strategy, provider, or profile that is exempt. A preview render and a final render carry the identical style contract.

## VSE-302 — Determinism

Given the same style configuration version, the style descriptor, its serialization, and its `style_hash` are byte-for-byte identical across machines, processes, and runs. Style application introduces no randomness (consistent with the platform Determinism constraint).

## VSE-303 — Two-Sided Enforcement

Style is enforced both as a pre-render precondition and as a post-render validation. Injection alone is insufficient; a render that was correctly stamped but returned off-style must still be rejected.

## VSE-304 — Forbidden-Style Exclusion

No render may exhibit any forbidden style. The blocklist is a hard constraint, not a soft preference. A single forbidden-style match is disqualifying.

## VSE-305 — Brand Consistency

All shots within a build, and all builds within a configuration version, share one visual register. Style must never drift shot-to-shot or scene-to-scene.

## VSE-306 — Fail Closed

If style conformance cannot be established, the platform produces no publishable asset for that shot. Style uncertainty escalates to Manual Review; it never resolves silently to "publish anyway" (Design Philosophy Principle 5).

## VSE-307 — Provider Neutrality

The style contract is provider-independent. The same mandatory vocabulary and blocklist apply to Runway, Motion Graphics, SVG, Typography, and any future provider. Providers render; they never define style (consistent with the provider boundary in **5.3.1**).

## VSE-308 — Auditability

Every render records the exact `style_hash` and style configuration version it was stamped with, and every style rejection records the matched violation evidence. Style decisions are reconstructable after the fact.

---

# Mandatory Style Vocabulary

The Rendering Platform defines exactly **five** mandatory style attributes. Every render must satisfy all five.

```yaml
mandatory_style:
  cinematic
  editorial
  enterprise
  realistic
  restrained
```

Each attribute is normative and carries a fixed operational meaning. These meanings are the reference definitions the style descriptor expands and the conformance check measures against.

| Attribute   | Mandatory Meaning                                                                                       |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| cinematic   | Filmic framing, controlled depth of field, motivated lighting, professional color grade, film-like grain and contrast. |
| editorial   | Documentary / journalistic register; composed, purposeful, information-forward; the look of premium long-form media. |
| enterprise  | Corporate, professional, credible; business environments and subjects; suitable for an executive audience. |
| realistic   | Photoreal; physically plausible light, materials, and human anatomy; no stylization or illustration. |
| restrained  | Understated and disciplined; no visual excess, no gratuitous effects, no saturated spectacle; confidence over flash. |

The five attributes are a **closed set**. Adding a sixth mandatory attribute, or removing one, is a configuration-version change (see Configuration Model) and never an ad-hoc per-shot decision. All five apply simultaneously; they are conjunctive, not a menu.

---

# Mandatory Style Schema

The mandatory style is expressed as a versioned, structured descriptor. This descriptor is the single source of style truth consumed by prompt assembly (5.5.1) and by style conformance validation (5.6.2).

```yaml id="vse-descriptor"
style_descriptor:
  style_version:            # e.g. "style-v2.0"; bumped only by governed change
  mandatory:
    - key: cinematic
      prompt_terms:         # ordered, fixed lexical terms injected into the prompt
        - "cinematic"
        - "filmic lighting"
        - "shallow depth of field"
        - "professional color grade"
      weight: 1.0
    - key: editorial
      prompt_terms:
        - "editorial"
        - "documentary framing"
        - "composed, purposeful"
      weight: 1.0
    - key: enterprise
      prompt_terms:
        - "enterprise"
        - "corporate, professional"
        - "credible business setting"
      weight: 1.0
    - key: realistic
      prompt_terms:
        - "photorealistic"
        - "physically plausible lighting"
        - "natural human anatomy"
      weight: 1.0
    - key: restrained
      prompt_terms:
        - "restrained"
        - "understated"
        - "no visual excess"
      weight: 1.0
  forbidden:                # the blocklist, see next section
    - anime
    - cartoon
    - cyberpunk
    - fantasy
    - neon_holograms
    - humanoid_robots
    - exaggerated_scifi_interfaces
  style_hash:               # sha256 over the canonical serialization below
```

The descriptor is **immutable for a given `style_version`**. Its `prompt_terms` are ordered and fixed so that serialization is stable and the resulting prompt segment is deterministic (VSE-302). Weights are reserved for the conformance scoring contract in 5.6.2 and default to `1.0`.

---

# Forbidden-Style Blocklist

The blocklist is a set of **normative prohibitions**. Each entry is a style that no render may exhibit. The blocklist is closed and versioned identically to the mandatory vocabulary.

```yaml id="vse-blocklist"
forbidden_styles:
  - anime
  - cartoon
  - cyberpunk
  - fantasy
  - neon_holograms
  - humanoid_robots
  - exaggerated_scifi_interfaces
```

Each entry is a hard constraint. The following table gives the normative rule and the sensory signature the conformance check (5.6.2) screens for.

| Forbidden Style               | Normative Rule                                      | Screened Signature                                                        |
| ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| anime                         | No render shall exhibit anime styling.              | Cel shading, large stylized eyes, anime line-art, Japanese-animation look. |
| cartoon                       | No render shall exhibit cartoon styling.            | Toon shading, exaggerated proportions, illustrative non-photoreal look.    |
| cyberpunk                     | No render shall exhibit cyberpunk styling.          | Neon-drenched dystopia, rain-slick neon streets, cyber-aesthetic overload. |
| fantasy                       | No render shall exhibit fantasy styling.            | Magical, mythical, medieval-fantasy or surreal-fantasy imagery.            |
| neon_holograms                | No render shall contain neon holographic elements.  | Glowing neon holograms, floating holographic UI, sci-fi neon projection.   |
| humanoid_robots               | No render shall depict humanoid robots.             | Android/robot humanoids as subjects or background actors.                  |
| exaggerated_scifi_interfaces  | No render shall depict exaggerated sci-fi UI.       | Over-the-top futuristic HUDs, implausible holographic control panels.      |

Normative statements:

* A render that matches **any** blocklist entry is **style-violating** and shall be rejected (VSE-304).
* The blocklist is **conjunctive with** the mandatory vocabulary: a render must satisfy all five mandatory attributes **and** match zero forbidden entries.
* The blocklist is **negative-injected** into the prompt as explicit exclusion terms (see Style Injection), and **positively screened** after render (see Post-Render Validation). Both sides are required (VSE-303).
* A near-miss (e.g. "stylized" but not clearly anime) that cannot be cleared with confidence is treated as uncertain and escalated to Manual Review, never auto-approved (VSE-306).

---

# Deterministic Style Descriptor & Hash

The style descriptor has a deterministic identity so that every render can record exactly which style contract it was subjected to.

```text id="vse-hash"
style_hash = sha256(
  style_version +
  canonical_json(mandatory) +   # keys and prompt_terms in fixed order
  canonical_json(forbidden)     # entries in fixed order
)
```

Rules:

* Serialization uses a canonical form: fixed key order, fixed array order, no insignificant whitespace, UTF-8.
* The same `style_version` always yields the same `style_hash` on every machine (VSE-302).
* Any change to the mandatory vocabulary or the blocklist requires a new `style_version` and therefore produces a new `style_hash`.
* Every assembled prompt records the `style_hash` used; every render manifest entry records it alongside the render (see **5.4.4**); every validation result records the `style_hash` it validated against (see **5.6.2**).

Because the `style_hash` is part of the render cache key contract (via provider version and profile in **5.6.3**), a change in style version correctly invalidates cached assets rendered under an older style, preventing a stale off-style asset from being reused.

---

# Style Injection into Prompt Assembly

Style is injected into every prompt by the deterministic prompt assembler defined in **5.5.1**. Visual Style Enforcement supplies the **visual style segment**; the assembler places it in the fixed prompt structure.

The prompt structure fixed by 5.5.1 is:

```text
Character + Location + Camera + Lighting + Blocking + Emotion + Shot Purpose + Visual Style
```

The **Visual Style** segment is owned by this section. It is constructed deterministically from the style descriptor:

```text id="vse-segment"
visual_style_segment =
  join(mandatory.prompt_terms, ", ")           # positive style terms, fixed order
  + " | avoid: "
  + join(negated(forbidden), ", ")             # explicit exclusion terms
```

Example rendered segment (single line, deterministic for a given `style_version`):

```text
cinematic, filmic lighting, shallow depth of field, professional color grade,
editorial, documentary framing, composed, purposeful, enterprise,
corporate, professional, credible business setting, photorealistic,
physically plausible lighting, natural human anatomy, restrained, understated,
no visual excess | avoid: anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms,
humanoid robots, exaggerated sci-fi interfaces
```

Injection rules:

* The style segment is **always appended**; no prompt is emitted without it (VSE-301).
* The segment is **byte-identical** for every shot in a build at a given `style_version` (VSE-305), which is what guarantees brand consistency — the same clothes on every shot.
* The segment includes both **positive** mandatory terms and **negative** forbidden exclusions in the same pass (VSE-303, VSE-304).
* The assembler must never receive article text, and the style segment likewise contains no article, character bible prose, or secrets — only the fixed style lexicon (consistent with 5.5.1 and the prompt-content boundary).
* The `style_hash` is attached to the assembled prompt record as metadata, enabling later audit (VSE-308).

The assembler in 5.5.1 treats the style segment as an opaque, mandatory, ordered contribution. It shall not reorder, deduplicate against, or drop style terms, and it shall not proceed to submission if the style segment is absent (see Pre-Render Precondition).

---

# Pre-Render Precondition (Style as Admission Gate)

Before any render task may be dispatched to a provider (see **5.4.1** admission and **5.3.3** submission), the following style precondition shall hold:

```yaml id="vse-precondition"
style_precondition:
  style_version_resolved: true         # a governed style_version is active
  style_descriptor_loaded: true        # descriptor validated at startup
  style_segment_present: true          # prompt carries the visual style segment
  style_hash_recorded: true            # style_hash stamped on the prompt + manifest
  forbidden_exclusions_present: true   # negative terms present in the segment
```

If any precondition is false, the render task is **not submitted**. It is held and routed to Manual Review as a configuration/assembly fault rather than dispatched with an unstyled or partially styled prompt. An unstyled submission is treated as a Fail-Closed condition (VSE-306), not a best-effort attempt.

This makes style a first-class admission gate: a prompt without the style contract is as inadmissible as a prompt without an approved storyboard.

---

# Post-Render Validation (Style Conformance Check)

After an asset is downloaded and passes technical validation (5.6.1), it is subjected to a **style conformance check** as part of visual quality validation (5.6.2). This section owns the contract of that check; 5.6.2 owns the scoring engine that executes it.

```yaml id="vse-conformance"
style_conformance:
  input:
    asset_uri:
    style_hash:                 # the style contract the asset must satisfy
    render_profile:
  checks:
    mandatory_present:          # all five mandatory attributes evidenced
      cinematic: pass|fail
      editorial: pass|fail
      enterprise: pass|fail
      realistic: pass|fail
      restrained: pass|fail
    forbidden_absent:           # zero blocklist matches
      anime: absent|present
      cartoon: absent|present
      cyberpunk: absent|present
      fantasy: absent|present
      neon_holograms: absent|present
      humanoid_robots: absent|present
      exaggerated_scifi_interfaces: absent|present
  result:
    style_conformant: true|false
    violations: []              # list of matched forbidden or missing mandatory
    evidence:                   # references retained for audit
```

Conformance rule (normative):

```text
style_conformant  ==  ( all five mandatory attributes == pass )
                     AND ( every forbidden entry == absent )
```

* If **any** mandatory attribute fails, `style_conformant = false`.
* If **any** forbidden entry is present, `style_conformant = false` (VSE-304).
* A non-conformant asset **cannot** contribute to a published trailer, independent of its numeric visual quality score. Style conformance is a gate that sits alongside the `minimum_score: 0.90` threshold in **5.6.2**; both must pass. A render may score above 0.90 on technical/aesthetic quality and still be rejected for style, and vice versa.

The style conformance result is recorded in the validation record and referenced from the Render Manifest (see **5.4.4**), carrying the `style_hash` it was evaluated against (VSE-308).

---

# Relationship to Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2)

Style conformance and visual quality are **distinct, both mandatory** gates. Their relationship is fixed:

| Gate                    | Owned By | Question Answered                                  | Failure Route     |
| ----------------------- | -------- | -------------------------------------------------- | ----------------- |
| Technical validity      | 5.6.1    | Is the file decodable and spec-correct?            | Retry / Reject    |
| Visual quality score    | 5.6.2    | Is it consistent, framed, coherent (≥ 0.90)?       | Retry / Manual    |
| **Style conformance**   | **5.5.5**| **Is it on-brand and free of forbidden styles?**   | **Manual Review** |

Ordering within validation:

```text
Asset ─► 5.6.1 technical ─► 5.6.2 quality score ─► 5.5.5 style conformance ─► READY
                                                          │
                                                  violation │
                                                          ▼
                                                  FAIL CLOSED ─► Manual Review
```

A style violation is **not** a retry trigger by default: re-submitting the identical deterministic prompt to the identical provider is expected to reproduce the same off-style output, so blind retry wastes budget. A style violation therefore escalates to Manual Review, where a human decides whether to adjust the storyboard/strategy, accept a documented exception, or reject the shot. Retry classification remains owned by **5.4.5**; this section only asserts that style violations are, by default, non-retryable and human-routed.

---

# Brand & Editorial Consistency Guarantee

The consistency guarantee is what turns per-shot style into a coherent trailer-wide brand register.

Guarantees:

* **Intra-build consistency** — every shot in a build is stamped with the identical style segment and identical `style_hash` (VSE-305). No shot may be rendered under a different style version than its siblings within the same build.
* **Inter-build consistency** — two builds sharing a `style_version` share the identical style contract, so trailers produced weeks apart look like the same brand.
* **Provider-agnostic consistency** — a Runway shot, a Motion-Graphics shot, an SVG diagram, and a Typography title in the same build all carry the same style contract appropriate to their medium; the enterprise/editorial/restrained register is shared even where "cinematic/realistic" is interpreted per medium (e.g. an SVG diagram is restrained and enterprise but not photoreal).
* **Version-locked consistency** — a build records the exact `style_version` used; reproducing that build reproduces that style. A style upgrade is an explicit, governed event that bumps the version and invalidates affected cache entries.

Medium adaptation note: for non-photographic strategies (SVG, Typography), the `realistic` attribute is satisfied by *not* stylizing into any forbidden register rather than by photorealism, while `editorial`, `enterprise`, and `restrained` apply literally. This adaptation is fixed per strategy in configuration and is itself deterministic (VSE-302); it is never a per-shot judgement.

---

# Deterministic Style Application

Style application is fully deterministic (VSE-302). This is required so that the same storyboard always yields the same styled prompt and the same cache behaviour.

Determinism guarantees:

* the style descriptor for a `style_version` is fixed and immutable;
* `prompt_terms` are ordered; serialization is canonical; `style_hash` is stable;
* the injected style segment is byte-identical for identical inputs;
* no timestamps, random seeds, locale, or environment leak into the style segment;
* the negative-exclusion list is generated from the same ordered blocklist every time.

Consequences:

* Two renders of the same shot under the same `style_version` produce the same styled prompt, enabling correct cache reuse (**5.6.3**).
* A change in style is always visible as a `style_hash` / `style_version` change, never as an invisible drift.
* Style behaviour is testable by golden-file comparison of the emitted style segment (see Testing Requirements).

---

# Rejection & Escalation of Style Violations

Style violations follow a strict Fail-Closed path.

```text
Style violation detected (pre-render precondition OR post-render conformance)
        │
        ▼
Mark render task STYLE_VIOLATION in Render Manifest (5.4.4)
        │
        ▼
Do NOT publish; do NOT auto-retry the identical deterministic prompt
        │
        ▼
Route to Manual Review Queue (5.4.1)
        │
        ▼
Human decision:
        ├── adjust storyboard/strategy → new render task
        ├── documented exception (governed, audited)
        └── reject shot → build cannot publish this shot (Fail Closed)
```

Normative rules:

* **Fail Closed.** If style conformance cannot be established for a shot, no publishable asset is produced for that shot (Design Philosophy Principle 5, VSE-306).
* **No silent override.** A style violation is never auto-approved, auto-downgraded, or silently dropped. It is recorded and escalated.
* **Evidence retained.** The violation record retains the matched forbidden entry or the missing mandatory attribute, the `style_hash`, and asset references, as immutable audit evidence (VSE-308).
* **Exceptions are governed.** Any acceptance of a technically off-contract render is an explicit, logged Manual-Review decision — never a code default. Configuration cannot disable the blocklist for a normal build.
* **Escalation, not blocking loop.** The build does not spin retrying an inherently off-style prompt; it escalates once and awaits a human or governed decision (consistent with 5.4.5 non-retryable classification).

---

# Events

Visual Style Enforcement emits events for every material style action.

```yaml
style_events:
  - style_version_loaded
  - style_descriptor_validated
  - style_segment_injected
  - style_precondition_passed
  - style_precondition_failed
  - style_conformance_passed
  - style_conformance_failed
  - style_violation_detected
  - style_violation_manual_review
  - style_exception_recorded
  - style_hash_mismatch_detected
```

Every event references the `render_task_id`, `shot_id`, `build_id`, `style_version`, and `style_hash`, and is durably recorded for audit and replay (VSE-308).

---

# Metrics

Operational dashboards shall report, at minimum:

```yaml
style_metrics:
  styled_prompt_count
  style_precondition_failures
  style_conformance_checks
  style_conformance_pass_rate
  style_violation_count
  forbidden_style_matches_by_type
  missing_mandatory_by_attribute
  style_manual_review_count
  style_exception_count
  style_version_in_use
  style_hash_mismatch_count
```

Style conformance pass rate and forbidden-style match counts feed brand-quality review. A rising forbidden-style match rate for a provider is a provider-quality signal even though the provider never defines style (VSE-307).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

Visual Style Enforcement operates under least privilege and carries no secrets.

Controls:

* the style descriptor contains only the fixed style lexicon — no article content, no character/location prose, no credentials;
* the style segment injected into prompts contains no secrets and no PII;
* style configuration is read-only at render time; changes require a governed version bump;
* the blocklist cannot be disabled or bypassed by a worker at render time;
* style events and violation evidence are tamper-evident and retained for audit;
* exception decisions are attributable to an authenticated reviewer.

The provider receives the styled prompt but never the ability to alter the style contract; style truth lives with the platform, not the provider (VSE-307, consistent with **5.3.1**).

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="vse-config"
visual_style_enforcement:
  style_version: "style-v2.0"
  mandatory:
    - cinematic
    - editorial
    - enterprise
    - realistic
    - restrained
  forbidden:
    - anime
    - cartoon
    - cyberpunk
    - fantasy
    - neon_holograms
    - humanoid_robots
    - exaggerated_scifi_interfaces
  injection:
    always_inject: true
    include_negative_exclusions: true
  enforcement:
    pre_render_precondition: true
    post_render_conformance: true
    fail_closed: true
    blocklist_disable_allowed: false     # must never be true in a normal build
  medium_adaptation:
    svg:        { photoreal_required: false, register: [editorial, enterprise, restrained] }
    typography: { photoreal_required: false, register: [editorial, enterprise, restrained] }
```

Configuration rules:

* Configuration is **validated at startup**. An invalid or empty mandatory set, an empty blocklist, or `blocklist_disable_allowed: true` fails closed and blocks all admission rather than rendering unstyled or unguarded.
* `always_inject`, `pre_render_precondition`, `post_render_conformance`, and `fail_closed` are `true` and are not runtime-toggled off for a normal build.
* Changing `mandatory` or `forbidden` requires bumping `style_version`, which changes `style_hash` and invalidates affected cache entries (**5.6.3**).
* Medium adaptation is fixed per strategy and deterministic; it never varies per shot.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* deterministic `style_hash` for a fixed `style_version` across processes;
* canonical serialization order of `mandatory` and `forbidden`;
* golden-file equality of the emitted visual style segment;
* presence of all five mandatory attributes in the segment;
* presence of all seven forbidden exclusions in the segment;
* style precondition fails when the segment is absent;
* conformance result is `false` if any mandatory attribute is missing;
* conformance result is `false` if any forbidden entry is present.

## Integration Tests

* style segment injected by prompt assembly (5.5.1) into the fixed prompt structure;
* `style_hash` recorded on prompt, manifest (5.4.4), and validation record (5.6.2);
* pre-render precondition blocks submission of an unstyled prompt;
