* post-render conformance check runs within visual quality validation (5.6.2);
* style violation routes to Manual Review (5.4.1);
* style_version bump invalidates affected cache entries (5.6.3);
* consistency: every shot in a build carries an identical style segment.

## Concurrency Tests

* concurrent builds under the same style_version share an identical `style_hash`;
* concurrent style-version reload does not emit a partially updated descriptor;
* conformance checks for many shots do not interleave `style_hash` records.

## Failure Injection Tests

* provider returns an anime-styled asset → conformance fails → Manual Review;
* provider returns a neon-hologram / humanoid-robot asset → rejected;
* asset scores ≥ 0.90 quality but violates style → still rejected;
* missing style descriptor at startup → admission blocked (fail closed);
* attempt to submit with an empty style segment → precondition failure;
* attempt to disable the blocklist in config → startup fails closed.

## Recovery Tests

* reconstruct which `style_version`/`style_hash` a build used from the manifest;
* replay a style violation from retained evidence;
* re-validate an asset against a bumped style_version and detect the mismatch.

---

# Style Quality Gate

```yaml
style_quality_gate:
  every_prompt_carries_style_segment: true
  style_segment_deterministic: true
  style_hash_recorded_on_every_render: true
  mandatory_vocabulary_complete: true
  forbidden_blocklist_present_and_nonempty: true
  blocklist_disable_not_permitted: true
  pre_render_precondition_enforced: true
  post_render_conformance_enforced: true
  style_and_quality_gates_both_required: true
  style_violations_fail_closed: true
  style_violations_routed_to_manual_review: true
  intra_build_style_consistency: true
  style_version_recorded_per_build: true
  style_exceptions_governed_and_audited: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant VSE-401

Every render is stamped with the mandatory style before submission; no unstyled prompt is ever dispatched.

## Invariant VSE-402

The mandatory vocabulary (cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained) applies conjunctively to every render, with no exemptions.

## Invariant VSE-403

No render may exhibit any forbidden style; a single blocklist match is disqualifying.

## Invariant VSE-404

Style is enforced on both sides of provider execution — as a pre-render precondition and as a post-render conformance check.

## Invariant VSE-405

Style application is deterministic; the same `style_version` yields the same style segment and the same `style_hash` everywhere.

## Invariant VSE-406

Style conformance is a gate independent of and additional to the visual quality score; both must pass for a render to be publishable.

## Invariant VSE-407

A style violation fails closed and routes to Manual Review; it is never auto-approved, silently downgraded, or blindly retried.

## Invariant VSE-408

Providers render but never define style; the style contract is provider-independent and platform-owned.

## Invariant VSE-409

Every render and every style decision records its `style_version` and `style_hash`, making style fully auditable and reconstructable.

## Invariant VSE-410

The blocklist cannot be disabled for a normal build; any accepted off-contract render is an explicit, governed, audited exception.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                              |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-542 | The platform mandates one fixed visual style — cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained — applied to every render without exemption. |
| ADR-543 | A forbidden-style blocklist (anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms, humanoid robots, exaggerated sci-fi interfaces) is a hard normative constraint that cannot be disabled for a normal build. |
| ADR-544 | Style is injected deterministically into every assembled prompt via 5.5.1, as both positive mandatory terms and negative exclusions, and stamped with a stable `style_hash`. |
| ADR-545 | Style is enforced twice — as a pre-render admission precondition and as a post-render conformance check within visual quality validation (5.6.2). |
| ADR-546 | Style conformance is a gate independent of the visual quality score; both the style gate and the `minimum_score` gate must pass for a render to be publishable. |
| ADR-547 | Style violations fail closed and escalate to Manual Review rather than being auto-retried or silently accepted; exceptions are governed and audited. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.5.5** is complete when:

* The mandatory style vocabulary (cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained) is defined as a closed, versioned, conjunctive set with fixed operational meanings.
* The forbidden-style blocklist is defined as a closed set of normative prohibitions, each with a screened signature, and cannot be disabled for a normal build.
* A deterministic, versioned style descriptor with a stable `style_hash` is specified and recorded on every prompt, manifest entry, and validation record.
* Style is deterministically injected into every assembled prompt through 5.5.1 as both positive terms and negative exclusions, byte-identical across shots of a build.
* Style is enforced as a pre-render admission precondition, so no unstyled prompt is ever submitted.
* Style is enforced as a post-render conformance check within 5.6.2, independent of and additional to the `minimum_score: 0.90` visual quality gate.
* The brand / editorial consistency guarantee is specified across shots, builds, providers, and style versions, including deterministic medium adaptation for non-photographic strategies.
* Style violations fail closed and route to Manual Review, are never auto-approved or blindly retried, and preserve immutable audit evidence.
* Events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement Visual Style Enforcement as a deterministic, two-sided, fail-closed guarantee that every render conforms to the Insynergy visual style and exhibits no forbidden style.


---

# Part 5.5.6 — Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.6

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section closes **Group 5.5 — Prompt Assembly & Consistency Injection** and defines the formal handoff into **Group 5.6 — Asset Validation, Cache & Build Profiles**.

Group 5.5 established *how an approved shot becomes the exact instruction a provider renders* — the deterministic assembly of a prompt from storyboard artifacts, and the automatic injection of character, location, and camera identity plus a mandatory visual style. It fixed the prompt structure, the injection sources, the provider-override prohibition, and the style enforcement rules. It deliberately specified **no** validation logic, **no** cache key mechanics, and **no** build-profile behaviour. Those belong to 5.6.

This section performs four jobs:

* it **summarises** what sections 5.5.1 through 5.5.5 collectively established;
* it **consolidates** the injection invariants of the assembly layer into a single normative reference — a deterministic prompt, no article text to any provider, identity drawn only from authoritative bibles and plans, providers unable to override injected values, and a mandatory style with a forbidden blocklist;
* it **defines the interface** — the exact per-render deterministic prompt-plus-identity package, the guarantees, and the obligations that Group 5.5 hands to Group 5.6;
* it **states the acceptance criteria** under which Group 5.5 is considered complete and the assembly layer is frozen for downstream validation and caching.

This section is a **synthesis and interface contract**. It introduces no new assembly rule. Where it appears to state a rule, that rule is a consolidation of a rule already established in 5.5.1–5.5.5, restated here so that 5.6 and every later group can reference one authoritative assembly boundary.

Group 5.5 is the **prompt-and-identity layer**. It sits inside the frozen frame handed down by Group 5.1 (see **5.1.6**), consumes the settled strategy plan from Group 5.2 (see **5.2.4**) and the submitted-task handles from Group 5.3 (see **5.3.5**), and hands a settled per-render prompt-plus-identity package to Group 5.6. Nothing downstream of this interface may re-assemble a prompt or re-inject an identity.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* a consolidated summary of the prompt and identity artifacts established by 5.5.1–5.5.5;
* the injection invariants of the assembly layer, restated as one normative set (INJECT-1..6);
* the **5.5 → 5.6 interface contract**: the per-render deterministic prompt-plus-identity package and its guarantees;
* the obligations 5.6 inherits and must never violate;
* the pre-conditions 5.6 may safely assume as already assembled and injected;
* the assumptions 5.6 must **not** make;
* the compliance checklist for Group 5.5;
* the Group 5.5 quality gate;
* the invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria that freeze the assembly layer.

This section does **not** specify:

* deterministic prompt assembly and prompt structure (defined in **5.5.1**);
* character injection from the character bible (defined in **5.5.2**);
* location injection from the location bible (defined in **5.5.3**);
* camera injection from the shot/camera plan (defined in **5.5.4**);
* mandatory visual style and the forbidden blocklist (defined in **5.5.5**);
* asset validation of technical properties (defined in **5.6.1**);
* visual quality validation and the minimum score (defined in **5.6.2**);
* render cache key and reuse (defined in **5.6.3**);
* preview and final rendering profiles (defined in **5.6.4** and **5.6.5**).

This section consumes the outputs of 5.5.1–5.5.5; it re-derives nothing and contradicts nothing. Where a rule was fixed upstream, this section cites it rather than restating a variant of it. It also inherits the foundational invariants FOUND-1..5 from **5.1.6** and never weakens them.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the assembly handoff.

> **Group 5.5 fixes exactly what a render depicts and how it must look; Group 5.6 judges only whether the returned asset honoured that fixed instruction — never re-writes it.**

The prompt and the injected identity are settled before a provider renders and remain the reference against which the output is later judged. Validation (5.6) checks the returned asset *against* the assembled prompt and injected identity; it never edits the prompt, re-injects a different character or location, or relaxes the style requirement to admit a non-compliant asset.

A corollary follows directly:

> **The deterministic prompt is both the instruction and the evidence. If validation or caching regenerates it rather than reading it, the identity anchor has been broken and that regeneration is a defect.**

The prompt hash produced by assembly is the anchor for both validation and caching. If judging or caching a render would require re-assembling the prompt, editing an injected value, or admitting a forbidden style, the action is rejected at the boundary — not accommodated. Providers only render an already-fixed instruction (consistent with **5.9** of the original specification and **5.3.1**); validation only measures the result against that fixed instruction.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        GROUP 5.5 — Prompt Assembly & Consistency Injection
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.5.1  Deterministic Prompt Assembly           │
        │  5.5.2  Character Injection                     │
        │  5.5.3  Location Injection                      │
        │  5.5.4  Camera Injection                        │
        │  5.5.5  Visual Style Enforcement                │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                 ┌─────────────────────┐
                 │   5.5.6  INTERFACE   │   ◄── this section
                 │  (settled prompt)    │
                 └─────────────────────┘
                            │
        deterministic prompt + injected identity + prompt hash
                            │
                            ▼
        GROUP 5.6 — Asset Validation, Cache & Build Profiles
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.6.1  Asset Validation                        │
        │  5.6.2  Visual Quality Validation               │
        │  5.6.3  Render Cache & Reuse                     │
        │  5.6.4  Preview Rendering Profile               │
        │  5.6.5  Final Rendering Profile                 │
        │  5.6.6  Section Acceptance & Interface to 5.7   │
        └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Group 5.5 is upstream of every validation and caching decision. The interface defined here is the sole channel through which the assembled prompt and injected identity reach the validation layer. 5.6 receives a **settled prompt-plus-identity package**; it validates and caches against it, it does not re-assemble it.

---

# Summary of Group 5.5

Group 5.5 established the following, section by section. Each item is authoritative in its origin section; this summary is a consolidated index, not a restatement.

## From 5.5.1 — Deterministic Prompt Assembly

* Rendering prompts are **deterministic**: identical shot inputs produce a byte-identical prompt on every run, with no randomness or time dependence (original **5.18**).
* The prompt is assembled from a fixed structure: **Character + Location + Camera + Lighting + Blocking + Emotion + Shot Purpose + Visual Style.**
* **The provider never receives article text.** Only the assembled prompt crosses the provider boundary; source article content is never included.
* The assembled prompt is the single instruction handed to a provider for a render task, and its deterministic hash is the anchor for reuse and validation.

## From 5.5.2 — Character Injection

* Characters are referenced by **ID** (e.g. `character.ref: maya_v1`); descriptions are **injected automatically** from the authoritative character bible (`character-bible.yaml`, original **5.19**).
* No character description is authored inside the render layer; the bible is the single source of character identity.
* Character identity is consistent across every shot referencing the same character ID.

## From 5.5.3 — Location Injection

* Locations are **immutable** and referenced by ID (e.g. `location.ref: operations_center_night`); descriptions are injected from the authoritative location bible (`location-bible.yaml`, original **5.20**).
* **Environment consistency is guaranteed**: the same location ID yields the same environment across every shot.
* No location description is authored inside the render layer.

## From 5.5.4 — Camera Injection

* Camera parameters (lens, movement, angle) originate **only** from the Shot Planner and the approved camera plan (`camera-plan.json`, original **5.21**).
* **Rendering providers may not override these values.** Camera language is fixed upstream and injected verbatim.
* The render layer authors no camera language; it transcribes the approved camera plan into the prompt.

## From 5.5.5 — Visual Style Enforcement

* Every render **must** satisfy the mandatory style set: `cinematic`, `editorial`, `enterprise`, `realistic`, `restrained` (original **5.22**).
* A **forbidden blocklist** is enforced: `anime`, `cartoon`, `cyberpunk`, `fantasy`, `neon holograms`, `humanoid robots`, `exaggerated sci-fi interfaces`.
* Style is injected as a mandatory prompt component and carried forward as a validation criterion; a render that violates the style is not publishable.

Together, sections 5.5.1–5.5.5 fix the platform's **prompt structure, identity injection sources, provider-override prohibition, and style enforcement**. Group 5.6 begins exactly where a deterministic prompt and its injected identity must be validated and cached.

---

# Consolidated Injection Invariants

The rules of 5.5.1–5.5.5 are restated here as a single normative set, so that every downstream section references one assembly boundary. These are the invariants the validation and cache layers rest on. They operate **inside** the foundational invariants FOUND-1..5 (**5.1.6**) and never override them.

## INJECT-1 — Deterministic Prompt

Prompt assembly **shall** be deterministic. Given identical shot inputs — the referenced character, location, camera parameters, lighting, blocking, emotion, shot purpose, and style — the assembled prompt **must** be byte-identical on every run. Random or time-dependent prompt generation is **prohibited**. The prompt and its hash are reproducible and auditable from the recorded inputs (**5.5.1**), consistent with the platform determinism requirement.

## INJECT-2 — No Article Text to Provider

The assembled prompt **shall never** contain source article text. Only the deterministically assembled prompt crosses the provider boundary; the originating article, its passages, and any raw editorial content **must never** be transmitted to a provider (**5.5.1**). The provider receives a rendering instruction, not the story it was derived from.

## INJECT-3 — Identity From Authoritative Sources

Character, location, and camera identity **shall** be injected **only** from their authoritative sources: characters from `character-bible.yaml` (**5.5.2**), locations from `location-bible.yaml` (**5.5.3**), and camera parameters from the Shot Planner's `camera-plan.json` (**5.5.4**). Identity is referenced by ID and injected automatically; no character, location, or camera description **shall** be authored inside the render layer. Locations are immutable and environment consistency is guaranteed across shots sharing an ID.

## INJECT-4 — Providers Cannot Override Injected Values

A provider **shall never** override an injected character description, location description, or camera parameter. The injected identity is fixed upstream and rendered verbatim (**5.5.2**, **5.5.3**, **5.5.4**). A provider that alters lens, movement, angle, character, or location is not rendering the approved plan — it is producing a non-compliant asset that validation must reject (FOUND-1, original **5.9**).

## INJECT-5 — Mandatory Style + Forbidden Blocklist

Every render **shall** carry the mandatory style set — `cinematic`, `editorial`, `enterprise`, `realistic`, `restrained` — as a required prompt component, and **shall** enforce the forbidden blocklist — `anime`, `cartoon`, `cyberpunk`, `fantasy`, `neon holograms`, `humanoid robots`, `exaggerated sci-fi interfaces` (**5.5.5**). Style is both an assembly input and a downstream validation criterion; a render that satisfies neither the mandatory set nor the blocklist is **not publishable**.

## INJECT-6 — Prompt Is the Identity Anchor

The deterministic prompt and its hash **shall** be the single anchor for both downstream reuse and validation. The prompt hash contributes to the render cache key (**5.6.3**) and is the reference against which the returned asset's identity, continuity, and style are judged (**5.6.2**). No downstream layer **shall** regenerate the prompt to obtain this anchor; it reads the anchor produced by assembly. Re-deriving the prompt non-deterministically would break both reuse and validation.

These six invariants are **jointly binding**. No provider, optimisation, cache policy, or configuration may satisfy one by breaking another. They are the acceptance floor the validation and cache layers inherit.

---

# The 5.5 → 5.6 Interface Contract

Group 5.5 hands Group 5.6 a **settled prompt-plus-identity package** per render task. The interface is defined as three explicit sets: the **handoff artifact** 5.6 receives, the **guarantees** attached to it, and the **obligations** 5.6 inherits.

## Handoff Artifact — Per-Render Prompt + Injected Identity

5.6 receives, per submitted render task, one deterministic prompt package: the assembled prompt, the injected identity it was built from, the mandatory style and forbidden blocklist, and the prompt hash that anchors validation and caching.

```yaml id="r5a-handoff"
handoff_to_5_6:
  render_prompt_package:           # produced by 5.5, read-only to 5.6
    render_task_id:                # ties the prompt to the submitted task (5.3.5)
    shot_id:                       # references the approved shot
    assembled_prompt:              # deterministic text; NO article content (INJECT-2)
    prompt_hash:                   # deterministic hash; cache + validation anchor (INJECT-6)
    injected_identity:
      character_refs:              # IDs resolved from character-bible.yaml (INJECT-3)
      location_ref:                # ID resolved from location-bible.yaml (INJECT-3)
      camera:                      # lens / movement / angle from camera-plan.json (INJECT-3)
      lighting:
      blocking:
      emotion:
      shot_purpose:
    style_profile:
      mandatory: [cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained]  # INJECT-5
      forbidden: [anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon_holograms,
                  humanoid_robots, exaggerated_sci_fi_interfaces]           # INJECT-5
  injection_invariants:            # frozen, non-negotiable
    - INJECT-1  # deterministic prompt
    - INJECT-2  # no article text to provider
    - INJECT-3  # identity from authoritative sources
    - INJECT-4  # providers cannot override injected values
    - INJECT-5  # mandatory style + forbidden blocklist
    - INJECT-6  # prompt is the identity anchor
```

The `prompt_hash` is the **single most important field** in the handoff. It is the deterministic identity of the render: it feeds the render cache key (**5.6.3**) and is the reference the returned asset is judged against (**5.6.2**). 5.6 reads it; it never re-assembles the prompt to obtain it. The `assembled_prompt` is the exact instruction the provider received — no more, no less — and carries no article text (INJECT-2).

## Guarantees Provided to 5.6

When 5.6 begins, it may assume the following are already true. These are guarantees, not aspirations; they are enforced by the Group 5.5 quality gate before the prompt is frozen.

```yaml id="r5a-guarantees"
guarantees_to_5_6:
  prompt_is_deterministic: true          # INJECT-1: byte-identical on identical inputs
  prompt_carries_no_article_text: true   # INJECT-2: provider saw no article content
  identity_from_authoritative_sources: true  # INJECT-3: bibles + camera plan only
  providers_could_not_override: true     # INJECT-4: injected values are fixed upstream
  mandatory_style_applied: true          # INJECT-5: style set present in every prompt
  forbidden_blocklist_applied: true      # INJECT-5: blocklist enforced at assembly
  prompt_hash_present: true              # INJECT-6: cache + validation anchor exists
  identity_is_consistent: true           # same ID → same character/location across shots
  package_is_complete: true              # every submitted task carries a prompt package
  package_is_read_only_to_5_6: true      # 5.6 validates and caches it, never edits it
```

The most consequential guarantee for 5.6 is `prompt_hash_present`: the deterministic identity anchor already exists. 5.6 never has to invent, wait for, or reconstruct a prompt or its hash; it validates the returned asset against an identity that is already fixed, and it caches on a key that already exists.

## Obligations 5.6 Inherits

5.6 accepts the package by honouring the following obligations. Violating any of them is a boundary defect, not an implementation choice.

* **Validation shall judge the returned asset against the injected identity.** Human/character consistency, continuity, and framing checks (**5.6.2**) measure the asset against the injected character, location, and camera — never against a re-derived or substituted identity (INJECT-3, INJECT-4).
* **Caching shall key on the deterministic prompt identity.** The render cache key (**5.6.3**) incorporates the `prompt_hash`; a cache hit means the same deterministic prompt and identity, and reuse is valid only under that equality (INJECT-6, FOUND-4).
* **5.6 shall not re-assemble or edit the prompt.** The assembled prompt and injected identity are read-only. Validation reads them; it never regenerates, rewrites, or re-injects a different value (INJECT-1, INJECT-6).
* **Style compliance shall be enforced as a validation criterion.** A render that violates the mandatory style set or matches the forbidden blocklist **shall** fail validation; it is not published (INJECT-5, FOUND-5).
* **5.6 shall preserve the no-article-text boundary.** No validation, caching, or profiling step **shall** reintroduce article text into a prompt, an asset record, or a provider interaction (INJECT-2).
* **5.6 shall fail closed.** If a returned asset cannot be validated against the injected identity and mandatory style, 5.6 escalates to Manual Review rather than admitting a non-compliant asset (FOUND-5).

The package 5.6 receives — a deterministic prompt with injected identity, style profile, and prompt hash per render task — is the pre-condition for asset validation (**5.6.1**), visual quality validation (**5.6.2**), and render caching (**5.6.3**).

---

# Interface Sequence

The handoff is a strict, one-directional sequence. No step reads back into a prior group or re-assembles a prompt.

```text
5.5 assembles deterministic prompt + injects identity
        │
        ▼
prompt package (prompt + identity + style + prompt_hash) ──┐
injection invariants (INJECT-1..6) ────────────────────────┤
                                                           ▼
                                    provider renders the fixed prompt (5.3/5.4)
                                                           │
                                              asset returned + downloaded
                                                           │
                                                           ▼
                                    5.6.1 Asset Validation (technical)
                                                           │
                                    5.6.2 Visual Quality vs injected identity + style
                                                           │
                            ┌──────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┐
                            ▼                               ▼                               ▼
                     passes validation              fails identity/style           cache on prompt_hash
                     → READY (5.4.3)                → Manual Review (FOUND-5)       → 5.6.3 reuse
```

Every judgement of the returned asset is made against the prompt package produced here; the `prompt_hash` travels the full length of the sequence unchanged and un-regenerated. If an asset cannot be validated against the injected identity and mandatory style, 5.6 escalates through Manual Review (**5.4.1**, **5.6.2**), consistent with Fail Closed (FOUND-5); it never edits the prompt or relaxes the style requirement to force a pass.

---

# What 5.6 Must Not Assume

The interface is generous with guarantees but strict about non-guarantees. 5.6 must **not** assume any of the following, because 5.5 deliberately did not grant them:

* **That it may edit the prompt or re-inject a different identity.** The prompt and injected identity are read-only and deterministic (INJECT-1, INJECT-6). Rewriting the prompt to "improve" a render or substituting a character, location, or camera value is prohibited; validation judges the asset against the prompt as assembled.
* **That the returned asset's identity is trustworthy without validation.** A provider may drift, override, or hallucinate identity despite the injected values (INJECT-4). 5.6 must validate character consistency, continuity, and framing against the injected identity (**5.6.2**); it never presumes the asset honoured the prompt.
* **That a style violation may be admitted.** The mandatory style set and forbidden blocklist are hard criteria (INJECT-5). An asset that reads as `anime`, `cartoon`, `cyberpunk`, `fantasy`, neon-hologram, humanoid-robot, or exaggerated sci-fi is rejected — never accepted "because the render is otherwise good" (FOUND-5).
* **That the prompt can be regenerated non-deterministically.** The prompt hash is the identity anchor (INJECT-6). Re-assembling the prompt inside validation or caching — especially through any non-deterministic path — breaks both reuse and the validation reference; 5.6 reads the anchor, it does not reproduce it.
* **That article text is available for validation or captioning.** The provider never received article text and neither does 5.6 (INJECT-2). Validation and caching operate on the assembled prompt and injected identity only; reintroducing article content is a boundary violation.

Confusing a non-guarantee for a guarantee is the most common way a validation layer silently breaches the assembly boundary. The interface names these explicitly so 5.6 cannot drift across the boundary by assumption.

---

# Interface Verification

The handoff is verified — not merely asserted — before the prompt package is frozen. The following checks constitute the interface test surface for Group 5.5.

## Assembly Tests

* two independent assembly passes over identical shot inputs produce byte-identical prompts and identical prompt hashes (INJECT-1);
* no assembled prompt contains any article text or source-passage fragment (INJECT-2);
* every render task carries a complete prompt package with a non-null `prompt_hash` (INJECT-6).

## Injection Tests

* every injected character description resolves from `character-bible.yaml` by ID; none is authored in the render layer (INJECT-3);
* every injected location resolves from `location-bible.yaml` by ID and is immutable across shots sharing that ID (INJECT-3);
* every camera parameter (lens, movement, angle) resolves from `camera-plan.json`; no camera language is authored in the render layer (INJECT-3);
* an attempt by a provider to override an injected character, location, or camera value is rejected downstream (INJECT-4).

## Style Tests

* every prompt carries the full mandatory style set `[cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained]` (INJECT-5);
* a prompt or asset matching any forbidden blocklist term is rejected and never published (INJECT-5, FOUND-5).

## Boundary Tests

* the assembly pass produces no asset, no validation verdict, and no cache entry (validation and caching belong to 5.6);
* the prompt package handed to 5.6 is read-only; an attempt to mutate it in the validation layer is rejected (INJECT-6);
* the `prompt_hash` used for caching equals the hash produced by assembly, never one re-derived downstream (INJECT-6).

These tests give Codex an executable definition of "the prompt is settled": the interface opens only when all of them pass.

---

# Compliance Checklist

Before Group 5.5 is frozen and the interface to 5.6 is declared open, every item below must hold.

```yaml
group_5_5_compliance:
  # Deterministic assembly (5.5.1 / INJECT-1, INJECT-2)
  prompt_assembly_deterministic: true
  prompt_structure_fixed: true              # character+location+camera+lighting+blocking+emotion+purpose+style
  no_article_text_to_provider: true
  prompt_hash_produced: true
  # Character injection (5.5.2 / INJECT-3)
  characters_referenced_by_id: true
  character_descriptions_from_bible: true
  no_character_authored_in_render_layer: true
  # Location injection (5.5.3 / INJECT-3)
  locations_referenced_by_id: true
  locations_immutable: true
  environment_consistency_guaranteed: true
  # Camera injection (5.5.4 / INJECT-3, INJECT-4)
  camera_from_shot_planner_only: true
  providers_cannot_override_camera: true
  # Style enforcement (5.5.5 / INJECT-5)
  mandatory_style_present: true             # cinematic/editorial/enterprise/realistic/restrained
  forbidden_blocklist_enforced: true        # anime/cartoon/cyberpunk/fantasy/neon/robots/sci-fi
  # Interface (5.5.6 / INJECT-6)
  prompt_package_complete_per_render: true
  prompt_hash_is_cache_and_validation_anchor: true
  identity_injection_consolidated: true
  handoff_read_only_to_5_6: true
```

Every field must be `true`. A single `false` blocks the freeze: the interface to 5.6 does not open until the assembly layer is whole. This mirrors the fail-closed posture of FOUND-5 applied to the specification itself.

---

# Group 5.5 Quality Gate

```yaml
group_5_5_quality_gate:
  prompt_assembly_deterministic: true
  no_article_text_ever_reaches_provider: true
  identity_injected_from_authoritative_sources: true   # bibles + camera plan
  providers_cannot_override_injected_identity: true
  mandatory_style_applied_to_every_render: true
  forbidden_blocklist_enforced: true
  prompt_hash_anchors_cache_and_validation: true
  injection_invariants_consolidated: true              # INJECT-1..6
  injection_invariants_jointly_binding: true
  prompt_package_read_only_and_complete: true
  fail_closed_on_identity_or_style_violation: true     # FOUND-5
  no_downstream_group_may_reassemble_a_prompt: true
```

The gate passes only when the prompt package is complete **and** demonstrably non-reassemblable by any downstream group. If 5.6 or a later section could satisfy its own goals by re-assembling a prompt, editing an injected identity, or admitting a forbidden style, the gate fails and the invariant is re-hardened here before proceeding.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant R5A-401

Group 5.5 is a **settled prompt**: no downstream group may re-assemble a shot's prompt, re-inject a different character, location, or camera value, or relax the mandatory style; the assembled prompt and injected identity are read-only to validation and caching.

## Invariant R5A-402

The injection invariants (INJECT-1..6) are **jointly binding** and operate inside the foundational invariants FOUND-1..5 (**5.1.6**); no provider, cache policy, optimisation, or configuration may satisfy one by breaking another, and none may weaken a foundational invariant.

## Invariant R5A-403

The 5.5 → 5.6 handoff is a **per-render deterministic prompt-plus-identity package** carrying the assembled prompt, injected identity, style profile, and a `prompt_hash`; the prompt carries no article text (INJECT-2), and the `prompt_hash` is the single anchor for both the render cache key (**5.6.3**) and visual quality validation (**5.6.2**), read downstream and never regenerated.

## Invariant R5A-404

Injected identity is authoritative and provider-inviolable: characters, locations, and camera parameters are injected only from `character-bible.yaml`, `location-bible.yaml`, and `camera-plan.json` (INJECT-3), providers may not override them (INJECT-4), and validation judges the returned asset against the injected identity — never against a re-derived or substituted one.

## Invariant R5A-405

Style is a hard, fail-closed criterion: every render carries the mandatory style set and enforces the forbidden blocklist (INJECT-5); an asset that violates either is rejected and escalated to Manual Review, never admitted for publication (FOUND-5).

## Invariant R5A-406

The Group 5.5 compliance checklist and quality gate must both fully pass before the interface to 5.6 is declared open; a single failing field **fails closed** and blocks the freeze.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| ADR-548 | Group 5.5 is frozen as a settled prompt-and-identity layer; the assembly rules are consolidated into a single jointly-binding invariant set (INJECT-1..6) — deterministic prompt, no article text to any provider, identity injected only from authoritative bibles and the camera plan, providers unable to override injected values, a mandatory style with a forbidden blocklist, and the prompt hash as the sole identity anchor — that no downstream group may reopen or re-assemble. |
| ADR-549 | The 5.5 → 5.6 handoff is a one-directional interface contract in which 5.6 receives, per render task, a read-only deterministic prompt-plus-identity package whose `prompt_hash` anchors both the render cache key and visual quality validation; 5.6 validates the returned asset against the injected identity and mandatory style and caches on the prompt identity — never re-assembling the prompt, re-injecting an identity, or admitting a forbidden style. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.5.6** is complete when:

* Group 5.5 (sections 5.5.1 through 5.5.5) is summarised into a single authoritative index of established facts — deterministic prompt assembly, character injection, location injection, camera injection, and visual style enforcement — contradicting none of its origin sections.
* The assembly rules are consolidated into one normative, jointly-binding invariant set — a deterministic prompt, no article text to any provider, identity injected only from authoritative bibles and the camera plan, providers unable to override injected values, a mandatory style with a forbidden blocklist, and the prompt hash as the identity anchor — restated as INJECT-1 through INJECT-6 and operating inside the foundational invariants FOUND-1..5.
* The 5.5 → 5.6 interface is defined as three explicit sets: the per-render deterministic prompt-plus-identity handoff artifact, the guarantees provided, and the obligations inherited.
* The handoff enumerates, per render task, the assembled prompt, the injected character, location, and camera identity, the mandatory style set and forbidden blocklist, and the `prompt_hash`, and carries no article text.
* The guarantees 5.6 may assume — a deterministic prompt, no article text reaching a provider, identity from authoritative sources, injected values that providers could not override, an applied mandatory style and enforced blocklist, a present prompt hash, and a complete read-only package — are enumerated and enforced by the Group 5.5 quality gate before the prompt is frozen.
* The obligations 5.6 inherits — validate the returned asset against the injected identity, cache on the deterministic prompt identity, never re-assemble or edit the prompt, enforce style compliance as a validation criterion, preserve the no-article-text boundary, and fail closed — are stated as boundary conditions rather than suggestions.
* The assumptions 5.6 must not make — editing the prompt or re-injecting a different identity, trusting the returned asset's identity without validation, admitting a style violation, regenerating the prompt non-deterministically, or reintroducing article text — are named explicitly so the validation layer cannot drift across the boundary.
* The compliance checklist and quality gate fully pass, and both fail closed on any single unmet field so the interface to 5.6 cannot open over an unsettled assembly layer.
* Invariants R5A-401 through R5A-406 and decision records ADR-548 and ADR-549 fix the assembly layer as non-reopenable by any downstream group.
* Together these provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the assembly-to-validation handoff — freezing the per-render deterministic prompt-plus-identity package, exposing the `prompt_hash` as the shared cache and validation anchor, and gating validation and caching against the injection invariants — without permitting any downstream layer to re-assemble a prompt, override an injected identity, or admit a render that violates the mandatory style.


---

# Part 5.6.1 — Asset Validation

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **Asset Validation** stage of the Rendering Platform.

Asset Validation is the **technical** gate that every downloaded render asset must pass before it may advance toward composition. It answers a single, narrow question:

> Is this file a technically well-formed, decodable asset that matches the technical specification of the shot it was rendered for?

It does **not** ask whether the asset looks good. Aesthetic and continuity judgment — human consistency, framing, lighting, motion artifacts, visual coherence — is the province of **Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2)**. This section is deliberately confined to objective, deterministic, machine-checkable technical properties: resolution, duration, codec, frame rate, decode success, and file integrity.

Every downloaded asset is validated. Invalid assets are rejected. A rejected asset is classified (**5.4.5**), routed, and recorded — never silently discarded, never silently accepted.

Asset Validation is the first of the two validation gates that together guard the boundary between rendering and composition. It runs while a render task is in the **`VALIDATING`** state of the render task state machine (**5.4.3**), immediately after the asset has been durably downloaded and stored (**5.3.4**), and immediately before Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**). A render asset that fails technical validation never reaches visual scoring, and a render asset that fails either gate never reaches the Composer.

Asset Validation is an execution-layer control. It renders no judgment about *what* should have been rendered; it verifies only that what was produced is technically usable. It never modifies the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard, and it never modifies the asset itself — it inspects, classifies, and records.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the technical validation check suite — the six mandatory checks and their pass/fail criteria;
* the per-check input, criterion, evidence, and schema for each check;
* the expected-specification contract against which an asset is validated;
* the ordering and short-circuit behavior of the check suite;
* integrity verification (at-rest re-verification) and decode verification (full decode probe);
* the relationship between technical validation and the `VALIDATING` state (**5.4.3**);
* the rejection path: mapping each technical failure to a canonical `failure_code` and disposition (**5.4.5**);
* the retryable-vs-non-retryable discriminator for technical failures;
* the technical validation record written into the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**);
* the composition gate — the rule that no asset advances without a technical pass;
* the ordering relationship to Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**);
* events, metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and decision records.

This section does **not** specify:

* visual quality scoring, the `minimum_score: 0.90` threshold, or aesthetic/continuity checks (defined in **5.6.2**);
* provider-side transport integrity during download, i.e. the shallow decode handshake performed by the adapter (defined in **5.3.4**);
* the retry backoff formula, attempt accounting, or terminal routing mechanics (defined in **5.4.5**);
* the legal state transitions of the render task (owned by **5.4.3**);
* the manifest write semantics, mutability rules, and persist-before-acknowledge ordering (owned by **5.4.4**);
* the render cache key and cache eligibility (defined in **5.6.3**).

Asset Validation **consumes** those contracts; it does not own them. It reads the expected specification from the approved plan and the manifest entry, produces a technical validation verdict, and hands classification and routing to the retry/failure subsystem (**5.4.5**).

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **A render asset is presumed technically invalid until every mandatory check has proven otherwise.**

Validation is **fail-closed**. The verdict `PASS` is emitted only when the complete check suite has run and every check has passed against a resolved expected specification. Absence of evidence is never treated as a pass: a missing probe result, an unreadable file, an unresolvable expected spec, a validator exception, or a partially-run suite all yield `FAIL`, never `PASS` and never `UNKNOWN-treated-as-pass`.

A corollary follows directly:

> **No asset advances to composition without a recorded technical `PASS`.**

The transition from `VALIDATING` to `READY` (**5.4.3**) requires a persisted technical `PASS` *and* a visual-quality pass (**5.6.2**). Technical validation is the necessary first condition. An asset with no technical verdict, or a technical `FAIL`, can never be `READY`, and therefore can never be `COMPOSED`. This is the rendering-side expression of Fail Closed (Principle 5, original 5.2): if technical validity cannot be established, no publishable trailer is produced from that asset.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        Runway / Motion / SVG / Typography Adapter
                    │  download() + transport integrity (5.3.4)
                    ▼
            Asset Repository (bytes durably stored)
                    │
                    ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
        │   VALIDATING state  (5.4.3)            │
        │                                        │
        │   ┌───────────────────────────────┐   │
        │   │  Asset Validation  (5.6.1)     │   │  ◄── THIS SECTION
        │   │  technical check suite         │   │
        │   │  file_integrity                │   │
        │   │  decode_success                │   │
        │   │  codec                         │   │
        │   │  resolution                    │   │
        │   │  frame_rate                    │   │
        │   │  duration                      │   │
        │   └───────────────┬───────────────┘   │
        │        PASS        │        FAIL       │
        │          │         │          │        │
        │          ▼         │          ▼        │
        │  ┌───────────────┐ │  classify (5.4.5) │
        │  │ Visual Quality│ │   ├─ CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD → RETRY
        │  │  Validation   │ │   └─ ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID → MANUAL_REVIEW
        │  │   (5.6.2)      │ │                  │
        │  └───────┬───────┘ │                  │
        └──────────┼─────────┴──────────────────┘
                   │ both gates PASS
                   ▼
                 READY  (5.4.3)
                   │
                   ▼
              FFmpeg Composer
```

Asset Validation sits **strictly downstream of download** (**5.3.4**) and **strictly upstream of visual quality validation** (**5.6.2**). It is the innermost, cheapest, most deterministic gate. Only an asset that has already been durably stored may enter it; only an asset that has passed it may reach visual scoring; only an asset that has passed both may reach the Composer.

The stage records its verdict to the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**) before acknowledgement, exactly as every other pipeline stage does (persist-before-acknowledge, **5.4.1** / **5.4.4**).

---

# Responsibilities

Asset Validation is responsible for:

* running the complete technical check suite on every downloaded asset;
* resolving the expected technical specification for the shot from the approved plan and manifest entry;
* re-verifying file integrity at rest (existence, size, checksum) independent of the adapter's transport-time check;
* performing a full decode verification (not merely a header handshake);
* verifying codec, resolution, frame rate, and duration against the expected specification with defined tolerances;
* producing a single deterministic verdict (`PASS` / `FAIL`) with per-check evidence;
* mapping a technical `FAIL` to a canonical `failure_code` and disposition (**5.4.5**), distinguishing transport artifacts from deterministic asset defects;
* writing the technical validation record into the manifest entry (**5.4.4**);
* enforcing the gate that only technically-passing assets proceed to visual quality validation (**5.6.2**);
* emitting validation events and metrics.

It is **not** responsible for:

* visual quality, continuity, framing, or aesthetic scoring (see **5.6.2**);
* transport-time integrity during the download stream (owned by the adapter, **5.3.4**);
* deciding whether a failure is retried, computing backoff, or performing terminal routing (owned by **5.4.5**);
* mutating render task state — it *requests* the `VALIDATE_OK` / `VALIDATE_FAIL` transition; the transition itself is owned by **5.4.3**;
* manifest write mechanics, concurrency control, or acknowledgement ordering (owned by **5.4.4** / **5.4.1**);
* cache eligibility of the validated asset (owned by **5.6.3**);
* selecting or altering the render strategy, profile, or prompt (owned by **5.2** / **5.5**);
* repairing, transcoding, re-muxing, or otherwise modifying a defective asset — validation is read-only with respect to asset bytes.

---

# Design Objectives

Asset Validation shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## AVL-301 — Total, Mandatory Suite

Every downloaded asset shall be subjected to the complete technical check suite. No check is optional. No asset — including previews (**5.6.4**), finals (**5.6.5**), or assets from cheap deterministic strategies (SVG, typography) — may skip technical validation. A cache-served asset (`CACHED`, **5.6.3**) is exempt only because it was validated when it was first produced and its digest is proven identical; a freshly downloaded asset is never exempt.

---

## AVL-302 — Fail Closed

The verdict `PASS` is emitted only when every mandatory check has run and passed against a resolved expected specification. Any missing check result, unresolved specification, validator exception, or ambiguous probe yields `FAIL`. Validation never defaults to acceptance (AVL-401).

---

## AVL-303 — Deterministic Verdict

Given identical asset bytes and an identical expected specification, the verdict, the per-check results, and the derived `failure_code` shall be byte-for-byte reproducible. Validation performs no sampling that depends on wall-clock time, no random probing, and no network calls that could alter the outcome. Determinism is consistent with the platform-wide determinism constraint and with **5.4.3** deterministic reachability.

---

## AVL-304 — Objective Checks Only

Every technical check shall be an objective, machine-decidable predicate over the asset's container, streams, and byte content — never a subjective judgment. If a proposed check requires aesthetic or perceptual interpretation, it belongs to Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**), not here (AVL-402).

---

## AVL-305 — Correct Failure Classification

Each technical failure shall be mapped to exactly one canonical `failure_code` and disposition from the closed taxonomy in **5.4.5**, correctly distinguishing a **transport artifact** (retryable — a re-download can plausibly fix it) from a **deterministic asset defect** (non-retryable — the identical asset can never pass). Misclassification that routes a deterministic defect back into retry, or a transport artifact into manual review, is prohibited (AVL-403).

---

## AVL-306 — Cheapest Gate First

The technical suite shall run before visual quality validation (**5.6.2**) and, within the suite, cheaper checks shall run before more expensive ones. Technical validation is deterministic and inexpensive; visual scoring is expensive. A technically invalid asset must be rejected before any visual scoring is attempted, so that expensive scoring is never spent on an unusable file (AVL-404).

---

## AVL-307 — Read-Only With Respect to Asset Bytes

Validation shall never modify, transcode, re-mux, repair, or re-encode the asset. It inspects and records. A defective asset is rejected, not fixed. The asset bytes that were validated are the exact bytes that (on pass) proceed to visual validation and composition.

---

## AVL-308 — Evidence-Bearing Verdict

Every verdict shall carry structured, non-sensitive evidence: the observed value and the expected value for each check, the check that first failed, and the derived classification. The evidence is written to the manifest (**5.4.4**) as auditable record, so that a rejection is always explainable and reproducible.

---

# The Expected Specification Contract

Technical validation is meaningful only against a known expected specification. Before any check runs, the validator resolves the **expected technical spec** for the render task from authoritative sources — never guessed, never inferred from the asset itself.

```yaml id="avl-expected-spec"
expected_technical_spec:
  render_task_id:          # references Render Manifest entry (5.4.4)
  shot_id:
  strategy:                # runway_video | animated_still | motion_graphics |
                           #   typography | svg | cached_asset
  render_profile:          # runway_preview | runway_final | ...
  media_class:             # video | image | vector
  container:               # expected container/format for media_class + profile
  allowed_codecs:          # closed allowlist for this media_class + profile
  expected_resolution:     # { width, height }  — from render_profile
  resolution_tolerance:    # exact match required unless profile permits scaling
  expected_frame_rate:     # fps — video only; null for image/vector
  frame_rate_tolerance:    # e.g. ±0.01 fps
  expected_duration_ms:    # planned shot duration (video/animated only)
  duration_tolerance_ms:   # e.g. ±40ms (≈ one frame at 24fps)
  min_file_bytes:          # lower bound guarding empty/near-empty files
  max_file_bytes:          # upper bound guarding runaway/garbage payloads
  spec_source_digest:      # digest binding this spec to the approved plan
```

Resolution rules:

* `media_class`, `container`, `allowed_codecs`, and `expected_resolution` are derived from the **render profile** (**5.6.4** / **5.6.5**) and the **strategy** (**5.2.1**), both of which are immutable fields of the manifest entry (**5.4.4**).
* `expected_duration_ms` originates from the approved Storyboard/Shot plan; the provider is never the authority on how long a shot should be.
* `resolution_tolerance` defaults to **exact match** for video and image assets; scaling is prohibited unless the profile explicitly permits it. Vector (SVG) assets are resolution-independent and skip the pixel-resolution check by media class (see the suite below).
* If the expected spec cannot be fully resolved, validation **fails closed** with `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` and routes to Manual Review — a validation that cannot know what "correct" means must never emit `PASS` (AVL-302).

The expected spec is derived, not stored on the asset. An asset's own metadata is treated as *claims to be verified against the expected spec*, never as the source of truth for what the asset should be.

---

# The Technical Validation Check Suite

The suite comprises exactly **six** mandatory checks, expanding the original 5.23 check list (`resolution`, `duration`, `codec`, `frame_rate`, `decode_success`, `file_integrity`). The set is **closed**: no additional technical check is defined here, and no aesthetic check is admitted (AVL-304).

The checks run in a fixed order, cheapest and most fundamental first, so that the suite short-circuits on the earliest, most diagnostic failure (AVL-306):

```yaml id="avl-suite-order"
technical_check_suite:
  order:
    - file_integrity      # 1. the file exists and is intact at rest
    - decode_success      # 2. the file is fully decodable
    - codec               # 3. the stream codec is in the allowlist
    - resolution          # 4. pixel dimensions match the profile
    - frame_rate          # 5. fps matches the profile (video only)
    - duration            # 6. duration matches the plan (video/animated only)
  short_circuit: true      # stop at first FAIL; record which check failed
  fail_closed: true        # any unrun/errored check ⇒ suite FAIL
```

Short-circuit is an efficiency and diagnostic choice, not a correctness relaxation: the suite records exactly which check failed and why, and a `FAIL` at any check is a suite `FAIL`. Because ordering is fixed and inputs are deterministic, the "first failing check" is itself deterministic (AVL-303).

Each check is defined below with its input, pass criterion, the `failure_code` it maps to on failure, and its evidence schema.

## Check 1 — file_integrity

Confirms the downloaded file is present and intact **at rest**, independent of the adapter's transport-time verification (**5.3.4**). The adapter verified the bytes as they arrived; this check re-verifies the bytes as they were stored, closing the gap between transfer and durable storage.

```yaml id="avl-check-file-integrity"
check: file_integrity
input:
  asset_uri:              # location in Asset Repository (5.4.4)
  expected_digest:        # asset.asset_digest recorded at download (5.4.4 / 5.3.4)
  min_file_bytes:
  max_file_bytes:
criteria:
  file_exists: true
  file_size_bytes > 0
  file_size_bytes >= min_file_bytes
  file_size_bytes <= max_file_bytes
  sha256(bytes) == expected_digest        # recomputed at rest
pass_when: all criteria hold
fail_codes:
  # missing, empty, truncated, or checksum-mismatched ⇒ transport artifact
  - CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD          # retryable (5.4.5, row 3)
evidence:
  observed_size_bytes:
  observed_digest:
  expected_digest:
  size_bounds: { min, max }
```

A failure here is treated as a **transport artifact** — a missing, empty, truncated, or checksum-mismatched file is exactly what a corrupted or partial download produces, and a re-download can plausibly fix it. It maps to `CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD`, which is **retryable** per **5.4.5** (row 3).

## Check 2 — decode_success

Confirms the asset is **fully decodable**, not merely that its header parses. This is deeper than the adapter's shallow decode handshake in **5.3.4**, which only opens the container, reads the header, and confirms frame count > 0. Here the validator performs a **full decode probe**: it walks the container structure and decodes every frame (or, for very long assets, every frame up to a configured decode budget with mandatory decode of the first and last GOP) confirming no decode error, no missing frames, and a coherent stream.

```yaml id="avl-check-decode"
check: decode_success
input:
  asset_uri:
  media_class:            # video | image | vector
  file_integrity_passed:  # must be true to enter this check
criteria:
  container_parses: true
  stream_present: true          # at least one decodable media stream
  full_decode_ok: true          # all sampled frames decode without error
  no_missing_frames: true       # decoded frame count coherent with index
  first_and_last_gop_decoded: true
pass_when: all criteria hold
fail_codes:
  # discriminator on the integrity result of the SAME asset:
  - CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD          # if bytes are incomplete/truncated ⇒ retryable
  - ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID     # if bytes are complete but stream undecodable ⇒ non-retryable
evidence:
  container:
  decoded_frame_count:
  indexed_frame_count:
  decode_error:                 # first decoder error, if any (non-sensitive)
  bytes_complete:               # from file_integrity: complete vs truncated
```

Decode failure carries a **discriminator** (see *Rejection & Failure Classification* below). If `file_integrity` already established the bytes are complete and checksum-correct, yet the stream still fails to decode, the provider produced a **deterministically broken asset**: re-downloading the identical bytes cannot fix it, so it maps to `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` (non-retryable, **5.4.5** row 9). If instead the decode failure coincides with an integrity fault (the bytes are incomplete), it is a transport artifact and maps to `CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD` (retryable). In the fixed suite order, a byte-truncation is normally caught at check 1; check 2 catches the case of complete-but-corrupt streams.

## Check 3 — codec

Confirms the media stream uses a codec on the closed allowlist for the asset's `media_class` and `render_profile`.

```yaml id="avl-check-codec"
check: codec
input:
  observed_codec:         # probed from the decoded stream
  allowed_codecs:         # from expected_technical_spec
criteria:
  observed_codec in allowed_codecs
pass_when: criterion holds
fail_codes:
  - ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID     # non-retryable (5.4.5, row 9)
evidence:
  observed_codec:
  allowed_codecs:
```

Codec is a **deterministic property** of what the provider produced. A wrong codec cannot be fixed by re-downloading the same asset. Failure maps to `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` (non-retryable → Manual Review). Illustrative allowlists (authoritative values are configured per profile in the Configuration Model): `video/mp4` → `{ h264, hevc }`; `image` → `{ png }`; `vector` → `{ svg }`.

## Check 4 — resolution

Confirms the asset's pixel dimensions match the expected resolution for its render profile.

```yaml id="avl-check-resolution"
check: resolution
input:
  observed_resolution:    # { width, height } probed from the stream
  expected_resolution:    # { width, height } from profile
  resolution_tolerance:   # default: exact
  media_class:
criteria:
  media_class == vector: SKIP        # SVG is resolution-independent
  else: observed_resolution == expected_resolution (within tolerance)
pass_when: criterion holds or SKIP
fail_codes:
  - ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID     # non-retryable (5.4.5, row 9)
evidence:
  observed_resolution:
  expected_resolution:
  tolerance:
```

Resolution is a **deterministic property**. Default policy is **exact match** (AVL: no silent upscaling or downscaling); a profile may permit a bounded tolerance only where explicitly configured. Vector assets are resolution-independent and this check is skipped by media class (the skip is recorded as evidence, not treated as a hidden pass of a pixel check). Failure maps to `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` (non-retryable).

## Check 5 — frame_rate

Confirms the asset's frame rate matches the expected frame rate for its profile. Applies to time-based assets (video, animated still with motion); skipped for static image and vector assets.

```yaml id="avl-check-frame-rate"
check: frame_rate
input:
  observed_frame_rate:    # fps probed from the stream
  expected_frame_rate:    # fps from profile
  frame_rate_tolerance:   # e.g. ±0.01 fps
  media_class:
criteria:
  media_class in {image, vector}: SKIP
  else: |observed_frame_rate - expected_frame_rate| <= frame_rate_tolerance
pass_when: criterion holds or SKIP
fail_codes:
  - ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID     # non-retryable (5.4.5, row 9)
evidence:
  observed_frame_rate:
  expected_frame_rate:
  tolerance:
```

Frame rate is a **deterministic property** of the produced stream. A tolerance accommodates rational-fraction frame rates (e.g. 23.976 vs 24) only where the profile declares them equivalent; otherwise exact match within `frame_rate_tolerance`. Failure maps to `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` (non-retryable).

## Check 6 — duration

Confirms the asset's duration matches the shot's **planned** duration from the approved plan, within tolerance. This guards against providers returning assets that are too short (truncated content) or too long (over-generated) relative to the shot's pacing budget.

```yaml id="avl-check-duration"
check: duration
input:
  observed_duration_ms:   # measured from decoded stream (frames / fps), not container claim alone
  expected_duration_ms:   # from approved Storyboard/Shot plan
  duration_tolerance_ms:  # e.g. ±40ms
  media_class:
criteria:
  media_class in {image, vector}: SKIP
  else: |observed_duration_ms - expected_duration_ms| <= duration_tolerance_ms
pass_when: criterion holds or SKIP
fail_codes:
  - ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID     # non-retryable (5.4.5, row 9)
evidence:
  observed_duration_ms:
  expected_duration_ms:
  tolerance_ms:
```

Duration is measured from the **decoded** stream (frame count ÷ frame rate), cross-checked against the container's declared duration; a mismatch between the container claim and the decoded reality is itself a decode-integrity signal. Duration is a **deterministic property** of the produced asset. Failure maps to `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` (non-retryable). It is never silently trimmed or padded to fit (AVL-307); a mis-durationed asset is rejected and escalated.

---

# Check Applicability by Media Class

Not every check applies to every media class. Applicability is fixed and deterministic; a skipped check is recorded as `SKIPPED` with its rationale, and a `SKIPPED` check is neither a pass nor a hidden failure.

| Check           | video | image (animated still, motion graphics) | image (static) | vector (svg) |
| --------------- | :---: | :--------------------------------------: | :-------------: | :----------: |
| file_integrity  |  ✅   |                   ✅                     |       ✅        |      ✅      |
| decode_success  |  ✅   |                   ✅                     |       ✅        |   ✅ (parse) |
| codec           |  ✅   |                   ✅                     |       ✅        |      ✅      |
| resolution      |  ✅   |                   ✅                     |       ✅        |   ⏭ SKIP    |
| frame_rate      |  ✅   |                   ✅                     |    ⏭ SKIP      |   ⏭ SKIP    |
| duration        |  ✅   |                   ✅                     |    ⏭ SKIP      |   ⏭ SKIP    |

For vector (SVG) assets, `decode_success` means the document parses to a valid, well-formed vector tree; `codec` degenerates to a format/mime check (`image/svg+xml`). The mandatory-suite guarantee (AVL-301) holds per media class: every *applicable* check runs, and the applicability map is itself deterministic and recorded.

---

# The Validation Stage in the Pipeline (VALIDATING State)

Technical validation is the work performed while a render task occupies the **`VALIDATING`** state of the render task state machine (**5.4.3**). The state's contract is owned by **5.4.3**; this section defines what happens *inside* it.

```text
   DOWNLOADING ──DOWNLOAD_OK──▶  VALIDATING
                                    │
                                    │  (5.6.1) technical check suite
                                    │     file_integrity → decode_success →
                                    │     codec → resolution → frame_rate → duration
                                    │
                       technical PASS │ technical FAIL
                                    │                 │
                                    ▼                 ▼
                        (5.6.2) visual quality   classify (5.4.5)
                          validation             ├─ CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD → FAILED→RETRY
                                    │             └─ ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID → FAILED→MANUAL_REVIEW
                    visual PASS │ visual FAIL
                            │            │
                 VALIDATE_OK│            │ VALIDATE_FAIL
                            ▼            ▼
                          READY        FAILED (→ 5.4.5)
```

Stage rules:

* **Entry precondition.** The task enters `VALIDATING` only after the asset is fully downloaded and durably stored (**5.3.4** / **5.4.3** entry condition for `VALIDATING`). Validation never runs on an in-flight or partially-transferred asset.
* **Technical first, visual second.** Within `VALIDATING`, Asset Validation (this section) runs first; only on technical `PASS` does Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**) run. The state-machine transition `VALIDATE_OK` (→ `READY`) fires only when **both** gates pass (**5.4.3** exit condition: "technical pass + visual score ≥ 0.90").
* **Exit on failure.** A technical `FAIL` produces the `VALIDATE_FAIL` transition to `FAILED` (**5.4.3**), carrying the derived `failure_code` to the classifier (**5.4.5**). Visual scoring does not run.
* **Attempt neutrality.** Running validation does not itself consume a render attempt; the attempt counter is owned by **5.4.5** and advances only when a retry is authorized. Validation is idempotent: re-validating the same stored bytes yields the same verdict (AVL-303), so an at-least-once re-delivery (**5.4.1**) that re-enters `VALIDATING` is safe.
* **Persist before acknowledge.** The technical validation record is written to the manifest (**5.4.4**) before the queue item is acknowledged (**5.4.1** / **5.4.4** persist-before-acknowledge). A crash after persist and before acknowledge results in a safe, idempotent re-validation.

---

# Integrity & Decode Verification In Depth

Asset Validation performs **two** independent verifications that together prove the asset is technically usable, and it deliberately distinguishes its role from the adapter's transport-time check (**5.3.4**).

## Two-layer integrity model

```text
Layer 1 — Transport integrity (5.3.4, adapter, during download)
  • byte length matches provider-reported size (if provided)
  • checksum matches provider-reported checksum (if provided)
  • shallow decode handshake: open container, read header, frame_count > 0
  • purpose: confirm the bytes ARRIVED intact; fast reject of obvious corruption

Layer 2 — At-rest integrity + full decode (5.6.1, THIS section, VALIDATING)
  • file exists, non-zero, within size bounds
  • sha256 recomputed at rest == asset_digest recorded at download
  • FULL decode probe: every sampled frame decodes, no missing frames,
    first & last GOP decoded, container duration coheres with decoded duration
  • purpose: confirm the STORED bytes are a decodable, spec-conformant asset
```

The two layers are complementary, not redundant. Layer 1 guards the transfer; Layer 2 guards the stored artifact and, crucially, goes deeper — a file can pass a shallow header handshake yet fail a full decode (e.g. a valid header over a corrupt or provider-broken payload). Asset Validation is authoritative for technical usability; the adapter's handshake is a fast-fail optimization, not a substitute.

## The integrity/decode discriminator

The single most important classification decision this section makes is: *when decode or integrity fails, is the fault a transport artifact (retryable) or a deterministic asset defect (non-retryable)?* The discriminator is the **byte-completeness** of the asset:

```text
if bytes are missing / empty / truncated / checksum-mismatched:
        ⇒ transport artifact
        ⇒ failure_code = CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD   (retryable, 5.4.5 row 3)

elif bytes are complete & checksum-correct, but stream/codec/dims/fps/duration is wrong:
        ⇒ deterministic asset defect
        ⇒ failure_code = ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID   (non-retryable, 5.4.5 row 9)
```

This is exactly the row 3 vs row 9 distinction owned by **5.4.5**: a re-download can plausibly cure incomplete bytes, but it can never cure a complete-but-wrong asset — the provider would have to re-render, which is a new attempt decision, not a transport retry.

---

# Rejection & Failure Classification

A rejected asset is never discarded. It is classified into the closed failure taxonomy of **5.4.5**, and only two `failure_code` values can originate from technical validation:

| Technical failure                                            | `failure_code`            | Disposition (5.4.5) | Routes to (on this attempt / on exhaustion) |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------- | ------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| Missing / empty / truncated / checksum-mismatched file       | `CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD`      | **Retryable**       | RETRY → (Dead Letter after exhaustion)      |
| Complete-but-undecodable stream                              | `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` | **Non-retryable**   | MANUAL_REVIEW                               |
| Wrong codec                                                  | `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` | **Non-retryable**   | MANUAL_REVIEW                               |
| Wrong resolution                                            | `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` | **Non-retryable**   | MANUAL_REVIEW                               |
| Wrong frame rate                                            | `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` | **Non-retryable**   | MANUAL_REVIEW                               |
| Wrong duration                                              | `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` | **Non-retryable**   | MANUAL_REVIEW                               |
| Expected spec unresolvable / validator exception            | `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` | **Non-retryable**   | MANUAL_REVIEW (Fail Closed, AVL-302)        |

Classification rules:

* **Asset Validation classifies; 5.4.5 disposes.** This section emits the `failure_code`; the retry/failure subsystem (**5.4.5**) owns whether and how a retry is authorized, the backoff schedule, attempt accounting, and terminal routing. Asset Validation never schedules a retry itself and never mutates the attempt counter.
* **`CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD` is the *only* retryable technical outcome.** It exists precisely because a partial/corrupt download is a documented retry reason (original 5.16 / **5.4.5** row 3). Every other technical failure is a deterministic property of the produced asset and is non-retryable (row 9).
* **Non-retryable technical failures escalate to Manual Review, not silent drop.** A wrong-codec or wrong-resolution asset is a well-formed task that produced an unusable result; it routes to Manual Review (**5.4.5**), preserving Fail Closed. It is never coerced into acceptance and never silently deleted.
* **Distinct from quality failure.** A technical `ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID` (this section) is not the same as `FAILED_QUALITY_VALIDATION` (**5.6.2**, row 6). Technical validity is objective; quality is a score against `minimum_score: 0.90`. Both are non-retryable and both route to Manual Review, but they arise from different gates and carry different `failure_code`s and evidence.

The failure record is appended to the manifest attempt history as a `render_failure_record` (**5.4.5**), carrying `failure_code`, `disposition`, `origin_stage = VALIDATING`, and non-sensitive evidence — never prompt text, descriptions, secrets, or article content.

---

# The Technical Validation Record (Manifest)

The verdict is durably recorded in the render task entry's `validation` block in the Render Manifest (**5.4.4**). This section owns the **technical** portion of that block; the visual portion is owned by **5.6.2**.

```yaml id="avl-validation-record"
validation:
  technical:                 # OWNED BY 5.6.1
    verdict:                 # PASS | FAIL
    validated_at:            # UTC timestamp
    validated_by_attempt:    # attempt whose asset was validated
    expected_spec_digest:    # spec_source_digest used (binds to approved plan)
    asset_digest_verified:   # sha256 confirmed at rest == asset.asset_digest
    first_failed_check:      # null on PASS; else the check name
    failure_code:            # null on PASS; CORRUPTED_DOWNLOAD | ASSET_TECHNICAL_INVALID
    disposition:             # null on PASS; RETRYABLE | NON_RETRYABLE
    checks:                  # per-check results, in suite order
      - name: file_integrity
        result:              # PASS | FAIL | SKIPPED
        observed:            # observed value(s)
        expected:            # expected value(s)
      - name: decode_success
        result:
        observed:
        expected:
      - name: codec
        result:
        observed:
        expected:
      - name: resolution
        result:
        observed:
        expected:
      - name: frame_rate
        result:
        observed:
        expected:
      - name: duration
        result:
        observed:
        expected:
  visual_quality_score:      # OWNED BY 5.6.2 — null until technical PASS
  visual_quality_passed:     # OWNED BY 5.6.2
  validated_at:
  validated_by_attempt:
```

This is the canonical expansion of the manifest entry's `validation.technical`, `validation.checks`, `asset.asset_digest`, `asset.resolution`, `asset.frame_rate`, `asset.codec`, and `asset.duration_ms` fields defined in **5.4.4**. Record rules:

* **Tied to a specific attempt.** `validated_by_attempt` binds the verdict to the exact asset it examined; a subsequent retry that produces a new asset gets a new verdict for the new attempt (**5.4.4** `validation.*` is mutable-per-attempt; `history[]` is append-only).
* **Evidence is mandatory and non-sensitive.** Every check records observed vs expected. On `FAIL`, `first_failed_check`, `failure_code`, and `disposition` are populated. The record never stores prompt text, secrets, descriptions, or article content (**5.4.4** / **5.4.1** payload boundary).
* **`technical.verdict = PASS` is a precondition for `READY`.** The state machine (**5.4.3**) will not transition to `READY` unless `technical.verdict == PASS` and the visual quality gate (**5.6.2**) also passes.
* **Persist before acknowledge.** The record is durably written (**5.4.4**) before the queue acknowledges the validation item (**5.4.1**).

---

# The Composition Gate

Asset Validation, together with Visual Quality Validation (**5.6.2**), forms the gate between rendering and composition. This section states the technical half of that gate normatively.

```yaml id="avl-composition-gate"
composition_gate:
  # An asset may transition VALIDATING → READY (5.4.3) only if ALL hold:
  technical_verdict: PASS               # owned by 5.6.1 (this section)
  visual_quality_passed: true           # owned by 5.6.2 (score >= 0.90)
  asset_digest_verified: true           # the READY bytes are the validated bytes
  # Consequently, an asset may reach the Composer only if it is READY.
```

Gate rules:

* **No pass, no advance.** An asset with `technical_verdict != PASS` shall never become `READY` and therefore shall never be `COMPOSED`. There is no override path within the automated pipeline; the only escape from a technical failure is retry (transport artifact) or Manual Review (deterministic defect).
* **The validated bytes are the composed bytes.** The `asset_digest` verified at validation is the same digest the Composer consumes. Because validation is read-only (AVL-307) and no stage between `READY` and `COMPOSED` mutates the asset, the trailer is composed from exactly the bytes that passed the gate.
* **Fail Closed at the gate.** If technical validity cannot be established for any shot's asset, that shot's asset does not advance; consistent with Principle 5 and the platform-level Fail-Closed posture, the platform never composes a publishable trailer from an unvalidated or invalid asset. Budget/quality escalations that block completion route to Manual Review (**5.4.5** / **5.4.1**) rather than yielding a partial trailer.
* **Technical gate precedes visual gate.** Because visual scoring is expensive and technical checks are cheap and deterministic (AVL-306), the technical gate is evaluated first; a technically invalid asset is rejected before any visual score is computed.

---

# Ordering Relationship to Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2)

Asset Validation (technical) and Visual Quality Validation (visual) are **two distinct gates** that both run inside `VALIDATING`. Their separation is deliberate and normative.

| Property                 | Asset Validation (5.6.1, this section)         | Visual Quality Validation (5.6.2)                 |
