* Event ordering is validated against the state machine (**5.4.3**); an out-of-order event pair is recorded as an anomaly, not used to compute a negative duration.
* Every measurement event references `render_task_id`, `build_id`, `attempt`, and the originating component, satisfying ROB-304.

---

# Metric Record Schema

Measurements are written as immutable records.

```yaml id="rob-record"
metric_record:
  metric_record_id:      # deterministic: sha256(render_task_id + attempt + metric_name + span_id)
  metric_name:           # e.g. render_time
  family:                # TIMING | COST | CACHE | OUTCOME | QUALITY | CAPACITY
  type:                  # counter | gauge | histogram | distribution
  value:                 # numeric
  unit:                  # ms | s | usd | ratio | count | score
  scope:                 # task | attempt | shot | scene | build | trailer
  # correlation
  render_task_id:
  build_id:
  attempt:
  shot_id:
  scene_index:
  provider:              # runway | motion | svg | typography | cache
  render_profile:        # runway_preview | runway_final | ...
  provider_task_id:      # null for cache-served or non-provider stages
  # provenance
  source_component:      # 5.4.1 | 5.4.3 | 5.4.4 | 5.6.1 | 5.6.2 | 5.6.3
  derived_from:          # manifest state timestamps / cache ledger / validation record
  observed_at:           # timestamp copied from authoritative state, NOT wall clock read here
  recorded_at:           # when the projection was written (informational only)
```

The `metric_record_id` is deterministic so that replay-based reconstruction (ROB-306) produces identical record identities and re-recording is idempotent. Records shall never contain prompt text, character/location descriptions, provider secrets, binary asset data, or article content — the same payload prohibition as queue items (**5.4.1**).

---

# Metric Storage Model

```yaml id="rob-storage"
metric_store:
  model: append_only
  partitioning: by build_id then by metric family
  retention:
    raw_task_metrics:    90d
    build_rollups:       400d
    trailer_rollups:     indefinite   # cost/quality history of shipped trailers
  immutability: true            # supersede, never edit-in-place (ROB-305)
  reconstruction_source:
    - render_manifest            # 5.4.4
    - queue_event_log            # 5.4.1
    - cache_ledger               # 5.6.3
    - validation_records         # 5.6.1 / 5.6.2
    - provider_task_records
```

Storage is append-only and partitioned so that a single build's full measurement history is retrievable as one contiguous read for post-build analysis. Trailer-scope roll-ups (notably `cost_per_trailer` and `quality_pass_ratio`) are retained indefinitely because they are the longitudinal baseline against which **Part 6** detects regression.

Write path guarantee: metric writes are asynchronous and buffered; loss of the write buffer degrades only historical fidelity and is fully recoverable by reconstruction (ROB-306). No render blocks on a metric write (ROB-302).

---

# Correlation & Distributed Tracing

Every render is traceable end to end.

```text
build_id ─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                                    │  (trailer scope)
   ├── render_task_id (shot A) ── attempt 1 ── span set │
   │        ├── queue_wait span                         │
   │        ├── submission span    (provider_task_id X) │
   │        ├── render_time span                        │
   │        ├── download span                           │
   │        ├── validation span    (quality_score 0.94) │
   │        └── ready span                              │
   │   render_task_id (shot A) ── attempt 2 ── span set │  (a retry)
   ├── render_task_id (shot B) ── CACHE HIT ── span set │  (no provider span)
   └── ...                                              │
```

Correlation rules:

```yaml
correlation:
  primary_key:   render_task_id          # one shot, one task (5.4.4, 5.11)
  build_key:     build_id                # groups all tasks of a trailer
  attempt_key:   attempt                 # separates retry timings/costs
  provider_key:  provider_task_id        # joins platform span to provider job
  trace_id:      sha256(build_id)        # deterministic root trace id
  span_id:       sha256(render_task_id + attempt + stage)
```

A trace is the ordered set of spans sharing a `build_id`, sub-grouped by `render_task_id` and `attempt`. Because `trace_id` and `span_id` are deterministic (derived from identifiers, not generated randomly), traces are reproducible across replays — satisfying ROB-303 and Determinism. A cache-served task produces a valid, complete trace with no provider span, making incremental reuse (Principle 4) directly visible in the trace tree.

---

# Roll-Up Model

Measurements aggregate up a fixed hierarchy. Each level is a deterministic function of the level below.

```text
metric (attempt scope)
        │  sum / max / concat attempts
        ▼
shot     (render_task_id)
        │  group by scene_index
        ▼
scene
        │  group by build_id
        ▼
build
        │  the build that produces one publishable trailer
        ▼
trailer  ── cost_per_trailer, cache_hit_ratio, quality_pass_ratio, t_build_end_to_end
```

Roll-up rules:

* Timings roll up as distributions (percentiles preserved), never as naive averages of averages.
* Costs roll up as sums, with retries counted at each attempt (so `cost_per_shot` ≥ single-attempt cost).
* Cache and outcome ratios roll up from underlying counts, not from averaging child ratios.
* Quality rolls up as pass/fail counts plus a score distribution.

The trailer scope is the reporting unit that matters to the business and to Part 6: one published cinematic trailer, fully attributed.

---

# Dashboards & KPIs

Operational dashboards shall present, at minimum, the following views. Each view is backed by named catalog metrics; no dashboard value is computed off-catalog.

## Rendering Health Dashboard

```yaml
health_dashboard:
  failed_render_ratio            # SLO gauge, target ≤ 2%
  retry_ratio                    # SLO gauge, target ≤ 5%
  cache_hit_ratio                # SLO gauge, target ≥ 70%
  quality_pass_ratio             # target ≥ 0.98 of validated assets ≥ 0.90
  provider_error_count           # by class (429 / 5xx / timeout / rejection)
  dead_letter_count
  manual_review_count
```

## Rendering Performance Dashboard

```yaml
performance_dashboard:
  render_time                    # p50 / p90 / p95 / p99
  queue_wait                     # p50 / p95
  download_time                  # p50 / p95
  validation_time                # p50 / p95
  t_task_end_to_end              # p50 / p95
  t_build_end_to_end             # per build
  provider_latency               # per provider
```

## Rendering Cost Dashboard

```yaml
cost_dashboard:
  cost_per_trailer               # headline
  cost_by_provider
  cost_by_strategy
  cost_saved_by_cache
  rendered_shot_count vs cached_shot_count
  incremental_reuse_ratio        # Principle 4 evidence
  budget_consumed_ratio          # feeds 5.4.1 backpressure
```

## Headline KPIs

| KPI                     | Definition                                    | Direction   |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ----------- |
| Cache Hit Ratio         | `cache_hit_ratio`                             | higher      |
| Retry Ratio             | `retry_ratio`                                 | lower       |
| Failed Render Ratio     | `failed_render_ratio`                         | lower       |
| Cost per Trailer        | `cost_per_trailer`                            | lower       |
| Quality Pass Ratio      | `quality_pass_ratio`                          | higher      |
| Build Time (p95)        | `t_build_end_to_end` p95                      | lower       |
| Incremental Reuse Ratio | `cached_shot_count / total_shots`             | higher      |

---

# Service Level Objectives & Metric Linkage

The Rendering Platform is accountable to explicit Service Level Objectives. Each SLO is computed from a named catalog metric and evaluated continuously by the SLO Evaluator (ROB-307).

```yaml id="rob-slo"
service_level_objectives:
  cache_hit_rate:
    metric: cache_hit_ratio
    target: ">= 0.70"          # ≥ 70%
    scope: build, trailing_30d
    rationale: Principle 4 (incremental) + 5.2.3 cost order; cache is cheapest tier
  render_retry_rate:
    metric: retry_ratio
    target: "<= 0.05"          # ≤ 5%
    scope: build, trailing_30d
    rationale: retries within max_retry 3 (5.4.5) should be rare, not routine
  failed_render_rate:
    metric: failed_render_ratio
    target: "<= 0.02"          # ≤ 2%
    scope: build, trailing_30d
    rationale: Fail Closed (Principle 5) allows zero partial trailers; failure must be rare
```

SLO evaluation rules:

* Each SLO value is fully traceable to the measurement set that produced it (ROB-307); an SLO number with no drill-down path is prohibited.
* An SLO breach raises an `slo_breach` signal (an event, not an action). Observability never itself remediates; remediation is a human or Part 6 policy decision.
* SLO windows are trailing and deterministic; recomputation over the same window yields the same result (ROB-303).
* The three SLOs interlock: a high retry rate that hides a high failure rate is still a failed-render SLO breach; a high cache-hit rate does not excuse a quality regression.

---

# Feeding Part 6 (Performance)

This subsystem is the **sole instrumentation surface** for the performance work in **Part 6**. The hand-off is contractual.

```yaml id="rob-part6-feed"
part6_feed:
  baseline_metrics:
    - t_build_end_to_end          # → Part 6 build-time budget
    - render_time (p95)           # → Part 6 provider-time budget
    - cost_per_trailer            # → Part 6 cost budget
    - cache_hit_ratio             # → Part 6 efficiency target
    - retry_ratio                 # → Part 6 reliability target
    - failed_render_ratio         # → Part 6 reliability target
    - quality_pass_ratio          # → Part 6 quality-hold target
  guarantees_to_part6:
    - append_only_history         # regressions computable against a stable baseline
    - deterministic_derivation    # A/B and before/after comparisons are exact
    - full_correlation            # a regression is attributable to task/provider/strategy
    - trailer_scope_rollups       # the unit Part 6 budgets against
  consumed_by_part6:
    - performance_budgets         # numeric ceilings on the baseline metrics
    - regression_gates            # release blocked if a baseline metric worsens beyond tolerance
    - optimization_verification   # Constraint 5: each optimization proven by a metric delta
```

The contract is one-directional: Observability **supplies** measurements; Part 6 **decides** budgets and gates. Observability shall not embed Part 6's numeric budgets (those live in Part 6), and Part 6 shall not add instrumentation outside this catalog. Every performance claim in Part 6 shall cite a metric defined here.

---

# Events

The Observability subsystem itself emits events for its own material actions (distinct from the render events it *consumes*).

```yaml
observability_events:
  - metric_recorded
  - span_opened
  - span_closed
  - span_incomplete            # closed at build end without a closing event
  - measurement_anomaly        # out-of-order events / negative duration rejected
  - rollup_computed            # task→shot→scene→build→trailer
  - slo_evaluated
  - slo_breach                 # cache < 70% | retry > 5% | fail > 2%
  - metric_store_degraded      # buffer loss; reconstruction required
  - metrics_reconstructed      # replay-based rebuild completed
```

Every observability event references the affected scope identifier (`render_task_id` and/or `build_id`) and is itself durably recorded, so the act of measuring is auditable. `slo_breach` and `measurement_anomaly` are signals only; they trigger no automatic change to any render.

---

# Metrics (of the Observability subsystem itself)

The subsystem measures its own health, so that a gap in visibility is itself visible.

```yaml
observability_self_metrics:
  measurement_coverage_ratio     # tasks with complete span sets / total tasks (target → 1.0)
  incomplete_span_count          # spans closed as incomplete
  measurement_anomaly_count      # rejected out-of-order / negative durations
  metric_write_lag               # recorded_at − observed_at distribution
  reconstruction_count           # times metrics were rebuilt from source
  slo_evaluation_count
  orphan_measurement_count       # measurements missing a correlation key (must stay 0, ROB-304)
```

`orphan_measurement_count` must remain zero: any nonzero value is a correlation defect and fails the Quality Gate. `measurement_coverage_ratio` is the direct measurement of ROB-301 (Universal Measurability).

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The Observability subsystem operates under least privilege and read-only access to render truth.

Required controls:

* read-only access to the Render Manifest, cache ledger, and validation records — Observability holds no mutation rights over render truth;
* metric records scoped to build namespace, with namespace isolation between concurrent builds;
* no provider secrets, prompts, or asset payloads in metric records (same prohibition as **5.4.1**);
* cost figures are estimates/attributions derived from provider metadata, never carrying billing credentials;
* encryption in transit and at rest for the metric and trace stores;
* tamper evidence on the append-only store; superseding writes are additive and audited;
* audit events for every rollup, SLO evaluation, and reconstruction.

A dashboard consumer receives aggregated measurements and correlation identifiers only; it never receives, and cannot reach, the prompts or credentials behind a render.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="rob-config"
observability:
  metrics:
    enabled_families: [TIMING, COST, CACHE, OUTCOME, QUALITY, CAPACITY]
    percentiles: [50, 90, 95, 99]
  storage:
    model: append_only
    retention:
      raw_task_metrics: 90d
      build_rollups: 400d
      trailer_rollups: indefinite
  tracing:
    enabled: true
    trace_id_source: build_id
    deterministic_span_ids: true
  slo:
    cache_hit_rate: ">= 0.70"
    render_retry_rate: "<= 0.05"
    failed_render_rate: "<= 0.02"
    evaluation_window: trailing_30d
  part6_feed:
    export_baseline_metrics: true
    export_scope: trailer
  non_interference:
    async_emission: true
    max_block_on_write: 0ms        # metric writes never block a render
```

All values are configurable and environment-overridable. Configuration is validated at startup; invalid configuration fails closed for *observability only* — it disables affected measurement rather than degrading or blocking any render (ROB-302). Disabling a metric family that a live SLO depends on is prohibited and rejected at validation.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* deterministic `metric_record_id` and `span_id` generation;
* per-stage timing derived correctly from manifest state timestamps;
* cache hit/miss and rendered-vs-cached shot counting;
* cost attribution across attempts (retries increase `cost_per_shot`);
* quality score recording and pass/ratio computation;
* SLO computation from underlying counts;
* rejection of out-of-order event pairs (no negative durations);
* orphan-measurement detection (missing correlation key).

## Integration Tests

* full trace assembled for a successful multi-shot build;
* cache-served task produces a complete trace with no provider span;
* retry produces a second attempt span set with distinct costs;
* roll-up task → shot → scene → build → trailer matches recomputation;
* dashboard KPIs match catalog metrics exactly;
* Part 6 baseline feed exports the declared metrics at trailer scope.

## Concurrency Tests

* concurrent measurement writes from parallel render workers (Principle 3 / **5.4.2**);
* SLO evaluation concurrent with in-flight measurement;
* rollup computed while later attempts are still writing;
* namespace isolation of metrics across concurrent builds.

## Failure Injection Tests

* metric store unavailable during active rendering → renders unaffected (ROB-302);
* write buffer loss → `metric_store_degraded` raised, reconstruction succeeds;
* out-of-order / duplicated render events;
* span never closed → recorded `incomplete`, not dropped;
* SLO evaluation over a window with missing measurements.

## Recovery Tests

* full metric reconstruction from Render Manifest + queue log + cache ledger + validation records;
* reconstructed metrics byte-for-byte identical to originals (ROB-303, ROB-306);
* trailer-scope cost/quality history intact after store rebuild;
* idempotent re-recording produces no duplicate `metric_record_id`.

---

# Observability Quality Gate

```yaml
observability_quality_gate:
  every_task_has_measurement_record: true        # ROB-301
  every_measurement_has_correlation_keys: true   # ROB-304
  orphan_measurement_count_is_zero: true
  metric_writes_never_block_render: true         # ROB-302
  timings_derived_from_authoritative_state: true # ROB-303
  metric_store_is_append_only: true              # ROB-305
  metrics_reconstructable_from_sources: true     # ROB-306
  reconstruction_is_byte_identical: true
  all_dashboard_values_backed_by_catalog: true
  slo_values_traceable_to_measurements: true     # ROB-307
  cache_hit_slo_wired: ">= 0.70"
  retry_rate_slo_wired: "<= 0.05"
  failed_render_slo_wired: "<= 0.02"
  part6_baseline_feed_exported: true             # ROB-308
  no_secrets_or_payloads_in_records: true
  every_optimization_has_a_metric: true          # Performance Constraint 5
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant ROB-401

Observability never mutates render truth; it reads the Render Manifest and derives measurements.

## Invariant ROB-402

Every render task, attempt, and build produces a measurement record — there is no unmeasured render path.

## Invariant ROB-403

Metric emission is never on the critical path of a render; a metric-store failure degrades visibility only.

## Invariant ROB-404

All timings are derived from authoritative state timestamps, not from clocks read inside the Observability subsystem.

## Invariant ROB-405

Every measurement and span carries `render_task_id`, `build_id`, and `attempt`; orphan measurements are prohibited.

## Invariant ROB-406

The metric store is append-only; a recorded measurement is immutable evidence and is superseded, never edited.

## Invariant ROB-407

All metrics are reconstructable from the Render Manifest, queue event log, cache ledger, validation records, and provider records.

## Invariant ROB-408

Metric derivation is deterministic; identical authoritative state yields byte-for-byte identical metrics and traces.

## Invariant ROB-409

Every dashboard value and every SLO value is traceable to the exact catalog measurements that produced it.

## Invariant ROB-410

SLO breach and measurement anomaly are signals only; the Observability subsystem takes no corrective render action.

## Invariant ROB-411

The metrics catalog is the sole instrumentation surface for Part 6; no performance claim exists without a backing metric.

## Invariant ROB-412

Every optimization asserted in Part 5 is backed by a named metric whose movement demonstrates its effect (Performance Constraint 5).

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                     |
| ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-581 | Observability is a passive read-and-record projection of render truth; it never mutates the Render Manifest or acts on a render. |
| ADR-582 | The metrics catalog extends the original eight metrics (5.29) with per-stage timings, cost-per-trailer, cache ratios, shot-source counts, and quality scores, and is the single instrumentation surface for Part 6. |
| ADR-583 | All timings and identities are derived deterministically from authoritative state timestamps and identifiers, so metrics and traces are reproducible across replays. |
| ADR-584 | Metrics are stored append-only and are fully reconstructable from the Manifest, queue log, cache ledger, validation records, and provider records — metrics are a projection, never a second source of truth. |
| ADR-585 | Every render is correlated end-to-end by `render_task_id` and grouped by `build_id`, with trailer-scope roll-ups as the reporting unit. |
| ADR-586 | The platform is accountable to three wired SLOs — cache hit ≥ 70%, retry rate ≤ 5%, failed render ≤ 2% — evaluated continuously as signals, with remediation left to humans or Part 6 policy. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.7.1** is complete when:

* Observability is formally separated as a passive read-and-record layer that never mutates render truth and never sits on the render critical path.
* The full rendering metrics catalog is defined, preserving the original eight metrics (`submission_time`, `queue_wait`, `render_time`, `download_time`, `validation_time`, `cache_hit`, `retry_count`, `provider_latency`) and extending them with per-stage timings, cost-per-trailer, cache hit/miss ratios, rendered-vs-cached shot counts, and quality scores.
* The observability requirement (Performance Constraint 5 — every optimization must be measurable) is discharged, with a Quality Gate assertion that every optimization has a backing metric.
* The metric record schema and append-only storage model are specified, including provenance, retention, and reconstructability from authoritative sources.
* Correlation and deterministic distributed tracing keyed on `render_task_id` and `build_id` are defined, including cache-served tasks and retries.
* The roll-up model from task to trailer scope is specified, with `cost_per_trailer`, `cache_hit_ratio`, and `quality_pass_ratio` as headline trailer-scope KPIs.
* Dashboards and KPIs are defined and provably backed only by catalog metrics.
* The three SLOs — cache hit rate ≥ 70%, render retry rate ≤ 5%, failed render rate ≤ 2% — are wired to named metrics, evaluated continuously, and raised as signals without automatic remediation.
* The metric feed to **Part 6** is contractually specified as the sole performance instrumentation surface, with append-only baselines, deterministic derivation, and trailer-scope roll-ups.
* Capacity metrics from **5.4.1**, cache metrics from **5.6.3**, and quality scores from **5.6.2** are re-exported without being redefined or altered.
* Events, self-metrics, security, configuration, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement a durable, deterministic, non-interfering rendering observability subsystem whose measurements are reconstructable and whose every optimization is provable.


---

# Part 5.7.2 — Public Interfaces & Integration Contracts

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section defines the canonical **public interface surface** of the Rendering Platform and the **integration contracts** that bind its internal components together and to the systems above and below it.

The Rendering Platform is not a monolith. It is a set of independently testable components that cooperate through explicit, versioned contracts:

* **RenderingPlatform** — the outward-facing facade over the whole rendering execution layer;
* **QueueManager** — durable scheduling and transport of render tasks;
* **RenderPlanner** — strategy assignment and render-task generation from the approved Storyboard;
* **RunwayProvider** — the concrete Runway adapter implementing the generic provider contract;
* **AssetValidator** — technical validation of downloaded assets;
* **RenderCache** — deterministic reuse of previously produced assets;
* **PromptAssembler** — deterministic assembly of render prompts from approved artifacts;
* **RenderQualityGate** — visual quality validation and the fail-closed publishing gate.

The purpose of this section is to specify:

* the **public method signature** of each component;
* the **inputs and outputs** each component accepts and returns;
* the **integration contract** between each pair of collaborating components;
* the **owning section** that holds the detailed behavioural specification for each component;
* the **dependency boundaries** that keep components independently testable;
* the **public API surface** that upstream (Orchestration, Preview/Final build drivers) and downstream (Asset Repository, FFmpeg Composer) callers may rely upon.

This section does **not** re-specify the internal behaviour of any component. It specifies the *seams*. Each interface is a promise; the owning section is where that promise is honoured.

The interfaces defined here are the contract that permits Codex to implement, mock, and test each rendering component in isolation, then assemble them into a working Rendering Platform whose behaviour is deterministic and provider-independent.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the eight public interfaces of the Rendering Platform;
* the facade responsibilities of `RenderingPlatform` and its public API surface;
* the public method signatures (in `python`) of every component;
* the input and output data contracts crossing each interface boundary;
* the integration contracts (who calls whom, in what order, with what pre/postconditions);
* the mapping from each interface to its owning detailed-specification section;
* the dependency-injection boundaries that make each component independently testable;
* the versioning and compatibility rules for the public surface;
* the fault-propagation contract across interface boundaries;
* interface invariants, decision records, and acceptance criteria.

This section does **not** specify:

* queue topology, leasing, or admission (owned by **5.4.1**);
* strategy selection or the decision matrix (owned by **5.2.1**, **5.2.2**, **5.2.3**);
* the generic provider contract semantics or submission protocol (owned by **5.3.2**, **5.3.3**);
* the Runway adapter's internal request/response mapping (owned by **5.3.4**);
* asset validation checks (owned by **5.6.1**);
* visual quality scoring (owned by **5.6.2**);
* cache key derivation and reuse rules (owned by **5.6.3**);
* prompt assembly ordering and injection (owned by **5.5.1**–**5.5.5**);
* observability metrics definitions (owned by **5.7.1**).

Each cited section is the single source of truth for the behaviour behind its interface. This section binds those behaviours into a coherent, callable, testable whole.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle.

> **A component is defined by its interface, owned by its section, and trusted only through its contract.**

The public surface is the only sanctioned way to invoke a rendering component. No caller may reach around an interface into another component's internal state. No component may depend on the concrete type of a collaborator when a contract type exists.

A corollary follows directly:

> **Every rendering interface is independently testable in isolation, with all collaborators replaced by contract-conformant test doubles.**

If any component cannot be exercised without instantiating the whole platform, its interface is too wide or its dependencies are not injected. Independent testability is not a convenience; it is the acceptance condition for the interface itself (Design Objective RIF-306).

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        Orchestration / Build Driver (Preview | Final)
                          │
                          ▼
                 ┌──────────────────┐
                 │ RenderingPlatform│   ← public facade (this section)
                 └──────────────────┘
                          │
     ┌──────────┬─────────┼──────────┬───────────┬───────────┐
     ▼          ▼         ▼          ▼           ▼           ▼
RenderPlanner  Queue   Prompt    Provider     Asset      RenderQuality
  (5.2)       Manager  Assembler  (5.3.2/     Validator      Gate
             (5.4.1)   (5.5.1)    5.3.4)       (5.6.1)      (5.6.2)
                          │          │           │
                          │          ▼           │
                          │      RunwayProvider   │
                          │       (adapter)       │
                          ▼                       ▼
                     RenderCache             Asset Repository
                       (5.6.3)                     │
                          │                        ▼
                          └──────────────►  FFmpeg Composer
```

`RenderingPlatform` is the sole entry point exposed to callers outside Part 5. Every internal component is reached through the facade or through a contract handed to it by the facade. Downstream systems (Asset Repository, FFmpeg Composer) consume outputs; they never invoke internal rendering components directly.

---

# Responsibilities

This section (the public-interface contract) is responsible for:

* declaring each component's public method signatures;
* declaring the input/output types that cross each interface;
* declaring the integration ordering and pre/postconditions between components;
* declaring the dependency-injection seams;
* declaring the facade's public API surface for upstream and downstream callers;
* declaring interface versioning and fault-propagation rules;
* mapping every interface to its owning behavioural section.

It is **not** responsible for:

* the internal algorithm behind any method (owned by the cited sections);
* queue durability, leasing, or ordering (see **5.4.1**);
* strategy or cost decisions (see **5.2**);
* provider API payloads (see **5.3.3**, **5.3.4**);
* validation thresholds or scoring math (see **5.6.1**, **5.6.2**);
* cache key composition (see **5.6.3**);
* metric emission (see **5.7.1**).

The public surface *composes* behaviours; it does not *implement* them.

---

# Interface Design Objectives

The public interface surface shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## RIF-301 — Single Facade Entry

All external callers reach rendering through exactly one facade, `RenderingPlatform`. Internal components are not part of the external public surface.

---

## RIF-302 — Contract-Typed Collaboration

Components collaborate through abstract contract types (interfaces / protocols), never through concrete collaborator classes. The Runway adapter is reached only through the generic provider contract (see **5.3.2**).

---

## RIF-303 — Explicit Ownership Mapping

Every public method maps to exactly one owning section that specifies its behaviour. No behaviour is defined in two places.

---

## RIF-304 — Stable Data Contracts

Inputs and outputs crossing an interface are typed, versioned data objects. A change to a data contract is a versioned change, never a silent field addition that alters meaning.

---

## RIF-305 — Fail-Closed Propagation

An interface never returns a partial or ambiguous success. Every method either satisfies its postcondition or raises a typed, classified error that a caller can route (retry, manual review, dead-letter) per **5.4.5**.

---

## RIF-306 — Independent Testability

Each component is exercisable in isolation with all collaborators replaced by contract-conformant doubles. No component requires the concrete construction of another to be unit-tested.

---

## RIF-307 — Dependency Direction

Dependencies point inward toward stable contracts. The facade depends on component contracts; components depend on collaborator contracts; no component depends on the facade.

---

## RIF-308 — Execution-Layer Purity

No public method exposes an operation that could modify the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard. The interface surface can only read the creative plan and produce assets.

---

# The Eight Public Interfaces

The Rendering Platform exposes the following interfaces. The mapping to owning sections is fixed and normative.

| Interface           | Responsibility (one line)                                  | Owning Section(s)              |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| `RenderingPlatform` | Facade over the whole rendering execution layer            | **5.7.2** (this section)       |
| `RenderPlanner`     | Assign strategy and generate render tasks from Storyboard  | **5.2** (5.2.1–5.2.3)          |
| `QueueManager`      | Durable scheduling and transport of render tasks           | **5.4.1**                      |
| `PromptAssembler`   | Deterministic prompt assembly from approved artifacts      | **5.5.1** (with 5.5.2–5.5.5)   |
| `VideoProvider`     | Generic provider contract (submit/status/download/cancel)  | **5.3.2**                      |
| `RunwayProvider`    | Concrete Runway adapter implementing `VideoProvider`       | **5.3.4** (contract in 5.3.2)  |
| `AssetValidator`    | Technical validation of downloaded assets                  | **5.6.1**                      |
| `RenderQualityGate` | Visual quality validation and fail-closed gate             | **5.6.2**                      |
| `RenderCache`       | Deterministic reuse of previously produced assets          | **5.6.3**                      |

Each interface is specified below with responsibility, public methods, inputs/outputs, integration contract, and independent-testability notes.

---

# Shared Data Contracts

The interfaces exchange a small set of typed data objects. These are the nouns of the public surface. Their authoritative schemas live in the cited sections; the shapes below are the contract façade.

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional, Protocol


class RenderStrategy(str, Enum):
    RUNWAY_VIDEO = "runway_video"
    ANIMATED_STILL = "animated_still"
    MOTION_GRAPHICS = "motion_graphics"
    TYPOGRAPHY = "typography"
    SVG = "svg"
    CACHED_ASSET = "cached_asset"


class RenderTaskState(str, Enum):
    NEW = "NEW"
    SUBMITTED = "SUBMITTED"
    RUNNING = "RUNNING"
    DOWNLOADING = "DOWNLOADING"
    VALIDATING = "VALIDATING"
    READY = "READY"
    COMPOSED = "COMPOSED"
    FAILED = "FAILED"
    RETRY = "RETRY"
    MANUAL_REVIEW = "MANUAL_REVIEW"


class RenderProfile(str, Enum):
    RUNWAY_PREVIEW = "runway_preview"
    RUNWAY_FINAL = "runway_final"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RenderTask:
    """A single unit of render work. One per shot. Authoritative fields
    are owned by the Render Manifest (5.4.4); this is the interface view."""
    render_task_id: str
    shot_id: str
    build_id: str
    scene_index: int
    shot_index: int
    strategy: RenderStrategy
    provider: str                 # "runway" | "motion" | "svg" | "typography" | "cache"
    render_profile: RenderProfile
    cache_key: str                # idempotency anchor (5.6.3)
    priority_score: float
    cost_estimate: float


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AssembledPrompt:
    """Deterministic prompt payload. Contains no article text (5.5.1)."""
    render_task_id: str
    provider: str
    render_profile: RenderProfile
    prompt_text: str
    negative_prompt: str          # forbidden-style enforcement (5.5.5)
    seed: int                     # deterministic seed
    camera: dict                  # lens / movement / angle (5.5.4, read-only)
    duration_seconds: float


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProviderJob:
    """Handle to a submitted provider render (5.3.2)."""
    provider_job_id: str
    render_task_id: str
    provider: str
    submitted_at: str


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProviderStatus:
    render_task_id: str
    provider_job_id: str
    state: str                    # "running" | "completed" | "failed"
    progress: float               # 0.0 .. 1.0
    asset_url: Optional[str]      # present only when completed
    error_code: Optional[str]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RenderedAsset:
    """A downloaded, not-yet-validated asset (5.6.1 input)."""
    render_task_id: str
    local_uri: str
    provider: str
    render_profile: RenderProfile
    bytes: int


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ValidationResult:
    render_task_id: str
    passed: bool
    checks: dict                  # resolution/duration/codec/... (5.6.1)
    failures: list[str]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class QualityResult:
    render_task_id: str
    score: float                  # 0.0 .. 1.0
    passed: bool                  # score >= 0.90 (5.6.2)
    dimensions: dict              # continuity/framing/lighting/... (5.6.2)


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CacheLookup:
    cache_key: str
    hit: bool
    asset_uri: Optional[str]      # present only on hit


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RenderResult:
    """Terminal outcome of one render task, returned across the facade."""
    render_task_id: str
    shot_id: str
    state: RenderTaskState        # READY | MANUAL_REVIEW | FAILED
    asset_uri: Optional[str]
    quality_score: Optional[float]
    from_cache: bool
    attempts: int
```

These types are frozen (immutable) and version-stamped by the module they live in. A field's meaning is fixed at a contract version (RIF-304). Every interface below trades exclusively in these types plus the typed errors defined under **Fault Propagation Contract**.

---

# Interface — RenderingPlatform (Facade)

**Responsibility.** `RenderingPlatform` is the single public entry point to the rendering execution layer. It composes `RenderPlanner`, `QueueManager`, `PromptAssembler`, the provider contract, `AssetValidator`, `RenderQualityGate`, and `RenderCache` into a coherent build-level operation. It owns no rendering behaviour of its own; it orchestrates the components that do.

**Owning section.** This section (**5.7.2**). The facade is a composition contract, not a behavioural one.

**Public methods.**

```python
class RenderingPlatform(Protocol):

    def render_build(self, request: "RenderBuildRequest") -> "RenderBuildResult":
        """Render every shot in an approved build.
        Precondition: request references an APPROVED Storyboard and a
        Render Strategy assignment (see 5.2). Fail-closed: raises
        StoryboardNotApprovedError if the creative plan is not approved.
        Returns a RenderBuildResult summarising per-shot RenderResults.
        This is the primary upstream entry point."""

    def render_shot(self, task: RenderTask) -> RenderResult:
        """Render a single already-generated render task. Used for
        incremental re-render of a modified shot (Principle 4)."""

    def cancel_build(self, build_id: str, scope: str = "build") -> None:
        """Cancel in-flight rendering for a build/scene/shot. Routes
        through the QueueManager Control Queue (5.4.1)."""

    def get_build_status(self, build_id: str) -> "RenderBuildStatus":
        """Read-only progress snapshot for observability (5.7.1)."""

    def health_check(self) -> "PlatformHealth":
        """Aggregate health of all injected components and providers."""
```

**Inputs.**

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RenderBuildRequest:
    build_id: str
    storyboard_ref: str           # approved storyboard artifact reference
    render_strategy_ref: str      # render-strategy.json reference (5.2)
    profile: RenderProfile        # runway_preview | runway_final
    budget_ceiling: float         # cost reservation (5.2.3)
```

**Outputs.**

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RenderBuildResult:
    build_id: str
    results: list[RenderResult]
    all_ready: bool               # True only if every shot reached READY
    manual_review: list[str]      # render_task_ids escalated (fail-closed)
    total_cost: float
```

**Integration contract.** `render_build` executes the canonical pipeline (see **Integration Contracts** below): plan → cache-eliminate → assemble → enqueue → submit → monitor → download → validate → quality-gate → ready/compose. The facade injects every collaborator; it never constructs a provider by concrete type (RIF-302, RIF-307). The facade returns only after every task is terminal (READY, MANUAL_REVIEW, or FAILED). It never returns a partial success as success (RIF-305): if any shot cannot reach READY, `all_ready` is `False` and the offending task ids appear in `manual_review`, honouring Fail Closed (Principle 5).

**Independent testability.** The facade is tested with every component replaced by a contract double. A test injects a stub `RenderPlanner` yielding two tasks, a fake `QueueManager` that dispatches synchronously, a fake provider returning a canned asset, and stub validators. The test asserts the pipeline ordering and the fail-closed aggregation without any real provider, queue, or filesystem. Because the facade owns no behaviour, its tests assert *composition and ordering*, not rendering logic.

---

# Interface — RenderPlanner

**Responsibility.** `RenderPlanner` converts an approved Storyboard plus its Render Strategy assignment into a deterministic set of `RenderTask` objects — exactly one per shot — assigning each shot a single strategy per the Rendering Decision Matrix. It never renders; it plans.

**Owning section.** **5.2** — the Render Strategy Model (**5.2.1**), the Rendering Decision Matrix (**5.2.2**), and Cost-Aware Strategy Selection (**5.2.3**).

**Public methods.**

```python
class RenderPlanner(Protocol):

    def plan(self, storyboard_ref: str, strategy_ref: str,
             profile: RenderProfile) -> list[RenderTask]:
        """Generate one RenderTask per shot from the approved Storyboard.
        Deterministic: identical inputs yield byte-identical task lists,
        including render_task_id, cache_key, and priority_score.
        Assigns exactly one RenderStrategy per shot (mixed strategies
        within a shot are prohibited, 5.2.1)."""

    def assign_strategy(self, shot: "ShotSpec") -> RenderStrategy:
        """Select the single strategy for one shot via the decision
        matrix (5.2.2), respecting the cost order Cache -> Animated Still
        -> Motion Graphics -> Runway (5.2.3)."""

    def estimate_cost(self, tasks: list[RenderTask]) -> float:
        """Sum expected provider cost for budget reservation (5.2.3)."""
```

**Inputs.** An approved `storyboard_ref` (shot-list.json, storyboard.json, camera-plan.json) and a `strategy_ref` (render-strategy.json). Read-only creative artifacts (RIF-308).

**Outputs.** An ordered `list[RenderTask]`. Order is deterministic by scene/shot index and cost rank.

**Integration contract.** The facade calls `plan` first. The resulting tasks are handed to `RenderCache` for cache elimination, then to `QueueManager` for admission. `RenderPlanner` must never emit a task whose `strategy` conflicts with the decision matrix or whose `cache_key` is not derivable per **5.6.3**. It reads the creative plan; it must never write it (RIF-308).

**Independent testability.** Tested with fixture storyboards. Given a storyboard with a "human facial emotion" shot, `assign_strategy` must return `RUNWAY_VIDEO`; given "architecture diagram", `SVG`. Determinism is asserted by planning the same fixture twice and comparing task lists byte-for-byte. No queue, provider, or cache is required.

---

# Interface — QueueManager

**Responsibility.** `QueueManager` durably holds, orders, and dispatches render tasks between pipeline stages. It is the sole admission point for provider work and the scheduling core of the execution layer.

**Owning section.** **5.4.1** (Render Queue Manager). All queue topology, leasing, ordering, concurrency, rate limiting, retry scheduling, and reconstruction are specified there. The interface here is only the callable seam.

**Public methods.**

```python
class QueueManager(Protocol):

    def admit(self, task: RenderTask) -> bool:
        """Admit a task to the Ready Queue. Idempotent: re-admitting an
        active queue_item_id is a no-op returning False. Cache-hit tasks
        must never be admitted (5.4.1 admission contract)."""

    def lease_next(self, queue_class: str, worker_id: str) -> Optional["QueueLease"]:
        """Acquire a time-bounded lease on the next visible item in a
        queue class, or None if none available. Ordering is deterministic
        (5.4.1)."""

    def acknowledge(self, lease: "QueueLease") -> None:
        """Remove an item after its manifest state and outputs are
        persisted. Acknowledgement MUST follow persistence (5.4.1)."""

    def route(self, lease: "QueueLease", to_class: str,
              visible_at: Optional[float] = None) -> None:
        """Move a task to the next queue class, optionally with delayed
        visibility (retry/monitor)."""

    def cancel(self, build_id: str, scope: str) -> None:
        """Control-queue cancellation (build/scene/shot)."""

    def reconstruct(self) -> None:
        """Rebuild queue state from the Render Manifest, retry schedule,
        leases, and provider records (5.4.1 recoverability)."""
```

**Inputs.** `RenderTask` references (never payloads) and `QueueLease` handles. Queue items carry references only — no prompts, secrets, or asset bytes (**5.4.1**).

**Outputs.** `QueueLease` handles and routing side effects. Delivery is at-least-once; consumers must be idempotent.

**Integration contract.** The facade admits planned, non-cache-hit tasks. Workers lease from a queue class, perform a stage effect (submit/poll/download/validate/quality-gate), persist manifest state, then acknowledge. The queue never owns render truth (that is the Manifest, **5.4.4**). Provider rate limits and concurrency are enforced at the queue boundary before any provider call (**5.4.1**), so the provider adapter is never invoked in violation of limits.

**Independent testability.** Tested with an in-memory manifest double and stub workers. Assertions: admission idempotency, deterministic ordering under identical inputs, acknowledgement-after-persist ordering, cache-hit rejection, and reconstruction from a manifest fixture. No real provider or network required. Detailed test matrix lives in **5.4.1**.

---

# Interface — PromptAssembler

**Responsibility.** `PromptAssembler` deterministically composes the render prompt for a task from approved artifacts: Character (by ref), Location (immutable ref), Camera (from Shot Planner, read-only), Lighting, Blocking, Emotion, Shot Purpose, and Visual Style. It injects the forbidden-style negative prompt. It never passes article text to a provider.

**Owning section.** **5.5.1** (Deterministic Prompt Assembly), with injection detail in **5.5.2** (Character), **5.5.3** (Location), **5.5.4** (Camera), and enforcement in **5.5.5** (Visual Style).

**Public methods.**

```python
class PromptAssembler(Protocol):

    def assemble(self, task: RenderTask,
                 character_bible: dict,
                 location_bible: dict) -> AssembledPrompt:
        """Deterministically assemble the provider prompt for a task.
        Identical inputs yield an identical AssembledPrompt including a
        deterministic seed. Article text is never included (5.5.1).
        Camera parameters are read from the shot plan and must not be
        altered (5.5.4)."""

    def negative_prompt(self) -> str:
        """Return the forbidden-style enforcement string: anime, cartoon,
        cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms, humanoid robots, exaggerated
        sci-fi interfaces (5.5.5)."""

    def verify_determinism(self, task: RenderTask,
                           character_bible: dict,
                           location_bible: dict) -> bool:
        """Assemble twice and assert byte-identical output."""
```

**Inputs.** A `RenderTask`, the `character_bible` and `location_bible` (read-only). Characters and locations are referenced by immutable ID (**5.5.2**, **5.5.3**).

**Outputs.** An `AssembledPrompt` — prompt text, negative prompt, deterministic seed, read-only camera block, duration. No article content (RIF-308, **5.5.1**).

**Integration contract.** The facade/worker calls `assemble` before provider submission. The output is handed to the provider adapter via the submission protocol (**5.3.3**). The assembler reads bibles and shot plan; it must never mutate them. The camera block is copied verbatim from the Shot Planner; the assembler and every downstream provider are prohibited from overriding it (**5.5.4**).

**Independent testability.** Tested with fixture bibles and shot specs. Determinism is the headline test: `assemble` the same task twice, assert identical `AssembledPrompt` and identical seed. Forbidden styles must always appear in `negative_prompt`. Article text must never appear in `prompt_text` (asserted by scanning for known article tokens). No provider required.

---

# Interface — VideoProvider (Generic Contract) and RunwayProvider (Adapter)

**Responsibility.** `VideoProvider` is the generic, provider-independent contract every rendering backend implements. `RunwayProvider` is the concrete adapter that fulfils this contract against the Runway API. Providers *only render*; they never generate stories, determine pacing, create dialogue, or design camera language.

**Owning sections.** The generic contract is owned by **5.3.2** (Provider Interface Contract). The submission protocol is owned by **5.3.3** (Task Submission Protocol). The Runway adapter specifics are owned by **5.3.4** (Runway Provider Adapter). Provider independence framing is **5.3.1**.

**Public methods (generic contract).**

```python
class VideoProvider(Protocol):
    """Every provider implements exactly this contract. No provider-
    specific logic may leak outside it (5.3.2). Providers are
    interchangeable (Principle 2)."""

    def submit(self, prompt: AssembledPrompt) -> ProviderJob:
        """Submit a render. Returns immediately with a job handle
        (asynchronous, Principle 3). Idempotent on cache_key /
        render_task_id per the submission protocol (5.3.3)."""

    def get_status(self, job: ProviderJob) -> ProviderStatus:
        """Poll provider job status. Non-blocking."""

    def download(self, job: ProviderJob, dest_uri: str) -> RenderedAsset:
        """Download a completed asset to dest_uri."""

    def cancel(self, job: ProviderJob) -> None:
        """Best-effort cancellation of an in-flight job."""

    def health_check(self) -> "ProviderHealth":
        """Provider availability probe."""
```

**RunwayProvider.** `RunwayProvider` implements `VideoProvider` and adds no public methods. Its Runway-specific request/response mapping, profile handling (`runway_preview`, `runway_final`), and error translation are internal and owned by **5.3.4**.

```python
class RunwayProvider(VideoProvider):
    """Concrete Runway adapter. Implements the generic contract only.
    Detailed request mapping, gen-model profile selection, and Runway
    error translation are owned by 5.3.4. Construction takes credentials
    from a secret store, never from queue items (5.4.1 security)."""
```

**Inputs.** An `AssembledPrompt` for `submit`; `ProviderJob` handles thereafter. Credentials are resolved at construction from a secret store, never passed through the queue.

**Outputs.** `ProviderJob`, `ProviderStatus`, `RenderedAsset`. No creative content is ever produced beyond the rendered asset.

**Integration contract.** The worker submits an assembled prompt, then the `QueueManager` monitors via delayed polling (**5.4.1**) calling `get_status`, then `download` on completion. The provider is reached *only* through the `VideoProvider` type (RIF-302); the facade and workers must never reference `RunwayProvider` concretely. Provider throttle responses (HTTP 429) feed queue rate limiting, not error paths (**5.4.1**). The provider must never emit story/pacing/dialogue/camera decisions (**5.3.1**); it consumes the camera block verbatim.

**Independent testability.** The generic contract enables a `FakeProvider` returning canned jobs/assets for all upstream tests. `RunwayProvider` itself is tested against a recorded/mocked Runway API: assert `submit` returns immediately, `get_status` maps Runway states to `ProviderStatus`, `download` writes a `RenderedAsset`, and Runway errors map to the platform's typed errors. Because every collaborator sees only `VideoProvider`, swapping Runway for a future provider (Veo, Luma, Pika) requires no change above **5.3.4** (Principle 2).

---

# Interface — AssetValidator

**Responsibility.** `AssetValidator` performs technical validation on every downloaded asset: resolution, duration, codec, frame rate, decode success, and file integrity. It rejects technically invalid assets before they reach quality evaluation or composition.

**Owning section.** **5.6.1** (Asset Validation).

**Public methods.**

```python
class AssetValidator(Protocol):

    def validate(self, asset: RenderedAsset,
                 task: RenderTask) -> ValidationResult:
        """Run technical checks (resolution, duration, codec, frame_rate,
        decode_success, file_integrity). Returns a ValidationResult;
        passed=False lists concrete failures. Invalid assets are rejected
        (5.6.1)."""

    def expected_spec(self, task: RenderTask) -> "AssetSpec":
        """Return the expected technical spec for a task's profile."""
```

**Inputs.** A `RenderedAsset` (downloaded, not yet validated) and its `RenderTask`.

**Outputs.** A `ValidationResult` with per-check detail and a boolean `passed`.

**Integration contract.** The worker calls `validate` after `download` and before the quality gate. On `passed=False`, the failure is classified by **5.4.5**: transient technical faults (corrupted download) may retry; structural faults (wrong codec from an unsupported input) route to Dead Letter. Validation is technical only; aesthetic judgement belongs to `RenderQualityGate`.

**Independent testability.** Tested with fixture asset files: a valid clip passes; a truncated file fails `file_integrity`; a wrong-resolution clip fails `resolution` with a concrete failure string. No provider or queue required; the validator reads bytes and returns a verdict.

---

# Interface — RenderQualityGate

**Responsibility.** `RenderQualityGate` performs visual quality validation — human consistency, continuity, framing, lighting, camera movement, motion artifacts, visual coherence — and enforces the fail-closed publishing gate at `minimum_score = 0.90`. A render below threshold is never published.

**Owning section.** **5.6.2** (Visual Quality Validation).

**Public methods.**

```python
class RenderQualityGate(Protocol):

    def evaluate(self, asset: RenderedAsset,
                 task: RenderTask,
                 prompt: AssembledPrompt) -> QualityResult:
        """Score visual quality across all dimensions. passed = score
        >= 0.90 (5.6.2). Runs only on technically valid assets."""

    def minimum_score(self) -> float:
        """Return the fail-closed threshold, 0.90 (5.6.2)."""

    def gate(self, result: QualityResult) -> bool:
        """Fail-closed decision: True only if the asset may be published/
        composed. Sub-threshold results MUST NOT pass (Principle 5)."""
```

**Inputs.** A technically valid `RenderedAsset`, its `RenderTask`, and the `AssembledPrompt` used (for consistency scoring against intent).

**Outputs.** A `QualityResult` with the composite score, per-dimension breakdown, and a boolean `passed`.

**Integration contract.** Invoked only after `AssetValidator.validate` passes. On `passed=True` the task advances to READY. On `passed=False` after retries are exhausted, the task routes to Manual Review — never to a published trailer (Fail Closed, **5.4.1** budget/quality escalation, Principle 5). The gate produces no asset; it judges one.

**Independent testability.** Tested with fixture assets and scores: a high-consistency clip passes; a clip with motion artifacts scores below 0.90 and `gate` returns `False`. The threshold `0.90` is asserted directly. No provider required; the scoring model is injected and stubbed for deterministic tests.

---

# Interface — RenderCache

**Responsibility.** `RenderCache` provides deterministic reuse of previously produced assets keyed on `sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile)`. A cache hit skips rendering entirely; cache elimination precedes all queueing.

**Owning section.** **5.6.3** (Render Cache & Reuse).

**Public methods.**

```python
class RenderCache(Protocol):

    def lookup(self, task: RenderTask) -> CacheLookup:
        """Look up an asset by the task's cache_key. On hit, returns the
        cached asset_uri; the task then bypasses the queue entirely
        (5.4.1 admission / 5.6.3)."""

    def store(self, task: RenderTask, asset: RenderedAsset,
              quality: QualityResult) -> None:
        """Persist a validated, quality-passed asset for future reuse.
        Only READY assets are cached."""

    def key_for(self, task: RenderTask) -> str:
        """Return the deterministic cache key for a task (5.6.3)."""

    def invalidate(self, cache_key: str) -> None:
        """Remove a cache entry (e.g. provider_version change)."""
```

**Inputs.** A `RenderTask` for lookup; a validated `RenderedAsset` plus its `QualityResult` for store.

**Outputs.** A `CacheLookup` (hit + asset_uri, or miss).

**Integration contract.** The facade calls `lookup` immediately after planning and before queue admission. On a hit, the task is marked READY→CACHED in the manifest and is *never* admitted to the queue (**5.4.1**, RIF invariant). On a miss, rendering proceeds; after the quality gate passes, `store` persists the asset. Only validated, quality-passed assets are cached (no substandard asset is ever reused). The cache key derivation is owned by **5.6.3**; the interface only exposes it.

**Independent testability.** Tested with an in-memory store: `store` then `lookup` returns a hit with the stored uri; a differing `provider_version` yields a miss (key sensitivity). Determinism of `key_for` is asserted across repeated calls. No queue, provider, or network required.

---

# Integration Contracts

The components compose into one canonical pipeline. The facade enforces the ordering; each arrow is a contract with a precondition and a postcondition.

```text
RenderBuildRequest (APPROVED storyboard + strategy)
        │  precond: storyboard approved (else StoryboardNotApprovedError)
        ▼
RenderPlanner.plan ──────────► list[RenderTask]      (5.2)
        │  postcond: one task/shot, single strategy, deterministic
        ▼
RenderCache.lookup ──────────► CacheLookup           (5.6.3)
        │  hit  → mark READY→CACHED, RenderResult(from_cache=True), SKIP queue
        │  miss ↓
        ▼
QueueManager.admit ──────────► Ready Queue            (5.4.1)
        │  precond: not cache hit, budget reserved, provider available
        ▼
PromptAssembler.assemble ────► AssembledPrompt        (5.5.1)
        │  postcond: deterministic, no article text, camera read-only
        ▼
VideoProvider.submit ────────► ProviderJob            (5.3.2/5.3.3/5.3.4)
        │  postcond: returns immediately (async)
        ▼
QueueManager monitor / VideoProvider.get_status       (5.4.1 + 5.3.2)
        │  loop until completed | failed | timeout(45m)
        ▼
VideoProvider.download ──────► RenderedAsset          (5.3.2)
        ▼
AssetValidator.validate ─────► ValidationResult       (5.6.1)
        │  fail → classify (5.4.5): retry | dead-letter
        ▼
RenderQualityGate.evaluate ──► QualityResult          (5.6.2)
        │  score < 0.90 after retries → MANUAL_REVIEW (fail closed)
        ▼
RenderCache.store ───────────► cached for reuse        (5.6.3)
        ▼
RenderResult(state=READY) ──► Asset Repository ──► FFmpeg Composer
```

**Contract ordering rules (normative):**

1. Planning precedes everything; nothing is rendered without a task from `RenderPlanner`.
2. Cache lookup precedes queue admission; a hit never enters the queue (RIF-403).
3. Prompt assembly precedes provider submission; a provider is never called without an `AssembledPrompt`.
4. Provider submission is asynchronous; the pipeline never blocks on a single shot (Principle 3, parallel per **5.4.2**).
5. Technical validation precedes quality evaluation; a technically invalid asset is never scored.
6. Quality evaluation precedes composition; a sub-0.90 asset is never composed (Principle 5).
7. Only READY, validated, quality-passed assets are cached and only they reach the Composer.
8. Retry scheduling and failure classification are owned by **5.4.5**; the facade routes but does not classify.

**Cross-component preconditions.**

| From → To                     | Precondition enforced                                   | Owner    |
| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | -------- |
| Planner → Cache               | task has a derivable cache_key                          | 5.6.3    |
| Cache(miss) → Queue           | not a cache hit; budget reserved                        | 5.4.1    |
| Queue → Assembler             | task leased; manifest entry exists                      | 5.4.1    |
| Assembler → Provider          | prompt deterministic; no article text                  | 5.5.1    |
| Provider → Validator          | asset downloaded and decodable                          | 5.6.1    |
| Validator → QualityGate       | ValidationResult.passed == True                         | 5.6.1    |
| QualityGate → Cache/Composer  | QualityResult.score >= 0.90                             | 5.6.2    |

---

# Public API Surface

The Rendering Platform exposes a deliberately narrow surface. Only the following are public to callers outside Part 5.

**Upstream callers** (Orchestration, Preview/Final build drivers) may call:

```python
RenderingPlatform.render_build(request)     # primary entry
RenderingPlatform.render_shot(task)         # incremental re-render
RenderingPlatform.cancel_build(build_id, scope)
RenderingPlatform.get_build_status(build_id)
RenderingPlatform.health_check()
```

**Downstream consumers** (Asset Repository, FFmpeg Composer) read only:

```python
RenderResult          # per-shot terminal outcome + asset_uri
RenderBuildResult     # build-level aggregate
render_manifest.json  # authoritative state (owned by 5.4.4)
```

**Not public.** `QueueManager`, `RenderPlanner`, `PromptAssembler`, `VideoProvider`/`RunwayProvider`, `AssetValidator`, `RenderQualityGate`, and `RenderCache` are **internal** to the platform. External callers must never construct or invoke them directly (RIF-301). They are reached only through the facade or through contracts the facade injects.

**Read-only guarantee.** No public method can modify the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard. The surface accepts references to the approved plan and returns assets; it has no write path into the creative layer (RIF-308, Principle from **5.1**). Rendering is an execution layer.

---

# Dependency Boundaries

Dependencies point inward toward stable contracts (RIF-307). The concrete graph:

```text
RenderingPlatform  ──depends-on──►  RenderPlanner (contract)
                   ──depends-on──►  QueueManager (contract)
                   ──depends-on──►  PromptAssembler (contract)
                   ──depends-on──►  VideoProvider (contract)   ◄── RunwayProvider implements
                   ──depends-on──►  AssetValidator (contract)
                   ──depends-on──►  RenderQualityGate (contract)
                   ──depends-on──►  RenderCache (contract)

No component depends on RenderingPlatform (no upward dependency).
No component depends on RunwayProvider concretely (only VideoProvider).
```

**Injection.** Every collaborator is supplied to the facade by construction (dependency injection). The facade never instantiates a concrete provider, queue, or validator internally. This is the seam that makes the whole platform testable with doubles.

```python
class RenderingPlatformImpl:
    def __init__(self,
                 planner: RenderPlanner,
                 queue: QueueManager,
                 assembler: PromptAssembler,
                 provider_registry: "ProviderRegistry",   # name -> VideoProvider
                 validator: AssetValidator,
                 quality_gate: RenderQualityGate,
                 cache: RenderCache) -> None:
        ...
```

**Provider registry.** Providers are resolved by name (`"runway"` → a `VideoProvider`). This is where Runway is bound to the `runway` key; swapping to a future provider changes only the registry entry (Principle 2). No code above **5.3.4** references Runway by type.

**Prohibited dependencies.**

* A component must never import the facade.
* A component must never import `RunwayProvider` concretely; it imports `VideoProvider`.
* No component reads another component's private state; only contract types cross boundaries.
* No component receives provider secrets through the queue; credentials are resolved at provider construction (**5.4.1** security).

---

# Interface Versioning & Compatibility

The public surface is versioned so downstream systems and Codex implementations remain compatible across changes.

```yaml
interface_versioning:
  contract_version: "5.7.2-v1"
  data_contracts_frozen: true          # frozen dataclasses; meaning fixed per version
  additive_change: minor_version       # new optional field -> minor bump
  breaking_change: major_version       # removed/retyped field -> major bump
  method_signature_change: major_version
  provider_contract_owner: "5.3.2"     # VideoProvider signature is owned there
```

Compatibility rules:

* A field's meaning is immutable within a contract version (RIF-304). Adding a field is a minor version; removing or retyping one is a major version.
* The `VideoProvider` signature is owned by **5.3.2**; any new provider must satisfy the current version exactly, adding no public methods (RunwayProvider adds none).
* The facade's five public methods are the compatibility surface for upstream callers; changing them is a major version.
* Owning sections may evolve internal behaviour freely as long as the interface contract and its version hold.

---

# Fault Propagation Contract

Every interface fails closed with typed, classifiable errors (RIF-305). No method returns a partial or ambiguous success.

```python
class RenderingError(Exception):
    """Base for all typed rendering faults. Carries render_task_id and a
    failure_class routable per 5.4.5."""
    render_task_id: Optional[str]
    failure_class: str    # "transient" | "permanent" | "quality" | "budget"


class StoryboardNotApprovedError(RenderingError): ...   # facade precond
class StrategyAssignmentError(RenderingError): ...       # planner (5.2)
class ProviderSubmissionError(RenderingError): ...       # provider (5.3.3)
class ProviderTimeoutError(RenderingError): ...          # transient (5.4.6)
class AssetValidationError(RenderingError): ...          # validator (5.6.1)
class QualityBelowThresholdError(RenderingError): ...    # quality (5.6.2)
class BudgetExhaustedError(RenderingError): ...          # budget (5.2.3/5.4.1)
```

Propagation rules:

| Error                        | Failure class | Routing (owner)                          |
| ---------------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `ProviderTimeoutError`       | transient     | retry ≤ 3, then Manual Review (5.4.5)    |
| `ProviderSubmissionError`    | transient/permanent | classify → retry or Dead Letter (5.4.5) |
| `AssetValidationError`       | transient/permanent | retry (corrupt) or Dead Letter (5.4.5)  |
| `QualityBelowThresholdError` | quality       | retry, then Manual Review (5.6.2)        |
| `BudgetExhaustedError`       | budget        | Manual Review, never partial trailer     |
| `StoryboardNotApprovedError` | permanent     | reject build; never render               |

The facade catches typed errors, records them to the manifest, and routes per the owning classification section. It never swallows an error into a silent partial success (Principle 5).

---

# Events

The public surface emits composition-level events (component-internal events belong to each owning section, e.g. **5.4.1**, **5.7.1**).

```yaml
rendering_platform_events:
  - build_render_started
  - build_render_completed
  - build_render_partial          # some shots in MANUAL_REVIEW (fail-closed)
  - shot_render_ready
  - shot_render_manual_review
  - shot_cache_hit
  - provider_bound               # provider name -> adapter resolved
  - platform_health_degraded
```

Every event references `build_id`, `render_task_id` (where applicable), and the `contract_version`, and is durably recorded for audit and replay.

---

# Metrics

The facade surfaces composition-level metrics; detailed rendering metrics are owned by **5.7.1**.

```yaml
rendering_platform_metrics:
  build_render_duration
  shots_total
  shots_ready
  shots_cached
  shots_manual_review
  cache_hit_rate            # target >= 70% (5.6.3)
  quality_pass_rate
  facade_error_rate
```

These feed the SLO review alongside the queue and observability metrics of **5.4.1** and **5.7.1**.

---

# Security & Secret Boundaries

The public surface operates under least privilege.

* Provider credentials are injected at provider construction from a secret store, never passed through the facade's public methods and never through queue items (**5.4.1**).
* The facade accepts references (storyboard_ref, strategy_ref), not raw creative content; it resolves what it needs from authoritative stores.
* Downstream consumers receive `RenderResult` asset URIs, not provider tokens or internal handles.
* No public method exposes a write path into the Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard (RIF-308).
* All build-level events are audited for provenance.

A caller receives only the outcome it needs; internal components and secrets never cross the public boundary.

---

# Configuration Model

```yaml id="rif-config"
rendering_platform_interface:
  contract_version: "5.7.2-v1"
  facade:
    max_parallel_shots: 8            # aligns with 5.4.1 global_max_active_renders
    fail_closed: true                # partial builds never publish
  provider_registry:
    runway: "RunwayProvider"         # 5.3.4 adapter bound to "runway"
    # future: veo, luma, pika, openai_video, hunyuan (Principle 2)
  quality_gate:
    minimum_score: 0.90              # owned by 5.6.2
  cache:
    enabled: true                    # owned by 5.6.3
  injection:
    require_all_contracts: true      # startup fails closed if any contract unbound
```

Configuration is validated at startup. If any required contract is unbound, or the provider registry has no entry for a task's provider, the platform fails closed and refuses to render rather than dispatching with an unsafe default.

---

# Testing Requirements

## Unit Tests

* facade injects and orders all seven collaborators; pipeline ordering asserted with doubles;
* each interface exercised in isolation with contract-conformant doubles (RIF-306);
* `RenderPlanner` determinism and decision-matrix strategy assignment;
* `PromptAssembler` determinism, no-article-text, forbidden-style negative prompt;
* `RenderCache` key determinism, hit/miss, provider_version sensitivity;
* `AssetValidator` pass/fail per technical check;
* `RenderQualityGate` 0.90 threshold gating;
* typed error construction and failure_class assignment.

## Integration Tests

* full pipeline with a `FakeProvider`: plan → cache-miss → assemble → submit → status → download → validate → quality → ready;
* cache-hit path bypasses queue and provider entirely;
* Runway adapter conforms to `VideoProvider` against a mocked Runway API (**5.3.4**);
* provider swap: replace `runway` registry entry with a second fake; no code above 5.3.4 changes;
* fail-closed aggregation: one sub-0.90 shot yields `all_ready == False` and a manual_review entry.

## Concurrency Tests

* parallel `render_shot` across shots never blocks on a single shot (Principle 3);
* facade cancellation concurrent with in-flight submission (routes through Control Queue).

## Failure Injection Tests

* `StoryboardNotApprovedError` on an unapproved request;
* provider timeout → transient classification → retry then Manual Review;
* validation failure → classification → retry or Dead Letter;
* budget exhaustion → Manual Review, never partial trailer;
* unbound provider in registry → fail-closed startup refusal.

## Recovery Tests

* facade resumes a build from manifest after restart (queue reconstruction via 5.4.1);
* re-render of a single modified shot reuses cached siblings (Principle 4).

---

# Interface Quality Gate

```yaml
rendering_interface_quality_gate:
  single_facade_entry_enforced: true
  all_collaboration_via_contract_types: true
  no_component_depends_on_facade: true
  no_concrete_runway_reference_above_5_3_4: true
  every_interface_independently_testable: true
  every_method_maps_to_owning_section: true
  data_contracts_frozen_and_versioned: true
  cache_hit_bypasses_queue_and_provider: true
  quality_gate_threshold_is_0_90: true
  no_public_write_path_to_creative_plan: true
  typed_errors_carry_failure_class: true
  fail_closed_on_partial_build: true
  provider_registry_resolves_by_name: true
  startup_fails_closed_on_unbound_contract: true
```

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant RIF-401

All external access to rendering passes through the single `RenderingPlatform` facade; internal components are not part of the external public surface.

## Invariant RIF-402

Components collaborate only through contract types; no component references `RunwayProvider` (or any concrete provider) by type above **5.3.4**.

## Invariant RIF-403

A cache-hit render task bypasses the queue and the provider entirely; `RenderCache.lookup` precedes `QueueManager.admit`.

## Invariant RIF-404

Every public method maps to exactly one owning section that specifies its behaviour; no behaviour is defined in two places.

## Invariant RIF-405

Data contracts crossing an interface are frozen, versioned objects; a field's meaning is immutable within a contract version.

## Invariant RIF-406

Every interface is exercisable in isolation with all collaborators replaced by contract-conformant doubles.

## Invariant RIF-407

Dependencies point inward toward contracts; no component depends on the facade, and the provider is reached only through the generic contract.

## Invariant RIF-408

No public method exposes a write path into the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard; rendering is read-only over the creative plan.

## Invariant RIF-409

Every interface method either satisfies its postcondition or raises a typed error carrying a `failure_class` routable per **5.4.5**; no partial success is returned as success.

## Invariant RIF-410

A build with any shot below the 0.90 quality threshold after retries never publishes; it reports `all_ready == False` and escalates to Manual Review (Fail Closed).

## Invariant RIF-411

Provider selection is resolved by name through the provider registry; binding Runway or any future provider changes only the registry entry.

## Invariant RIF-412

Startup fails closed if any required component contract is unbound or a required provider is unregistered.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                   |
| ------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-587 | The Rendering Platform exposes a single facade (`RenderingPlatform`); all internal components are private and injected.     |
| ADR-588 | Components collaborate exclusively through contract types; concrete providers are reached only via the generic `VideoProvider` contract. |
| ADR-589 | Every public method maps to exactly one owning section; interfaces are seams, behaviour lives in the owning section.        |
| ADR-590 | Interface data contracts are frozen, versioned objects; additive changes bump minor, breaking changes bump major versions. |
| ADR-591 | Every interface is independently testable with contract-conformant doubles; untestable-in-isolation is a contract defect.  |
| ADR-592 | Providers are resolved by name through a registry, making Runway swappable without changes above **5.3.4** (Principle 2).   |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.7.2** is complete when:

* The eight public interfaces — `RenderingPlatform`, `RenderPlanner`, `QueueManager`, `PromptAssembler`, `VideoProvider`/`RunwayProvider`, `AssetValidator`, `RenderQualityGate`, `RenderCache` — are each specified with responsibility, public methods, inputs, outputs, and integration contract.
* Every interface maps to its owning behavioural section (QueueManager→5.4.1, RenderPlanner→5.2, RunwayProvider→5.3.4 with contract 5.3.2, AssetValidator→5.6.1, RenderCache→5.6.3, PromptAssembler→5.5.1, RenderQualityGate→5.6.2, RenderingPlatform facade over all).
* The canonical integration pipeline is defined with per-arrow preconditions and postconditions, ordered plan → cache → assemble → submit → monitor → download → validate → quality-gate → store/compose.
* The public API surface distinguishes upstream-callable facade methods from private internal components, and downstream consumers read only `RenderResult`/`RenderBuildResult`.
* Dependency boundaries point inward toward contracts, providers are resolved by name, and no component depends on the facade or on Runway concretely.
* Every interface is shown to be independently testable with contract-conformant doubles, and the fail-closed fault-propagation contract routes typed errors per the owning classification sections.
* Interface versioning, security boundaries, configuration, events, metrics, testing, quality gate, invariants, and ADRs provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the Rendering Platform's public surface as a set of independently testable, contract-bound components composed behind a single fail-closed facade.


---

# Part 5.7.3 — Part 5 Acceptance Criteria & Transition to Part 6

## Insynergy Cinematic Thought Leadership Platform

### Master Specification v2.1

---

# Purpose

This section closes **Part 5 — Rendering Platform & Runway Integration** in its entirety and defines the formal transition into **Part 6 — Performance Architecture**.

Every earlier section of Part 5 closed a *group*: 5.1.6 froze the foundation, 5.2.4 froze the decisioning layer, 5.3.5 closed the provider boundary, 5.4.7 froze execution control, 5.5.6 froze prompt assembly, and 5.6.6 freezes validation, caching, and build profiles. This section closes the **Part**. It is the single, authoritative statement of what "Part 5 is complete" means, and the single channel through which the completed Rendering Platform hands its guarantees to the performance layer.

This section performs five jobs:

* it **states the complete Part 5 acceptance criteria** — every original criterion from the source specification (original **5.31**), preserved verbatim in intent and expanded into a testable, section-anchored obligation;
* it **provides a Part-wide compliance checklist** that aggregates the six group checklists into one boolean gate for the whole Part;
* it **consolidates the invariant families** of Part 5 — FOUND, STRAT, PROV, EXEC, INJECT, and VALID — into one reference index, so Part 6 and every later part cite a single rendering boundary;
