* it **defines the Part 5 → Part 6 transition** — how the rendering execution layer feeds the performance goals of Part 6: build-time budgets, the ≥ 70 % cache-hit objective, and shot-level parallelism;
* it **states the closing acceptance** under which Part 5 is declared complete and frozen, and the interface to Part 6 is declared open.

This section is a **synthesis and transition contract**. It introduces no new rendering mechanism. Where it appears to state a rule, that rule is a consolidation of a rule already established in 5.1 through 5.7.2, restated here so that Part 6 references one authoritative rendering boundary. It never contradicts an origin section; where a fact was fixed upstream it is cited, not re-derived.

Part 5 is the **execution layer** of the platform. It converts approved Storyboard artifacts into validated cinematic assets and never authors creative content. Part 6 measures, budgets, and optimises the platform's throughput — including the rendering work Part 5 performs — but may never relax a Part 5 invariant to meet a performance target. This section fixes that relationship before any performance optimisation is designed.

---

# Scope

This section specifies:

* the complete, comprehensive Part 5 acceptance criteria (PTA-311..321), each preserving an original criterion and expanding it into a section-anchored, testable obligation;
* the Part-wide compliance checklist aggregating the six group gates;
* the consolidated Part 5 invariant families — FOUND-1..5, STRAT-1..5, PROV-1..5, EXEC-1..7, INJECT-1..6, and VALID-1..7 — as one normative index;
* the Part 5 quality gate (the part-level boolean gate);
* the **Part 5 → Part 6 transition contract**: the rendering telemetry, budgets, cache-hit contribution, and parallelism guarantees Part 5 hands to Part 6;
* the obligations Part 6 inherits and must never violate;
* the assumptions Part 6 must **not** make;
* the closing acceptance, invariants (PTA-401..406), and decision records (ADR-593..596) that freeze Part 5.

This section does **not** specify:

* rendering foundations, boundary, or principles (defined in **5.1**);
* render strategy, the decision matrix, or cost-aware selection (defined in **5.2**);
* provider independence, the interface contract, submission, or the Runway adapter (defined in **5.3**);
* the queue, parallel execution, the state machine, the manifest, retry, or timeout mechanics (defined in **5.4**);
* prompt assembly or character/location/camera/style injection (defined in **5.5**);
* asset validation, visual quality validation, the render cache, or preview/final profiles (defined in **5.6**);
* rendering observability internals or the public interface contracts (defined in **5.7.1** and **5.7.2**);
* performance goals, budgets, parallel-build execution, cost control, or caching architecture (defined in **Part 6**).

This section consumes the outputs of every Part 5 group; it re-derives nothing and contradicts nothing. It inherits every invariant family from the group interface sections and never weakens them.

---

# Governing Principle

The platform adopts the following principle for the Part-level closure and transition.

> **Part 5 decides whether a render is correct; Part 6 decides how fast and how cheaply that correct render is produced — and speed is never bought by lowering correctness.**

Performance is an optimisation over an already-correct execution layer. Part 6 may eliminate work, reuse validated assets, parallelise independent shots, and reduce provider latency — but every path it takes must land on an asset Part 5 would have accepted anyway. The correctness boundary drawn across Part 5 is fixed before the first optimisation is designed.

A corollary follows directly:

> **An optimisation that produces a result Part 5 would reject is not a speed-up — it is a regression that happens to be faster.**

The transition is one-directional. If a performance improvement would require rendering asynchronously *less* strictly, binding to a specific provider, issuing more than one task per shot, making scheduling nondeterministic, passing article text to a provider, skipping validation, publishing a substandard asset, or re-rendering an unchanged validated shot, the improvement is rejected at the boundary — not accommodated. Part 6's own constraints (Story First, Determinism, Provider Independence, Incrementality, Observability) are the restatement, at the performance layer, of the invariant families frozen here.

---

# Architectural Position

```text
        PART 5 — Rendering Platform & Runway Integration
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  5.1  Rendering Architecture & Foundations   (FOUND) │
        │  5.2  Render Strategy & Decisioning          (STRAT) │
        │  5.3  Provider Abstraction & Runway          (PROV)  │
        │  5.4  Render Queue & Execution Control       (EXEC)  │
        │  5.5  Prompt Assembly & Consistency          (INJECT)│
        │  5.6  Validation, Cache & Build Profiles     (VALID) │
        │  5.7.1  Rendering Observability & Metrics            │
        │  5.7.2  Public Interfaces & Integration Contracts    │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                 ┌─────────────────────┐
                 │   5.7.3  TRANSITION  │   ◄── this section (FINAL)
                 │  (Part 5 frozen)     │
                 └─────────────────────┘
                            │
        validated assets + render telemetry + budgets
        cache-hit contribution + parallelism guarantees
                            │
                            ▼
        PART 6 — Performance Architecture
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  6.1.1  Performance Goals & SLOs                     │
        │  6.1.2  Architectural Performance Principles          │
        │  6.1.3  Caching & Work Elimination                    │
        │  6.1.4  Parallel Execution Model                      │
        │  6.1.x  Cost & Budget Control                         │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Part 5 is upstream of every performance decision that concerns rendering. The transition defined here is the sole channel through which the rendering layer's guarantees, telemetry, and cost characteristics reach the performance layer. Part 6 receives a **frozen, correct execution layer**; it optimises the layer's throughput, it does not redefine the layer's correctness.

---

# Responsibilities

This section is responsible for:

* aggregating and preserving every original Part 5 acceptance criterion into one comprehensive, testable set;
* aggregating the six group compliance checklists into one Part-wide gate;
* consolidating the six invariant families into one authoritative index;
* defining the Part 5 → Part 6 transition contract, including the rendering telemetry and budget contributions Part 6 depends on;
* stating the obligations Part 6 inherits and the assumptions it must not make;
* declaring, through the closing acceptance, that Part 5 is complete and frozen.

This section is **not** responsible for:

* defining any rendering mechanism (owned by 5.1–5.7.2);
* defining performance goals, budgets, or optimisations (owned by **Part 6**);
* re-opening any group decision frozen by 5.1.6, 5.2.4, 5.3.5, 5.4.7, 5.5.6, or 5.6.6;
* introducing a new invariant that any group did not already establish (it consolidates; it does not legislate anew).

---

# Design Objectives

This section shall satisfy the following objectives. Each objective is normative.

## PTA-301 — Completeness of Acceptance

The Part 5 acceptance criteria stated here shall be **complete**: every criterion from the original specification (original **5.31**) is preserved and expanded, and no group's acceptance is omitted. A criterion that cannot be traced to a Part 5 section is prohibited; an original criterion that is dropped is a defect.

## PTA-302 — Testability

Every acceptance criterion shall be **testable** — reducible to a check a build system or Codex can execute, anchored to the section that owns the mechanism. A criterion that cannot be verified through telemetry or a test is not an acceptance criterion.

## PTA-303 — Invariant Consolidation

The six invariant families shall be consolidated into one index that **cites, never mutates** their origin sections. The consolidation preserves every invariant exactly; it adds no invariant and weakens none.

## PTA-304 — Jointly Binding Closure

All Part 5 invariants shall be **jointly binding across the whole Part**: no group, provider, optimisation, or configuration may satisfy an invariant in one family by breaking one in another. Part 5 is accepted only as a whole.

## PTA-305 — One-Directional Transition

The Part 5 → Part 6 transition shall be **one-directional**: Part 6 consumes a frozen, correct rendering layer plus its telemetry and budgets, and may not push any change back into a Part 5 mechanism. Performance reads rendering; it does not rewrite it.

## PTA-306 — Correctness Dominates Performance

The transition shall establish that **correctness dominates performance**: every Part 6 optimisation must land on a result Part 5 would accept. No performance target may be met by relaxing a rendering invariant. This is the Part-level statement of Fail Closed (FOUND-5).

## PTA-307 — Measurability of Contribution

Rendering's contribution to the performance goals — cache-hit rate, render/queue/download/validation timings, retry and failure rates, parallel throughput — shall be **measurable and already emitted** by the observability layer (**5.7.1**), so Part 6 can budget and optimise against real telemetry rather than estimates (Part 6 Constraint 5 — Observability).

---

# The Complete Part 5 Acceptance Criteria

The original specification stated eleven acceptance criteria for Part 5 (original **5.31**). Each is **preserved** here and **expanded** into a section-anchored, invariant-referenced, testable obligation. The original wording is retained as the lead sentence of each criterion; the expansion states where the mechanism lives and how the criterion is verified. Part 5 is complete only when **all** of the following hold.

## PTA-311 — Rendering Is Fully Asynchronous

*Original: "Rendering is fully asynchronous."*

Every provider submission returns immediately with a `provider_task_id`; no caller above the provider boundary blocks on render completion. Completion is observed only through delayed-visibility polling (`poll_interval: 20s`) and manifest updates.

* **Owned by:** 5.3.3 (submission), 5.4.1 (delayed-visibility monitor), 5.4.6 (poll/timeout policy).
* **Invariants:** FOUND-3 (asynchronous execution), PROV-5 (asynchronous submission), EXEC-6 (bounded timeout, delayed-visibility polling).
* **Verified by:** `submit()` returns before completion; no worker awaits a rendered asset inline; the pipeline never waits for one shot before submitting another.

## PTA-312 — Providers Are Abstracted Behind a Common Interface

*Original: "Providers are abstracted behind a common interface."*

Every provider implements exactly `submit()`, `get_status()`, `download()`, `cancel()`, `health_check()`. No provider-specific logic — endpoint, payload shape, authentication, throttle signal, error code — leaks above the adapter boundary, and no component branches on provider identity.

* **Owned by:** 5.3.1 (independence), 5.3.2 (contract), 5.3.4 (Runway adapter).
* **Invariants:** FOUND-2 (provider independence), PROV-1 (interchangeable providers), PROV-2 (no provider-logic leakage).
* **Verified by:** substituting the Runway adapter for a stub requires no change to strategy, queue, state machine, manifest, or validation; the five contract methods are the only surface any other layer calls.

## PTA-313 — Every Shot Generates Exactly One Render Task

*Original: "Every shot generates exactly one render task."*

Each approved shot produces exactly one render task submitted to exactly one provider per attempt. A shot is never fanned out to multiple concurrent provider tasks for the same attempt; a retry produces one replacement task with a new handle, not a duplicate of the old one.

* **Owned by:** 5.3.3 (one task per shot), 5.4.5 (retry produces one replacement), 5.2.1 (one strategy per shot).
* **Invariants:** STRAT-1 (one strategy per shot), PROV-4 (one task per shot), EXEC-5 (retry produces one replacement task).
* **Verified by:** every shot in `shot-list.json` maps to exactly one submitted task and one `provider_task_id` per attempt; a shot with zero or multiple tasks is rejected before submission.

## PTA-314 — Queue State Transitions Are Deterministic

*Original: "Queue state transitions are deterministic."*

Given identical queue contents, priority scores, plan, and timestamps, dequeue order and every render-task state transition are deterministic and reproducible. Random scheduling is prohibited. The canonical lifecycle is **NEW → SUBMITTED → RUNNING → DOWNLOADING → VALIDATING → READY → COMPOSED**, with the failure path **FAILED → RETRY → FAILED → MANUAL REVIEW**; the scheduling lifecycle **Pending → Queued → Submitted → Running → Completed → Validated** frames the same progression.

* **Owned by:** 5.4.1 (deterministic ordering), 5.4.2 (parallel determinism), 5.4.3 (state machine).
* **Invariants:** STRAT-2 (deterministic decision), EXEC-3 (deterministic ordering & execution), EXEC-4 (parallel isolation).
* **Verified by:** two runs over identical inputs produce byte-identical dequeue order and transitions; parallel interleaving never changes the resulting set of outputs (consistent with Part 6 Constraint 2 — Determinism).

## PTA-315 — Render Prompts Are Assembled Exclusively From Storyboard Artifacts

*Original: "Render prompts are assembled exclusively from storyboard artifacts."*

Every prompt is assembled deterministically from the fixed structure **Character + Location + Camera + Lighting + Blocking + Emotion + Shot Purpose + Visual Style**, drawn only from approved storyboard artifacts. The provider never receives source article text. Prompt assembly authors no creative content.

* **Owned by:** 5.5.1 (deterministic assembly), 5.4.7 (prompt is a submission precondition).
* **Invariants:** FOUND-1 (execution-only boundary), INJECT-1 (deterministic prompt), INJECT-2 (no article text to provider), EXEC-3/R4A-404 (prompt determinism is a correctness precondition).
* **Verified by:** two assembly passes over identical inputs yield byte-identical prompts and identical `prompt_hash`; no prompt contains article text; a task without a deterministic prompt is not admissible for submission.

## PTA-316 — Character, Location, and Camera Consistency Are Automatically Injected

*Original: "Character, location, and camera consistency are automatically injected."*

Characters and locations are referenced by ID and injected automatically from `character-bible.yaml` and `location-bible.yaml`; camera parameters (lens, movement, angle) originate only from the Shot Planner's `camera-plan.json`. Locations are immutable; the same ID yields the same identity across every shot. Providers may not override any injected value.

* **Owned by:** 5.5.2 (character), 5.5.3 (location), 5.5.4 (camera).
* **Invariants:** INJECT-3 (identity from authoritative sources), INJECT-4 (providers cannot override injected values).
* **Verified by:** every injected description resolves from its bible by ID; no identity is authored in the render layer; a provider attempt to alter lens, movement, angle, character, or location is rejected as a non-compliant asset.

## PTA-317 — Asset Validation Rejects Technically Invalid Outputs

*Original: "Asset validation rejects technically invalid outputs."*

Every downloaded asset is validated for resolution, duration, codec, frame rate, decode success, and file integrity. A technically invalid asset is rejected and never advances toward composition.

* **Owned by:** 5.6.1 (asset validation).
* **Invariants:** VALID-1 (technical asset validation), FOUND-5 (fail closed).
* **Verified by:** an asset failing any technical check is rejected; an invalid asset is never marked READY; rejection routes to retry classification (5.4.5) or Manual Review, never to publication.

## PTA-318 — Visual Quality Validation Blocks Substandard Renders

*Original: "Visual Quality Validation blocks substandard renders."*

Every asset that passes technical validation is scored for human/character consistency, continuity, framing, lighting, camera movement, motion artifacts, and visual coherence against the injected identity. An asset scoring below `minimum_score: 0.90` is blocked and never published.

* **Owned by:** 5.6.2 (visual quality validation), judged against the 5.5 prompt anchor.
* **Invariants:** VALID-2 (visual quality threshold 0.90), INJECT-6 (prompt is the identity anchor), FOUND-5 (fail closed).
* **Verified by:** an asset below the minimum score is rejected; validation judges the asset against the injected identity and `prompt_hash`, never against a re-derived identity; a style-blocklist match is a hard rejection.

## PTA-319 — Cache Prevents Unnecessary Regeneration

*Original: "Cache prevents unnecessary regeneration."*

A render cache key `sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile)` identifies each render deterministically. A matching cache entry skips rendering entirely; a cache-hit task bypasses the queue and is never submitted to a provider. Only modified shots are rendered; previously validated assets are reused.

* **Owned by:** 5.6.3 (cache & reuse), 5.4.1 (cache elimination precedes queueing).
* **Invariants:** FOUND-4 (incremental rendering), VALID-3 (cache identity & reuse), EXEC-2 (idempotent, cache-keyed effect), INJECT-6 (prompt hash feeds the cache key).
* **Verified by:** the same unchanged task yields the same `cache_key` and the second submission is eliminated by cache; a cache-hit task is never enqueued for provider submission (consistent with Part 6 Constraint 4 — Incrementality).

## PTA-320 — Preview and Final Rendering Profiles Are Independently Configurable

*Original: "Preview and Final rendering profiles are independently configurable."*

Preview (`runway_preview`) and Final (`runway_final`) profiles are configured independently. Preview validates pacing and continuity at minimised cost with lower fidelity acceptable; Final renders at full fidelity and occurs only after preview approval, with quality taking precedence over speed. Neither profile is silently upgraded or downgraded at execution time.

* **Owned by:** 5.6.4 (preview profile), 5.6.5 (final profile).
* **Invariants:** VALID-4 (profile separation), STRAT-3 (cost-priority order), R2A-403 (5.3 executes the assigned profile, never changing it).
* **Verified by:** preview and final profiles are configured and selected independently; final rendering is gated on preview approval; an execution-time attempt to change the render profile for cost or speed is rejected.

## PTA-321 — The Rendering Platform Operates Solely as an Execution Layer

*Original: "The Rendering Platform operates solely as an execution layer and never modifies the approved story, screenplay, or storyboard."*

The platform converts an approved visual plan into validated assets and authors no creative content at any layer. It never generates or re-times story or pacing, never creates or edits dialogue, and never designs or overrides camera language. Approved inputs are read-only; the platform's outputs are its exclusive product.

* **Owned by:** 5.1.1 (rendering boundary), enforced at every downstream layer (5.3.1, 5.5.1, 5.6.2).
* **Invariants:** FOUND-1 (execution-only boundary), PROV-3 (providers never make creative decisions), and its restatement at every layer.
* **Verified by:** an approved input file is unmodified after a full render pass; no rendering component authors story, pacing, dialogue, or camera language; a provider or prompt attempt to author creative content is rejected (consistent with Part 6 Constraint 1 — Story First).

---

# Part-Wide Compliance Checklist

Before Part 5 is frozen and the interface to Part 6 is declared open, every group gate and every Part-level criterion below must hold. This checklist aggregates the six group compliance gates (5.1.6, 5.2.4, 5.3.5, 5.4.7, 5.5.6, 5.6.6) into one Part-wide gate.

```yaml
part_5_compliance:
  # Group gates (each already fails closed internally)
  group_5_1_foundation_frozen: true        # 5.1.6
  group_5_2_decisioning_frozen: true       # 5.2.4
  group_5_3_provider_boundary_closed: true # 5.3.5
  group_5_4_execution_controls_frozen: true# 5.4.7
  group_5_5_assembly_frozen: true          # 5.5.6
  group_5_6_validation_frozen: true        # 5.6.6

  # Part-level acceptance criteria (PTA-311..321)
  rendering_fully_asynchronous: true       # PTA-311
  providers_abstracted_common_interface: true # PTA-312
  one_render_task_per_shot: true           # PTA-313
  queue_transitions_deterministic: true    # PTA-314
  prompts_from_storyboard_artifacts_only: true # PTA-315
  character_location_camera_auto_injected: true # PTA-316
  asset_validation_rejects_invalid: true   # PTA-317
  visual_quality_blocks_substandard: true  # PTA-318
  cache_prevents_regeneration: true        # PTA-319
  preview_final_profiles_independent: true # PTA-320
  execution_layer_never_modifies_creative: true # PTA-321

  # Transition readiness
  render_telemetry_emitted: true           # 5.7.1
  public_interfaces_testable: true         # 5.7.2
  invariant_families_consolidated: true    # FOUND/STRAT/PROV/EXEC/INJECT/VALID
  correctness_dominates_performance: true  # PTA-306
```

Every field must be `true`. A single `false` blocks the freeze: the interface to Part 6 does not open until the Rendering Platform is whole. This mirrors the Fail-Closed posture of FOUND-5 applied to the specification itself, at Part scope.

---

# Consolidated Part 5 Invariant Families

The six invariant families established across Part 5 are consolidated here as one index, so Part 6 and every later part reference a single rendering boundary. Each family is authoritative in its origin section; this index **cites, never mutates**. The families are **jointly binding across the whole Part** (PTA-304): no member of one family may be satisfied by breaking a member of another.

## FOUND — Foundational Invariants (5.1.6)

The frozen frame under which the entire platform operates.

* **FOUND-1 — Execution-Only Boundary.** The platform never modifies the approved Story, Screenplay, or Storyboard; it authors no creative content.
* **FOUND-2 — Provider Independence.** No component upstream of the adapter boundary depends on a specific provider.
* **FOUND-3 — Asynchronous Execution.** Rendering never blocks the pipeline; all provider execution is asynchronous.
* **FOUND-4 — Incremental Rendering.** Only modified shots are rendered; a cache-matching shot reuses its validated asset.
* **FOUND-5 — Fail Closed.** If rendering quality cannot satisfy validation, no publishable trailer is produced.

## STRAT — Strategy Invariants (5.2.4)

The settled decisioning plan, operating inside FOUND.

* **STRAT-1 — One Strategy Per Shot.** Exactly one render strategy per shot; mixed strategies prohibited.
* **STRAT-2 — Deterministic Decision.** Identical shot intent yields an identical strategy; no randomness.
* **STRAT-3 — Cost-Priority Order.** Cache → Animated Still → Motion Graphics → Runway; cheapest viable strategy first.
* **STRAT-4 — Story-First Over Cost.** Cost never overrides narrative need; STRAT-4 constrains STRAT-3, never the reverse.
* **STRAT-5 — Provider-Independent Decision Surface.** The plan names strategy classes only; no provider endpoint, credential, model version, or rate limit appears in it.

## PROV — Provider Invariants (5.3.5)

The closed provider boundary, operating inside FOUND and STRAT.

* **PROV-1 — Interchangeable Providers.** Substituting a provider is an adapter-only change.
* **PROV-2 — No Provider Logic Leakage.** Provider specifics stay private to the adapter; nothing above the boundary branches on provider identity.
* **PROV-3 — Providers Never Make Creative Decisions.** A provider renders; it authors no story, pacing, dialogue, or camera language.
* **PROV-4 — One Task Per Shot.** One shot, one task, one `provider_task_id` per attempt.
* **PROV-5 — Asynchronous Submission.** `submit()` returns immediately with an opaque handle; completion is observed by polling.

## EXEC — Execution-Control Invariants (5.4.7)

The frozen control layer, operating inside FOUND, STRAT, and PROV.

* **EXEC-1 — Manifest Is Authoritative.** The Render Manifest is the single source of truth; persistence precedes acknowledgement; the manifest wins on disagreement.
* **EXEC-2 — At-Least-Once Delivery, Idempotent Effect.** Transport is at-least-once; consumers are idempotent; exactly-once effect is cache-keyed; a render is never issued twice for the same validated result.
* **EXEC-3 — Deterministic Ordering & Execution.** Ordering and transitions are reproducible; parallel interleaving never changes the result set.
* **EXEC-4 — Parallel Isolation.** Tasks execute in parallel, bounded and isolated; one task never blocks or corrupts another.
* **EXEC-5 — Bounded Retry & Failure Classification.** `max_retry: 3` with exponential backoff; only retryable failures are retried; a retry produces one replacement task.
* **EXEC-6 — Bounded Timeout & Deadline.** `submission_timeout 30s`, `poll_interval 20s`, `download_timeout 120s`, `overall_timeout 45m`; delayed-visibility polling, never busy-wait.
* **EXEC-7 — Fail Closed to Manual Review.** Exhaustion, non-retryable failure, budget exhaustion, or unmet quality escalates to Manual Review; no partial or degraded published trailer.

## INJECT — Injection / Assembly Invariants (5.5.6)

The settled prompt-and-identity layer, operating inside FOUND.

* **INJECT-1 — Deterministic Prompt.** Identical shot inputs yield a byte-identical prompt.
* **INJECT-2 — No Article Text to Provider.** The prompt never contains source article text.
* **INJECT-3 — Identity From Authoritative Sources.** Character, location, and camera identity are injected only from the bibles and the camera plan.
* **INJECT-4 — Providers Cannot Override Injected Values.** A provider that alters an injected value produces a non-compliant asset.
* **INJECT-5 — Mandatory Style + Forbidden Blocklist.** Mandatory `[cinematic, editorial, enterprise, realistic, restrained]`; forbidden `[anime, cartoon, cyberpunk, fantasy, neon holograms, humanoid robots, exaggerated sci-fi interfaces]`.
* **INJECT-6 — Prompt Is the Identity Anchor.** The `prompt_hash` feeds the cache key and is the reference validation judges against; downstream layers read it, never regenerate it.

## VALID — Validation, Cache & Profile Invariants (5.6.6)

The validation and caching layer, operating inside FOUND, STRAT, PROV, EXEC, and INJECT. These are consolidated here as the Part-level statement of the invariants frozen by **5.6.6**.

* **VALID-1 — Technical Asset Validation.** Every downloaded asset is validated for resolution, duration, codec, frame rate, decode success, and file integrity; technically invalid assets are rejected (**5.6.1**).
* **VALID-2 — Visual Quality Threshold.** Every technically valid asset is scored for human consistency, continuity, framing, lighting, camera movement, motion artifacts, and visual coherence; an asset below `minimum_score: 0.90` is blocked (**5.6.2**).
* **VALID-3 — Cache Identity & Reuse.** The render cache key is `sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile)`; a matching entry skips rendering and bypasses the queue (**5.6.3**, FOUND-4).
* **VALID-4 — Profile Separation.** Preview (`runway_preview`) and final (`runway_final`) profiles are independently configurable; final rendering is gated on preview approval; profiles are never silently up- or down-graded at execution time (**5.6.4**, **5.6.5**).
* **VALID-5 — Fail Closed on Validation.** If a returned asset cannot be validated against the injected identity and mandatory style, it is escalated to Manual Review, never published (**5.6.2**, FOUND-5).
* **VALID-6 — Validation Judges Against the Anchor.** Validation measures the returned asset against the `prompt_hash` and injected identity; it never re-assembles the prompt or substitutes an identity to force a pass (**5.6.2**, INJECT-6).

These thirty-four invariants across six families are the **complete correctness surface of Part 5**. Part 6 inherits every one of them and may weaken none.

---

# Part 5 Quality Gate

```yaml
part_5_quality_gate:
  foundation_frozen_and_non_relaxable: true          # FOUND-1..5
  decisioning_settled_and_non_reopenable: true       # STRAT-1..5
  provider_boundary_closed_and_non_bypassable: true  # PROV-1..5
  execution_controls_frozen: true                    # EXEC-1..7
  assembly_frozen_and_non_reassemblable: true        # INJECT-1..6
  validation_and_cache_frozen: true                  # VALID-1..7
  all_invariant_families_jointly_binding: true       # PTA-304
  all_original_acceptance_criteria_preserved: true   # PTA-301
  every_criterion_testable_and_anchored: true        # PTA-302
  render_telemetry_available_for_performance: true   # PTA-307
  transition_is_one_directional: true                # PTA-305
  correctness_dominates_performance: true            # PTA-306
  no_later_part_may_relax_a_rendering_invariant: true
```

The gate passes only when Part 5 is complete **and** demonstrably non-relaxable by any later part. If Part 6 or a later part could satisfy its own goals by relaxing a rendering invariant — rendering less asynchronously, binding to a provider, issuing more than one task per shot, making scheduling nondeterministic, passing article text, skipping validation, publishing a substandard asset, or re-rendering an unchanged validated shot — the gate fails and the invariant is re-hardened here before proceeding.

---

# The Part 5 → Part 6 Transition Contract

Part 5 hands Part 6 a **frozen, correct execution layer plus the telemetry and cost characteristics needed to budget and optimise it.** The transition is defined as three explicit sets: the **handoff artifacts** Part 6 receives, the **guarantees** attached to them, and the **obligations** Part 6 inherits. Part 6's mission — end-to-end throughput, developer productivity, cloud cost, and operational predictability without compromising narrative integrity — is an optimisation over this frozen layer.

## Handoff Artifacts

Part 6 receives the validated rendering output plus the observability and cost surface it optimises against. It does not receive, and may not reach into, any rendering mechanism.

```yaml id="pta-handoff"
handoff_to_part_6:
  rendering_output:
    validated_assets: video_assets/           # only validation-passing assets (VALID-1,2)
    preview_assets: preview_assets/            # preview-profile output (VALID-4)
    thumbnail_assets: thumbnail_assets/
    render_manifest: render_manifest.json      # authoritative render-task state (EXEC-1)
    render_results: render_results.json
    metadata: metadata.json
  render_telemetry:                            # emitted by 5.7.1, per shot and per build
    submission_time:
    queue_wait:
    render_time:
    download_time:
    validation_time:
    provider_latency:
    cache_hit:                                 # feeds cache-hit-rate KPI (Part 6)
    retry_count:
    rendered_shot_count:
    cached_shot_count:
  cost_surface:
    cost_per_shot:                             # from cost-aware admission (5.2.3 / 5.4.1)
    build_render_budget:
    budget_consumed:
  parallelism_profile:
    shot_level_parallel: true                  # EXEC-4: independent shots run in parallel
    concurrency_bounds:                        # global / per-provider / per-class (5.4.1)
    deterministic_under_interleaving: true     # EXEC-3
  invariant_families:                          # frozen, non-negotiable
    - FOUND-1..5
    - STRAT-1..5
    - PROV-1..5
    - EXEC-1..7
    - INJECT-1..6
    - VALID-1..7
```

The **single most consequential handoff** for Part 6 is the pairing of `cache_hit` telemetry with the frozen incremental-rendering guarantee (FOUND-4, VALID-3): Part 6's headline performance philosophy — *"the fastest render is the one that never needs to be rendered"* — is realised entirely by rendering work Part 5 already eliminates. Part 6 measures and raises the cache-hit rate; it does not invent the cache, and it may never satisfy the cache-hit objective by weakening validation equality.

## Guarantees Provided to Part 6

When Part 6 begins, it may assume the following are already true. These are guarantees, not aspirations; they are enforced by the Part 5 quality gate before the transition opens.

```yaml id="pta-guarantees"
guarantees_to_part_6:
  assets_are_validated: true                # VALID-1,2: only accepted assets are emitted
  no_substandard_asset_published: true      # VALID-5 / FOUND-5
  rendering_is_deterministic: true          # EXEC-3, STRAT-2, INJECT-1: reproducible plans
  rendering_is_incremental: true            # FOUND-4, VALID-3: cache eliminates work
  rendering_is_parallel_and_isolated: true  # EXEC-4: shot-level parallelism, bounded
  submission_is_async: true                 # FOUND-3, PROV-5: rendering never blocks
  one_task_per_shot: true                   # PROV-4
  provider_independent: true                # FOUND-2, PROV-1,2
  telemetry_is_emitted: true                # 5.7.1: every stage measured
  interfaces_are_testable: true             # 5.7.2: each public interface independently testable
  manifest_is_authoritative: true           # EXEC-1: reconstructable state
  cost_surface_available: true              # 5.2.3 / 5.4.1: per-shot cost + build budget
```

The most consequential guarantees for Part 6 are `rendering_is_deterministic`, `rendering_is_incremental`, and `rendering_is_parallel_and_isolated` — because these three are precisely the top three items of Part 6's fixed optimisation order (Eliminate Work → Reuse Existing Work → Parallelize Remaining Work). Part 5 has already built the mechanisms; Part 6 tunes and budgets them.

## How Rendering Feeds the Performance Goals

Part 6 states quantitative goals; Part 5's frozen mechanisms are the substrate those goals are met on. The mapping is fixed and one-directional.

### Build-Time Budgets

Part 6 allocates a maximum execution budget to each pipeline stage and warns on overrun. Rendering owns several of these budgeted stages, and Part 5 already enforces bounds that fit inside them.

| Part 6 Budgeted Stage | Part 6 Budget | Part 5 Mechanism (bound) |
| --------------------- | ------------- | ------------------------ |
| Render Queue Submission | ≤ 120 s (≤ 2 min) | `submission_timeout: 30s` per task; async submission returns immediately (5.4.6, 5.3.3) |
| Provider Rendering | external | delayed-visibility polling `poll_interval: 20s`, `overall_timeout: 45m` (5.4.6) |
| Download | ≤ 300 s | `download_timeout: 120s` per asset; parallel download class (5.4.6, 5.4.1) |
| Validation | ≤ 120 s | asset + visual quality validation under bounded lease (5.6.1, 5.6.2, 5.4.1) |

Part 6 rolls these stage budgets into the build-time objectives **Preview Build Time ≤ 25 min** and **Final Build Time ≤ 35 min**. Part 5 guarantees that each rendering stage advances under a bounded, configurable deadline (EXEC-6), so a build never stalls indefinitely inside rendering; a task that cannot meet its deadline is failed and classified (5.4.5), never left hanging. Part 6 may tighten these budgets; it may not remove the deadlines, because unbounded rendering would make the build-time SLO unenforceable.

### Cache Hit Rate ≥ 70 %

Part 6 sets an SLO of **`cache_hit_rate: >= 70%`**. This objective is met entirely by Part 5's incremental-rendering and cache mechanisms.

* The render cache key `sha256(shot_hash + provider + provider_version + render_profile)` (VALID-3, **5.6.3**) gives every render a deterministic identity; the same unchanged shot always hits.
* A cache-hit task bypasses the queue entirely and is never submitted to a provider (FOUND-4, EXEC-2, **5.4.1**), so a hit costs no provider time or budget.
* The `cache_hit`, `rendered_shot_count`, and `cached_shot_count` telemetry (**5.7.1**) let Part 6 compute the cache-hit ratio directly from emitted metrics (Part 6 Constraint 5 — Observability).

Part 6 raises the cache-hit rate by increasing the proportion of unchanged shots and by warming the cache across builds; it **may never** satisfy the ≥ 70 % objective by loosening cache equality — for example, by treating a different `prompt_hash`, provider version, or render profile as a hit. Cache identity is exact; a false hit would reuse the wrong asset and silently violate INJECT-6 and VALID-6. The cache-hit objective is a target for *legitimate* reuse, not a licence to reuse loosely.

### Parallelism

Part 6 mandates shot-level parallelism ("Parallelize independent work, never dependent work") and sets a parallel overall-build target of roughly 20–35 min against a sequential ~60–90 min. Part 5 already provides the parallel substrate.

* Rendering is fully parallel: the pipeline never waits for one shot before submitting another (EXEC-4, **5.4.2**).
* Parallelism is **bounded** globally, per provider, and per class, and respects provider rate limits (**5.4.1**), so throughput never violates PROV-1/PROV-5 or provider constraints.
* Parallel tasks are **isolated**: one task's failure, backlog, retry, or timeout never blocks or corrupts another (EXEC-4).
* Parallelism preserves determinism: given identical inputs, the result set is reproducible regardless of interleaving (EXEC-3, Part 6 Constraint 2).

Part 6 tunes concurrency to hit its build-time targets; it **may never** raise parallelism past the provider and class bounds Part 5 enforces, and it may never let interleaving change the result set. Parallelism is a throughput lever inside fixed safety and determinism bounds, not a way to exceed them.

## Obligations Part 6 Inherits

Part 6 accepts the transition by honouring the following obligations. Violating any of them is a boundary defect, not an optimisation.

* **Every optimisation shall land on a Part-5-acceptable result** (PTA-306). Eliminating, reusing, or parallelising work may not produce an asset validation would reject.
* **Optimisation shall not author or alter creative content** (FOUND-1, PROV-3, Part 6 Constraint 1 — Story First). Reducing shot count or re-timing to save render cost is prohibited unless approved through the upstream Story Quality Gates — not by the performance layer.
* **Optimisation shall preserve determinism** (EXEC-3, STRAT-2, INJECT-1, Part 6 Constraint 2). No performance decision may introduce random scheduling or a nondeterministic render plan.
* **Optimisation shall remain provider-independent** (FOUND-2, PROV-1, Part 6 Constraint 3). No optimisation may depend exclusively on Runway-specific behaviour.
* **Optimisation shall preserve incrementality with exact cache identity** (FOUND-4, VALID-3, Part 6 Constraint 4). A validated artifact is never regenerated without a material input change, and a cache hit is only ever an exact-key match.
* **Optimisation shall be measurable** (PTA-307, Part 6 Constraint 5 — Observability). An optimisation that cannot be evaluated through the rendering telemetry Part 5 emits shall not be adopted.

The state Part 6 receives — validated assets, an authoritative manifest, complete render telemetry, a cost surface, and a bounded parallel profile — is the pre-condition for every performance goal, budget, and optimisation defined in **Part 6**.

---

# Metrics — Rendering Telemetry Feeding Part 6

Part 6 treats performance as measurable (Constraint 5 — Observability) and requires that every optimisation be evaluable through telemetry. Part 5 already emits that telemetry (**5.7.1**). This section fixes the mapping so Part 6 computes its SLOs and KPIs from rendering metrics rather than estimates, and so no metric a Part 6 objective depends on is missing at the transition.

The mapping from Part 5 rendering metrics to Part 6 objectives is fixed:

| Part 5 Metric (5.7.1)        | Feeds Part 6 SLO / KPI            | Objective / Constraint |
| ---------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------- |
| `cache_hit`, `cached_shot_count`, `rendered_shot_count` | Cache Hit Ratio; `cache_hit_rate: >= 70%` | Eliminate Work (priority 1) |
| `render_time`, `provider_latency` | Average Provider Render Time     | Reduce Provider Latency (priority 4) |
| `queue_wait`, `submission_time` | Queue Submission Time; Render Queue Submission ≤ 2 min | build-time budget |
| `download_time`              | Average Download Time            | Download stage budget ≤ 300 s |
| `validation_time`            | Average Validation Time          | Validation stage budget ≤ 120 s |
| `retry_count`                | Retry Count per Build; `render_retry_rate: <= 5%` | operational predictability |
| render failure outcomes (5.4.5) | `failed_render_rate: <= 2%`   | operational predictability |
| `cost_per_shot`, `budget_consumed` (5.2.3 / 5.4.1) | Total Cost per Trailer        | cloud-cost objective |
| parallel throughput (5.4.2)  | Average Preview / Final Build Time | Parallelize Work (priority 3) |

Two properties of this mapping are load-bearing:

* **Every Part 6 rendering objective has a Part 5 metric.** No performance SLO or KPI that concerns rendering depends on a value the platform does not already emit. If a Part 6 objective referenced an unmeasured rendering quantity, the transition would be incomplete and the Part gate would fail (PTA-307).
* **The metrics are emitted before optimisation, not derived from it.** Part 6 optimises against telemetry that Part 5 produces during normal operation; an optimisation that cannot move one of these emitted metrics is, by Part 6 Constraint 5, not adopted.

All rendering metrics are recorded for historical trend analysis (**5.7.1**) and feed the SLO review. Persistent deviation from the ≥ 70 % cache-hit, ≤ 5 % retry, or ≤ 2 % failed-render objectives triggers architectural review at the Part 6 layer — never a relaxation of a Part 5 invariant to make the number look better.

---

# Transition Sequence

The transition is a strict, one-directional sequence. No performance step reaches back into a rendering mechanism.

```text
Part 5 frozen (all six families jointly binding)
        │
        ▼
validated assets + render_manifest ──┐
render telemetry (5.7.1) ────────────┤
cost surface (5.2.3 / 5.4.1) ────────┤
parallelism profile (5.4.2) ─────────┤
invariant families (FOUND..VALID) ───┤
                                     ▼
                        Part 6 Performance Architecture
                                     │
        1. Eliminate Work   ── uses FOUND-4 / VALID-3 cache (≥70% hit)
        2. Reuse Work       ── uses validated-asset reuse (VALID-1,2)
        3. Parallelize      ── uses EXEC-4 bounded parallelism
        4. Reduce Latency   ── tunes provider polling within EXEC-6 bounds
        5. Optimize Local   ── composition / packaging (outside Part 5)
                                     │
                                     ▼
        build-time SLO met on a correct execution layer
```

If a performance target cannot be met without relaxing a rendering invariant, Part 6 does **not** relax the invariant. It escalates — consistent with Fail Closed (FOUND-5) and Story First (Part 6 Constraint 1) — rather than trading correctness for speed. Rendering correctness is the floor beneath every performance number.

---

# What Part 6 Must Not Assume

The transition is generous with guarantees but strict about non-guarantees. Part 6 must **not** assume any of the following, because Part 5 deliberately did not grant them:

* **That it may lower a validation threshold to hit a build-time budget.** `minimum_score: 0.90` and the technical checks are fixed (VALID-1, VALID-2). A slow build is escalated or re-budgeted, never made faster by admitting a substandard asset (FOUND-5).
* **That a near-match may count as a cache hit.** The cache key is exact (VALID-3, INJECT-6). Treating a different `prompt_hash`, provider version, or profile as a hit to inflate the cache-hit rate is a correctness violation, not an optimisation.
* **That parallelism may exceed the provider or class bounds.** Concurrency is bounded and rate-limited (EXEC-4, **5.4.1**). Raising parallelism past those bounds to cut build time breaches PROV-5 and provider limits.
* **That it may reduce shot count or re-time pacing for speed.** Creative content is fixed upstream (FOUND-1, Part 6 Constraint 1). The performance layer never edits the plan; only the Story Quality Gates may.
* **That the queue or a cache is authoritative state.** The Render Manifest is authoritative (EXEC-1). Performance tooling reads telemetry and the manifest; it never treats a volatile store as truth.
* **That an optimisation need not be measured.** If an optimisation cannot be evaluated through emitted rendering telemetry, it is not adopted (Part 6 Constraint 5, PTA-307).

Confusing a non-guarantee for a guarantee is the most common way a performance layer silently breaches the rendering boundary. The transition names these explicitly so Part 6 cannot trade correctness for throughput by assumption.

---

# Transition Verification

The transition is verified — not merely asserted — before Part 5 is declared frozen. The following checks constitute the Part-level acceptance test surface.

## Acceptance Tests

* every original acceptance criterion (PTA-311..321) maps to a passing, section-anchored test;
* every Part-wide compliance field is `true`; a single `false` blocks the freeze (PTA-301, fail closed);
* no acceptance criterion depends on a mechanism outside Part 5.

## Invariant Tests

* each of the thirty-four invariants across the six families is exercised by at least one test in its origin group and re-asserted at Part scope (PTA-303);
* a constructed scenario in which one family's invariant is satisfied by breaking another's is rejected (PTA-304 joint binding);
* the consolidation contradicts no origin section (byte-comparable statements).

## Transition Tests

* the handoff exposes validated assets, an authoritative manifest, complete telemetry, a cost surface, and a bounded parallel profile — and nothing that reaches into a rendering mechanism (PTA-305);
* `cache_hit`, `rendered_shot_count`, and `cached_shot_count` are emitted and sufficient to compute the ≥ 70 % cache-hit rate (PTA-307);
* each rendering stage's bound fits inside its Part 6 stage budget (submission ≤ 120 s, download ≤ 300 s, validation ≤ 120 s).

## Correctness-Dominance Tests

* a simulated optimisation that would admit a sub-0.90 asset is rejected (PTA-306, VALID-2);
* a simulated cache "hit" on a mismatched key is rejected (VALID-3);
* a simulated parallelism increase past provider/class bounds is rejected (EXEC-4).

These tests give Codex an executable definition of "Part 5 is frozen and the transition to Part 6 is open": the interface opens only when all of them pass.

---

# Closing Quality Gate

```yaml
part_5_closing_gate:
  all_group_gates_pass: true                     # 5.1.6 / 5.2.4 / 5.3.5 / 5.4.7 / 5.5.6 / 5.6.6
  all_acceptance_criteria_pass: true             # PTA-311..321
  all_invariant_families_consolidated: true      # FOUND/STRAT/PROV/EXEC/INJECT/VALID
  all_invariant_families_jointly_binding: true   # PTA-304
  telemetry_sufficient_for_performance_slos: true# 5.7.1 / PTA-307
  transition_contract_one_directional: true      # PTA-305
  correctness_dominates_performance: true        # PTA-306
  build_time_budgets_have_rendering_bounds: true # EXEC-6 fits Part 6 stage budgets
  cache_hit_rate_mechanism_present: true         # FOUND-4 / VALID-3 → ≥70% SLO
  parallelism_bounded_and_deterministic: true    # EXEC-3 / EXEC-4
  part_5_frozen: true
  interface_to_part_6_open: true
```

The closing gate passes only when Part 5 is complete, all six families are jointly binding, the transition is one-directional, and correctness provably dominates performance. Any single `false` fails closed and blocks the freeze: Part 5 is not declared complete, and the interface to Part 6 does not open.

---

# Architectural Invariants

## Invariant PTA-401

Part 5 is a **frozen, correct execution layer**: no later part may relax rendering's asynchronous execution, provider independence, one-task-per-shot mapping, deterministic ordering, storyboard-only prompt assembly, automatic identity injection, technical or visual validation, incremental caching, profile separation, or the execution-only boundary.

## Invariant PTA-402

The six invariant families — FOUND-1..5, STRAT-1..5, PROV-1..5, EXEC-1..7, INJECT-1..6, VALID-1..7 — are **jointly binding across the whole Part**; no group, provider, optimisation, or configuration, in Part 5 or any later part, may satisfy an invariant in one family by breaking one in another.

## Invariant PTA-403

The complete Part 5 acceptance criteria (PTA-311..321) **preserve every original criterion** (original **5.31**) and expand each into a testable, section-anchored obligation; a dropped criterion or an untraceable criterion is a defect that fails the Part gate.

## Invariant PTA-404

The Part 5 → Part 6 transition is **one-directional**: Part 6 consumes validated assets, the authoritative manifest, render telemetry, a cost surface, and a bounded parallel profile, and optimises throughput against them; it mutates no rendering mechanism and pushes no change back into Part 5.

## Invariant PTA-405

**Correctness dominates performance**: every Part 6 optimisation must land on a result Part 5 would accept. No build-time budget, cache-hit objective, or parallelism target may be met by lowering a validation threshold, admitting a false cache hit, exceeding provider/class concurrency bounds, or altering creative content (FOUND-5, Part 6 Constraints 1–4).

## Invariant PTA-406

The Part-wide compliance checklist, the Part 5 quality gate, and the closing gate must all fully pass before Part 5 is declared frozen and the interface to Part 6 is declared open; a single failing field **fails closed** and blocks the freeze.

---

# Architectural Decision Records

| ADR     | Decision                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| ------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ADR-593 | Part 5 is closed by a single Part-level acceptance section that preserves every original acceptance criterion (original **5.31**) and expands each into a testable, section-anchored obligation (PTA-311..321); Part 5 is accepted only as a whole, and a dropped or untraceable criterion fails the Part gate. |
| ADR-594 | The six invariant families established across Part 5 — FOUND, STRAT, PROV, EXEC, INJECT, and VALID — are consolidated into one authoritative index that cites and never mutates its origin sections, and are declared jointly binding across the entire Part so no family may be satisfied by breaking another. |
| ADR-595 | The Part 5 → Part 6 transition is a one-directional contract in which the performance layer receives validated assets, the authoritative manifest, render telemetry, a cost surface, and a bounded parallel profile, and meets its build-time budgets, ≥ 70 % cache-hit SLO, and parallelism targets by tuning Part 5's frozen mechanisms — never by relaxing a rendering invariant. |
| ADR-596 | Correctness dominates performance: every Part 6 optimisation must land on a result Part 5 would accept, and any performance target that would require lowering a validation threshold, admitting a false cache hit, exceeding concurrency bounds, or altering creative content is a boundary defect, rejected and escalated (Fail Closed) rather than accommodated. |

---

# Acceptance Criteria

Section **5.7.3** is complete when:

* The complete Part 5 acceptance criteria are stated as one comprehensive set (PTA-311..321) that preserves every original criterion from the source specification — rendering fully asynchronous; providers abstracted behind a common interface; exactly one render task per shot; deterministic queue state transitions; prompts assembled exclusively from storyboard artifacts; character, location, and camera consistency auto-injected; asset validation rejecting technically invalid outputs; visual quality validation blocking substandard renders; cache preventing unnecessary regeneration; preview and final profiles independently configurable; and the platform operating solely as an execution layer that never modifies the approved story, screenplay, or storyboard — and expands each into a testable, section-anchored, invariant-referenced obligation.
* A Part-wide compliance checklist aggregates the six group gates (5.1.6, 5.2.4, 5.3.5, 5.4.7, 5.5.6, 5.6.6) and the eleven Part-level criteria into one boolean gate that fails closed on any single unmet field.
* The six invariant families — FOUND-1..5, STRAT-1..5, PROV-1..5, EXEC-1..7, INJECT-1..6, and VALID-1..7 — are consolidated into one authoritative index that cites and never mutates its origin sections and declares them jointly binding across the whole Part.
* The Part 5 → Part 6 transition is defined as three explicit sets — handoff artifacts, guarantees provided, and obligations inherited — and shows precisely how rendering feeds the performance goals: build-time budgets (each rendering stage bound fitting inside its Part 6 stage budget under EXEC-6), the ≥ 70 % cache-hit SLO (met by FOUND-4 / VALID-3 incremental caching and measured from emitted telemetry), and shot-level parallelism (EXEC-4 bounded, isolated, and deterministic).
* The assumptions Part 6 must not make — lowering a validation threshold for speed, counting a near-match as a cache hit, exceeding provider or class concurrency bounds, reducing shots or re-timing pacing for cost, treating a volatile store as authoritative, or adopting an unmeasurable optimisation — are named explicitly so the performance layer cannot trade correctness for throughput by assumption.
* The Part-wide checklist, the Part 5 quality gate, and the closing gate fully pass, and each fails closed on any single unmet field so Part 5 cannot be declared frozen — and the interface to Part 6 cannot open — over an incomplete rendering layer.
* Invariants PTA-401 through PTA-406 and decision records ADR-593 through ADR-596 fix Part 5 as a frozen, correct execution layer that no later part may relax, and establish that correctness dominates performance.
* Together these provide sufficient detail for Codex to implement the Part-5-to-Part-6 handoff — verifying every rendering acceptance criterion, enforcing the six jointly-binding invariant families, exposing the validated assets, authoritative manifest, telemetry, cost surface, and bounded parallel profile the performance layer optimises against, and rejecting any performance path that would produce a result the Rendering Platform would not accept — without permitting Part 6 or any later part to relax a rendering invariant in pursuit of a performance target.


---
